item.title: [Business of Sport] F1 Returns: The Rules Have Changed…BUT Have They Done Enough?
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个意外的观察：F1 正在享受"声誉的复利红利"

最让我意外的不是规则失败本身，而是这次失败几乎没有引发问责风暴。两位主持人无意中点出了一个深刻的系统性现象——当一个组织积累了足够厚的"叙事资本"（Liberty Media 接手以来的成功故事），它就获得了一种近乎金融意义上的"信誉对冲"：同样的失败事件，落在 Chelsea 这样的"负面叙事"组织上会被解读为体制性溃烂，落在 F1 身上却被自动翻译为"敏捷迭代"。

这其实是叙事层面的马太效应。声誉不只是结果，它本身就成了下一轮判决的输入参数。一个 20 岁车手以 50G 撞墙——在另一个叙事环境下，这可能就是头条丑闻和高层下台；在 F1 这里，它被吸收进"功能良好的治理系统"的故事里。Bman 的撞车不是规则的反例，反而被叙事吞噬成了规则有效性的证据。

跨领域连接：F1 的"超车通胀"和社交媒体的"参与度通胀"是同一个病

Verstappen 嘲讽这是"马里奥赛车"——这句话比表面看起来更尖锐。F1 此次改革的副作用是让超车从稀缺事件变成了常态事件，而稀缺性正是戏剧性的来源。这和社交媒体平台为了 KPI 不断降低互动门槛是完全同构的现象：当你把"点赞"从一种判断动作变成滑动反射，每个赞的信号价值就趋近于零。

银石赛道全场起立的那一刻，本质上是一种信息论意义上的高熵事件——因为超车困难，所以一次成功超车携带的信息量极大，观众的情绪共振也最强。一旦超车被工程化为每圈都发生的事情，它就从"事件"退化为"背景噪音"。F1 的真正风险不是技术规则失败，而是它可能在不自知中把自己最值钱的资产——稀缺性——给稀释掉了。这是体育版的"格雷欣法则"：低质量的超车驱逐高质量的超车。

隐藏的系统性真相：B 队结构是体育的"影子银行"

关于 Toto Wolff 想买 Alpine 这一段，最深的洞察其实主持人没有明说——多队所有权问题在结构上等同于金融体系里的"影子银行"：它存在于规则的灰色边缘，名义上是独立实体，实际上承担着主体的风险敞口和战略意图。Red Bull 的二队在 2005 年被允许，是因为当时 F1 处于"流动性危机"（凑不齐发车格），任何愿意进场的资本都是救星。这恰好平行 2008 年监管者放任影子银行扩张的逻辑——危机时期的应急豁免，在繁荣期变成了系统性套利通道。

Zak Brown 提到 Ricciardo 抢最快圈的那个细节是关键证据：协调行为不需要明文指令就能发生，仅仅是所有权结构本身就改变了博弈的纳什均衡。UEFA 在 Crystal Palace 案中的强硬处理透露了一个深层原则——在体育运动中，"感知的公正"比"实际的公正"更重要，因为这门生意的产品本质上是不确定性，而不确定性一旦被怀疑被操纵，整个产品就崩塌了。

这也解释了为什么 F1 现在反而更难处理这个问题：当资产价值从"赠送"变成"数十亿"，过去的临时安排就被资本化、合法化、路径依赖化。Liberty 真正面临的考验不是混合动力的工程问题，而是如何在不引爆既得利益的前提下，把当年危机时期的"应急 patch"从系统中拆除。这和拆除任何成熟监管体系里的影子结构一样——拖得越久，拆除成本越呈指数级上升。

📝 转录文本

开场：F1 回归与五周空档

Charlie: Formula 1 is back. The Miami Grand Prix this weekend. So, it's it's probably a bit of a relief to the rulers of the sport that they've had this break.

Well, it's funny you should say that. It's very funny you should say that cuz — and I wouldn't often say that. Normally, it'd be an absolute nightmare, but it sort of feels to me like they've needed this break.

Well, yes. And why have they needed this break?

And I think they can look at it. So, just if everyone remembers, this break happened between Suzuka and Japan and now the Miami Grand Prix because there were two races scheduled in the Middle East — one in Bahrain and one in Saudi in Jeddah — and with the Iran war, the US–Iran war, those two races were canceled. So that creates a five-week gap in the calendar.

Normally, I don't know how that would be felt — you know, in a year where there hadn't been massive regulation change and everything was as normal, would have been quite an interesting time to ask some of these team principals whether the breaking momentum would have harmed them. However, as has been talked of so much around this Formula 1 season, this year was a standout year. It was the most radical regulation changes in F1 history had been implemented as they moved to a hybrid car. So half electric, half combustion.

规则刚改完，又改规则

The question that I think we need to really look at here is: in that time period that we just had in those 5 weeks, why has there had to be regulation change already to the regulation changes?

So there have been a number of changes that have been put in by the FIA and Formula 1 that have addressed issues that came up in those first few races but also in testing before the start of the season, which drivers and team principals had raised as concerns for mostly safety of the drivers.

Now, why did it take for a 20-year-old to smash into a wall at 194 km an hour, 50 gs of force, to make this happen? You said something to me the other day that was quite interesting and I'm going to look at it on both sides and see your thoughts, but you think that Formula 1's got off a little lightly with these changes because this has been positioned as such a momentous moment in F1's history.

批评视角 vs 正面视角

Now, I'll present you a critical and a positive. I kind of wrote two down and just let's start with that and see your thoughts.

So if I'm being critical about changes having to be made within the first three weeks, I'd go: this is a governance failure.

Yeah. Shambles.

It's a potentially very costly one.

Yeah.

Um, everything's been built up to this moment and you're basically saying, well, you got that wrong.

Yeah.

If I'm putting the positive case across, it's: new regulation is unlikely to ever be perfect. Acting within three races with unanimous buy-in from all stakeholders is actually a sign of a functioning system.

Two things can be true at the same time. It can be a total shambles which is then addressed in a competent way.

为什么说 F1 "侥幸过关"

So what did I mean by saying I think they got off lightly? Well, because when you start a sporting competition and within a couple of months of starting that sporting competition you have to rip up your rule book because it hasn't worked for whatever reason, that is a crashing failure. Crushing and crashing.

And it's the type of thing under normal circumstances which would get people pointing fingers left, right, and center. It was his fault. It's that fault, or should never have been here, or shouldn't have been there. Sackings, resignations, and all this type of stuff.

I think the general sentiment around Formula 1 over the last 5 years has been so positive, and the view about what Liberty have done since taking over the running of Formula 1 has been so positive, that there's this sort of inset narrative that basically: sport's going great, industry's going great, everything's great, and the people that run it are great, and so on and so forth. So that when something terrible happens, there's almost a sort of see-no-evil hear-no-evil. "Oh, um, right, we have to change a few regulations. Oh, that's a bit embarrassing. Uh, well, it's still all great by the way."

叙事的力量：以 Chelsea 作对比

Whereas if you look at, for instance, on a different topic — once a narrative sets in, like it has done in your head and the general sporting public's head, that the people running Chelsea are a bunch of muppets who don't know what they're doing, then even things which happen which actually are not that abnormal suddenly become supposedly symptomatic of much broader failures, right? And which maybe they are, right? It's not abnormal for a manager to be fired, but the extent to which that's taken on an existential meaning, a much broader meaning, is because of an established narrative that's been taking place over a period of time.

And F1's rulers are benefiting right now from the exact opposite, which is: there is an established narrative that the people who are running Formula 1 are brilliant, know what they're doing, really get it, etc. So that when something terrible happens like this, it's almost sort of: "Well, it clearly can't really be their fault really because they are geniuses who know exactly what they're doing. So it's just an adjustment we need to make and let's just try and brush it under the carpet and forget it ever happened."

这次失误的代价究竟多大？

So I guess it's a way of saying I don't want to sort of hang people out to dry just for the sake of it. But we should acknowledge — you know, when we're talking about, I think, you know, we asked the question last week with reference to Live about great sporting disasters, great sporting commercial disasters, and we were saying CrickIt, right, six billion on something like that — that surely is the biggest sporting disaster of all time without any question, etc. — which it is.

And people then were sending in other stuff, like for instance talking about, you know, Michael Johnson's athletics Grand Slam Track that we were discussing just now, which lost 30 million bucks. I rather suspect this mistake costs more than 30 million bucks. I just gently suggest that this is quite a large sum of money that's going to be wasted here.

Oh, completely. And if you think about the broader value of what this regulation change brings in — if it actually isn't that consumable and people don't enjoy it, and the drivers don't like it, and the success that the sport's quickly grown and the financial power that it has being then corrupted and harnessed — people have said, well, you know, that impact is going to be huge. So to make the change is not good.

Bearman 撞车事件还原

But, you know, let's just — I want to just be really clear that people understand this because it has been a bit technical, and even these changes have been a bit technical. So, you know, I wrote some stuff on it beforehand, but I mentioned the Ollie Bearman crash in Suzuka.

So he was basically approaching Franco Colapinto, who drives for Alpine, at a closing speed of 50 km an hour in a turn — not because either of them were driving recklessly, but because the regulations basically created this situation. Now what do I mean by that? Bearman was deploying his battery boost mode. So something that's come in with the electric.

So what was the speed again?

50 km an hour. I mean really we're talking 190 he crashed at. So Bearman was deploying his battery boost mode.

Yeah.

Colapinto was harvesting his — like super clipping it's called — which basically meant the car effectively is decelerating. Bearman goes off the track going round and at a huge speed of 190 km an hour and hits the wall with 50 gs of force.

Now, the stewards investigated this and they didn't take any action against either driver, and their reasoning was clear on that because the fault was structural. It wasn't human.

车手的预警：Norris 与 Sainz

Now, Charlie, if you remember, we've talked about some great quotes from drivers — mostly laughing at some of Max Verstappen's comments being rather unhappy — but, you know, that there was one in particular that stood out for me from Lando Norris that we had brought up before, right after the Melbourne Grand Prix, so the first race of the season. And he said, "It's chaos and we're going to have a big accident."

Carlos Sainz followed that up just after Suzuka when Bearman had crashed and he said, "We've been warning this kind of accident will always happen. In Suzuka we were lucky there was an escape road. Imagine going off in Baku or Singapore or Las Vegas with those speeds."

Now just to be clear for people that don't know, those are what you call city tracks. They are very close, tight-walled races. Whereas the racetrack, you know, you normally have far long sweeping corners which give you some chance to decelerate — but still hit it pretty hard.

改动的方向与历史先例

The four to five changes that have been made — without getting too technical again for people — they're basically done to improve safety.

What I did want to say is, you know, F1 has a history of regulation change. What's happened this year isn't new, and sometimes it does take a long time for them to actually prove valuable, why, you know, why they work.

The ground effect cars in the 1980s produced aerodynamic instability. Um, if you — well, I don't remember that, but you probably will. The refueling era which then came after that created a serious fire hazard in a lot of the cars.

But I'm looking at this again from the positive side of going: well, they've made a change relatively soon after implemented because it's been seen to be dangerous.

Toto Wolff 的"手术刀"比喻

But I think Toto Wolff's key point on this seems about right, because he basically called for a scalpel, not a baseball bat. They've taken swift action. They haven't let the whole thing play out over 6 months, 8 months, or even to the end of the season with problems escalating left, right, and center. They've jumped in and they've gone, "Okay, we've taken it on board. We are changing it."

And I guess you would say that is the sign of functionality within a sport and an industry. They haven't waited for the whole thing to become sort of, you know, too dramatic, too bad. They have applied their scalpel quite quickly and efficiently. Well, we hope efficiently. We haven't seen yet. We haven't seen what the outturn of it is, but we assume that they've got it right the second time around.

No. And we'll get a good grasp of it this weekend and see, and I'll be interested to see how the drivers speak about it as well. Drivers interviewed throughout an entire weekend.

对观众而言：超车多就是好吗？

Yeah, it will. I think that the other point which you kind of touched on at the start of this conversation is: we still don't know yet what the impact this is going to be on viewers. Um, on whether these changes actually are good for racing, full stop.

Because the outturn from an uneducated perspective — so from a normal viewer who just likes watching cars racing, as opposed to the more technical level of people who's really interested in the intricacies of what's happening with the engines — basically, what this has led to is more overtaking.

So the question is — you know, something that Max Verstappen is sneery about and which Lewis Hamilton says he likes — we don't yet know whether the viewing public likes to see what I think it was Verstappen or somebody else called "Mario Racing", where it's literally backwards and forwards, backwards and forwards, backwards and forwards. Or if, you know, actually they prefer it when an overtaking maneuver happens but it's a rare event. So when it does happen, it's actually dramatic.

Because I have to say, when I've been live, been at Silverstone or when I was working on the Bahrain Grand Prix, and you're watching the race and you're aware that it's a big battle and it's very hard to get past — when the overtaking maneuver eventually happens, the whole place lifts off, because they're in recognition that was an amazing piece of driving to be able to pull that off under the circumstances.

So, I just am interested on whether making it a more common occurrence genuinely adds to the sport for the mass viewing public, or it doesn't.

I have found the first few races an enjoyable consumer experience, but I also firmly understand that what we do not want to see is a return to days where drivers get in cars, have major accidents, and die.

Yeah, absolutely.

So, you know, I want to enjoy it, but I don't want it to be dangerous to do so.

转向：Toto Wolff 与 Alpine 收购争议

Finally, just on Formula 1 before we move on — have you seen some of the comments that Zak Brown's made about the potential Toto Wolff?

Yes. Yes. Now, this is something that we highlighted quite early on in our breakdown journey — is that there was the possibility, or in fact not the possibility, there was a sort of corporate battle going on in terms of who would be able to buy — which team was it? Is it, Charlie?

The Alpine team. And there were two contenders for this. One of which was a consortium led by Christian Horner coming back into the sport to introduce a new independent team that would be able to compete. And the other one of which was Toto Wolff, the Mercedes boss, effectively being allowed to buy up a second team to help his overall effort within within the industry.

And I think at the time our conclusion was that we wanted Horner back and we wanted another independent team. We wanted more competition.

What's Zak Brown said about it?

Zak Brown 的立场

Well, Zak's actually — Zak Brown being the McLaren boss. Zak Brown is the CEO of McLaren. He said something very similar. Zak's always been very consistent on his position on this.

So I'm going to say this 'cuz he's treading a slightly careful line in saying that he doesn't want Toto and Mercedes to have it because Mercedes supply the power unit for McLaren. So he needs to have a good relationship there. But I think he can say what he said because he has always been very consistent. He does not agree with teams with an A and B team structure, should we say. He doesn't agree, in this day and age where Formula 1 is so strong, two owners having two teams.

Yeah.

Sorry. The same owner having two teams.

Yes.

Alpine 的所有权拆解

Otro Capital and Renault own Alpine. Otro Capital is a financier. They have 24%. Renault is the car manufacturer. They have 76% — but, key, a bit Jim Ratcliffe and Man United: Otro Capital's 24% is, like, the — should we say — the sporting operational side. So whoever owns that 24% will have a huge say of how the team is run.

Zak's key point, right, is that it creates unfair advantages for teams to have a direct relationship. And he's not wrong. Of course he's not wrong.

历史回顾：Red Bull 的二队为何被允许

The reason it exists in one part on the grid at the moment in Red Bull and Racing Bulls — now the environment for Formula 1 was very, very different when that deal happened. It happened in 2005 where teams were basically not valuable assets. They were being given away pretty much.

They weren't just not valuable, Charlie. There were seasons. There were seasons when you couldn't fill the whole grid up.

Mhm.

So anyone who wanted a team — yeah — you were kind of like, "Come in and do it." So, Red Bull taking two — brilliant. That's one less we have to worry about.

Absolutely.

Now, fast forward 21 years, the sport is in a completely different place and these are now multi-billion assets even at the back end of the grid. So, Zak's point is this compromises sporting integrity and fairness. The sport is in the strongest it has ever been. And his quote: "Can you imagine a Premier League game where you've got two teams competing owned by the same person?"

Yeah. No. Couldn't. It's banned. Right. Can't have that.

对现状的呼吁

So, he's basically — he has previously been suggesting that, you know, FIA should force Red Bull to sell one of those teams.

Yeah.

Now, that's of the existing landscape, let alone letting Mercedes purchase a very significant stake in another team.

不公平的具体证据

And he, you know, previously has highlighted examples of why it's unfair. Daniel Ricciardo, which we've mentioned before, in 2024, took the fastest lap, basically changed his tires for the last lap of the race and took it off Lando Norris, which got an extra point at the time that had a say in the title in the championship race.

But also, this is the other interesting one which people, you know, must consider — and it's the movement of the executive between teams. So Red Bull, when Christian Horner left, just took Laurent Mekies from Racing Bulls, a very respected and established engineer/team principal. McLaren have just signed Verstappen's race engineer called Gianpiero Lambiase from Red Bull, but he can't join until 2028 because he has to see out his contract at McLaren.

The free flowing movement of executives on one side versus we're getting a top level engineer from another and we have to pay compensation and wait for his contract to end. So there's a driver perspective and what happens on the track. There's a development perspective — what happens behind the scenes.

Toto 的辩解与足球类比

Toto, very specifically to be clear, this is not a feeder team play. His quote is "No," and I'm telling you in big capital letters, "No, we do not want to create a feeder team."

Well, but surely it will be.

Look, there's a very good reason why in football, not only you not allowed to own two clubs in the same domestic ecosystem — even at UEFA level, as became clear with the whole controversy over Crystal Palace last summer, where the fact that John Textor had owned 40% of Palace, but also owned Olympique Lyon, that was enough basically to push Palace out of the Europa League.

Okay. So that even though they were in totally different countries, even though the likelihood of them actually ending up facing each other was very, very small, Palace were pushed down and out of the Europa League because UEFA couldn't accept it.

假想情景：感知与现实的双重风险

It's partially to do with reality. So let's just imagine that there was a situation where you were coming to the last race of the season and it was a pitch battle, as you would want it to be, between Mercedes and McLaren, and there was a situation that happened out on the track where Toto Wolff, being desperate for Mercedes to win, was in a position to influence how his other team acted during that race.

No one can tell me there wouldn't be more than a temptation — a major, serious, obvious incentive — to try and control things in that way, as you mentioned with Ricciardo in the Red Bull group.

But then there's also perception as well, and perception is really important because ultimately it's all about the punters. It's all about the audience that drives the commercials and drives the TV audience.

So even if Toto could put his hand on his heart and say, "No, I would never do that," the questions would be out there every race. "Surely he must be directing what's going on. He controls the sporting operation there. He controls a sporting operation here." That is a major perception problem. You just can't get around it. It just is.

结论：需求已变，规则该变

So I think, you know, you and I agreed on this 20 years ago. It was a question of needs must, and that's when you had Red Bull and Toro Rosso, as it was then called. And Bernie, who was running the sport at the time, needed to fill out the grid. "Okay, if you guys want to have two teams, have two teams, because I need to make sure we've got enough cars racing. Otherwise, we got a serious problem."

They don't need that anymore. If there is a spare place on the grid for an expansion franchise, let's say — imagine what that would go for. It would go for absolutely vast amounts of money.

So, there's no need to bend to somebody's desire to start monopolizing different parts of the grid. And I agree with Zak Brown. I think there should be a three-year period when Red Bull are told, "Right, over the next three years you need to divest that team." Right? It's a seller's market. Why wouldn't you? I mean, make a lot of money out of it — going to make a lot of money out of it. But from the sporting perspective, the more competitive teams you have on the grid, the better.

Completely agree.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个意外的观察：F1 正在享受"声誉的复利红利"
最让我意外的不是规则失败本身，而是**这次失败几乎没有引发问责风暴**。两位主持人无意中点出了一个深刻的系统性现象——当一个组织积累了足够厚的"叙事资本"（Liberty Media 接手以来的成功故事），它就获得了一种近乎金融意义上的"信誉对冲"：同样的失败事件，落在 Chelsea 这样的"负面叙事"组织上会被解读为体制性溃烂，落在 F1 身上却被自动翻译为"敏捷迭代"。
这其实是**叙事层面的马太效应**。声誉不只是结果，它本身就成了下一轮判决的输入参数。一个 20 岁车手以 50G 撞墙——在另一个叙事环境下，这可能就是头条丑闻和高层下台；在 F1 这里，它被吸收进"功能良好的治理系统"的故事里。Bman 的撞车不是规则的反例，反而被叙事吞噬成了规则有效性的证据。
## 跨领域连接：F1 的"超车通胀"和社交媒体的"参与度通胀"是同一个病
Verstappen 嘲讽这是"马里奥赛车"——这句话比表面看起来更尖锐。F1 此次改革的副作用是**让超车从稀缺事件变成了常态事件**，而稀缺性正是戏剧性的来源。这和**社交媒

item.title: [All-In Podcast] OpenAI Misses Targets, Codex vs Claude, Elon vs Sam Trial, Big Hyperscaler Beats, Peptide Craze
item.dc:creator: All-In Podcast
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

三个让人意外的系统性观察

1. 这一集的真正主角不是 OpenAI，而是"电力"——AI 行业正在经历"软件公司物理化"的隐秘逆转

表面上大家在讨论 OpenAI 错失目标、Codex vs Claude 的对决。但 Chamath 不经意间揭示了一个被严重低估的范式转移：AI 公司正在从"轻资产软件"重新坍缩回"重资产工业"。微软为了三里岛的核电，签下了 2 倍现货价格的远期购电协议——这不是软件公司的行为，这是 19 世纪末美国铁路公司的行为。

让人意外的是：在 2000 年互联网泡沫中破产的不是思科，是 WorldCom 这种铺光纤的电信公司。如果历史押韵，真正会出问题的不是 OpenAI 本身，而是为它建数据中心、铺电网、签长期 PPA 的"新电信公司"——也就是今天的超大规模云厂商。 Sax 说"没有暗 GPU"是对的，但他忽略了：暗 GPU 不会出现，暗变压器、暗气体涡轮、暗审批许可正在堆积。Chamath 提到"已宣布项目里 40% 会被取消"，这跟 2001 年的光纤 over-build 几乎是同一种结构性误差，只是这次卡在电力供给端而不是需求端。

2. Greg Brockman 的日记是一个跨领域信号——AI 时代的"discovery maxing"将重塑高管行为学

Jason 那句调侃"is you taking notes on a criminal conspiracy"其实指向了一个正在浮现的、非常严肃的现象：当 LLM 可以在毫秒内检索、归纳、总结任何文本，"写下来"这个行为本身的风险溢价正在指数级上升。

跨领域连接：这与对冲基金 1990 年代发现"电邮 = 永久证据"后产生的 email hygiene 完全同构。当年华尔街花了十年才学会"不要在邮件里写下你真正在想什么"。接下来五年，硅谷创始人会经历同样的痛苦学习曲线，但这次速度会更快、也更隐蔽——因为打开 ChatGPT 输入"帮我整理我对这个董事会的真实想法"，本身就是在创造一份可被传唤的证据链。 Brockman 不是 outlier，他是先驱：未来每一位创始人的 prompt 历史，都是潜在的 deposition exhibit。

3. 这一集的隐藏主题是"代理责任真空"——从 vibe coding 到 retatrutide，再到最高法院 Monsanto 案，三个看似无关的故事在讲同一件事

把这三件事并排看，会发现一个让人后背发凉的同构：

  • Vibe coding 删库事件：AI agent 删了 Pocket OS 的生产数据库，没有人能被"问责"——是开发者？Cursor？Anthropic？还是那个 9 秒钟的随机决策？
  • Retatrutide 狂潮：人们把临床三期数据当成"上升的斜率"，但谁为脱靶代谢效应负责？Lilly？开处方的医生？还是 Reddit 上那个匿名分享 protocol 的"swim"？
  • Monsanto vs EPA：整个案件的核心问题是——当联邦机构"批准"了某物，州法院是否还能让生产者承担"未警告责任"？ 这是一个纯粹的"责任分配"危机。

跨领域真相：我们正在同时在三个维度（软件、生物、化学）失去"谁为系统输出负责"的清晰链条。 Sax 的论断"agent 必须被监督，必须有人 accountable"不只是工程问题——它和 EPA 联邦优先权的法律问题、和 GLP-1 长期使用的医疗问责问题，是同一个深层结构。

最大胆的预测：未来五年最值钱的不是模型公司，也不是芯片公司，而是"算法责任审计层"——能在 AI agent、生物制剂、化学产品之间提供可追溯、可保险化、可法律化的"问责痕迹"的基础设施。CrowdStrike 之于网络安全的位置，将出现在 AI 行为审计、临床真实世界证据、化学暴露追踪三个领域。今天还没人在做，但这是必然出现的"因果链商品化"赛道。

附赠一个反常识洞见：Jason 说的"retard maxing——别写日记、别 ruminate、就一直往前推"看起来是搞笑梗，但它和 Sax 关于 AI agent 的话其实是镜像——人和 agent 的最优策略恰好相反：agent 必须被监督、必须暂停、必须在 destructive action 前自问 "am I sure"；而人类反而要少自问、少回顾、多行动。这暗示了一个深层人机分工原则：未来"思考密度"会从人移动到 agent，"行动密度"会从 agent 移动到人——而不是反过来。

📝 转录文本

开场：Miss Thing Pod 的"Gay Name or Straight Name"

Jason, do you want to tell us about your new favorite podcast?

Oh, it's so good. My feed is now — because, you know, since cancel culture ended, Sacks, everybody uses the R word and the F word right now. My entire feed on Instagram is either gay, or down syndrome, or bulldogs. It's one of those three. And then I stumbled upon the Miss Thing Pod — Miss Thing — and they do a bit called "Gay Name, Straight Name."

Here's "Gay Name or Straight Name" for David. This is good news and bad news, Freberg. Here we go.

Gay Name or Straight Name. David. David to me is straight. Okay. But he has my perfect body. It can be confusing because I'm kind of like, "Are you gay?" And it's like, no, I just want to be you, David. Totally. Well, it's so like the Michelangelo's David, the male ideal. It's like incredible body. Yeah. Kind of small. Sorry. Yeah.

Oh, it's a little rough. What are you watching there, J. Cal? They basically nailed these two, but okay, keep going. I don't think Chamath is on their short list, but I know Jason will come up at some point.

Gay name or straight name. Maybe this is it. Chamath. On the count of three. Three, two, one — gay. I'm thinking like Italian sweater, like really kind of like a loud, vibrant sweater. He like wears it to like poker night with his like his boys — and like, not, I'm not talking like straight poker. I'm talking like gay poker nights, like at the bar. Always talking about wine. Talks about wine so much. Always sort of like swishing it. Swirls. Yeah, exactly. Yep. Also, it's so like the guy at the gym taking off his shirt, taking selfies. Photos. Yeah, and everyone else is kind of like, "excuse me Chamath, I'd like to use the mirror." Yeah. I'd like to see myself. See you at the next day poker night. Totally. Can you bring the wine? We'll bring the sweater. Yeah.

There it is. Wow. They did do a Chamath. That is fantastic. That is fantastic. A shout out to my guys at the Miss Thing Podcast. Wow. That was awesome. I think I'm gay. I never knew.

We'll let your winners ride. Rain Man David Sacks. And instead, we open source it to the fans and they've just gone crazy with it. Love you, Ben. Nice.

What did you do? Like cameo? Did you pay them to do that? Did it for me as a favor. So I did it. That's awesome. Well, thanks to those guys. Thanks to the Miss Thing Pod. I've seen those guys before in clips. I find them very funny. It's so great. Shout out to my guys. That was awesome.

All right, everybody. Seriously, welcome back to the number one podcast in the world. It's the All-In Podcast with me again, David Freberg, Chamath Palihapitiya, David Sacks. And of course, I'm Jason Calacanis. You can call me J Cal if you're here for the first time.

话题一：OpenAI 错失目标 & ChatGPT 5.5 vs Opus 4.7

Topic one. OpenAI — they missed their targets for ChatGPT, Friedberg, both on users and revenue. Let's talk about it.

The Wall Street Journal says in a breaking investigative report on Tuesday that OpenAI expected to hit one billion WAUs — weekly active users — before the end of 2025. They missed that, and they still haven't hit the milestone four months into 2026. Also, Chamath, they missed their 2025 revenue target for ChatGPT. Exact number wasn't specified, but as we've talked about here, they're at a $20–$30 billion run rate. There's a little bit of accounting nuance that is yet to be worked out in the industry.

Two reasons why this matters, Sacks. OpenAI has $600 billion in spending commitments for compute. Just to put that in perspective, that's about what they're trading for on secondary markets — in other words, the entire value of the OpenAI enterprise equals their spend commitments in the coming year. CFO Sarah Friar, who is coming to Liquidity, is reportedly worried — hey, that revenue isn't growing fast enough to keep up with expense. And OpenAI wants to IPO later this year. This has put Friar and Altman in conflict, or maybe there's some natural tension there. Friar doesn't think OpenAI is ready for public reporting standards, according to the Wall Street Journal. Altman obviously wants to move faster. So they released a joint statement: "this is ridiculous, yada, yada, yada."

Let's go to you, Sacks. What do you think is going on here? Are these major headwinds, or is this just managing expectations as the leader of the pack in the most important race of our lifetimes — the race towards superintelligence?

Sacks 的反共识观点：产品层面 OpenAI 其实赢了

Well, I actually have a little bit of a contrarian take on this. I know that OpenAI had a really bad week. Like you said, they had that Wall Street Journal article, which said that they've missed their numbers. They missed their 1 billion user growth target. They missed their revenue numbers. That's called into question whether they can afford the data center commitments that they've made. And then in addition to that, they've also had the lawsuit with Elon happening this week. So in the press, it ended up being, I think, a pretty bad week for them.

But I have a contrarian take on this, which is — I think that over the past week or two, if you look at kind of what's happening at the product level, it's been a pretty good couple of weeks for them. They released ChatGPT 5.5, and the reviews from people I talk to in Silicon Valley have been really strong. You talk to developers, coders — they're very happy with it.

At the same time, Opus 4.7, which is the latest Anthropic release, appears to be a bust. People are complaining about it. In a lot of cases, they're rolling back to 4.6. They're saying that Opus 4.7 is rationing compute. It's reducing thinking time. Not as good. There were some bugs in Claude. So if you just compare ChatGPT 5.5 to Opus 4.7, it does appear that OpenAI has had a better couple of weeks on a product level.

And I think there's reason to believe that the product improvements will continue. GPT 5.5 is based on a new base model called Spud, which is the first base model upgrade they've done in, I don't know, over a year. And having a new base model will pave the way for future improvements as well. I think OpenAI is feeling pretty optimistic about their product right now. And I think you're starting to see on X, some of the developer mojo is shifting. I'm seeing a lot of people saying that they are shifting their coding usage from Opus to GPT 5.5.

So I think that Sam may end up being right, but for the wrong reason. And what I mean by that is — when he made these big compute commitments, it was based on those estimates of hitting the billion users on the consumer side and hitting those revenue targets. The consumer business ended up being weak. So they missed those targets. But in the meantime, coding has become the all-important sector of AI. And because they made all these compute commitments and they built out these data centers, they have more compute than Anthropic right now. Anthropic is token constrained. It's reducing their ability to serve Mythos, for example. It's causing them to engage in compute gating with Opus 4.7. And I understand why Dario made that decision. I'm not saying — I mean, it was a prudent business decision. I'm not criticizing him for it. But I think, again, I think Sam may end up being right here for the wrong reason, which is — he missed on consumer, but enterprise is going gangbusters and is giving him the ability now, I think, to catch up on coding.

Cursor, Polymarket — which is the all-important market right now. Of course. And we talked about Grok and Cursor teaming up last week. Elon and the team over there. Polymarket showing now a 32% chance that OpenAI goes public by the end of 2026. This is down from 60% in December.

Chamath：真正的瓶颈是电力，不是需求

And Chamath, you gave a bit of a warning. Hey, there's only so many dollars to go around. SpaceX IPO is obviously getting our first. And now if OpenAI doesn't go out this year, and Anthropic does, this sets up an interesting dynamic. What are your thoughts here, generally speaking, about the massive commitment that OpenAI has made? Are they gonna run off the cliff or will it wind up being brilliant — even if it wasn't strategically — for the exact reasons?

I think they're gonna be fine. I think this is a multi-trillion dollar company. I think Anthropic is a multi-trillion dollar company. I think the thing that's happening right now is a complete misunderstanding of what's actually happening inside of the world of AI. And there is one very specific choke point that is constraining everything, which is access to the power that's necessary to drive these tokens.

To the extent that OpenAI missed, I think what that is, is an insight to not enough compute capacity today. And that problem is only getting worse. You've already seen that with Anthropic as well, where they just found a way to economically induce Amazon to give them enough capacity so that you don't have to route through Bedrock to get to the Anthropic models. You're also seeing them do differentiated deals now with economic participation on top of what they already had from folks like Google to give them more capacity.

What is my point? Everything in this market is power constrained. The reason that these folks may miss a number or a forecast have nothing to do with demand. It is entirely 100% due to the supply of the power necessary to generate the output token.

There was a really interesting thing that was just announced today will make this problem even worse, which is — what you're starting to see now is backlogs build up of not just the access to the power, but then the componentry that's actually necessary, not just receipts and not just nat-gas turbines, but now you're talking about transformers and all the actual tactical grid infrastructure. Why is this important? If you look at the actual amount of gigawatts that are under construction, we have a huge mismatch now. People have announced all these projects, Jason, but less than half of it is actually being built. Less than half. Most of it is stuck in red tape. Most of that is because there are the supply chain delays, so there's no credible strategy to turn any of this stuff on.

Who will this hurt? It will hurt Anthropic and OpenAI the most. Who will this benefit? It will benefit the hyperscalers, specifically Oracle, Amazon, Meta, Microsoft, and Google. And now what you're going to see is a negotiation and a trade back and forth: how much equity do I have to give up? How much control do I have to give up to get access to the compute, versus how badly will I miss my growth forecasts if I don't?

And now what that means is — and we spoke about this last week — that's a huge lane for Grok to just run through, and SpaceX to run through, because they have a ton of excess capacity. And so I think the Cursor deal was the appetizer. But if I were Elon now, I'd be running all over this market, because if the models catch up in quality, I think he could also do something really crazy with Anthropic or OpenAI right now. Maybe not OpenAI because of the —

We'll get into the lawsuit in a moment. The baggage. Yeah. But man, he and Dario should do a deal tomorrow.

So you're framing — hey, the limited resource here is compute. The demand is off the charts. No, the limiting resource is power. Power, which then powers compute, which then provides tokens, which then services the massive developer and co-work and all these other projects that consumers and enterprises can't get enough of. Got it.

And Jason, the other factor that complicates that for Anthropic and OpenAI is all the stuff that's sort of sitting around thumb-twiddling — 40% of that is going to get canceled because they've done such a poor job of creating a good positive halo around AI, that 40% of all the announced projects get canceled, because 40% of all projects in the last four years have been canceled. Yeah, and there are some bad feelings about data centers, AI, jobs, etc. And that's causing some headwind. People are literally doing violent things in society and blaming data centers and AI for it. I don't want to give it too much airtime.

Freberg：BCG 的 4-2-1 法则与 MIT 的剪枝论文

Freberg, what's your take on the chessboard we're looking at here, either through compute, energy, or through going public on a business level? You know, the strategic nature of capital, compute, and energy now playing a role in this massive amount of demand. Still a ball in the air kind of game.

BCG had this — I think I talked about this once before — called the rule of three, where they've shown time and again that any stable, mature, competitive market evolves to a four-to-two-to-one ratio of market share for basically 90% of the market. So there's a market leader that has four times the market share of the second place. That's two times the market share of the third place. This is the case in pretty much every mature kind of competitive market.

So you can kind of think about AI probably evolving into a consumer market and an enterprise market. OpenAI, even if they're not at a billion, they're still at 900 million weekly users, which is well ahead of whatever Claude is at. I think Claude is probably sub-100 million, Sacks, you may know. And then Gemini is probably closer to them at 700 to a billion, somewhere in that range, probably pretty neck and neck with OpenAI.

So, you know, the consumer market looks like it's trending towards a ChatGPT slash Google fight for first place and second place, and then probably Anthropic in third place. And maybe Elon emerges and takes off, enabled by his compute capacity. And then the enterprise market is a little bit of a different story. And that's its own market, which is kind of Anthropic, or probably Google in the lead, actually, if you look at all the Vertex use. Google claims that 75% of GCP customers are active users of Vertex. So there's probably a pretty sizable market share that Google's captured on the enterprise side as well. This is also probably why Google stock has absolutely ripped over the last couple of months — is they're literally in first place or fighting for first place in enterprise and consumer.

But I still think that there's a lot of opportunity, to Chamath's point about the compute and energy capacity constraints, in improving how we actually scale and deploy models in both the enterprise and the consumer setting. And it is such early days. And I just want to highlight this paper that came out from MIT from these two scientists. And these guys published a paper on pruning techniques in neural networks. This paper showed that you could actually reduce the size of these networks by 90% and get the same accuracy out, by pruning very large models down to smaller models, and then you can make a selection on which model to run for inference. And by doing this, you can actually reduce inference costs by 10x. You can get 10x the output per energy unit that goes into the data center with no loss of accuracy.

And so it's a really interesting, call it, algorithmic technique that can be applied to the existing large models to actually make them much lower energy use. So if you think about it, you're firing up a very large model to answer a very simple question. You can actually prune away that model. Now, this is probably going to be the case in AI applications as it is in traditional Google search. There's a long tail of searches, but there's a few searches that account for a large percentage of search volume. It's like — what is the weather? What are the movie times? What's the stock price? Like there's a certain set of things that make up the bulk of consumer energy. And there's probably a certain set of things that probably make up the bulk of coding output as well.

And so if you can get that 80% of searches or chat interfaces or coding requests reduced down through pruning techniques to smaller models, and then you have a whole set of smaller models that can be called dynamically, and you reduce inference costs by 90%, you can make much more use — call it 10 times the use — on data center and energy capacity than we can today. So I would argue that we're still in the very early days of getting efficiency in terms of output and tokens. And we're just in a very kind of early stage of that, which also unlocks the opportunity for guys like Elon to reinvent how this is done and potentially compete pretty aggressively.

SLM、VSLM 与谷歌的逆袭

There are two ways to win. You could throw compute at it, or you can do SLMs — small language models — and VSLMs, verticalized small language models. So if you had a verticalized small language model for the weather, let's say — that doesn't exist, but you can use it as an example. They will have one for travel as an example. When you hit Google for flight information, it's obviously going to route you to something lighter and faster that uses Google Flights, and Google Flights has been incorporated into Gemini. Gemini now is right behind — 700, 750 million users. And it's exactly what we discussed, I don't know, 18 months ago on this podcast, Freberg — that what if they put it at the top, and what would that do to their search revenue? Search revenue is surging, and they are also surging. So they figured out a way to balance those two competing forces, having search results that are AI-enabled and still getting people to click on links. They've done it brilliantly, apparently, and the stock is rewarding them.

I'll just add one statement to what you said, J. Cal, which is — like, you're using what I would call a human heuristic on smaller models. And I think what we're evolving to is — humans don't intuitively know what this model is. It's not just a verticalized model, but there are going to be models that will be discovered through automated pruning techniques that will then be working in concert. So lots of small models that link together, and we don't define each model by some human heuristic, like "this is a search travel model." Yeah, this is a maps model. We don't know why these models work the way they do when they get broken down, but I do think that that's really where the evolution is happening. So effectively a model becomes a macro model. It's got lots of smaller models underneath it that can be dynamically called, and that allows you to have 10x the inference for the same unit of energy.

Sacks：Codex 的反扑与竞争的重要性

Sacks. Let me just build on your point about Google, J. Cal. I would say that if there is a single reason why OpenAI did not hit its user targets and its revenue targets — certainly around consumer — you'd have to say it's because Google managed to take meaningful share. You know, they were basically nowhere a year or so ago. Sergey came out of retirement, helped focus the company. And like you said, they did a brilliant job improving Gemini and putting it at the top of search, incorporating it.

Now, that being said, again, I don't think the news is all bad for OpenAI, because I do think that the 5.5 release was great. We're hearing really good things about Codex. I do think that Codex is taking share in coding tokens right now. And I just think we're in a really interesting place where these companies are constantly one-upping each other.

I mean, two weeks ago, it looked like Anthropic was going to be completely dominant, right? I mean, Anthropic was growing at 10x, OpenAI was growing at 3x, and it looked like — and then the servers started going down. Did you see that this week, Sacks? The servers were going down. People in my office were complaining, "we can't get on Claude."

Listen, competition brings out the best in everyone. Anthropic forced OpenAI to compete. Google forced OpenAI to compete in consumer. I just hope the market stays competitive for as long as possible. I do think that's what's best for consumers, our economy, and for our country overall.

话题二：Cyber 战场——Mythos vs GPT-5.5 Cyber

Let me just say one other area where I think OpenAI had a good week — is in this red hot area of cyber. Obviously, Anthropic made a huge splash with Mythos. It hasn't been commercially released. They're compute constrained. But as a proof of concept or training model, it hit a new level of capabilities with cyber.

OpenAI has released a new model called GPT 5.5 Cyber, which has just been through a bunch of tests. And they've shown — this was testing done by the AI Security Institute — that GPT 5.5 is the second model to complete one of their multi-step cyber attack simulations end to end. So it has the same level of capability as Mythos, and it does appear to be commercially ready. They've got the compute to serve it.

So I do think that that's a big accomplishment. I mean, look, we knew that other cyber models were coming. It wasn't just going to be Mythos. In fact, within six months or so, all the frontier models are going to have Mythos-level cyber capability. But it's impressive that OpenAI got this GPT 5.5 cyber out so quickly. And I think 5.5 might be the first cyber model that cyber defenders actually get to use — because again, I don't think they're as compute constrained as Anthropic is.

And this is an incredible opportunity, you know, for the CrowdStrikes and Palo Alto Networks of the world, both of which have been on the program. They come out and they start attacking this space. Man, you could really see everything get tightened up. And this could be an incredible revenue stream for everybody who's got, whether it's Cursor, Claude, or OpenAI or Gemini. This is an amazing opportunity to tighten up — as much as it is to get attacked.

Cyber 不是末日机器：漏洞已经在那儿了

Can I make a point about that? Because there is so much fear right now, almost a level of panic about Mythos. People are treating it like a doomsday weapon or something like that. It's not. It's simply that the frontier models have reached the point where they're capable of automating cyber activities, just like they're capable of automating coding. But that means that a model could power up a cyber attacker or cyber defender the same way they can power up a coder, and allow them to discover a lot more vulnerabilities.

So there is obviously a risk there, but I think it's important to understand that Mythos or GPT 5.5 — it doesn't create the vulnerabilities. It just discovers them. The bugs were already in the code. They were sitting there waiting for some hacker to discover. If we can now use AI to find these bugs in advance, these vulnerabilities, and patch them, then you actually harden our infrastructure. Correct. And you harden our security.

I also believe that this leap from let's call it pre-AI cyber to post-AI cyber, it's going to be, I think, a big one-time upgrade cycle, because again, you're going to find all these dormant bugs and vulnerabilities. But I think that once we get past that upgrade cycle, you're going to reach a new equilibrium between AI-powered cyber offense and AI-powered cyber defense. It's going to become a lot more normal. It's not going to feel like this huge disruption. Which is to say, I think, you know, people are treating this like some existential threat. I don't think it is — as long as everyone does what they're supposed to do, which is use the new capabilities to harden their codebases and infrastructure and security before the hackers get hold of these capabilities.

Yeah. And if Chamath, if you were to look at this — to build on Sacks's point — there are about 5 million or so security experts in the world. And we talked about token costs — 40 hours of tokens, just pounding it, you know, a week. You could create another 5 million for $100 per chief security officer per security expert. So it's the volume of security expert agent. Sacks, to your point — yeah, you could have 50 million of them, 100 million of them. They're not finding right? Something also. They're just — yeah, they never sleep. They're relentless in their pursuit of these problems.

It's a really great point. Just kind of just refine that. So yeah, there's probably 5 million people in the cyber industry, but there's probably only a few thousand really elite hackers. Sure. Those hackers didn't have the time to go after the entire surface area of every possible attack vector out there. And so if you train a model to do what they do, obviously, like you said, it can operate with a scale and speed that a human hacker can't. So obviously, you know, what you need to do is get these tools in the hands of the white hats, let them do the cyber attacks themselves to then find the vulnerabilities and patch them before the black hats get hold of these capabilities.

But I think it's just one last point on this, and I'll stop, is just — it's really important to understand that the Chinese models are gonna have these capabilities within approximately six months. Oh, they have them now in DeepSeek 4 for sure. They've got some level. Well, no, DeepSeek 4 — I mean, DeepSeek 4 is impressive in a lot of ways, but its capability is not at the frontier. It's maybe 80, 85% of let's call it the American frontier.

Chamath：人 vs 机器 → 机器 vs 机器

Chamath, you wanted to get in on this. Let's get Chamath in.

Two things. The reason that this is even possible is because humans are error-prone. And when humans code, they create holes. And so humans exploiting humans is where we've been for a long time. Now we have computers exploiting humans, because the computers go and seek out all these bugs that humans wrote. In the next phase, it'll be machines versus machines.

And so I think the nature of cyber is going to completely change probably in the next five or six years — there'll be so much reason to rewrite all of the software that runs the world. In one part, because you're going to be asked to show more operating leverage and revenue growth. But in another part, because everything else that was handmade in the past is just fundamentally insecure. Either way, all roads will lead to: all the operational software that runs the world will get rewritten. More and more of it will be written by machines, more and more of it will be impregnable as a result. But then the cyber threat actually will only increase, because then you're going to try to figure out how to use a machine to inject something into another machine so that some agentic loop injects some malware or injects a bad token. And I think that's a very complicated thing.

What I will tell you is — I'm not even sure if I'm allowed to say this — but a very good, probably the best cybersecurity company in the world, run by one of the very best CEOs in the world, who may or may not be speaking at Liquidity, would tell you that they have penetrated and can essentially manipulate every model. Let me just say it roughly that way. Okay, perfect.

Yeah, and at the Breakthrough Prize, which three of the four of us were at, I talked to George Kurtz. The other person you were kind of describing was not that person. Yeah, I was sitting beside Nikesh. Yeah, I'm talking about Nikesh Arora. Well, Nikesh and George are the two guys leading this — Palo Alto Networks, CrowdStrike. And they understand — what George told me was, there is just a line out the door of people who want this product or service.

And if you look at it, Freberg, like the murder rate — like, we're sitting here with the lowest murder rate in the history of humanity. It has gone down massively in our lifetimes, but massively over the arc of history. I think that's what's gonna happen with cyber. There is only so many attack vectors, and the remaining attack vectors are just gonna be human factors, right? Freberg, that's always been the case. And as we make the software more resilient, then the weak link is the secretary who puts her post-it note with the password there, or the accountant who, you know, uses their dog's name plus 123 for their password, right? That's the historic one.

话题四：超大规模厂商的 CapEx 大爆炸

Big tech smashed their earnings on Thursday. Google, Microsoft, Amazon and Meta all reported. I don't know why they do this on the same night, folks, but they do. And performance was spectacular. It was great.

However, the CapEx announcements were really the story here. Let me just cue this up and show the chart. $725 billion in CapEx guidance in 2026 from but four companies — Amazon, Microsoft, Google, and Meta. Amazon leading the pack with $200 billion, $190 billion each for Microsoft and Google, $145 billion for Meta. You add Grok, you add OpenAI, and some other players to these plans, and we haven't heard from the new Apple CEO yet, but he's going to be taking over and he's going to have some plans here, I'm sure.

We are going to see a trillion dollars in build-out over the next year. I don't know if this is even possible, but this is all being driven by AI and cloud computing.

  • Google Cloud (which includes the Google Suite): grew 63% year on year. Let that number sink in. 63% on $20 billion in revenue. That's in a quarter.
  • Microsoft Cloud (includes Azure, Windows Server, SQL Server — they bundled some things together to get the number to go up): grew 30% on $34.7 billion in revenue.
  • Amazon Web Services, the original cloud — that's a bit of a pure play, just counts Amazon's Web Services: grew 28% on $37.6 billion in revenue.

Obviously, these are all moving to NeoClouds. These are all serving AI jobs and tokens now. They have a massive customer base, and the customers from the smallest startups all the way to the biggest frontier models cannot get enough compute, and it is going to the bottom line.

But this is shrinking — Chamath — cash flow massively. These were free cash flow machines, the largest money-printing machines in the history of humanity. But they are giving up on free cash flow, stock buybacks and dividends, in the focus on those three to invest in infrastructure.

  • Amazon's free cash flow down 97%
  • Google, Microsoft and Meta down 12%, 12% and 8% respectively.

Your thoughts on this free cash flow, the end of the free cash flow deluge and the massive, massive investment we're seeing in CapEx, Chamath?

Chamath：资本市场的结构性转向

I think we're seeing a very important structural shift in the capital markets. I think the last 20 or 30 years — well, 20 years — it's been that the Mag Seven just kind of ran away with it, that these big companies got bigger and bigger and it absorbed all of these investment dollars. And the biggest reason was that it had these very asset-light business models, right? You just build some more software and it just has all this leverage and it all just kind of worked — except maybe for Amazon because they needed physical infrastructure for warehouses and delivery and whatnot. But by and large, it was a very asset-light investment cycle.

Now all of a sudden, the pendulum is swinging violently in the other direction. And there's something that I think people misunderstand, which is — as it moves back to these asset-heavy infrastructure investments, the hyperscalers are signing checks that, I mean, I suspect their body can cash, but there's a world in which they can't.

I'll give you an example. When Microsoft convinced the owners of Three Mile Island to turn their nuclear site back on, do you know what their forward purchase agreement was? It was for more than 2x the prevailing spot rate for energy. More than 2x. The problem is that's not for an enormous percentage of their overall energy needs.

So if you play that out, and you think these five or six companies all of a sudden are not just spending, Jason, $700 billion a year of CapEx, which they are, but then from an operating cash flow, they're going to be spending 2x the prevailing spot rate because they just want guaranteed demand into the future. Where's all this cash going to go? It's not going to go to the shareholder, and it's not going to stay on the balance sheet. These companies will now get levered. They're going to get highly sophisticated around the financial engineering. They'll have more debt. They'll have all kinds of different vehicles and term loans and revolvers and all of this stuff.

And so they're going to look like this big, bulky industrial business in five years. And I'm not sure that there's a good valuation case to be made at that point. And so I think it may be simpler — and this is what I tweeted — to just follow the dollars. Like, a trillion dollars a year going out of the hyperscalers — where is it going? Just follow those dollars and buy those companies, because those companies are already underpriced.

Cisco vs 今天：是泡沫重演还是不一样？

This is obviously reminiscent of something we all experienced. Nick, can you pull up the Cisco chart I just sent you and put it at max?

We had a massive build-out of the infrastructure of the internet in the late 1990s and into 2000. And what that caused was a lot of aggressive companies to do massive amounts of spending, a lot of retail investors to embrace these stocks like we're seeing with people trying to get into these private companies. And Sacks, look at the 2000 peak of Cisco. This is the most extraordinary chart ever. It took them 25 years to get back to that peak. And they had a lost two decades. And we had a massive amount of fiber that wound up getting bought. We talked about that a couple of years ago on the program. But there's something for you to build off of here when you look at this massive infrastructure. Do you think it's going to be Cisco Systems all over again, WorldCom, et cetera?

No, I really don't. The issue we had in 2000 was dark fiber. You had all this infrastructure being built out and it wasn't being used. There's no dark GPUs today, as Brad Gerstner likes to say. What's driving the CapEx now is the voracious demand for compute, for tokens. And the demand is now pulling forward this additional investment in infrastructure.

So I think what's happened here is that the bull thesis for AI just got validated in a single afternoon. I mean, again, you got Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud, Amazon AWS, Meta — they're all basically exceeding expectations, exceeding guidance in terms of where their cloud revenue would be, and therefore how much they're going to reinvest in CapEx. This year, I think we were supposed to have $660 billion of hyperscaler CapEx, up from $350 last year. I think there's now the new estimate is it's going to be over $700.

So this is, you know, again, it's more than 2% of GDP. This is a huge tailwind to GDP. There's another article saying that I think in the last quarter, AI was 75% of GDP growth. And by the way, this is just the CapEx part. This is the physical infrastructure. This is not the economic impact of the tokens that are generated inside the token factory. This is the building of the factories.

How do those tokens get used? Like we're seeing — they're being used not just to do research or to answer questions, but to create code. And so we're seeing this explosion of productivity in software development. And we're seeing an explosion of bespoke software being created. And that's gonna accelerate every part of the economy. Every business that now wants to get code will be able to get code for the first time. Before, they couldn't even hire the engineers they needed to generate it. Now they will be able to. So that is a huge unlock of productivity across the economy.

Sacks：Agent 必须被监督，AI ≠ 终端到终端

Then you're getting into these new use cases, like the co-working use cases and agents — right? So the workflow automations that are happening. It's still early. I don't believe that this is going to replace humans. We had that — in the past week, we had that crazy case of an agent deleting a production database in nine seconds. So great. Because of a bug.

Look, what that said to me is that it's not that agents aren't valuable. They are valuable, but they have to be supervised. You know, this idea that you're just going to be able to like automate all the jobs away — it is a massive amount of hand-waving over the real technical problems and issues. The agents have to be supervised. Someone has to be accountable. It's not going to be the CEO. The CEO doesn't want to be accountable for thousands of agents. You need people —

Yeah, despite what Jack had blocked said. Yeah, you're still going to need — 6,000 direct reports is a great goal, but it's not realistic. Yeah. You need IT people who are savvy, who can supervise this and make sure it's working. They have to be accountable to the CEO. Someone has to drive the productivity.

It's like Balaji always said: AI is not end-to-end, it's middle-to-middle. You have to have someone to do the prompting, and you have to have someone do the validating, and I would add the supervision and accountability.

So anyway, the larger point, though, is — I'm speaking to the fact that I don't think there's going to be this huge job loss associated with this productivity boom that we're going to get. And in fact, I think what's actually happening now is that AI is becoming synonymous with the American economy. I mean, the fact that it's generating 75% of GDP, you have this CapEx explosion, this energy explosion that feeds it, and again, just the beginning of the applications that are being unleashed by these new token factories. I think it's all a very, very positive thing.

And all these doomers who are trying to throw a wet blanket on it are constantly scaring the daylights out of people. I mean, what do they want the American economy to do? Just to stop? I mean, they just don't want any progress. I mean, like, again, when you talk about stopping AI or halting AI progress, what you're really doing is stopping the American economy now. You're basically saying you don't want economic growth. AI is now synonymous with the growth of the American economy. And if there's no economic growth, there's not going to be money to pay for all the social programs. There's not going to be money to pay down the national debt. There's not going to be money to basically build up our national defense. All these things we want to spend money on — we have to have a vibrant economy. And that is now synonymous with AI.

I know that AI may not be popular — I see those polls — but having a strong economy is popular. And I believe that those things are now synonymous.

It's almost like there was some architect or czar who set up the chessboard in the first year of this to make sure that it was ultra-competitive. Well done. President Trump set the table on this. Absolutely. With some good advice, I think, maybe.

Freberg, your thoughts? It's always good to have good advisors. Always good to have good advisors. Absolutely. Absolutely.

No, but look, I've said it before. The president just wants America to win. Literally, there are people who, if we were looking at this, I don't know, 100 years ago, it'd be like — people were like, "yeah, you know what? We shouldn't build the highway system." Or, "we have built the highway system. Let's stop. Let's stop building the highways." No, the highway system was funded by the federal government. There was no competition. It was the most expensive, on an inflation-adjusted basis — I think it was the most expensive project in U.S. history. And the railroads before that. Like, you can't stop these things. They have to keep going.

You blame Dario. Okay, that's what I thought. That's the correct answer. Correct answer. Blame Dario. He's the one who says it's a doomsday machine. Come on the pod any time, Dario. 17th invite. I would have invited the guy like 17 times. He is totally going to be — he wants nothing to do with this podcast.

Sacks：这不是 AI 在搞鬼，是 AI 不知道它不知道

Actually, let me speak to that. So I think there's maybe a misperception that this error occurred because of, you know, quote-unquote, "AI scheming" — like kind of in that video that the AI decided that the best way to get rid of bugs is to basically eliminate the codebase. This is kind of like the, you know, "AI is going to turn the world into paperclips" type thing — where somehow it'll like miss-scheme. That's not really what happened here.

This is a case of just old-fashioned bugs occurring at an edge case. You know, you've got the fact that this API was not designed for permissioned usage. You've got the fact that a credential was left kind of lying around, probably it should not be. There's kind of like a perfect storm that caused the AI to do something — or the agent to do something — that it didn't quite understand what it was doing.

I think that if there is a systemic problem here, rather than just kind of like a random edge case, it's that AI still doesn't know what it doesn't know. Like a human would stop before deleting a production database and just say, "oh, I'm about to do something like really serious, really destructive. Am I sure I wanna do this?" And a human would have stopped and said, "oh, wait a second, like I need to be more confident in what I'm doing before I take that action." And AI still has this issue where, again, it can be kind of overconfident. This is where like the hallucinations come from — is it doesn't know when it should have a low confidence in its output. Right? But this is why it has to be supervised.

The longer the time horizon for a task, the more likely it is to go off the rails. It'll drift. And adrift, exactly. And this is why I think people are starting to realize that this idea of eliminating all software developers was the peak of inflated expectations.

Aaron Levie / Matt Yglesias：我不想 vibe-code，让专业公司去 vibe-code

"Five months in, I think I've decided I don't want to vibe-code. I want professionally managed software companies to use AI coding assistance to make more, better, cheaper software products that they sell to me for money."

Just lower your prices. Don't make me vibe-code is the translation. Yeah. I mean, I think — like, rare win for Matt Yglesias there. Anyway, Aaron Levie then says:

"Agentic coding is a huge move for software developers that want to get more done. And it's fantastic for anyone curious to learn how to start coding. What it's less great for is casually building complex software that you have to maintain on an ongoing basis and take all the risks for. Upgrades, maintenance, keeping up to date with latest security issues, the bugs, cyber. Those are taxes on most knowledge workers who aren't familiar with the system."

It's not a tax. It's a huge risk. Yes. It's a risk that has to be managed. It's a risk. People will get fired. Because there will be some public companies where some goofball tries to vibe-code their way out of something and they're going to torch the enterprise value. It's going to be glorious to watch — because we're all going to laugh and realize that was stupid and should never have happened in the first place.

Yeah. I mean, it's — there is a chance that this improves to the point, passes trial of disillusionment and becomes super productive. And you'll be able to get an agent to do reasonable things without deleting your data set. But we have a way to go.

Here is your — you know, this is the tech adoption chart. Basically, you got a technology gets triggered. You have this peak of inflated expectations. You go into the trough of disillusionment, and then the slope of enlightenment, invest, and eventually it becomes de rigueur and it's an opportunity.

话题六：Retatrutide 狂潮——Lilly 的下一个 GLP-1 神药

Hey, Friedberg, you have become "Reddit Truetide curious." You have. And also — tell me about Reddit, Friedberg, because I want it. I want to get on it. I want to use it. And I need you to tell Nat that it's okay for me to take it. I have a friend who has some advice as well. Over to you, Friedberg.

Phase 3 数据爆炸

The coverage is coming out of this Phase 3 clinical trial data release that Lilly put out last month. So everyone's going crazy over the data, which continues to show pretty amazing results.

So unlike tirzepatide, which is kind of Lilly's main product today, which is a dual agonist — it's got two peptides in it that bind to different receptors, the GLP-1, the GIP receptor — this other one now also binds to glucagon, which is a third receptor. And that glucagon receptor binding peptide causes the cells to increase their metabolism, which actually accelerates fat energy consumption over what would typically be muscle energy consumption. It's more likely to burn up fat early on, which causes more quick fat loss, but also reduces muscle loss.

And some of the other data that's now coming out shows:

  • Non-HDL cholesterol down 27%
  • Triglycerides down 41%
  • Liver fat down 80% — 80% reduction of liver fat
  • A1C drops from 7.9% to 6% in 40 weeks, which is amazing, by the way. If you're diabetic and your A1C drops that much in a couple of months, it's literally a life-saving product.
  • The average user in this Phase 3 trial saw their weight decline from 214 pounds — they lost 37 pounds — that's compared to 6 pounds on placebo in 40 weeks.
  • And modest side effects: 20% of people felt more nauseous than the people that were on the placebo.

There's a lot of other separate studies that are being done now that are showing significant reductions in inflammatory signaling molecules. So systemic signaling of like, "hey, cells are in distress" triggers this kind of inflammatory process that can have a lot of other damage to your body, can accelerate aging. And so one of the other conversations is that retatrutide might actually be kind of a de-aging drug as well.

Hercules, Hercules, Hercules. And a lot of the studies, by the way, are done on the very high dose, 12 milligram dose, but you could probably get this thing dosed down to two milligrams and still see a lot of the anti-inflammatory maintenance and other benefits. I'm no doctor, but people are going nuts over this being more widely useful than just for clinical obesity or type 1 diabetes.

When will the FDA approve it? When's the projected date for you, bro? 2027 — mid-27. That's what they're saying. Could happen sooner. I mean, the data's in. The FDA will take their time to evaluate it, but I think given the way this is all looking, could happen sooner, could happen sometime later this year.

Chamath 的"Swim"和肌肉增长

Swim — Chamath's swim said it's incredible and that it's living up to the hype in their experience. Who? Swim. What is that? What is that? Someone who isn't me. Swim. Oh. This is a Reddit term. Someone who isn't me said — Swim has a guy and has cycled, uh, on Reddit truetide and does pushups, and says muscle gain has been spectacular, no muscle loss, and a lowering of fat.

If you go on X and you just search up — it's like incredible. You see these like 65-year-old guys that go from a dad-bod to looking like an incredibly ripped athlete in weeks. And I, I mean, I'm shocked. And then for me, I don't need that help per se, but my liver health is important to me, my cardiac health, because I'm South Asian. Just looks like a wonder drug. I can't wait.

When you starve your body, when you turn off the appetite — which is the GLP-1 agonist function — normally your body goes into this kind of mode of starvation, and you have this process by which your body tries to generate energy from your existing cells. And because muscle is much denser than fat, you can have a favoring of muscle tissue being kind of broken up over fat tissue. But what this new agonist, this glucagon agonist they put into this retatrutide is — it favors fat burning over muscle burning. And so that actually can drive short-term use at low dose for people to cut weight and maintain muscle and get ripped. And so that's why a lot of people in the kind of fitness community are talking about, "hey, I wanna get access to this and get on it for a while." So you'll see a lot more hype probably in that community as well as all the health effects.

It just feels like we're about to have an absolute avalanche of peptides to choose from.

话题七：Freberg 在最高法院的"Courtside"体验
