走进神圣大理石

I mean, honestly, I think it was one of the most amazing experiences I've ever had. There was a massive protest out front. We went through the marshal's office to get in. And that building you walk in, it's like sacred. It's all marble. You're not allowed to talk. You have to be super quiet when you're in the building. Like they keep going, "shh, shh, shh," like you're in some quiet library. It's like people treat it with this level of kind of sanctity and respect. And they're like, "there is no politics here. There is no bullshit, there is no freedom of speech. This is the court. When you come into this court, the justices tell you how you will speak, how you will behave, what you will do, and you will not speak unless spoken to."

You put all your stuff in a locker, you go up the stairs, you go into the courtroom. And the courtroom — it's just so amazing being in there. They have this amazing marble frieze above the justices that has some of the great people of human history, Moses and these kind of amazing historical figures. And then below them are the nine justices. And the court case — if you guys haven't watched the case, you can listen to them, I think, online. Yeah, they're all on you. They're worth listening to.

Hold on, wait, wait, wait, I have questions. So does Roberts sit in the middle because he's the chief? Yes. And then do all of the right justices sit on the right? No, they're mixed. So they're, I think, I don't know the exact seating, but they're mixed in terms of — probably based on appointments of the court. Yeah, I think that's right. I think that's right. And then so yeah, that's right. And then it kind of goes out from the middle with Roberts in the middle. Roberts occasionally will name the justices and say, "hey, do you have a question? Do you have a question?" If no one's talking, but otherwise the justices will jump in with their questions when they want and they'll ask.

Now, honest to God, watching this is like watching LeBron James play basketball. These lawyers are so mind-blowingly impressive on both sides that you would just like sit there — and I was like in awe. It was so — I felt like my energy was completely sapped for me at the end of this process. Because you are just so engaged and so caught into the way that these guys are thinking and talking.

Did you take a Ro Sparks? Did you take a Ro Sparks when you were there? No.

法律解释，而不是事实

And if you're familiar enough with the case or the case history or the law that's being debated, because — again, when you get to the Supreme Court, you never debate the case. What you're debating is the legal interpretation of the decisions that were made on the case. And so is this constitutional? How do you interpret this particular act, this law, this federal law? What's the right way to think about it? So you don't actually talk about the case, you talk about the interpretation of American law, of our laws, of the Constitution.

What was the specific case? Give us the details. You're saying the facts have already been determined. That's right. Right, at a lower court. There's questions of fact and questions of law. The facts have already been determined by the lower court. It's just Supreme Court is ruling on questions of law. That's right.

And so they have a full briefing with the full history of the case. And remember, they only hear two cases a day. So they're one hour each for each hearing. So you go in and they only do it Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday on the last two weeks, and they only hear cases from October to April. There's only a handful of cases that are selected. Wow, so you're really on a shot clock then to make your case. You're on a shot clock and you only have — and it's 30 minutes a side, and then the justices will ask question.

Monsanto / Roundup / FIFRA / Federal Preemption

So this was Monsanto and Roundup, right? So what was the law that was being debated?

For yours, the regulatory body, the EPA, sets the label for pesticides. Does this cause cancer or not? What are the warnings? This can be damaging for birth defects, pregnancy, all the things that we're all used to seeing on labels when you buy a product, a chemical product. And the EPA in their regulatory authority determined that Roundup does not cause cancer. When you sell a pesticide, you first have to register it with the EPA, get it approved, and then the EPA gives you a label. And the label is written by the EPA. It says exactly what you're supposed to say. And in this case, it said all this stuff. Doesn't say cancer because they determined it does not cause cancer.

And I'm not going to debate whether or not it causes cancer, but the case that was made is that the EPA is the regulatory body under a federal act called FIFRA — Fungicide, Insecticide, or Rodenticide Act. And that's where the EPA has been given the regulatory authority to put the label on these products.

And all of the cases that have been lost have been state failure-to-warn cases. To date, Bayer, which now owns Monsanto, has paid out $10 billion in these lawsuits, and they have reserved $10 billion on their balance sheet. They have 90,000 cases still outstanding in the courts. 90,000. Wow.

And so this one case got kind of appealed up to the Supreme Court. Last year, the White House Solicitor General — and if the Solicitor General steps up and asks the Supreme Court to take a case, it's more likely the case gets taken. So the White House said, "please take this case. We need to have federal preemption, meaning the federal government has the right to set the label." Because all of the cases that have been lost and that are being adjudicated are in state courts where the state has a law, like in California, called a failure to warn law, which means if a manufacturer knows that a product carries a risk, you have to warn the consumer.

And so the lawyers have been arguing that Monsanto or Bayer knew that this product caused cancer and didn't warn the consumer. And they've been winning cases, they've been losing cases, but they've won enough cases that this has now become a multi-deca-billion-dollar problem. And so the argument is that the EPA says it doesn't cause cancer, and they have federal preemption. So the EPA has the right to determine. So that's the one argument.

But then when the other attorney came up — this guy was, like, literally, like watching LeBron James. And so going in, we were like, "oh, 6-3 Bayer is gonna win." And then the other guy comes up and he was like, "well, hey, you guys overturned the Chevron doctrine last year. You guys remember that case?" Yeah. Where basically when the Chevron doctrine got overturned, it basically said that no longer the federal agency get to decide — it has to be a direct reading of the law. So now — so he's saying, like, the states should have a right to read the law themselves, they shouldn't have to just defer to the EPA. And that's what this will come down to. So at the end of it, we were like, "oh my god, this could be a 50-50 coin flip — 5-4 either way." And going into it, we were kind of like trying to say, "hey, maybe this could be 6-3."

30 分钟的口头辩论艺术

So honestly, the whole experience was incredible. The case is interesting. These are very complicated matters. How are these people able to make a fulsome argument? Like one side gets 30 minutes, the other side gets 30 minutes, there's a little Q&A, and then you're done in an hour?

There's this whole art and science — and Sacks, you're probably familiar with this — on how do you distill down a Supreme Court case in the briefing doc? Like what is it you're petitioning around the court? And you're trying to distill it down to the exact legal interpretation you want the judges to rule on. Not all the other bullshit. And this is oral arguments, yes? Oral arguments — just a discussion. And then the judges jump in, and all they're doing is asking the lawyer questions, one lawyer at a time, the one side and then the other side. And by the way, the Solicitor General came up in the middle and kind of made a few comments and asked some questions from the White House and she sat down. And then the two sides kind of went back and forth, and they just — it's like 30 minutes Q&A each on that one specific legal question.

And Ketanji Brown Jackson said, "but what if after the EPA issued the label, they found out information that it does cause cancer? Shouldn't they update the label?" And he's saying, "well, no, they're not allowed to. They can only issue the label the EPA says." And he says also, "and it's a criminal case if they find out that it does cause cancer and they don't report it to the EPA." And then she's saying, "well, what if the EPA doesn't act? And shouldn't the states have a right to protect their people?"

So those are the legal arguments, the discussions that are going on in all of this. And there's interesting implications, which is fundamentally — if the states get to interpret federal law and ignore federal regulatory bodies, it opens up a whole new can of worms in terms of, like, all the states can start to ignore federal regulatory bodies like the EPA or the FDA or the USDA, and on and on and on. So the whole case has a whole bunch of really interesting implications wound up in it. When you hear these guys and they're just talking about that exact, like, interpretation of the law — and that's what this comes down to. It's not the actual case that matters.

闭门会议与"Airlock"

And after that, Sacks, they go from oral arguments and then they have like a private conference where they'll write their papers and give their final judgment. Yeah. Sacks.

Yeah. I think what happens is that, so I guess there's some discussion that happens behind closed doors and they figure out where the majority is, and then the chief gets to assign who writes the opinion for the majority.

In that meeting, nobody is allowed in. And in fact, you have a double door system where, like, if anything needs to come in and out, you have to like, kind of like knock on the door that you're led into this antechamber. Oh, is it an airlock? It's effective. I don't know if you were there, Jason, but we had Ted Cruz come to play in the poker game. I wasn't there for that. Ted Cruz clerked for William Rehnquist. If you want to have an incredible dinner, ask him about the Supreme Court and Bill Rehnquist. He's a real student of the Supreme Court.

一个仍在运转的机构

It just makes the Supreme Court, Freberg, to your point, sound like the most incredible body that's ever been created anywhere. By the way, more than the White House, more than the Capitol building, more than any of these other big agencies. This place has — it's almost like being in England. It has these ways that people operate. The security is so different. They stand there in the court, and they all exchange places every 20 minutes. It's very coordinated. They're dressed very differently than any other security. How many people are in the courtroom listening? Maybe like 150, I would say. How do you get the tickets? Are they on StubHub or something? I actually think everyone is a guest of a clerk or someone that works at the court. I don't think that it's very publicly available to get in there. You can't line up? There's no line up? I don't know if there's a line up. This was a connection through the —

I know for the Elon case, we got in through the chief justice. He gave us the pass, but I think it was very — I think at the Elon versus OpenAI case, you can line up and then the judge gave like 30 tickets to the press. But that's not the Supreme Court. No, no, yeah, but I think there's a lineup for the Supreme Court as well. There's some public access that they're committed to. It did not look like anyone from the public was in this court. Maybe it's a lottery or something.

Everyone is dressed respectfully. I mean, this court has an incredible amount of, like, you know, kind of behavior. That's a super cool experience. It's a great time.

I would just say enjoy it while you can. I mean, I think the Supreme Court is one of the last highly functional institutions in the United States. 100%. You know, at some point we're gonna have like 13 or 21 or some crazy number of justices up there. The court's gonna get packed.

And get jerseys and everybody show up with their jerseys. It's gonna be a rule after rule of justices there. And so enjoy it while it's still in the current form it's in. Can you imagine showing up with jerseys with the justices' names on them and like having sections and like somebody selling Cracker Jacks? The Idiocracy version of the Supreme Court. Exactly.

The popularity of the court really depends on whether it's issuing decisions that people agree with. That's what it comes down to. Like, if you ask people whether they like the Supreme Court or not, it really just depends on whether they agree with the decisions are. Sure. Recency. As opposed to the process of the decisions and how well argued it is and all these things that you're pointing to.

And actually the court — I mean, I just checked the numbers. The court is relatively popular right now. I think that — got as low as 35 in the 2024 Gallup survey, but I think it's back up to, you know, like 44 to 50 favorability, which for something that's involved in politics is relatively high, right? Like you look at Congress or any particular politician, they're going to be lower than that typically.

I just felt so assured of, like, the institution when I visited, and saw these guys interact and behave and how they behave, the process — it was like, "man, what an amazing country." Yeah. Well, the reason I say what I say is there was an interview with James Carville recently. Did you guys see this? I saw that. I saw that. He said, look, when we get power, we're packing the court. So we're not going to worry about it. Yeah, he said get to 13, right? He said we're going to make — yeah, they're going to go from 9 to 13. And then they're going to create some new states and all the rest of it. So that'll be that. Enjoy it while it lasts. Enjoy it while it lasts.

Way to end on a high note. It's the end of the empire. That'll be that.

By the way, there is a Supreme Court — I was correct, there is an online ticketing lottery. So we can all sign up and you can get a four-pack of tickets. I think they should make this — we should talk to Howard Lutnick. Maybe he can make this an auction and get a revenue stream in the US. We could sell, like, 10 of the tickets as courtside seats for 20 grand. Jason, you're exactly what they're trying to protect against happening in the Supreme Court. Exactly. How can we monetize the Supreme Court?

收尾

All right, everybody, that's it. That's the world's greatest podcast for you. For Chamath Palihapitiya, David Freberg, and David Sacks, I am the world's greatest moderator. We'll see you next time. You're like the Chief Justice. I'm like the Chief Justice of the All In Podcast. We should all just get a room and just have one big huge — I'm gone!

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 三个让人意外的系统性观察
### 1. 这一集的真正主角不是 OpenAI，而是"电力"——AI 行业正在经历"软件公司物理化"的隐秘逆转
表面上大家在讨论 OpenAI 错失目标、Codex vs Claude 的对决。但 Chamath 不经意间揭示了一个被严重低估的范式转移：**AI 公司正在从"轻资产软件"重新坍缩回"重资产工业"**。微软为了三里岛的核电，签下了 2 倍现货价格的远期购电协议——这不是软件公司的行为，这是 19 世纪末美国铁路公司的行为。
让人意外的是：**在 2000 年互联网泡沫中破产的不是思科，是 WorldCom 这种铺光纤的电信公司。如果历史押韵，真正会出问题的不是 OpenAI 本身，而是为它建数据中心、铺电网、签长期 PPA 的"新电信公司"——也就是今天的超大规模云厂商。** Sax 说"没有暗 GPU"是对的，但他忽略了：暗 GPU 不会出现，暗变压器、暗气体涡轮、暗审批许可正在堆积。Chamath 提到"已宣布项目里 40% 会被取消"，这跟 2001 年的光纤 over-build 几乎是同一种结构性误差，只是这次卡在电力供给

item.title: [No Priors: AI, Machine Learning, Tech, & Startups] Baseten CEO Tuhin Srivastava on Custom Models, and Building the Inference Cloud
item.dc:creator: No Priors: AI, Machine Learning, Tech, & Startups
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：中国开源模型，本质上是中国政府对美国企业的"隐性补贴"

访谈中最被低估的洞见来自 Aladdin 那段几乎被礼貌性带过的评论——当中国国家资本补贴 DeepSeek、Moonshot、Qwen 等模型的训练成本，而这些权重被免费释放给全球时，真正的受益者是采用这些模型的美国企业。这构成了一个奇特的"反向重商主义"：传统贸易战中，补贴是为了倾销本国商品挤压对手；但在智能这一新型生产要素上，补贴方反而把"剩余价值"转移给了对手国的应用层。

跨领域连接：这非常像1970-80 年代日本对半导体 DRAM 的国家补贴——当时美国痛失 DRAM 产业，但同时美国 PC 厂商（Dell、Compaq、Apple）享受了人类历史上最便宜的内存，从而催生了整个 PC 革命与软件帝国。今天的开源模型，可能正在为下一轮"美国应用层霸权"提供同样的燃料。问题不在"中国模型是否安全"，而在于：当智能本身被补贴时，价值会沿着供应链向哪一层迁移？答案是：向最贴近用户信号的那一层。

二、Tuhin 隐藏的"反摩尔定律"：post-training 与 inference 正在合并为同一个问题

表面上这是一个"我们收购了一个研究团队"的常规叙事。但仔细听 Tuhin 的话——"how you train the model affects how you need to quantize for inference"——他实际上在宣告一个范式转移：训练与推理的边界正在溶解。

这件事的深层意义被低估了。过去十年云计算的胜利逻辑是"专业化分工"（训练归训练云、推理归推理云、存储归存储云）。但 Baseten 看到的未来恰恰相反——当 RL post-training 用推理时产生的 reward signal 反哺模型，inference cluster 本身就变成了一个永不停歇的训练厂。这意味着"推理云"这个词的定义将在 2-3 年内失效，取而代之的是某种"持续学习基础设施" (Continuous Learning Infrastructure)。

跨领域连接：这非常类似生物学中神经系统的演化——大脑的"推理"（信号传递）和"学习"（突触可塑性）从来不是分开的两个系统，它们是同一个过程的两个面。云计算正在从"机械式分工"演化为"神经式融合"。Baseten 收购研究团队不是补短板，而是在押注：未来五年里，把 inference 和 post-training 拆开运营的公司，会像把"思考"和"记忆"拆开运营的大脑一样不可能存在。

三、被所有人忽视的资本结构革命：软件公司被迫学会"重资产融资"

这一段几乎是被快速带过的——Tuhin 提到 "we're software people and we don't pick like this... our business has very interesting working capital requirements"——但这是整个访谈最具结构性意义的转折信号。

SaaS 时代的全部融资逻辑（高毛利、轻资产、高 ARR 倍数估值）正在被 inference cloud 这种新物种打破。当一个 1000 张 B200 的合同需要 3-5 年期、20-30% 预付的承诺时，Baseten 这类公司本质上正在变成"披着软件皮的电力公司 / 航空公司"——它们的核心能力不再只是写代码，而是资本配置的速度与精度。

最反直觉的推论：这会倒逼一批 AI infra 公司提前 IPO，不是为了套现或品牌，而是为了降低资本成本以赢得算力期货市场。Tuhin 几乎承认了这一点（"I think you'd go sooner"）。

跨领域连接：这让我想起 20 世纪初铁路公司的演化路径——它们最初被视为"运输服务公司"，但很快人们意识到真正的护城河是对土地、债券和长期固定资产的金融工程能力。最终，胜出的不是技术最好的铁路，而是资本结构最聪明的铁路。今天的 inference cloud 正站在同一个十字路口：未来的赢家可能不是 GPU 优化最强的团队，而是最先把自己重新设计成"AI 时代的伯克希尔"的那家公司。

附加冷观察

Tuhin 说 "no one wants a vegan inference"（没人想要纯素推理）——这句玩笑实际上隐藏了他对 NVIDIA 生态的真实判断：chip 的多样化只会发生在"特定 decode 任务"这样的边缘地带，而通用推理在可见的未来仍是 CUDA 的领地。这与市场上主流的"NVIDIA 必将被颠覆"叙事形成了一个有趣的、来自最贴近真实算力供应链的人的反对票。

📝 转录文本

Baseten CEO Tuhin Srivastava on Custom Models, and Building the Inference Cloud

开场介绍

Hi listeners, today Aladdin and I are here with Tuhin Stuba, the founder and CEO of B 10, the AI inference cloud. We're here to talk about capacity constraints for AI compute, why inference is the last market, how the workload is changing, the open-source and perhaps multi-chip future, and what 30x scale in a year looks like. Tuhan, welcome back.

Hi. Great to see it. Thanks for having me.

一年 30 倍增长背后的市场逻辑

All right. You are in one of the uh craziest markets, AI inference. Uh uh it's very important. There's a lot going on. You guys have grown 30x over the last year. And I think I can say you're expecting to do more than a billion dollars in revenue this year.

Mhm.

What's going on? Tell us about scale.

Yeah. No, it's been it's been nuts. I I think what's happened over the last honestly at 24 months but just kind of keeps getting bigger and bigger is that I think everyone is re realizing that you can put AI everywhere. Um you have you have all these great options available from closed source to open source models. the open source models have c crossed some sort of chasm in terms of their baseline capability and that I think RL techniques and post- training is um for specialized models um has become mainstream enough and you know there's enough examples of it work of it working the customers realizing they can you know kind of own their inference um more and more and what that's meant for us is um more you know the longtail models coming true. Customers in a lot of that intelligence themselves and as the application layer just gets you know bigger and bigger and bigger and that's growing when we are we are just someone index on that and we've been around to be able to collect the demand.

应用层是否还有独立存在的空间？

There's an existential question in here that I think everybody is uh continually asking of does the independent application layer get to exist at all versus the labs like how do you you have to believe this why do you believe it?

Yeah, look, I I I think it'd be it'd be a sad thing if it didn't exist in general, and I think that's like my but you know, sadness is fine. Um the all the time sadness is fine. Um but like that's not the reason why I think the application layer will exist.

I think the application layer will exist for a number of reasons. One is because you know I think this idea um that what is what is valuable to a company um is you know the the user signal that they can gather that only they can gather um and to the extent that that is encoded um in a model I think a lot of their business will um be at risk but to the to the extent that it is encoded in workflows um that is where they will be able to develop notes.

So a good I think a good example of that is say a company like a bridge where the clinicians edits of the notes and what they do with those notes after the fact and the um the thing that happens in um inside the EMR three steps down and that becomes a workflow that only

can you explain what a bridge does?

Sorry. So bridge is a um ambient um scribe um that is used by physicians in um like you know almost all hospitals in the US. I think large investor um great chef's amazing great great company great team um great product um and you know they they've basically uh you know got this very very deep integration into into hospitals into clinician workflows and my argument would be here is that actually you know it's very very hard for um a frontier model company to be able to eat away at that because they just don't have access to that user signal and what will happen over time is the folks who have access to that user signal can start to post-train models on that reward signal and and start to get long long horizon agentic models running that and I think to the extent that that is possible and that signal is differentiated and unique and in and is um somewhat um rare to to get access to there will be an application layer.

And I think you know like support company is another example of that where you know a support a support task isn't oneshotted Usually at a company like Ban when a ticket comes in there's like what like 1 2 10 20 actions that get taken and that is where you know someone can develop a specialized model.

AI 原生应用 vs. 企业自建：市场结构

So there's almost two versions of this then there's new companies like a bridge or decagon or some of these other things that you mentioned that are doing

these new types of applications that are using AI and they sell it to customers.

The other is enterprises building things inhouse or building their own models. What proportion of the market today do you think is um these new application companies versus enterprises just adopting AI?

Yeah. And how do you think that looks in a couple years?

Yeah, I I think that's a that's you we did I think you asked me the same question two years ago on

I had to be repetitive. Yeah.

It it is crazy consistent.

The answer is just that it's crazy that the answer is still I think I think if you look by inference count

Yeah.

it'd be 99% the full.

Yeah. Um I think that is that kind of represents the scope of the opportunity here is that the majority of the market hasn't come online and and and um and added AI into the

Yeah. most of enterprise adoption is well ahead of us and I think that's one of the very exciting things about AI.

Yeah.

There's just so much still to come and people are underestimating that I think

100%. And but but what's cool is that we're seeing the transition happen. Right. Before it was like hey are they are they using AI tools?

I don't think that was immediately obvious two years ago. I think it's obvious now that yes they are. are they using closed source model APIs? Um I think they're starting to get there. I think once you do that and then you kind of see what is possible then comes the whole custom model adoption. I think that is all that is ahead of us

today.

服务前沿客户的策略：Stripe 模式

So if the majority of your customer base today is as you described the the former like application companies AI natives the um fast growing I mean some of them are at considerable scale now like the a bridge cursor um

open evidence

open evidences of the world. uh what you know what do they what do they teach you? What does that push the company to do? How do you think about serving them versus evolving for the enterprise?

Yeah. Um I I think firstly like you just learn a lot by building with the companies greatest scale doing the most interesting things.

Um we we think of it two ways like I think there's like the the the most obvious way which is just build for the highest scale. um you know most uh the customers that will push you the most from technologically and everything kind of will fall into play. I think the Stripe evolution as a company showed that we're like Stripe now like serves like so many enterprises but 12 years ago that wasn't the case but they just built for the frontier and kind of went with them.

Um I think the second way we think about that this is to just think about building for companies that are serving enterprises. So yes we don't serve the enterprise but our customers serve enterprises. Um a bridge service open evidence decagon um all these writer gamma all these clay all all these companies serve enterprises in mass and what we actually get like a translation of the requirements from them which is like you know they're like hey we need this data retention we need this type this where models need to be deployed um this is the types of GPUs or the latencies they're okay with this is the model requirements from like a transparency perspective that they care about and so I think that is actually the more nuance answer.

It's that if you listen to what their needs are, we actually get a full translation of what the enterprise would require. Like I would say that by serving companies like a bridge and open evidence, we're probably pretty well suited to go serve the healthare system given that they are selling and latent health given that they are selling to them.

开源模型的演化与中国模型的崛起

How much of a shift are you seeing in terms of the types of open source models that are being used? And so I think we've seen an evolution where two three years ago I think the main thing was kind of mistral and then a few other things and then meta kind of came along with llama and then it kind of really shifted

in terms of the misperformant models or of Chinese origin and different ways.

Do you see that sort of mix reflected in terms of what's being used by our customers? Yeah.

Yeah. I I I think customers at least the customers we are serving are very and these are like the fastest growing AI companies in the world that are very forward thinking. they they want to use the best model and they they are optimizing.

I think there is there there are a there's a subset of tasks which I think is small today where people really start to start with cost.

Mhm.

Um but everyone comes from capability first because that's really where the economic growth is being unlocked where the value is being delivered and then they optimize. And I think that's like actually been, you know, and so with that in mind, you know, you you you name like you name it. Everything from GPTOSS all the way to um Moonshot model to Deep Seeks um

to um Canopy or Oreus, which is like really good text to speech

um models um

customers generally want to use whatever's at the frontier. And and I think the um the difference has just been um I think we have a lot more visibility into how to run these and how to run these really well and secondly that they're good. Now

中国模型的安全顾虑与地缘政治

there have been a number of different concerns raised about the use of Chinese models in particular security or is there something embedded in the models or you know Trojan horses or other things.

Um a do you think there's any real concern there and b you know people often talk about how there should be like US counterweights to this from a geopolitical perspective. Do you think that's something that's legitimate or something we should be worried about or how do you think about the sort of origins of these models versus their uses?

Yeah, look, I I I think these these models firstly are fantastic. They're amazing. We work with these teams. They're truly awesome.

item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：中国开源模型，本质上是中国政府对美国企业的"隐性补贴"
访谈中最被低估的洞见来自 Aladdin 那段几乎被礼貌性带过的评论——**当中国国家资本补贴 DeepSeek、Moonshot、Qwen 等模型的训练成本，而这些权重被免费释放给全球时，真正的受益者是采用这些模型的美国企业**。这构成了一个奇特的"反向重商主义"：传统贸易战中，补贴是为了倾销本国商品挤压对手；但在智能这一新型生产要素上，补贴方反而把"剩余价值"转移给了对手国的应用层。
跨领域连接：这非常像**1970-80 年代日本对半导体 DRAM 的国家补贴**——当时美国痛失 DRAM 产业，但同时美国 PC 厂商（Dell、Compaq、Apple）享受了人类历史上最便宜的内存，从而催生了整个 PC 革命与软件帝国。**今天的开源模型，可能正在为下一轮"美国应用层霸权"提供同样的燃料。**问题不在"中国模型是否安全"，而在于：**当智能本身被补贴时，价值会沿着供应链向哪一层迁移？**答案是：向最贴近用户信号的那一层。
---
## 二、Tuhin 隐藏的"反摩尔定律"：post-tra

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] Was Kissinger the 20th Century's Machiavelli? – Ada Palmer
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最不"马基雅维利"的人，恰恰是马基雅维利本人

这段对话埋着一个令人不寒而栗的反讽：写下《君主论》——那本被后世奉为权术圣经的书——的人，在自己人生最关键的权力博弈中，做出了一个彻底反《君主论》的选择。

按他自己书里的逻辑，正确做法是 Dante 式的：去罗马，做枢机主教的座上客，把流放变成杠杆。这是教科书级的 Machiavellian play。但他没去。他坐在托斯卡纳乡下"烂掉"，写信回家说"让我服务祖国，否则什么都不要"。

所以《君主论》也许根本不是一本教科书，而是一封从未寄达的求职信，一份单恋者写给情人的情书。我们读了五百年的"权术圣典"，本质是一个被流放者跪着写完的、试图证明"我还有用"的简历。Kissinger 的类比在这里崩塌——Kissinger 会去罗马的，他真的会。这就是为什么 Palmer 说类比"只在前三分之二人生成立"：分岔点不是能力，是身份依附的方向。

二、佛罗伦萨的"流放即外交"——一个被遗忘的"网络国家"原型

Palmer 点出的这个细节有惊人的系统性意义：佛罗伦萨没有贵族，于是把"被驱逐者"改造成了分布式领事系统。流放 = on probation，但同时 = 被托付国家机密。这不是惩罚的一种弱化，这是冷战级别的人力资源工程——把内政污点直接转化为外交资产。

让我跨三个领域做镜像观察：

  • 现代海外华人/犹太网络：身份的"被迫离散"反而构造出母国无法直接拥有的全球神经网络。
  • 开源社区的 BDFL 流放史：被项目"驱逐"的核心维护者往往成为另一个项目的桥梁节点，反向扩大了原项目的影响半径。
  • 学术界的访问学者制度：表面是临时身份，实际是国家间最低成本的情报与影响力管道。

佛罗伦萨在十五世纪发明的东西，其实是 Balaji Srinivasan 二十一世纪命名的 Network State 原型——一种"以身份而非领土定义边界的国家"。这意味着所谓"网络国家"不是新概念，而是任何缺乏贵族结构的小型城邦在生存压力下都会自然演化出的拓扑。

三、他通过的那场"测试"，其实是一场对系统的反劫持

最反直觉的观察：当佛罗伦萨说"去乡下烂掉"，所有人——包括佛罗伦萨自己——都预期他会逃跑。也就是说，这个测试的设计前提是失败。它不是为了看他能不能通过，而是为了看他多快放弃，从而合法化对他的最终遗弃。

可他通过了。

这让事情变得诡异：当系统期待你背叛时，忠诚反而成为最具破坏性的越界行为。他用自己的"不离开"，把佛罗伦萨的诡计反向劫持，迫使这座城市必须重新解释它对他的所有判断。

这种结构跨域共振得惊人： - 甘地的非暴力不合作（"我比你更服从你的法律"） - 日本职场的"过劳服从式抗议" - AI 对齐研究里讨论的 malicious compliance（恶意合规：通过字面意义上的过度遵守来瓦解指令的真实意图）

三者共享同一个深层结构：用过度履行规则来摧毁规则的有效性。

所以这个故事真正 Machiavellian 的地方在于——马基雅维利没有"通过"那场测试。他让那场测试失去了意义。这是一次更高阶的权术演练：不是赢得游戏，而是让游戏失效。也许这才解释了为什么他在乡下"烂掉"的那十五年，写出了人类历史上最锋利的政治哲学——失败者并不一定是那个走错棋的人，有时候他是那个拒绝继续下这盘棋的人。

📝 转录文本

Kissinger's Core Move

"And the big idea there that Kissinger had was: you want to not ally with a powerful person — because then both your powers will grow commensurately, and you just made somebody strong even stronger. You don't want that. Instead, you want to ally with weak powers. What do you make of this analogy between Kissinger and Machiavelli?"

Where the Analogy Holds — and Where It Breaks

I think it's a good analogy for the first two-thirds of their lives. Um, I think it's important to remember how much he falls from power, and how much more power he could have had in the last chunk of his life — if he had been willing to work for somebody that isn't Florence.

Um, because when Florence exiles you, they tell you: "Go to X place and wait, and if you're good, we'll invite you back." And Florence has been doing this for ages, because Florence actually used this as the core of its diplomatic corps.

Why Florence Turned Exiles into Diplomats

Right? When you have no nobility, you can't have ambassadors in the full-on noble-ambassador sense. There's nobody in the city of sufficient rank to go talk to, uh, the kings who are, you know — and have play chess with the sultan, and all of these things that you have to do to be a proper ambassador.

Um, so what Florence did instead is that they would exile people and say:

"Okay, we're exiling you. You go to Bruges — uh, be our contact in Bruges. You go to London — be our contact in London. Be good. Send us letters informing us what's going on. When we have diplomatic needs to talk to the king, we're going to send letters to you, and you're going to forward them. And if you're good, you get to come back."

So being in exile is sort of being on probation, but also being entrusted with state stuff.

Machiavelli's Exile Was Different

That's not quite what they did with Machiavelli. With Machiavelli, they banished him to a hamlet in the middle of the Tuscan countryside, near nothing important, and said: "Go sit in the country and rot, and if you're good, we'll invite you back."

What they expect — what everyone expects — is that Machiavelli will break that promise and leave. Because he's a well-known statesman, and a scholar, and a playwright, and a historian, and there are dozens of cardinals in Rome and other cities that would love to employ him.

Um, kings of England love employing Florentines to work for them as secretaries. Kings of Naples love employing Florentines to work for them as secretaries. He might go get a job tutoring the daughters of the Duke of Milan, uh, the way Francesco Filelfo did when he was kicked out of Florence for opposing the Medici.

There are lots of places that it's expected an exiled Florentine intellectual will go — where he will have the ear of power, and he will be able to exert influence. He will be a mover and shaker at the court of Milan, or the court of Naples, or the court of, uh, of England.

The Test He Passed by Refusing to Leave

Uh, instead, when they say "Machiavelli, sit in the country and rot," this is a test. He passes the test, and sits in the country faithfully, and rots.

Uh, and if he had wanted to go be an intellectual power broker, the correct move is to run off to Rome — right? — and say: "I will give up the chance to go home, the way Dante did. Uh, but I will be a Florentine in exile, and I will write important things, and I will live at the house of wealthy men who will support me and take me in and give me the ear of power, and I will exert my influence in that way."

He does not do that. He stays in the country, and he rots. And he continues writing letters home saying: "I will serve you or nothing. Bring me home to serve my country."

That is a weird thing to do, right? And not normal for the many other Florentine intellectuals who experienced similar banishments in the same period.

Outro

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →

item.title: [WhynotTV] Danfei Xu：人类数据，行为克隆，机器人GPT-3，全栈，EgoMimic，遥操作，UMI，斯坦福 | WhynotTV Podcast #5
item.dc:creator: WhynotTV
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个反直觉的发现：他不是"选择"了机器人，而是"无法忍受"其他一切

整场访谈最让人意外的并不是 Danfei 对机器人的热爱，而是他反复提到的那种"反向推力"——"不想做某件事的力量比想做某件事的力量更强"。从中学时拒绝应试教育，到博士时拒绝 DeepMind 的自上而下，再到坚持不做行为克隆就"政治不正确"时仍然要做——他的整个职业生涯不是被"吸引"的，而是被"排斥"塑造的。

这是一个被严重低估的人生算法：大多数励志叙事强调"找到你的热爱"，而 Danfei 的故事说明，更可靠的导航器其实是"识别你绝不能忍受的环境"。前者需要漫长的自我探索，后者只需要一次糟糕的体验。从信号论的角度看，"厌恶"比"喜欢"更高频、更强烈、更早出现，因此更适合做决策梯度。

跨领域连接：人形机器人、人类数据与 LLM 的"基础设施寄生"现象

Danfei 反复提到一个关键张力——LLM 之所以能起飞，是因为互联网、键盘、智能手机这些基础设施"恰好"产生了人类语言数据作为副产品；而机器人却必须专门去创造人类数据。这本质上是"数据寄生"和"数据耕作"的差异。

但这里藏着一个让人意外的二阶推论：人形机器人不是因为"像人很酷"才被选中，而是因为它是唯一能让人类身体数据"可消费"的硬件接口。换句话说——人形机器人不是目标，是数据的播放器。就像 MP3 文件需要 MP3 播放器一样。这与音乐产业的演化几乎是镜像的：先有压缩格式（MP3），才有 iPod；先有人类第一人称视频+手部姿态这种"压缩格式"，才有人形机器人。硬件不是在追求拟人化，是在追求"数据可执行性"。

更进一步，这与生物学中的"协同进化"惊人相似——蜂鸟的喙形与花的形状在数百万年里相互塑造。Danfei 说的"两者一起兴起，一起衰落"，本质上是把进化压力从生物时间尺度压缩到了资本时间尺度。

隐藏的系统性真相：行为克隆的污名史揭示了 AI 研究的一种"美学陷阱"

整场对话最深的洞见隐藏在一句轻描淡写的话里："做行为克隆在政治上不正确。" 这不是技术判断，而是美学判断——社区觉得"机器人应该自己学习"才"高尚"，监督学习"不够纯粹"。

这暴露了一个跨领域的系统性模式：当一个学科把"方法的优雅度"看得比"问题的可解性"更重时，它就进入了集体审美陷阱。物理学有过这种时刻（弦论 vs 实证）；经济学有过（DSGE 模型 vs 行为经济学）；现在轮到机器人学。Danfei 在 2019 年看到 BC 在 DeepMind 后台默默 work，却没人愿意发表——这跟孟德尔的豌豆论文被压三十年、跟 Barbara McClintock 的转座子理论被嘲笑几十年，是同一种社会动力学。

更让人后背发凉的洞察是：他和 Ajay 在 2020 年的 RSS 论文已经几乎构建出了 ALOHA 的全部要素，但因为只有两个人 + COVID + 找教职的分心，错过了三年的窗口。这意味着研究范式的转折点往往不是被"发现"的，而是被"足够多人同时相信"才成立。一个人看到真相不算数，需要临界质量。这跟物理学中的相变完全一致——磁畴方向不会因为一个原子改变而翻转，需要足够多原子同步。

所以 Danfei 在博客里反复说"研究品味"——本质上是在说：学会识别哪些是真理，哪些只是美学共识。这种区分能力，才是科研最稀缺的元能力。

📝 转录文本

开场：一列疾驰的列车

Right now, it feels like a train barreling ahead. A few labs are up front laying track like crazy, and behind them all the capital starts pouring fuel into it.

If you put enough sensors on a person, you can basically turn them into a robot. I want to behavior-clone a human. That was so exciting. Back then, every day was basically waking up and thinking, let's do this.

So I started calling every company with robotics in its name. We talked for a long time, and then he said, "All right, come join us. We can't pay you, but come anyway."

In the second year of my PhD, Fei-Fei asked if I wanted to keep doing scene graphs. I said no. I wanted to do robotics.

But why wasn't DeepMind doing behavior cloning? It was because their flagship product, or flagship research agenda, was RL. So they basically pushed behavior cloning aside. I really didn't want to work in that environment.

If you look at teleop data, teleop data itself isn't perfect either. But the gap between humans and robots, and between one robot and another, is it really that big?

主题引入：人类数据

If the leap in large language models came from the massive archive of human language on the internet, then for robots, is there an equally crucial kind of data — not text, not images, but a person picking up a cup or opening a drawer, walking into a room, or interacting with another person, and the traces those actions leave in the physical world?

And that is exactly the key phrase of this episode: human data.

Today's guest is Danfei Xu. Danfei has always seen himself as a roboticist. He's not just someone who trains models in front of a screen, he's also the kind of person who likes sitting next to a robot, watching it move, watching it break, and then fixing it.

From early tinkering with microcontroller cars to building robotic systems from scratch at Stanford, from once-overlooked behavior cloning to asking today how human data can become the underlying fuel for robot learning — for him, the through-line was never a single algorithm, but a full-stack systems problem.

How do we get to the GPT-3 moment for robotics?

In this episode, we'll talk about how Danfei Xu got into robotics, whether human data can become the foundation of robot learning, whether human data and humanoid robots are making each other possible, and when robots start learning human actions, human embodiment, and even human interaction, how smart can they get?

This is WhynotTV Podcast. Now let's step into Danfei Xu's world.

一、童年与成长：兴趣驱动的非主流路径

Earlier, when we were talking, you told me you've always seen yourself as a roboticist. Could you tell me roughly when you first got interested in robots?

I think it was probably in middle school or high school. Back then I was really into making little things by hand, and messing with these little microcontroller cars. And I remember there weren't many resources, so I just tinkered on my own. I didn't really build much. But the idea stayed with me. Yeah, around middle school and high school. Yeah.

So where did you grow up?

I was born in Taiyuan, Shanxi, and then I moved to Shanghai around sixth grade, going into middle school. I did middle school and high school in Shanghai.

So back then, say in middle school or high school, messing with microcontrollers and building little cars, that was just a hobby?

Yeah, it was just a hobby then. I remember I bought all that stuff on Taobao, found some tutorials, followed them a bit, and started tinkering on my own. Yeah.

So do you think as a kid, you were someone with very clear goals early on, or did you find your interests by trial and error?

No, not at all. Yeah, back then, I was really driven by whatever interested me. If I didn't want to do something, I just wouldn't do it. And if I did want to do something, I'd put in anywhere between 50 and 100 percent effort. But if I really didn't want to do something, it got zero percent effort from me.

Any examples? What was something as a kid you really didn't want to do but other people wanted you to?

I actually used to do really badly in school. It's because I really, really, really disliked the whole class-and-exam format. So back then I was really in this state of not knowing why I was doing it at all.

So you weren't the kind of person who got a lot of achievement and positive reinforcement from school.

Yeah school didn't really mean much to me. It was just a place to socialize and where I spent most of my day. That was basically it.

So what were you mainly doing at school then, working on your own stuff?

Yeah, just tinkering with my own stuff, going to class, and doing homework, or not. Right haha.

So in that case, was there a lot of pressure growing up, from teachers and parents?

Yeah, but I think my parents — I mean, how do I put it — weren't really sure how to handle it either. And I didn't really consider their feelings that much back then. So everyone was in kind of an ambiguous place, on both sides. That lasted until around 10th or 11th grade, when I decided to go abroad. At that point, suddenly, the motivation kicked in. After that, everything started revolving around my goal of studying in the U.S. Everything moved in that direction.

决定出国：自驱的转折点

At the time, was going to the U.S. for undergrad your decision or your parents'?

Mine. Yeah. I remember the trigger was probably that in 10th grade I took a trip to the U.S. And I thought, I'm actually pretty interested in that. If I could move somewhere else and experience that kind of life, and do something different...

So that planted the seed of coming to the U.S.?

Yeah. I remember after that trip, I pretty much made up my mind to do it. There was no turning back after that.

So what changed at that point? You started seriously preparing your applications, working really hard?

Yeah, very motivated. And I did everything myself. I don't know what it's like now, but for U.S. college applications, I remember back then there were basically two camps: some people did it all DIY, handling everything themselves, and I was one of them. I didn't use any agency. I did everything myself, yeah.

How did you gather information back then?

QQ groups, haha. Back when QQ was still a thing. And there was also a forum called CUUS, not sure if you've heard of it. It was a really old-school study-abroad forum. And back then, for U.S. college applications, people preparing to apply — a lot of DIY applicants were on there, sharing information and so on. Yeah, and info about the SAT, the TOEFL, all that was on there. People shared a lot there.

Right. So which high school were you at in Shanghai?

I went to a school called Shangnan High School. It was basically a well-regarded district high school.

Was applying to U.S. colleges common there?

No. Not at all. I remember because again, I was an average student, that school was also pretty middle-of-the-pack, more or less. So at the time, I think in our grade, the number of people going to the U.S. was maybe two or three. Yeah. In that class year, in the whole grade, basically only two or three people applied to U.S. colleges.

Right. You were basically one of the very few.

Right. Definitely a very unusual path.

Did that feel stressful at the time, because most classmates were on the gaokao track, and you...

Again, like I said, I'm a very interest-driven person. If something didn't align with my interests, I had no desire to do it at all. So I didn't really feel pressure. I didn't want to do it anyway.

Right. So for you, what mattered most was interest. Not whether it was mainstream.

Right. That kind of thing had nothing to do with me.

You've been like that since you were little?

More or less.

Wow, that's impressive. So you never felt that, since you had to step away from that exam-focused system and barely even go to school?

At the time, I was actually very happy. Because I guess maybe at the time... I can't really remember what state I was in then, but I never felt that being outside the mainstream was something that put pressure on me. I felt being outside the mainstream was actually really, really cool.

It made you feel even more excited, maybe.

Yeah exactly.

申请过程：孤身一人

Was the US college application process smooth then? Did you run into any problems along the way?

Honestly, it was pretty rough. I did everything myself. And then my parents also really didn't understand that system at all. They hadn't gone to college either, so they weren't insiders, or anything like that. So all they could do was worry. About this whole thing. So I was basically doing it all alone. And most of the information I got came from people I met online.

So in the end, the results were just okay. Because I ended up at a liberal arts college called Dickinson College. At the time I was choosing among a few schools, sorting through several options. And back then there was kind of a small group who thought LACs were great, and I was probably one of them. And honestly, I later felt that choice was actually fine. I felt that at an LAC, there was more room to explore on my own. And you could be much closer to professors. So that also led to things later on, like doing research.

That really surprises me. Because I think usually students applying to US colleges get a lot of family support. Sometimes their parents had already arranged everything. You were nothing like that.

Not at all. Right. Back then, my parents basically just did this: if I said I wanted to take a test, they'd say okay. Like if I said I wanted to take the SAT, I arranged everything myself. All I needed was for them to pay for it.

So you basically stopped going to school from tenth grade on.

Yeah, I went for about half of eleventh grade.

So when you were fifteen or sixteen, or sixteen or seventeen, you were basically alone in Shanghai, with a backpack, preparing your applications.

Pretty much.

So you didn't really have classmates then, right?

A few, people I kind of knew, like some middle school classmates, or some high school classmates who would maybe study with me sometimes. We'd prepare for exams together. There were just a few people like that.

That's impressive. If I were in that situation, I'd feel a lot of uncertainty. I'd be a little scared.

There was definitely uncertainty, but how do I put it... not wanting to do it... the drive to avoid things I wasn't interested in was stronger. That reverse push was stronger.

If I sent you back to prepare for the gaokao, you'd hate that even more.

Much more. Yeah haha.

What do you think you'd hate most about the gaokao path?

I just don't know what the point of doing it is.

Mm right.

If something feels meaningless, I can't convince myself to do it. I really admire people. I mean, they know that in the long run, some things that seem pointless right now turn out to be very helpful later. But when I was younger, I couldn't really accept that. If, at the time, something felt meaningless or just meaningless to me, I didn't really want to do it.

Among your experiences before 18, looking back now, is there anything that has deeply shaped your life?

It probably made me realize that when something is highly uncertain and has no framework at all, I'm actually very good at it. I can come up with lots of ways to solve a highly uncertain problem.

Wow, that sounds perfect for research.

Yeah yeah.

So back then, when you were applying to US colleges, you had this confidence that even with uncertainty, you could still handle it?

Only in hindsight. This is really only in hindsight. At the time, I was definitely panicking too. But again, the push in the other direction was stronger. It's just that later, looking back, after doing this kind of thing again and again, I started to feel like I could do a lot of things on my own.

二、本科：从打电话到康奈尔的西海岸冒险

Then you crossed the ocean to the US for college. That was your second time in the US?

Yeah, my second time.

When you first got there for undergrad, did you know what you wanted to study or what you were interested in?

Two things: CS and physics. Those were probably the two subjects I was most interested in in middle and high school, or at least didn't resist as much. They were also two of the few subjects I was good at. So I started out as a CS major with a physics minor.

And later, didn't you transfer during undergrad to Columbia?

Yeah, that's right. Because at the time, a lot of liberal arts colleges had 3+2 programs with universities. You'd spend three years at an LAC, and then go to one of those universities for two more years. So I ended up at Columbia. I was there from around 2013 to 2015.

And I saw that your first undergraduate paper was an ICRA 2013 paper. Was that done at Columbia?

No, that was at the LAC. That part is interesting too. Because at the time, I remember in my first or second year, maybe my second year. I can't quite remember. Actually, it was probably my first year when my desire to do research really crossed a threshold. So I started calling all kinds of companies with 'robotics' in their name. I literally just called them.

You'd Google their number and call.

I'd ask if they were hiring interns, like summer interns. I think I made around 20 calls. That included Boston Dynamics and some pretty weird companies. In the end, one company was called SynTouch Robotics. I think it was SynTouch LLC back then. One of their products was called BioTac. It's that tactile sensor you've probably seen on all kinds of robots. It looks like a green finger, basically. It's a bio-inspired sensor, probably state-of-the-art at the time.

But anyway, back to the story, after I made that call, the person who picked up ended up becoming my internship mentor and is now a lifelong friend, Jeremy Fishel. And he basically thought, this kid was pretty impressive. How did he even think to call us? We talked for a long time, and then he said, "Then come on over. We won't pay you either. Just come anyway." Haha. Basically, come do research, just come do it. So I went.

So none of that research was done at school?

No. Yeah, I literally cold-called them. I wanted to do this so badly. So someone just happened to see how serious I was about it and think, what do I have to lose? But he was also just — how should I put it, haha — a really nice person, and willing to take chances on people. I remember my spoken English was terrible then. So after talking for a while, we probably only half understood each other. He probably only half got what I wanted to do. But he could tell I was really passionate about it.

So you just Googled their number, called and said, "I'm an undergrad here, can I come do robotics?"

Right, the company was in LA, right next to USC.

So you flew out to the West Coast for it.

Yeah. Then I started doing research and working on that project. I remember it was, in my paper, described as a Bayesian approach to tactile sensing. The main idea back then was how to use this sensor, the BioTac, this tactile sensor, to classify what kind of object it was touching.

Basically, the sensor had three modalities. One was pressure, basically how much deformation there was. Another was heat capacity. This part was really amazing: the sensor would heat itself to about human body temperature, around 37 degrees, or really skin temperature, basically skin temperature. And based on the material's rate of heat transfer, it could classify the material. That was another modality. The last modality was vibration. It had a high-frequency vibration sampler, around 2,000 hertz. If you slid your finger over it, across the object's surface, you'd get a characteristic vibration. Wow.

But when you made that call, I feel like for U.S. undergrads, especially at a liberal arts college, very few students would say as freshmen or sophomores, "I want to do research, I want to do robotics."

I just wanted to do robotics research so badly. So I made some attempts that are hard to imagine now, that sort of thing.

Right. I think most people just follow the standard path.

Right.

But once again, just like when you applied to U.S. colleges, you were making these calls while in college, and flying to the West Coast.

And later there was something even crazier. I'll tell you about that later.

Right.

After that project, that was probably my first time working with a dexterous hand. People later heard about the Shadow Hand, right? The one from Shadow Robotics. By the time people heard of it, I'd already broken several of them. Hahaha. Because back then, SynTouch was probably one of the first companies to get Shadow Hands. And that summer, I probably broke seven or eight fingers. Wow.

But back then, as an undergrad, as a computer science student, most people were probably doing pure algorithms or the kind of work internet companies cared about, right? So why robotics?

I just really wanted to do robotics. I really wanted to make things move. I wasn't that interested in doing pure algorithms. Right. When it came to hardware, looking back, I think I have a natural affinity for hardware. I really love being able to actually sit next to the robot, watch it move, break it, and fix it. And I really, really enjoyed that process.

CMU 之夏：开车去敲门

So this was between your sophomore and junior years, when you flew to LA for an internship at SynTouch. And I saw that as an undergrad, you also had a first-author paper at IROS 2014. Was that through that CMU summer program, the one called RISS? And that story was also pretty wild.

This is the second wild story you mentioned.

Right, the second wild story.

So I applied at the time, I had heard about RISS, because it was their first year.

You were in the first RISS cohort?

Yeah. And since it was the first year, everything was pretty chaotic. Because they were basically matching students with professors, and there may have been interviews. At the time, I emailed one of the faculty working on autonomous vehicles, who was basically a research professor. And he replied, and said my background might be a fit. So I thought maybe there was a chance.

And then you drove to CMU.

[laughs] And just showed up at his door and said, "Should we talk about this? If you thought it might work, should we talk?"

You just knocked on his door? You found his office on the website?

Yeah. Then we talked for half an hour, and he said, "come join us." Because at the time, my school was about four hours from CMU, by car. [laughs]

So you drove to Pittsburgh, and went to NSH, no, actually, Smith Hall.

Smith Hall. Right. So as long as there was a chance, I wanted to make it happen.

Yeah. It was just this sense of, I mean, why not? Right? So what was the project then?

It was self-driving car localization. And it was also a very classic problem: how to do city-wide localization without GPS.

Using RGB?

Using RGB, yes. We had a Jeep back then, a modified Jeep, with six cameras mounted on it. Then another researcher and I drove out every day to collect map data.

So you weren't just working off a dataset?

No. We just drove around Pittsburgh. I remember that in a typical week, I'd spend two days on the road. There'd be a research engineer next to me, and I'd be there with a laptop, basically just live-logging the data.

Right. [laughs] So two days a week, you were out on the road.

Yeah. That went on for a few weeks, basically just collecting data. When you first started on the algorithm, you were probably in the office most of the time, and then in the last few weeks before the deadline, you were just collecting data nonstop. [laughs] There were six computers in the back of that car. They were basically handling data streaming and live data collection. [laughs]

I bet you were having a blast then.

Super happy. [laughs] Yeah. I just thought, wow, this is so cool. [laughs] I got to be around hardware every day, with six computers in the back. Yeah, every night I'd go work on, for example, one big issue we had was NTP, camera sync.

Time alignment.

Time alignment across servers. I remember that night I stayed in the lab all night, working on the network time alignment.

Right. But the RGB-based localization you were doing then, AlexNet should already have existed by then.

No.

Oh.

Actually yes. It already existed in 2012. But back then, we really had none of that. Across all of CMU, hardly anyone was talking about it. Across the whole Robotics Institute, everyone was using very classical methods. I remember field robotics was the hottest area. People were doing self-driving, off-road, off-road driving, and helicopters and things like that. That was the hottest thing then.

So in undergrad, deep learning was totally not part of it?

Not at all.

Right. Really not until I started my PhD. So you...

Actually no. There was one other time. But basically, around senior year, Jim Fan and I were classmates, and we started a project. I think we implemented a ConvNet from scratch in C++.

So senior year was your first exposure to it?

But by then, had you already applied to PhD programs yet?

Around then.

It was around that time.

三、博士选择：去斯坦福的"机器人沙漠"

Right. But by then you'd done a lot of robotics, a lot of hardware, yet when you applied for PhD programs, you got into Stanford, and Fei-Fei gave you an offer.

Yes, Fei-Fei and Silvio both gave me offers.

But that really didn't match your idea of hard-core robotics, with lots of hardware. So about that choice...

That's a great question. I wrestled with that for a very long time. [laughs] I got offers from CMU, UW, and Stanford. At UW, the advisor was Dieter. I was really torn between Dieter and Stanford for a very, very long time. I remember I didn't decide until April 14, the day before the deadline. Yeah haha. And that was exactly why. Because I felt Stanford had too few people doing robotics. There really wasn't anyone, other than Oussama. [laughs] I remember Jeannette only arrived in my second year. Then later there was Chelsea, Dorsa, Shuran, Karen, people like that. They came during my PhD. At that time, there wasn't a single person doing robotics in CS.

So when you got the offer, like when you went to Stanford's open house, you felt it was a robotics desert.

Exactly. There was nobody. Not to mention robot learning. No, yeah, "robot learning" wasn't even a concept back then. That just didn't exist.

Right. But you still chose Stanford. What was the deciding factor?

I think I just had this vague feeling there was something bigger there, something bigger. It just felt bigger. There was something bigger I could do there.

What signs gave you that feeling?

UW felt too certain. Because I knew what I'd do there. Probably 3D vision, some state estimation, some planning, things like that. But at Stanford, I had no idea what I'd do. And I found that really exciting.

So it's not just that you're good under uncertainty, you actually go looking for it. You seek uncertainty.

Later on I started doing some uncertainty-seeking things.

But in undergrad, did you ever hesitate about doing a PhD?

No. I never even thought about not doing one. Because after doing research in my first two years, I just really wanted to do research. Haha yeah. I never even considered getting a full-time job.

But that was also among U.S. undergrads, a pretty rare choice.

Yeah, very rare. Maybe one or two. Haha yeah.

四、博士早期：探索与回归机器人

You got to Stanford for your PhD in 2015, right?

Right.

Tell us what the Stanford CS department was like then.

Most people were doing vision then. A lot of people were working on vision. I remember that in Fei-Fei's group, Yuke was working on Scene Graphs. And Silvio's group was doing 3D vision. And then, everyone had already started using deep learning. I remember Cewu had a paper around then on Scene Graph classification. That was probably when we started thinking about Scene Graphs. And on Silvio's side, they were doing some 3D reconstruction work. Our first project was on end-to-end 3D reconstruction.

To me, that paper was the kind of topic you probably wouldn't be that interested in, because it was purely algorithmic.

Exactly. But both of those were actually done during my rotations. Stanford had a rotation program then, and each term you joined a different group. So my first one was 3D-R2N2 with Silvio. Then my second term was with Leo Guibas, and we worked on something that was actually egocentric, kind of VR-related, doing human data capture and modeling. That was really what I was working on then. Nothing much came out of it, but I hacked together a whole pipeline from VR to human data capture, the whole system.

I remember I did something pretty crazy. I bought an Oculus DK myself, the developer kit, right when it first came out. With my own money. And I brought it to the office for my own research. And I remember I mounted a Leap Motion on the DK to do hand capture. It was basically the same as the EgoMimic work we did later, the initial prototype was identical. VR plus a camera for hand tracking, to do egocentric hand capture.

That was your second rotation, something you did in winter 2015? And after that?

Then with Fei-Fei's group, Yuke and I worked on Scene Graph generation.

At that point you were trying to explore as much as possible?

Yeah, explore broadly. At the time I was just exploring, deep learning felt incredibly cool. I kept wondering, how does this even work? And later I started working on more computer vision problems. Then in my second year, Fei-Fei asked if I wanted to keep doing Scene Graphs. I said no, I wanted to do robotics. Haha. So then I went back to robotics.

When Fei-Fei asked if you wanted to keep doing Scene Graphs and you said no, "I want to do robotics," did you know what you were signing up for? That you'd have to do everything yourself, maybe without even the hardware?

I'm perfectly comfortable with that. Haha. I just really felt that if there were something that let me fully own it myself, I'd be extremely happy. I hate other people telling me what to do. As long as she gave me the green light, I just went full steam ahead.

So I guess she agreed. She let you do robotics.

Yeah, there were actually other people doing it too then. Yuke started doing it too. So Yuke and I, and Ajay had also joined by then, there were about four of us, and we started forming basically what might still have been called the Stanford Vision Lab. Or maybe it was just called Vision Lab then. Not SVL. We renamed it later. And then that was when we started doing robotics.

You doing robotics then, I can understand that. You're a robotics person. That's your passion. But for others, like Yuke and Ajay, what brought the others into robotics? What did you all see, what signs were there, that made people feel robotics was promising?

Yeah, that's a great question. I... when I think back on it, should probably check with Yuke. But I think people just felt vision wasn't that interesting anymore. That was the feeling then. And then everyone we started getting into robotics around 2016 or 2017.

当时机器人学的两大阵营

So what was robotics like at the time?

I think there were maybe two camps. One was more CV-centric. Around then, the first GraspNet-type work started appearing. It treated robotics as a vision problem. And the second was that RL was starting to work. Because of AlphaGo, people felt RL was really powerful. People like Sergey and others started doing online RL, offline RL, all that stuff. That was starting to happen. Those were basically the two main camps.

And there was very little work on real robots. Basically everyone was either doing vision-based single-action pick-and-place, or this motor babbling plus exploration. Those were the two broad types.

So in 2017, in robotics, vision and deep learning were starting to work, but not with anything like today's success.

Right, there was this feeling that there were two ways of thinking. One was that priors are great. You should put as much as possible into your system as prior knowledge. That prior could be structure, neural-symbolic ideas, programs, dynamics, but the key was structure. The second view was that supervised learning was almost shameful. You weren't supposed to do supervised learning in robotics. Robots were supposed to learn on their own. They needed to learn by themselves. People looked down on supervised learning.

Right. Yeah. So either you had to be smart about your priors, or find a way for robots to learn by themselves. So in that context, you said some people treated robotics as a vision problem, and some did RL. You came from a very traditional robotics background. You knew control, planning, dynamics, all that. Right. How did you position yourself in all this? What was your view of learning for robotics back then? Because I saw that after Scene Graph, you actually spent some time on task and motion planning.

Right. The real trigger was that we were thinking about one thing: how to do what was probably a pretty unrealistic idea: we wanted demonstration in, action out. We called it one-shot imitation learning.

A single episode?

Yeah, a single episode. And I remember after I started on it, halfway through, I suddenly found OpenAI had done basically the same paper. So we changed course. We made it more structured, adding some structural priors. So that became Neural Task Programming, NTP. That probably shaped my tastes during my PhD. I really liked structured approaches. And later, the extreme version of that was task and motion planning, right?

Looking back now, do you still hold on to that preference for structure?

Later I figured something out. Compositionality is a very good thing. But it doesn't necessarily have to be a method. It can also be a problem statement. How do you enable compositionality?

My understanding of task and motion planning is that the problem it tries to solve is first the task level: what do I do first, what do I do next, and how do I handle dependencies? And second, for a specific task, how does my robot arm actually carry it out? What that motion should look like. It also has to avoid obstacles. There are requirements like that. Right. That's the explicit formulation. But underneath, I think at its core it's really to take a huge space that you can't search directly, decompose it, and then search within those local problems, and then figure out how those spaces fit together, which becomes another search problem.

What I'm really curious about is, I think a lot of robotics tasks have structure that's hard to hand-design. Right. And I think imposing that structure, the upside I can see is that it naturally brings generalization. Right. But I feel like human-imposed structure can also become a pretty hard ceiling on what we can solve. Right. Could it be that now, looking back, because The Bitter Lesson hadn't been written yet, it seems like task and motion planning is basically against the Bitter Lesson, just really the opposite of the Bitter Lesson?

Right. I think in constrained factory settings, it should still be very useful. But when people actually deploy in factories, they may use methods that are less principled, like behavior trees and other finite-state-machine methods.

Later you had this idea that I remember you mentioning a lot, that you wanted to do generative task and motion planning, generative task and motion planning. What was the insight behind that then? And do you think now that generative task and motion planning fits the Bitter Lesson well enough?

It's getting there. Because now we're already starting to do video-based generative TAMP. Because those trajectory plans can be videos, right? Because once you turn it into a deep generative model, it can generate plans in any space. So we're getting there, I think. We're finally close to a Bitter Lesson version of TAMP.

五、DeepMind 之夏与行为克隆的觉醒

During your PhD, when did you realize that, like we were just discussing, the structures in task and motion planning were something you liked at first, but gradually came to see the limitations of? And I noticed that later you stopped and started doing teleoperation and behavior cloning, instead of continuing to work on task and motion planning. What was that shift like for you? What sparked it?

It was actually when I went to DeepMind in 2019 for a summer internship. And at the time, DeepMind was basically the Mecca of robot learning, right? There really wasn't anywhere else back then. Maybe OpenAI had just started, and DeepMind was the place. One thing I worked on there was generative imitation.

That paper of yours, when I was preparing for this podcast, it looked really familiar. Then I suddenly realized it was a paper I presented at a lab meeting my freshman year. Hahaha. I even dug up the slides.

Haha right. Those were the good old days. Back then, generative imitation was a really interesting problem. But actually, I want to talk to everyone who's worked on GAIL, because I think it led me down a lot of detours in how I framed problems. Because it really sounds so interesting, so exciting. The setup is that you have some demonstrations, and you can still interact with the environment, but there's no reward. In fact, I think the real world is the opposite. Interacting with the environment is very, very hard. Right. But you can get plenty of demonstrations.

Right. But what I think I learned back then wasn't really whether how scalable the algorithm was or wasn't scalable. What I saw then was a lot of DeepMind's infrastructure, their robotics systems, and that behavior cloning really worked. I saw it with my own eyes: behavior cloning works.

But why weren't they doing behavior cloning? Because their flagship product, or flagship research agenda, was reinforcement learning. So they basically pushed behavior cloning aside. Doing behavior cloning just wasn't politically correct.

Or rather, it wasn't just DeepMind. At the time, the whole community basically thought that behavior cloning was not the politically correct approach. The view was that robots had to learn on their own. You couldn't just have humans teach them. Because people naturally felt supervised learning wasn't good enough, or scalable enough. Right haha. People naturally felt RL was the right paradigm for robotics.

Right because it could already play board games, so why couldn't it do robotics? Right? Haha.

You just had to scale it up. Right. But after seeing all that, I thought, if BC works so well, why not do BC? Then when I got back, around 2019, Ajay and I spent about three months putting together a behavior cloning project on a Franka arm.

什么是行为克隆

For people outside the field, could you explain what behavior cloning is, in robotics?

Behavior cloning for robots, or just behavior cloning, is basically treating robotics as a supervised learning problem. In other words, you turn it into that kind of learning problem. You directly provide the supervision. You can take, say, an iPhone or a VR controller, and use it directly to control the robot. At the same time, you also record what it sees, all the camera images, and then the commands you use to control it, the control commands, become your inputs and labels, which you feed into a large model, or a smaller one, to learn a control policy.

I think back then, when I first joined an undergrad lab in 2019, to work on imitation learning, every paper was trashing behavior cloning.

Right haha. In the first two paragraphs of the introduction, there was always a line saying behavior cloning didn't have good sample efficiency, and suffered from compounding error. Right. And at the time, I just believed it. So I thought we definitely couldn't do behavior cloning. Behavior cloning was the weakest baseline.

Right. So what did you see at DeepMind back then?

They had a huge amount of data. They really were using — I think a SpaceMouse for teleoperation, with a Sawyer arm, and they were collecting extremely high-quality data. They also had some failed trajectories. They also did reward labeling. But if you filtered out all the less-than-optimal data, plain BC would just work. You didn't need RL. Right. It could handle most tasks. It's a competitive baseline for almost everything. And that made me think, why not just do behavior cloning?

After seeing those results at DeepMind, I felt it should work. But the issue was that academia just didn't really give this enough attention. People also hadn't really figured out how to do teleoperation well. and then how to really make behavior cloning work on top of it. Basically, nobody had done that before.

与 Ajay 的合作：BC 真的 work

So I thought, Ajay and I started working on it. Ajay is incredibly capable. He's also one of my best friends. Back in 2017 and 2018, with Yuke and Animesh, who were others in our lab, he worked on this for about two years. Back then, they had a project called Arm Farm. We bought four or five robots and used iPhones as a teleoperation interface for data collection. And we pooled all that data.

But at the time, they were still pretty concerned about this stigma around BC, so what they kept pushing was offline RL. Offline reinforcement learning was a more — how should I put it, less likely to work but theoretically workable kind of approach, and easier to write a paper about. I remember it just never really worked.

Then later, Ajay and I kind of immediately clicked. He thought BC would work. He was convinced BC would work. We didn't need offline RL. I thought BC would work too. We didn't need offline RL either. So we clicked right away and started working on it.

So tell us about that paper.

That paper was actually very simple at its core. What we actually showed was very simple: BC works. On a Franka Panda, we built a force-controlled teleoperation setup that made teleoperation feel incredibly smooth. And then we built the whole pipeline, from data collection through behavior cloning. And we made a lot of arbitrary choices.

For example, we decided to put a wrist camera on the robot. Why not, right? No one was using wrist cameras then. So we did. Then we decided to use ResNet-18 as the encoder. Why not. Then we used this thing called spatial... I think it was spatial softmax, a spatial softmax layer. Why not. Then we added an RNN. An RNN. We needed some history, so why not.

Everything was basically just ideas we made up on the spot. And somehow it worked. It really could learn lots and lots of behaviors we'd never seen before on robots. For example, a very real 30-second task at 1x speed, with no speed-up, was putting something into an oven: pulling out the oven tray, putting the item in, closing the oven, and sliding the tray back in. Before that, we hadn't seen anyone do anything at that level. Even though it wasn't generalizable, not at all, it was still a big sign of life, right?

But even then, we still added another layer, a more paper-friendly story. [laughs] But the reason we had to do that was basically this stigma around BC. If you just did BC, you worried the paper wouldn't get accepted.

Yeah exactly.

Right. Even though both of us strongly believed BC was going to work, to get the paper published, we still had to add something on top. [laughs]

学术界的范式之争

I think every PhD student, hearing you say to get this paper accepted, we had to come up with some extra novelty, can relate to that. So now you're not just a PhD student anymore. You're also a professor now. You're taking on more responsibility in academia. Right? So what do you think? How can we do better in academia? How do we let PhD students do work that really matters, instead of work that just satisfies reviewers?

I don't think it's that simple. It's not that you should only do work that really matters, or that you should do whatever will be hot in the next two or three years. I think for a PhD student, the most important thing is, of course, to do really solid work. Second, you need to go through the process of developing taste. Only doing work that really matters is one kind of taste, but it's not the only kind. Right? Of course.

That said, From the community's point of view, we really do need to change some of these rigid norms. We have to tear them down. If you're doing work that really matters, you shouldn't be shamed for it. Right? That's something we need to fix.

In robot learning, as a field, if we think of it as a systems problem, a full-stack systems problem, what should we do? It's not just an algorithm problem, it's a systems problem from hardware to algorithms. So how exactly do we evaluate a piece of work? I mean, if you put A plus B plus C together and build a really strong system, is that actually novel? Or should you even judge it by novelty, for a system like that?

Looking back now, at the time I felt the whole field was in a frenzy over reinforcement learning for robotics. Most people, like me, I was just a freshman or sophomore then, and I just followed the crowd. Right? When you chose BC, one reason was that at DeepMind you saw that BC actually worked. What else in RL made you feel there was a wall you couldn't get past?

I found it hard to believe that it could really scale up, that this method could do what we wanted it to do, like cooking a meal, or handle the tasks we talked about every day in talks. I just couldn't see a path at that point.

Mm.

And when I was training GAIL, I used 10% of DeepMind's total compute.

For the record, GAIL is a way of using RL for imitation learning.

Right.

So back then, you went against the grain. The whole community was doing RL, whether offline RL or generative RL, right. After you did BC, what kind of impact did it have?

Not much. COVID hit. The only impact I remember was I gave a talk at Stanford, and I remember Chelsea Finn being really excited. She spoke with me for a long time, asking how it worked and all that. I could tell she was extremely excited. Other than that, it honestly didn't make much of a splash. Internally, we all felt very strongly that this thing really worked. But at the time everyone was focused on the COVID shutdown and not having access to robots. People were probably just in a very chaotic state.

So even after you did that, not many people cared.

Not many people cared. Because it was just BC, right? [laughs] Everyone just thought, okay, We all know behavior cloning. Stanford students just did it with a better system. That's it.

Yeah, that was it. People didn't see it as a paradigm shift.

Not at all.

Right. But internally, did you think this was going to be big?

We were ready to go all in. We were going to publish four or five papers on it that year. But then COVID hit.

So if COVID hadn't happened, you would've...

Yeah. It would've been completely different.

Then COVID kept you out of the lab. No access to real robots.

Because the second paper we planned was DAgger. Our immediate next step was DAgger for BC, right? We wanted to make DAgger work. [Laughs] But unfortunately, at that time, we only had simulation experiments.

系统的重要性与那场未发生的革命

I actually read that RSS 2020 paper with Ajay, and I had one question: Why didn't you do bimanual, a two-arm setup?

Because back then, in just three months, we built the whole Franka stack starting from C++ all the way to the algorithms. Even the learning-rate tuning, we did all of that in three months.

So were you working overtime every day? Till 3 a.m. every night?

[Laughs] Every night till 3 a.m. Yeah. [Laughs] The two of us were down at Gates every day, till 3 a.m. every night.

But it must've been fun.

It was so exciting. Back then, every day I just woke up thinking, let's do this.

Did it feel like you were setting the field straight?

Yeah, I felt that BC absolutely worked.

Today, people make behavior cloning sound simple. Like, once you have data, you just train. Right. But anyone who's actually done behavior cloning knows the hardest part usually isn't the model. It's the system itself. The data distribution and evaluation setup. Was that understanding something you developed through that project?
