Yeah, because back then we felt that if you really want to build a robotics system well, from the robot hardware itself, to camera placement, to the controller, what its behavior should be, all the way to control response time, and camera latency, all of it really matters. If you overlook even one piece, you'll definitely have problems.

But at the time, did you feel the whole community had shifted?

A little bit. I remember by late 2020, and into 2021, people were gradually starting to accept that BC could work. And that shift had already begun. But I think the real paradigm shift was probably ALOHA, that line of work. By 2023, people were gradually starting to feel this just works.

Does that feel like a shame? Because in 2020 you were already doing single-arm teleoperation. You weren't that far from ALOHA, right?

Very close. I'd argue we had a better system. It's just that there were only two of us working on it. Some things probably weren't fully thought through, and we didn't have time to fill in the gaps. And in 2020, during COVID, we couldn't run many systematic experiments. That was just an objective constraint.

But subjectively, in terms of how you saw it, what now feels unfortunate in hindsight, if you'd understood it better then, might things have gone differently?

Yeah, that's a great question. I think back then, we only had our own evidence showing that this worked. But which direction to push in, we really weren't sure. Was it the model, or the action space? Or was it the data itself? There were just too many things we could try. But which direction to go in wasn't really something we had a strong hypothesis for. The problem itself had too many possibilities. So you probably needed a much bigger research agenda, with lots of people working on it together, and pushing in all sorts of directions, before you could really get good results. So with just the two of us, it was hard. So later we only did more selective work, like DAgger and benchmarks.

In a way, because at the time you started your PhD in 2015, and after the RSS paper in 2020, you were already in year five, almost year six, about to go on the faculty job market. Was the faculty job search actually something that distracted you from this?

Yes. At the time, that research direction was a huge gold mine.

Yes exactly. [Laughter]

Mainly, in hindsight, speaking with 20/20 hindsight, that's true. I really should have kept pushing it.

六、博士期间的实习与教训

You also did a few internships during your PhD. I noticed Autodesk in 2016, Zoox in 2017, which was autonomous driving, right?

Yes self-driving.

And then in 2019, like you just said, you were at DeepMind, where you saw that BC actually works. Those internships during your PhD, how did they change your perspective or your way of thinking?

For me, internships were how should I put it... after each one, every internship had a real impact on me. One of them was Zoox. It made me feel self-driving was too boring. [Laughter] I definitely never wanted to do self-driving again. Back in 2017, everyone was doing self-driving, so I thought, fine, I'll do self-driving too. I had also worked on self-driving as an undergrad, and I thought it was pretty interesting. But once I started, I looked at the problem and thought, how had it become like this? For everyone, it had basically turned into a 3D vision problem. Right? And at the time I thought it was really boring.

And back then, self-driving everyone's pipeline was perception, planning, and control.

Right perception.

Exactly. It was all about making 3D perception work.

So what you found boring was that the problem had collapsed into a vision problem.

Yes, it had become a vision problem. Not enough of a systems problem, not full-stack enough.

Right exactly. It wasn't really a robotics problem anymore.

What made 2013 so exciting for me was that I could actually sit in the car and tinker with all kinds of cameras and other low-level stuff. It was the whole full stack. Now self-driving has become a very engineering-heavy problem. We had a separate 2D vision team, 3D vision team, planning team, and simulation team. There wasn't much communication between them, because there didn't need to be. You just had to push your own benchmark up.

Right. Do you think as robotics matures, it could become like that one day?

I don't think so. I think the thing is, if you really want to do robotics well, I don't think it's something that division of labor can solve. Fundamentally, at least for the research path I believe in, division of labor won't solve it. Everyone needs to know everything.

So you think full-stack is the only way out. We can come back to full-stack in a bit. I want to end by looking back at your PhD. You've already partly answered this, but what unpopular ideas did you have back then that now look right?

Behavior cloning, right?

But was there a direction you thought was right back then that turned out to be wrong?

I think this whole neural-symbolic language for robotics was something I really believed in back then. Later, after seeing the RT series, I felt it was still too far off. Using language, this kind of deliberately grounding language in action, as a route, I don't think it's very good.

You mean Google's RT?

Right, RT is from Google. It's basically high-level language modeling with low-level policy learning.

Let me put that more precisely. I think making language the foundational capability in robotics, for example, having a language model write programs to control robots, or having a language model generate a plan, with the whole plan driven by the language model, deciding things like what to do next, I think that's the wrong approach. Meaning, the symbolic layer and the physical layer are too far apart.

So that symbolic layer, not just the traditional academic neural-symbolic approach, You think even LLMs aren't enough?

They're still not enough.

Where do you think the gap is?

The kind of prior it gives us, that is, our prior knowledge, is still far from what robots actually need, from what really makes robots hard. Task planning, that whole problem, is actually very, very simple compared with dexterous manipulation. The gap between them is just too large. If we can solve dexterous manipulation, the rest is much easier.

So you think dexterous manipulation is the hardest and most important problem today?

Dexterous manipulation, and this kind of physical common sense. The kind of physical common sense that robots can express through their own manipulation, actually embodying that common sense, is an extremely hard problem.

七、走向教职：自由的代价

By the end of your PhD, you decided to look for faculty positions. Right. Was that because you'd always wanted to be a professor, or was that a decision you came to later? How did you decide to pursue faculty jobs?

That's a good question. Before my DeepMind internship, I was never really sure. After the internship, I was sure. Because I really didn't like that internship environment.

What kind of environment was DeepMind?

It was shifting from a very open, very exploratory research environment to a much more top-down organization. Around the summer of 2019, when they were working on AlphaFold. So basically, whatever Demis says goes.

Right.

Exactly. That made me feel I really didn't want to work in that kind of environment. I don't want other people telling me what to do. I need to work on what interests me. So how could I work on what interested me? Either start a company or go for a faculty job, right?

So you feel you have a very strong conviction about what you want to do in the future, and you don't want external factors to affect that.

Right. And academia gives you the most freedom.

OK, so that was 2020 or 2021, right? I think now, in 2026, the situation looks very different again. In some sense, I'd still say academia gives you that freedom. Right. But the field has become increasingly resource-intensive. Right. Is freedom without resources still really freedom?

That's a very, very good question. I think I'm not the best person to answer it, because right now I do have some industry resources while in academia. But most people don't have that option. So if you really asked me, if I didn't have those resources, if I had to choose again, I would probably lean toward going into industry first. There are some things that, unless you are in industry, you really cannot do.

八、机器人学习与人类数据

Next, I want to focus on robot learning and human data. Could you briefly explain what robot learning is and where it diverges from traditional robotics?

I think robot learning is really not so much a boundary as it is this: we are trying to solve the same problems, how to make robots plan better, do finer manipulation, and achieve better whole-body control. The difference is just that all those methods are now data-driven. We need data to feed those models.

For example, the very hot whole-body control problems today are a great example. Previously, people would write down the dynamics, the whole-body dynamics, and then formulate an optimization problem. Now, people may solve it entirely with reinforcement learning.

So traditional whole-body control involved writing down the physics, writing the dynamics, and solving the optimization. How was traditional manipulation done, and how is it done today?

Traditional manipulation for some people also meant writing down the dynamics equations, the interactions between the robot and the object, I mean, for example, the linear and nonlinear dynamics. For example, before and after contact, the dynamics are completely different. Then how do you handle that? You turn it into a mixed-integer program, that kind of problem. Right? Now, though, people may skip that modeling entirely. We only care whether the robot outputs the right action, based on the training data, or, with reinforcement learning, whether the object reaches the goal, right? All that intermediate modeling may just disappear.

In robot learning over the last few years, what has been most overrated, or most underrated?

That is a good question. The most overrated thing, I think, is how much importance people assign to that whole thing, models and algorithms, that whole side of things. The most underrated thing, is probably still the systems side, the whole hardware-software integration, and how important that is.

数据的种类

We were just talking about how back in 2020 you were doing teleoperation and behavior cloning. But today, in 2026, one of your most important bets, something you really believe in, is human data for robot learning. Can you tell us what counts as robot data and what counts as human data?

When most people say robot data, they usually mean teleoperation, teleop data. Human data, comes in many forms. The most common now is first-person data. Imagine wearing a camera on your head, and watching your hands do a task. That kind of data. Of course, there is also third-person data, where a side camera watches you, or multiview third-person data.

This kind of human data that you are talking about, I assume you mean more than just using human data to supplement robot data a bit. I think what you really mean is it could fundamentally change how we train robots. Right. Today, everyone does robot learning in a data-driven way. What kinds of data matter most, and why is your biggest bet human data?

The main kind of data is probably teleoperation data, collected across many different robot platforms. That is probably the dominant data today. The second most common data is I think the second category is synthetic data from different physics engines. And I think the third category is non-robot data. Broadly speaking, people might think data collected from YouTube, for example, could be useful.

And if you really want to break down real-world non-robot data, there are probably two types. One type is data downloaded directly from the internet, with no constraints on it, like YouTube data. And the other type is where we actually have people collect first-person hand-manipulation data.

九、EgoMimic 项目：从零开始

One project I especially want to discuss is EgoMimic. Tell us what kind of data EgoMimic uses, what its backstory is, and why you wanted to do the project.

Right. EgoMimic was something I started after I joined the faculty, maybe as my first or second project. At the time, I was working with a PhD student from another group, Simar. Simar had a very strong idea: he wanted to do first-person data. I asked him why first-person data, and he thought it might be the most scalable. So we started discussing it, and I remember we spent about a week on that question. In the end, we agreed, "Let's just do egocentric." I felt it could work. The reason I hesitated a little was that I knew building an egocentric data-collection setup was very hard at the time. There just wasn't a very mature collection solution. So I was worried this would have to be built from scratch and basically turn the project into a very long one. Because we'd be building from the hardware up.

But later, he really wanted to do it, so I said, let's start. And then we started building it, this whole first-person human-manipulation data collection system.

At the beginning, we used Oculus, the VR headset, right, and mounted a Leap Motion on it at first. It was exactly the same as what I built in 2015. Yeah. Except Leap Motion had become Orion, its latest generation, or actually, I can't remember what the latest version was. Anyway, it was the newest one. And Oculus had become Quest, right?

So we built that system. Then we found it worked very poorly because the calibration was very unstable. Basically, well, you can imagine, there was one camera that captured hand data, and the video data came from another camera. And the VR headset was used to track where your head was. So I had to bring everything into the same coordinate system, calibrate it all into one frame. So the misalignment there was huge. We worked on it for a few weeks, and then decided it was just too hard.

Then it happened that one of our colleagues was working on Ego4D, the one from Meta, Meta's Ego4D. And they had something called Project Aria, their glasses. It was a pair of glasses Meta built themselves. And it turned out those glasses matched all our needs perfectly. They could estimate hand pose, meaning I could recover the positions of all ten fingers. The glasses could also localize themselves, so I knew where my head was, and at the same time they could capture first-person RGB video. So we said, "Why don't we use this?"

Then we got access to it, and that was when the whole project on the data-collection side, started to go much more smoothly. But as one of the earliest efforts, actually the first project to use it for real robot learning, We hit a huge number of pitfalls. Throughout that whole process, by the end, Meta had basically become our full-time support team. They even had someone on the team who opened a P0 ticket for us. Basically all our support requests were treated as P0s. Just the data collection piece took about four or five months before we got it working with Meta's Aria glasses.

自己造机器人

Another thing I wanted to do was make the robot more like a human. What that meant was, if we really wanted to learn from human data, learn manipulation skills, learn those manipulation priors, and transfer them better to robots, then the robot has to become more human-like. It needs two hands, and it needs a more human-like shoulder and torso joint setup. And we needed a camera placed roughly where the head would be. At the time, there wasn't a single robot that met our requirements.

So we built our own. And I built that robot myself. I went to Vention. They have this kind of systems-integration model. They sell all kinds of aluminum parts and let you build full robot systems. That's basically what they do. So I bought all kinds of parts and built a dual-arm rig with shoulders and two support posts, into a working setup.

That must have been a lot of fun for you.

Super fun. I remember during that period, if I wasn't teaching, I was in the lab building that robot.

So back then, as an assistant professor, you were out there tightening screws yourself.

Yeah exactly. Everything was designed by me. And at the time, the first author was handling the data collection side. He was very focused on data collection, and I handled the robot hardware.

从遥操作到人类数据的转变

Back then, did this project start in mid-2022 or mid-2023?

It was probably mid-2023.

Mid-2023. At the time, everyone in the field thought teleop worked really well, it really did. ALOHA had just come out. And people thought the coolest demos still had to use teleop. Human data always seemed like a subset of what teleop could actually do. Why did you then see the value of human data and move from teleop data in 2020 to human data in 2023?

That was probably a gradual shift. But later in the project, I had a very strong realization: If you look at teleop data, teleop data itself isn't perfect either. Every layer of the system creates its own gap. Here's a simple example. Suppose I have a robot, and I collect teleop data on it, then I slightly change its low-level controller, say I tweak the gains a bit, and then the whole system when you send it a control command, responds completely differently. So even within one robot embodiment, there can be a big gap.

But between humans and robots, or between one robot and another, is the gap really that big? Because if we can really take a human's motion and turn it into usable actions, and turn perception into usable policy inputs, then I don't think it's impossible. In other words, directly using human data as robot data isn't impossible. By the middle and later stages of the project, I gradually came to realize that a human is just another robot.

Yeah exactly. Or you could say a robot is just another human.

A robot is just another human. If you put enough sensors on a person, you can basically turn them into a robot. Operationally, from a control perspective.

十、人类数据的多模态

视频：能学到什么

I see. I want to dig into that a bit. For example, EgoMimic uses Aria glasses. Human data actually has many modalities. There are a lot of things I want to ask you about. We can start with the first one, which I think is important: video. namely EgoMimic. That basically only uses video data, right? Including the hand pose estimation, all of that comes from video. Right. I want to know: from human ego video, what can robots actually learn? When we have a huge amount of first-person human video data, are we learning a better encoder, specifically a visual encoder, or are we really learning human planning priors, or can we actually learn human visuomotor manipulation skills? What do you think we're really learning from ego video?

I can start from a broader angle to talk about this, and then explain what EgoMimic actually does. Broadly speaking, I think there are three parts. If you think about a robot or a human interacting with the human world, you can break that into three subproblems, three interaction components.

One is what change you want in the world, how the world should change. Second, how an embodiment can cause those changes, how it physically makes them happen. For example, if I want to pick up this cup, or push it forward by five centimeters. And third, what kind of control signals make the embodiment produce those motions. For example, how much force do my motors need?

What's the difference between the second and third levels?

The second is the interaction between the embodiment and the world. The third is how to generate the embodiment's actions.

Human data is actually very useful for the first one: how the world should change. That part is already there. As long as you remove who caused those changes, the change itself is no different for a human or a robot. As long as your robot is capable of doing it.

The second layer can also be learned, namely, say I have a robot with a five-finger dexterous hand, and compared with a human, its action space isn't that different, and it can apply similar forces. Then I can probably learn the second kind too, which is how an embodiment can affect the world, roughly where I should push, or where I should grasp the cup to lift it. Right?

But the third one, from human video, is very, very hard to learn: how to make an embodiment produce those actions. For example, an extreme case is throwing a ball. How exactly does a robot throw a ball? That's an extremely hard problem. Because you need to figure out how to make every joint produce enough force to send the ball along that trajectory. You can't learn that directly from video data.

Another difficulty for us is that human actuation is driven by muscle electrical signals. We also can't just build muscles. Right. You can't directly map EMG signals to the motor at each joint one by one, and it wouldn't be meaningful anyway.

第一人称 vs 第三人称

But one thing I've really wanted to ask is this: You just said that you and Simar spent a week coming to a conclusion: that ego video is the most scalable. Right. If I were to push back, I'd say third-person is the most scalable. Because YouTube has a huge amount of third-person videos of people doing things. And humans can learn from third-person views too. For example, I often replace a car cabin air filter just by watching a third-person video, and I learn it right away. Right. I have that ability, so why are you emphasizing first-person so much?

I think it comes down to two things. One is the tradeoff between fidelity and scalability. For a given dataset, how scalable it is versus how much useful signal it contains. In practice, these two things are largely impossible to optimize at the same time.

For example, third-person data is highly scalable. You can download it straight from YouTube, but as a data source, it's very hard to process. Because our current understanding is that you want the data distributions to be as similar as possible, between human and robot data. Out of all the data on YouTube, you have to extract the subset that actually aligns with robot data. That is extremely hard, and then actually normalizing it so the distributions match is even harder. So the amount you can actually use is very small from YouTube data.

By contrast, if we actually asked people to collect first-person data, and had good equipment, with lots of people willing to do it, it actually wouldn't be that hard.

What I'm curious about is: because I'm thinking, NLP used to have many subtasks. People working on translation might have felt that summarization data was useless to them. Do you think that was because they didn't have the right paradigm? They just hadn't found something like GPT's next-token prediction paradigm yet, and today, even if YouTube data seems limited, for robot learning, maybe we're also missing a bigger, better paradigm?

I think that's very possible. It's just that to a large extent, someone has to show that it can work. Going from 1 to 99 probably carries less uncertainty than going from 0 to 1. So people tend to choose the less uncertain path. So for that 0-to-1 step, if someone really can make it happen, then going from 1 to 99 will move very quickly. But if you want to get from 0 to 1, you have to get a lot of things right. For example, learning entirely from YouTube data, how do you do that? I think I don't have a good method for that. But at least I know first-person data is feasible, and we already have signs that it can work.

So would you say EgoMimic is, in some sense, a 0-to-1 project?

Exactly.

视频学习的天花板

I want to dig a bit deeper into video. You just said there are three layers. One is: how the world needs to change. The second is: how the robot should interact with it to produce that change. The third is how to control the robot to achieve that interaction. So can you imagine a day when, for example, if we had first-person video of Jordan playing basketball or Federer playing tennis, we might get to a point where a robot could then watch first-person video of Federer playing tennis and learn Federer's tennis skills? Where exactly is the boundary here, and what is the ceiling?

That's a very good question. I think the boundary probably depends on how good that third layer is, namely the robot's control, whether it can match the states humans can demonstrate, how good it is at hitting that control target. For example, say a human does something like throwing this ball 30 meters, and I know what the end-effector velocity is, then my robot can accurately track that end-effector velocity or end-effector acceleration, then I think it's doable.

But if you can get that working, of course, it's actually a hard problem. But if you can solve it, I think learning basketball from first-person video isn't too far away. That kind of learning isn't that far off.

Of course, there are still many other issues. First-person data is a partially observed problem, because, for example, there are also signals from the hands, things like tactile and haptic feedback, that we still aren't capturing. But suppose we could capture all that first-person data. all the data from the human body itself, everything we can possibly capture, then I think something like basketball wouldn't be impossible.

SLAM 与 VIO

Since we're on that topic, I want to ask about the next modality. One thing I didn't mention about EgoMimic is that besides video, you also have IMU data for SLAM and VIO, odometry based on video and IMUs. Why are SLAM and VIO so important for human data? Where do they get used?

That's a really, really good question. Because as of 2026, there are two ways to think about human data, and they're fundamentally different. One is the world-model approach: you only need to know what the world looks like in the next frame. The second treats humans as another kind of robot. It uses human data as another kind of BC data. Those two uses of human data are completely different.

So for the first one, you only need first-person visual data. Video alone is enough. The second says you need to capture human actions, plus things like tactile signals, all the inputs and outputs. You treat the human as a system with inputs and outputs, and capture as much as possible. Then when it comes to capturing the actions, you run into a question: what exactly counts as the action? Like you mentioned earlier, kinematics. What is kinematics? It's where your hand, or your end effector, is in the world. Right? So how do you measure that? You can only get it through hand pose estimation, using the first-person view to see where your hand is. So knowing where your hand appears in the camera still isn't enough, because you don't know where the camera is. That's where the IMU and VIO, visual-inertial odometry, come in. You need to localize yourself first, or more precisely, the glasses themselves. So it becomes very important.

So SLAM is crucial for that human-as-robot route.

Right. Because from human data, we want to extract as many action labels as possible.

I do have a question. I keep comparing it to how I learn. If I'm watching someone else's video to learn how to assemble furniture, or fix a car, I don't need their inertial data. And I don't need precise IMU measurements either. So where exactly is the gap? Is it because current video models aren't strong enough, so we have to add action labels? Could SLAM just be a temporary fix, that over the long run won't matter?

Possibly. But under the current paradigm, the one we're pushing now, the idea is basically to take human data and use it as robot teleoperation data. Right? If you extrapolate that paradigm, then you actually need more precise state estimation, not less. Right? But if you mean the other human-data paradigm, where human data just tells you how to do the task, and you already know how to reach each state, say it shows you how to cook a meal, and you already know how to do every step, then that's a different paradigm. That one may not need such precise state estimation.

So depending on your research, and your own theory, the data you need may be different.

But right now, the path you believe in most is still to extract action labels from humans and do behavior cloning?

Yes because I really believe what we are most lacking right now is solving low-level dexterous manipulation, as well as full-body loco-manipulation. We still haven't solved that yet. So I think human data is the only data source that can scale enough to solve this problem.

SLAM 的护城河

I want to talk a bit more about SLAM. Because SLAM is a pretty traditional robotics stack. Most people doing robot learning today probably have never learned how to do SLAM. But it turns out SLAM has become extremely important. One of your tweets mentioned this too. Right. Tell us where SLAM is today. What's the state of the art? Right. Why is it so hard? Where is the moat, really?

As for SLAM, the state of the art is in these VR companies, like Meta and Apple, and a few self-driving companies. But the SLAM we care about, like sub-centimeter SLAM, really only exists at those AR/VR companies. That part is beyond dispute.

Right, they've been at it for ten years.

And if you look at the benchmark Project Aria released for city-scale SLAM, SLAM across an entire city, the open-source and academic SLAM pipelines are really far behind them. It's basically like comparing a VLM to SIFT. The gap is really that big.

Right. I ran a test once. I had just bought an Apple Vision Pro, and I wore it walking down the street. Because I was visualizing its VIO in real time, I walked two blocks and came back, and it was still locked in place.

Right. Let me give you an example of what Project Aria can do. One of the most important things in SLAM is that when you have six cameras, the relationship among them has to be extremely stable. But as a pair of glasses, it can't stay perfectly stable, because it flexes, right? And then, for example, it even depends on the temperature that day, so the camera geometry changes too. What they can do is online calibration, taking temperature into account. They basically have a model of the relationship between camera geometry and thermal effects, and calibrate from that. [laughs]

Does that ever feel a little frustrating to you because you really want to own the full stack?

If we want to do human data well, we definitely need this piece. But unfortunately, this stuff sits inside VR companies. I think there are a lot of people interested in this, and in terms of capital investment, I think it's doable. Right, it's just that this is an engineering problem. It's purely an engineering problem. It's not some problem where we need to invent the next GPT to solve it. This is purely an engineering problem. If you have money and resources, and spend time sweating the details, you can absolutely build it.

And also, one very good thing right now is that learning-based approaches degrade gracefully. If your data is better, your transfer is better. If your data is a bit worse, your transfer is a bit worse. It's not all or nothing.

触觉感知

Okay, I want to talk about a third modality: tactile sensing in human data. This was also your first undergraduate research topic. The reason I want to talk about touch is that it seems like in all these human-data projects today, touch is the missing piece. And for human manipulation, touch is also incredibly important. I don't even know whether it's more important than vision. I can spin this pen right now with my eyes closed. With my eyes closed, I can also take out my wallet. Right. So how much tactile data do we really need? What place do you think touch will have within human data?

I think it will be the second most important modality. I still think video data is the most important. And then, there's another modality that I actually haven't studied very systematically, which is wrist cameras. How does that really compare with touch? For example, imagine a camera mounted on your wrist, and the camera can see the interaction between your hand and the object. It solves a whole class of problems with egocentric video. In egocentric video, when you're holding something, it's often mostly occluded by your own hand. But if you have a wrist camera, you can see what your hand is doing. Once you have that camera, how much do you still need tactile data? I've never been very sure about that.

Tactile sensing is definitely very important, because I have a theory: that every agent, every robot, every human, is fundamentally a force-exertion engine. In other words, your purpose in this world is to exert force on the world. You exert that force, and it changes the state of the world. Right? So the key thing isn't measuring tactile signals, it's measuring force. How much force are you actually applying to it? Tactile is only the most direct but imperfect measurement of force.

What I'm really curious about is how far tactile sensors have come by now, and how ready they are.

I think the biggest problem is that they're too fragmented. Everyone builds tactile sensors differently. There are too many kinds: things like GelSight, optical tactile sensors, pressure-based ones, resistive ones, magnetic ones, right? There are just too many tactile sensors, and they all have different properties. Their tradeoffs are all different. But if we really want a standardized representation like cameras have, I think we're still a ways off.

So tactile sensors don't have a unified representation like RGB.

Right, and each one, because of its physical properties, has completely different characteristics. Right. You can think of them as different sensors.

So does that mean if tactile really matters, but tactile sensors still haven't converged, we may end up just using RGB wrist cameras?

Possibly. Very possibly. It's like riding a beat-up car. That car is still going to take us somewhere.

模态淘汰赛

I put together what I think are all the possible human-data modalities. Okay. I want you to start with the least important and eliminate them one by one. Okay. Okay. In the list we have let's see what's in it. ego video, first-person video, hand pose and hand data, whole-body pose, full-body motion, force and tactile, audio, language, and smell. Let me start. If I had to give one up,

Right.

first I'd drop smell.

Right. I think that's one area where we're just not there yet. [laughs]

Right. If I had to give up a second one,

hmm. I think it would probably be under the current paradigm, audio.

Audio.

Because with audio, our capture hardware is still too crude. Because the most important thing is probably localization: where the audio is coming from. On that front, I think our current microphones still aren't there yet. I could build it, but nobody is paying attention to it. Right. As for the third one, I mean, it depends on what you believe. I think language and whole-body pose could be next. Actually, force and tactile, we still don't know how to use them.

With force and tactile, what I'm more curious about is, if we're going to learn from them, should they be inputs or outputs?

That's a great question. Basically, you're a force-exerting machine. So in theory, you need to measure the output, right? But the forces you're feeling also matter. You need to use that force feedback to estimate how much force to apply. So you need both input and output.

OK next.

And then I guess we haven't solved manipulation yet, so excluding whole-body pose, that leaves language, hand pose, and video.

So your top choice is video, second is hand pose, third is language annotation, fourth are whole-body pose and tactile sensing, and least important are audio and smell.

十一、UMI 与人类数据的边界模糊

So that's the human data we were talking about. If we're talking about collecting it, but actually, I think the boundary between robot data and human data is getting blurrier and blurrier. A great example is UMI data. Can you tell us what UMI data is, and whether UMI data really sits more on the robot-data side or the human-data side?

Right, UMI data, Universal Manipulation Interface, basically works like this: just imagine you replace your hand with a robot arm's end effector, basically a gripper. That gripper itself has its own state estimation: where it is, and how open or closed it is. You use your human hand to control the gripper. So you basically act like your hand is that gripper, something like that.

It basically reduces the human hand to a gripper.

Right exactly. But the benefit is that it has very good state estimation, because you can mount a camera on it, or a tracking module, so it knows where it is in 3D space. The second point is these grippers are mounted directly on robots, so there's no embodiment gap. That means between data collection and deployment, there's almost no gap in the end effector or the collision model. There's no gap there. But there is a big gap in the rest of the body, right? Because your action space is different from the robot's. Your hand has more than seven degrees of freedom, while the robot may be constrained in its degrees of freedom. But with pure human video, you have the same problem.

Right. So to answer your question, is it robot data or human data? I think it's human data, because fundamentally the human is directly interacting with the environment. You can still feel through the gripper, however indirectly, the forces. You can also feel that you have very strong control over it. There's no robot controller in the middle making it more indirect.

So if we look at the first three: teleop, UMI, and human data, I mean pure human data, without a gripper, all of them today have an important role in this field. How do you think this will change over time? Because right now UMI seems like a sweet spot. It works best: it gives you fine manipulation, and it scales better than teleop, with much better fidelity than pure video. How do you think about that? Because you just mentioned fidelity and scalability. I actually believe final performance is the product of the two. Right. So among those three, especially UMI, how do you see it?

I think UMI and human data will eventually become indistinguishable. I think we're already seeing that trend. Take Generalist, for example. Their gripper already looks a lot like a human hand. They basically put two fingertip caps on a human hand and mounted cameras on them. If you take off those fingertip caps and keep only the cameras, that becomes what we now call sensorized human data. It just adds those fingertip caps, so compared with a robot gripper, the operation gap is much smaller. But the tradeoff is lower fidelity, right? Because you can't do dexterous manipulation, true five-finger dexterous manipulation.

So in the end, I think for most people, everything above the data layer will be shared. Everything else is basically the same as with human data. It's just that at the data level, your transfer, that is, from human to robot, how much of it you get is still unclear. Right. In other words, the more robot-like the human data is, like gripper data, the more it transfers. If you directly use a human hand, then it depends on how human-like your robot hand and its capabilities are, and that determines how much transfers.

Do you think, in the long run, as you said, it'll get harder and harder to tell these kinds of data apart? Do you see any big trends? Because right now the most effective UMI still uses a gripper, right? So you do think five fingers are necessary.

I think it's basically this: namely, truly effective human data has to be built from the bottom up. On the robot side, your actuators and controller need to execute at roughly human speed. For example, when I close my hand, that speed and the robot hand's closing speed need to be about the same. Most people haven't gotten that right. From what I can tell, the only one that has is Generalist. Their controller is extremely good.

You mean the gripper setup?

I mean the integration of the whole arm.

My conviction is that if we really want transfer from a human dexterous hand to a five-finger dexterous robot hand, that transfer actually doesn't have that big a gap. I just need a hand slightly smaller than the Shadow Hand, since the Shadow Hand has 22 degrees of freedom, just a bit smaller than that, and able to execute a bit faster. And then we may need to switch to a faster robot arm, meaning a higher-performance arm. I won't name names here. Then wire it all together and get the whole low-level actuation right. Then we can reach UMI-level transfer.

So you think the transfer from five-finger human data right now is bottlenecked by hardware?

I think it's mostly bottlenecked by robot hardware, not the data-collection hardware. It's the robot arm. Right. It's not that the arm itself is bad, it's just that most people haven't integrated it all.

十二、人形机器人与硬件彩票

I have a question: if human data is going to be hugely successful, does that mean humanoid robots with two legs and five fingers will also be hugely successful?

Yes.

So the two rise together and fall together.

I don't know about the two legs, but at least the upper body. So at minimum, an upper body with human-like arms and five fingers, and a lower body that's at least holonomic, so it can move omnidirectionally.

There's a concept about technology as a whole called the hardware lottery. The idea is that deep learning only took off because of GPUs. That's what made it take off. So without GPUs, we might never have known that deep learning could work this well. What I'm really curious about is: do you think robotics is a hardware lottery or a data lottery? In other words, does human data enable humanoids, or do humanoids enable human data?

Good question. I think it's both. Humanoids themselves, that human-like embodiment, don't really have intrinsic meaning, except that they can use tools and look less scary. But once you have human data, humanoids have a real purpose. That's the future, right? Conversely, if you don't have humanoids, human-to-robot transfer gets harder. So the two really reinforce each other.

So human data basically helps robots become more human, through human-centered data. I'm curious, whether, to some extent, this also locks robots into human-level performance. If what we want to build today is a robot whose waist can rotate freely, with hands that can also rotate freely, a super-humanoid, a superhuman robot, then because of human data, maybe we can't build that kind of robot. Is that a real risk?

That's a very good question. Two aspects. First, if I take a step back, I think manipulation, fine manipulation, or teaching robots how to manipulate objects, is really just one part of human data. The other part I haven't mentioned is interaction between robots and humans. Now, if we treat that as a learning problem, as a data-driven problem, right now there's no way to use teleop to collect that data. You can only collect it through human-human interaction. So the other piece is HRI, human-robot interaction. That's actually a huge gap in human data.

Coming back to your question, whether this really locks us into human data and a humanoid embodiment, I don't think so. Because in fact, in robotics, people talk about null space: if I have enough degrees of freedom, I can dumb myself down to imitate a lower-degree-of-freedom system. If I have a 20-DOF hand, for example, I can imitate a simple gripper. So I don't necessarily need a robot with only human-level mechanical degrees of freedom to imitate a human. We could instead have a robot with more degrees of freedom and use it to emulate a human with fewer degrees of freedom, right?

Mm-hmm.

But in that case, it may be hard to unlock its full potential. Right, so my feeling is once we get past that GPT-2/GPT-3 moment, RL fine-tuning after that can really be on the table, right?

Today we're still centered on human data. A lot of labs are saying the same thing. Yann LeCun has been advocating it. He says we can't even build a squirrel today. We don't even have squirrel-level intelligence. So by your logic, if we wanted crow, squirrel, or octopus intelligence, would we also need to put sensors on them to get there? We went straight for human-level intelligence. LLMs are human-level. We didn't start with communication between squirrels, right? That's because we had human data, so we could build LLMs. So I'd make the same argument: because we have first-person human data, or human manipulation data, that's what lets us build a human-level robot. I guess my question is, if we want animal-level sensorimotor intelligence, do we also have to do what EgoMimic does today and put sensors on them?

But I think if we can reach human physical intelligence, then dumbing it down is actually easier.

十三、人类数据的终极愿景

I want to ask you about your real vision for human data. Because just now we spent most of the time on manipulation, on fine manipulation. If data were unlimited, compute were unlimited, and robot hardware were good enough, what do you think we could ultimately learn from human data about the level of intelligence we can achieve? What's the upper bound?

I think the upper bound is a human-like robot that's indistinguishable from humans. You can't tell whether it's a person or a robot. It's a human-level Turing test, where it can talk like a human, behave like a human, and interact like a human. Not just in how it engages with the physical world, or how it interacts with objects, but how it interacts with other people, where all of those physical aspects they all become indistinguishable. That's the upper bound of what you get from human data.

So basically, you're trying to model all human sensory data.

I want to clone human behavior.

So what you're trying to do is human-level behavior cloning.

Exactly.

So can I say that today's LLMs are also just behavior-cloning human language?

Right. Well, not just that, right? Because we definitely did a lot of supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning, and only then could they become superhuman coders. Right? Robotics hasn't even reached step one yet.

数据基础设施的挑战

So I think to realize your vision of cloning human behavior, if we look at language models and where they are today, the reason they got there as you said in your CoRL talk, is that they had the Internet. They had keyboards and mice, touchscreens, people typing, which became data on the Internet. But the Internet, phones and computers, weren't built specifically to train LLMs. But today's human data is being created to train robot models. How do we get the kind of infrastructure that language models have? I think that's the decisive question for how far this research agenda can go.

Because right now there are already a lot of companies. I've counted maybe over a hundred by now starting to collect this data. Why? Because people think this is doable and promising, so the data started to appear. If we can keep moving forward, if our trajectory keeps trending upward, and the slope stays good, then more people will contribute data. On the flip side, if we stagnate for a long time and stop making progress, then fewer people will contribute data.

I'm hopeful. I think this is doable. That we can get it done before people lose interest.

So what you're hoping for is that this thing you're doing, human data for robot learning, works better and better, so more people join in, and more people are willing to pay for the data, so more data companies collect more of it. I think the comparison is with language models and video models. They have the Internet and YouTube. For them, that kind of data came as a byproduct, but you have to create it deliberately.

Right. I originally hoped it would come as a byproduct too. When I gave that talk in 2024, what I was saying was: later on, things like Meta glasses, like Ray-Ban glasses, those kinds of AI wearables, will have cameras, and generate a lot of this data. At the time I thought, OK, over the next five years, five years from now, there could be a huge amount of data. But I didn't expect that because human data took off, so many people would contribute it, so the whole timeline moved up.

Put it this way, you thought you'd be a supporting character, but in robot learning, human data became the main character.

Right. You were planning to ride the wave of wearables.

Right. You were originally waiting for that, but then the whole community got moving.

Right exactly.

数据规模的估算

So on that point, you just said you want to make it happen, and clone human behavior. What scale of data and funding do you think it takes? Because I think it definitely won't need less data than language models. If anything, it'll need more.

Only more. Only more. Well... Mm. My estimate is probably around 100 million hours of data.

A hundred million hours.

Right, a hundred million.

A hundred million.

And today, I think the largest dataset I've heard of is around 100,000 to 200,000 hours. If a very rich company bought up everyone's data, it could maybe scrape together a couple million hours. Maybe around 1 to 2 million hours of high-quality data. So we're still 100x away from that.

Still 100x away.

A hundred times doesn't even sound that crazy.

Not that crazy.

Right, it can be done.

Yeah exactly. After that, it's probably just a data-transfer bandwidth problem and a storage problem. Then there are management issues, like filtering and exploration, and infrastructure. But the issue is: whether it's language models or video models, they have standardized representations and formats like MP4. For human data, we still don't have that, right? We haven't even converged on the modalities or sensors. So if we just push for 100 million hours of data, that could ultimately be wasteful.

Right. Exactly. This whole thing feels to me right now like a train moving at full speed, and the people with money are laying the tracks.

Laying track, right.

That's basically the situation. Basically, a few labs are out front frantically laying track, and then all the capital behind them starts feeding the train more fuel.

偶然产生的人类数据

I've also noticed you often emphasize human data. You keep saying we want incidental human data, not data collected specifically for robot tasks. Why?

Because I think human physical intelligence, to a large extent, is something we don't think about. When we do things, like opening a door with my foot, or closing a drawer with my elbow, or picking something up without bumping something else. You can't really script that. In ordinary life, you do these things. But if you ask someone to go to a data collection center and do a bunch of assigned tasks, then when they do them, they'll probably end up with fewer interactions rich in physical intelligence. Because you need to optimize for task completion, right?

So I think in the end, the paradigm is probably something in between. We may need some intentionally collected data to show that something is possible. But most of the data collected will probably still just come from daily life. And eventually it becomes more like Tesla, where the issue is data filtering: which data matters, then pull that data out and train the model again.

But the tricky part is, today's human data providers are basically working for you. Right. If you ask them to collect incidental data, they don't even know how to collect it.

Right, and that's why the research matters. If we can show this year that incidental data is useful, a lot of people will start collecting that kind of incidental data. Right?

I was thinking, I have a lot of incidental data that might mean nothing for robot learning. For example, my neck isn't great, so I like to do this. Right. What do you do with that?

I'm stretching.

That just becomes a filtering problem, right? There are definitely filtering issues. For example, there are also lots of modeling questions. If you really think it through, how do you do behavior cloning on a human? That's extremely hard. Take me, for example. I'm cooking, and I suddenly glance at the clock. Why did I look at the clock? Right? Should that be cloned too? Because I don't know the time, but the robot does. Should we clone that action? Right? All of this is why behavior cloning an agent is extremely hard.

I think there's another difference from autonomous driving. In autonomous driving, you're not really putting sensors on a person. You're putting sensors on the car. It's a constrained task. You just need to get from point A to point B. That's what most people do anyway.

Right. Most people's driving actions are valid. People rarely collect inattentive driving footage. That would be dangerous.

Exactly.

We just talked about whole-body pose, tactile sensing, and all that. My feeling is that cameras may subsume many other sensor modalities.

Mm-hmm.

For example, with hand tracking, if nothing is occluded, video can track hands pretty well. Same with whole-body pose estimation. If your camera has enough focal length, you can estimate that too. Will it eventually become a very video-heavy model?

I think so. I think that's very likely. That trend is already underway. It depends how you frame the problem. Right? These are all partial measurements. Whatever gives you a measurement, you use it. Right? It's fidelity versus scalability. Cameras already give us excellent fidelity. And it's a mature industry with low costs and solid supply chains.

Right. Like that startup you worked at in college, the tactile sensing startup. You said it had temperature sensing and all kinds of other sensors. I can't imagine how much one setup costs.

$10,000 each. [laughs]

Looking ahead, I mean, we're on a train moving at full speed, with everyone pushing forward while laying the tracks as we go. So what kind of human data do you think, in the long run, will matter most?

I actually don't know the answer. But I think these data collection companies need to work very closely with research institutions. For example, at our Georgia Tech lab, we need data to do research. And they also need to know what kind of data is useful. So it's a mutually beneficial relationship. What does the data distribution look like? What data is actually useful? How do you help one person collect better data more efficiently? I think that's something everyone needs to work on together.

数据是壁垒还是商品？

You're in academia and in industry, working with ego human data. I'm sure you can see a lot of industry trends. What I'm curious about is, do you think human data, including the capture hardware and the whole pipeline, will become a moat for frontier labs, or as competition heats up and it becomes more widespread, does it become a commodity?

I think it becomes a commodity. Because even frontier labs don't know what data they want. So basically, frontier labs end up teaching these data vendors what data to collect. But they can't tell them to sell only to them. Those vendors will sell to others too. So it becomes essentially data vendors serving as a middle layer, a platform for exchange. With this kind of interaction between frontier labs and data vendors, communication becomes unavoidable. So what data you collected, what equipment you used, it all gets shared in the end.

Right. Right, so could there be mm, a company ambitious enough to do everything in-house and build it all itself? Would you prefer that path, or should a frontier lab do the research and buy from data vendors?

I'd want something like Tesla: own everything. But as an academic, I think research should be driven by everyone. So I think it may still end up as a frontier lab driving a few data vendors.

EgoVerse 与开放科学

You recently had a project called EgoVerse. That was also about turning human data into an open collective effort for academia? Yeah. Why do you still believe in that? Because it sounds like if you need 100 million hours of data, and each hour costs tens of dollars, that's astronomical for academia. Right.

I think it comes back to that train-and-tracks metaphor. Basically, our enthusiasm for data has far outpaced the foundational research.

Mm.

The train may already be moving, and not even on the tracks anymore. You know? It's already gone. So I think we need more people doing more fundamental research: what kinds of data are useful, how to use that data better, and how to use it for different things. For example, human-robot interaction, and other more interesting research. I think data as a platform is a very good way to enable collaboration.

Second, on the data vendor side, I want some centralization. Meaning I need, through my own or my collaborators' credibility, to earn their trust. If they give me data, and do some level of quality checks, our research can make their data more credible. Then it becomes a mutually beneficial relationship.

But do you ever think that if you really pull this off, if it actually works, the endgame will inevitably become what language models are today: highly commercialized and very closed.

Yeah, I think so. If you really need 100 million hours of data, there's no way an open-science model can work. Like those earlier open-science language models, they weren't especially successful either. It's basically the same story. You need highly centralized operations, something driven by capital, to make it happen. So even if the outcome isn't as good as I hope, I still think we need to try.

In a way, your success becomes your failure.

Right. Yeah exactly. If I succeed with "scale is all you need," open science loses.

Right. Ten years ago, everyone was working on self-driving. At the beginning, people wanted to explore all kinds of data sources. Some wanted to learn from YouTube driving videos. But after ten years, self-driving converged on data tightly tied to automakers and tightly tied to hardware. Do you think robotics will take a different path? Aren't you worried robot data will also end up tightly tied to robot manufacturers?

The reason it ends up that way is that they don't share the data. Right? They just don't want to make it public. That kind of company data would help other people too. Say your car has six cameras. If another car has a roughly similar camera layout, the data can actually transfer. But because of capital interests, it doesn't get transferred. Right? Human data works the same way. If someone really buys 100 million hours of data, and doesn't give it to anyone else, you'll get the same problem. So ultimately, it's not tied to hardware, it's tied to business, to business incentives.

如果失败：哪个假设是错的？

If I gave you a prediction that human data ultimately doesn't become the foundation of robot learning's GPT moment, which assumption do you think was wrong?

I think maybe for example, my understanding of simulation-based learning is fundamentally wrong, that its scalability is nothing like I expected. That's one example. But or maybe the gap between humans and humanoids, for example, at the second or third layer, is too large, and there's just no way to get zero-shot or few-shot transfer. Another possibility is that non-humanoid robots really proliferate and take off, making human-data transfer harder and harder.

Mm right. Maybe we end up building robots with three-finger claws, tall bodies, and unlimited rotation, right.

Right. Maybe but I still think this robotics boom is driven by data. Wherever data is needed, I think robot companies will build robot embodiments that are tightly coupled to the data.

十四、全栈与系统观

Lastly, I want to ask about some other topics. One is full-stack robotics. You emphasized before that today people overemphasize algorithms and underestimate systems a bit. Right. Why are systems so important in robotics?

Because systems are part of everything. I think it's like this: Take computer science. CS itself has a systems discipline, right? PL compilers databases distributed systems. Robotics, or robot learning, is still at a stage where people haven't studied the overall system deeply enough, or rather, we still don't have very convenient systems yet. In that situation, that means people don't understand it all that deeply. So that leads to some projects, or some directions, advancing more slowly. At least I think that's probably because the systems side still isn't mature enough. So I think people need to take seriously the question of why full-stack matters so much.

Does full-stack mean building everything yourself?

It doesn't mean building everything yourself. It means you need to know what actually affects your final outcome, so you need a deep understanding of every detail in the whole stack.

Right. A lot of teams, robotics teams today, whether startups or big companies, now that the robotics ecosystem is maturing, we can buy great hardware, and there are plenty of data vendors too. Right. For a team that's truly capable of solving robotics and achieving physical AGI, how should it make the buy-or-build decision? What should be bought, and what should be built in-house?

I think you can buy everything, but your integration has to be deep enough. Once you understand it well enough, you'll know for yourself whether to buy or build.

So looking at it today, what absolutely can't be outsourced, what has to stay in-house?

For example, the whole evaluation and training loop, that's something a team definitely has to build itself. Post-training data collection, and then pre-training data filtering, and you need to check for yourself how well the data was collected, and whether it matches your expected data distribution, and the closed loop as well. For example, what we do at school is the students collect the data themselves, train the model themselves, and deploy it themselves. Every researcher needs a solid grasp of what each kind of data does, and how each slice changes the system's behavior. That requires very deep understanding. If your data comes from a vendor, it's definitely more of a black box to you. Right? How did they collect it? How good is their synchronization? Right. Would that be a challenge if you want to get to 100 million hours?

Yeah. That's a huge, huge challenge. I think a lot of people are struggling with this.

十五、模型与建模

Lastly, I want to talk about modeling. This is really what the whole community has been talking about most today. World models or VLAs. But I want to discuss it from the angle of human data, because I think every kind of data brings its own inductive bias to modeling. If human data becomes hugely successful, what kind of modeling method would it favor?

I think long context is extremely important. Again, like I said, if you're trying to explain human behavior cloning, what you're really doing is using context to explain actions. That's the core problem. Right. So if you don't have enough context, you can't explain the action. You just treat it as a very broad distribution. You could do A, or you could do B, because you don't have enough context to understand why they did it.

The human model you just mentioned, the foundation model for human data, is it a System 2 model or a System 1 model?

Both.

Right. Have you thought about what the interface between them would be?

We need a new language. One thought I have is this: Imagine someone who can't speak. When they interact normally with the physical world, they aren't using System 2 or System 1, or thinking of language as System 2 or 1 to explain their own behavior. Right? Planning and acting might happen in some other space. But what that space is, I have no clue right now.

Maybe that space has to be learned from human data.

Exactly. We need to learn that space from scratch. I think with System 1 and System 2 right now, we're taking it quite literally because we have an LLM. But again, I think grounding an LLM in action is still too far away. I think if we had enough data, we could rebuild this from scratch.

In one of your slides, you mentioned a crow called Betty the Crow and its behavior. How far are today's robot systems from that level of intelligence?

Very far, extremely far. Betty the Crow is from a very famous experiment. There was a crow, and researchers found that it could use tools to reach things. There were several tubes of different lengths, with tools inside them. It would pull a tool from one tube and use it to reach a tool in another tube, then another tool in yet another tube, until it finally got the food out. It was seeing this setup for the first time. It had never seen that task before.

I think robot planning at that level is still very, very far away. If you really want it zero-shot, and we haven't done any pre-training on that system for that kind of task, then we're still extremely far away. Because I think problems like this still depend on the ability to use the components in your system to generate a new solution on the fly. Right now, the most I've seen is maybe cups in different colors. I can pick it up and pour water from it. Like that, right? So, I think we're still pretty far off.

And for a task like Betty the Crow, if we use RL to overfit one setup, that's definitely possible. You can definitely overfit it, but generalization is still a long way off, I think.

十六、作为教授：建立实验室文化

Danfei, to wrap up, I want to talk with you about your role as a professor at Georgia Tech, and your thoughts on the future. Now that you're a professor, what kind of advisor are you?

I think I'm somewhere between very hands-on and fairly hands-off, and I bounce back and forth. Some things I really want to do myself, and some things I really let the students handle. For some open-ended exploration, and I've only been doing this for less than four years, not even four years total as a professor. So that balance, I'm probably still figuring it out. Yeah. But there really are some things I really want to push forward, and I really will just do them myself.

For example, with EgoMimic, I really did that one myself.

You were the one who built that robot back then.

Yeah, and I even made myself a gripper. During the summer, I set up a 3D printer at home and designed a gripper myself. Because before that, the gripper had broken too many times. So I made one myself.

Built one myself.

Yeah. Then one day I just handed it to a student and told them to use this. Use this one.

But you're still very interested in hands-on engineering.

Certain parts of it. I think the more it involves hardware, the more interested I am. (Laughs) Yeah. I guess I'm drawn to two extremes. On one end, I'm interested in algorithms too. Some really top-level design work is genuinely fascinating to me. And things that are very tied to hardware, like doing control, or choosing hardware, I find that very interesting too. The stuff in the middle, I usually let the students handle more themselves.

Which do you enjoy more, being an IC and writing code yourself, or working more as a manager on the faculty side, in that kind of role?

I think what I probably enjoy more is well, the freedom. I can choose what I work on. It depends on the project, but I still prefer being able to pursue my own vision with some people helping me make it happen. In a way.

Then you've probably always enjoyed that. It feels like from undergrad to your PhD to now, you've always been doing what you wanted to do.

Yeah. And now there are people helping me do what I want to do, which is even better, right? (Laughs)

So it'd be hard for you to have a PhD student like yourself in your lab.

Right. That's something that I've discussed with my wife several times. I always say I'd be a terrible student. I absolutely wouldn't want to recruit myself. (Laughs)

Right. What values or standards do you insist on in your lab now?

I actually have a lab culture doc, and a few points in it are especially important. One is that I really dislike rigid division of labor. Because right now our projects are actually quite large, and a lot of people just focus only on their own part. I think that kind of thing isn't something I like. Because you need to care about the whole thing, and you need to understand everything. No work is beneath anyone. For example, one time a motor on our robot broke. I just went in, since nobody knew how to solder it, I soldered it myself. I want all my students to be truly full-stack. who are willing to be full-stack, or even enjoy it. That might be one thing about my lab that makes it pretty unique.

So when you pick and mentor students now, what trait matters most to you?

One bias I have is: you can't hate hardware. Haha.

Right. So you hate students who hate hardware?

I hate students who hate hardware.

十七、对学术界的看法与给年轻人的建议

Right haha. What is it about academia that really attracts you now?

I've always cared a lot about thought leadership. I value that a lot. This was basically what Fei-Fei told me early on: what academia should ultimately pursue is outsized impact. You need to use your ideas to influence others, and get other people to see that something is right, and be able to persuade them of it, so you can push it forward together.

If you were giving a young student or a young researcher one piece of advice, in today's industry and academia, in the current environment, about how to find their place,

This is what I want, but I don't know how to do it. I'd want that student to develop their own taste early on, know for themselves what's right, what isn't, and what they believe or don't believe. And then, as soon as possible, turn their understanding of the whole system into something concrete. In a project, don't just care about one thing. At the beginning, try to work on things that are as full-stack as possible. Then after the second year, you can start deciding whether you want to work on more open-ended problems, or whether you just want to take one problem and really solve it, or whether you want to, for example, collaborate with industry, and take some industry problems and make them more rigorous.

Right. I think you really need to build that foundation well.

For people starting a PhD this year, doing a robotics PhD in 2026, compared with your cohort ten years ago, doing robotics research around 2016, is it harder or easier?

Harder. It's because the FOMO is just too strong now. I think right now, if you're not connected to the mainstream, you really feel like you'll be left out. So, developing taste really means being able to really think through why something is worth doing, and think through the different paths, and why people do it or don't. Right? You need that patience.

Why did people have that patience? Because maybe there wasn't a winner-takes-all dynamic. But now, clearly, in industry, there may already be a winner.

There is a winner.

Right now, there is a winner. So people may not have as much patience to develop their own taste.

Right.

I think that makes it harder. But on the other hand, there are more resources, and more tools. Now you have tools like Claude Code. Those things can make research somewhat easier. So, it's harder to stay focused, but if you want to get something done, if you choose correctly, it's much easier to do it really well.

On taste, how do you judge, over this 10-plus-year journey, how do you tell whether a direction only looks important or will really change the field? What does your internal test actually look like?

I'm pretty evidence-based. I need to see something showing signs of life before I really believe in it. But that's not the whole story. I mean, there are really two possibilities. One is first-principles reasoning: this is definitely going to happen. The other is that it's already starting to emerge. I've already seen some early signs. That's when I start to believe it.

But taste is a bit different. I don't think it's quite the same. I think taste is really about what kinds of problems you choose to work on. I'm not saying I have great taste. I just think taste matters a lot. Right now, my taste is very practical. That was completely different during my PhD. I'm not saying my taste is great now. It's just that taste, when it comes to research, is incredibly important. Right. It determines what kind of research you do.

And for you personally, what are your career goals?

I don't really have a specific goal. I just hope that one day we'll see the field of robotics reach its GPT-3 moment. That's not a personal goal of mine. I just hope I can contribute in some small way to helping this move forward. I'd be very happy if that happens. But again, when it comes to research, I don't have much ego. It doesn't matter who gets it done.

You just mentioned robotics' GPT-3 moment. How would you define that moment?

In any setting, anything a person can do, a robot can do with about a 40 to 50 percent success rate.

If you were to give younger people some advice, a lot of students are entering robotics now, and AI too, right? what would you say to yourself ten years ago, when you were just starting out?

Look more at the paths of great researchers. Ask why. Try to understand the researchers you admire and why they do what they do, not just what they did. I think that's the most important thing. Because you're really learning their gradient, not their trajectory. And if you can really figure out why they do it, in a way that makes sense to you, you'll also gain a clearer view of your own research.

If you really want to do robotics, then treat robotics as a whole field. You can't just say "That's not robotics." For example, "Control isn't robotics," "hardware isn't robotics," or, as people used to say, "planning isn't robotics." Or that "object detection isn't robotics." All of those things are robotics. A lot of people confuse methods with problems. You're trying to solve a problem, and there are many different methods. Not everyone has to use the same method to solve that problem. That doesn't mean the method is the problem, or the problem is the method.

结语：时间胶囊

Danfei, thanks for your time. Well, at this point in time, we're recording this episode, and it will stay here. I hope, before we end, you can leave a few words on this podcast as a time capsule. Maybe five, ten, or even twenty years from now, you and the audience will come back to it. If you were to leave something here, what would you say?

I think being able to take part in a field that can change the world, at the right time, and in the right place, is a very lucky thing. I don't think that's because I've always wanted to change the world or accomplish something huge that I happened to be in the right place at the right time. Rather, I think that most of the time, for me, I think of myself as someone without extraordinary talent, just an ordinary person. I just think for most people, for the vast majority of people, doing what you actually want to do will probably make your career and your life a bit easier. And even if sometimes you're a little outside the mainstream, the consequences usually aren't that bad.

So be brave and do what you want to do. Don't carry so much baggage. What's to lose, right?

Okay, thanks for your time. We'll stop here for today. Thank you.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个反直觉的发现：他不是"选择"了机器人，而是"无法忍受"其他一切
整场访谈最让人意外的并不是 Danfei 对机器人的热爱，而是他反复提到的那种**"反向推力"**——"不想做某件事的力量比想做某件事的力量更强"。从中学时拒绝应试教育，到博士时拒绝 DeepMind 的自上而下，再到坚持不做行为克隆就"政治不正确"时仍然要做——他的整个职业生涯不是被"吸引"的，而是被"排斥"塑造的。
这是一个被严重低估的人生算法：**大多数励志叙事强调"找到你的热爱"，而 Danfei 的故事说明，更可靠的导航器其实是"识别你绝不能忍受的环境"**。前者需要漫长的自我探索，后者只需要一次糟糕的体验。从信号论的角度看，"厌恶"比"喜欢"更高频、更强烈、更早出现，因此更适合做决策梯度。
## 跨领域连接：人形机器人、人类数据与 LLM 的"基础设施寄生"现象
Danfei 反复提到一个关键张力——**LLM 之所以能起飞，是因为互联网、键盘、智能手机这些基础设施"恰好"产生了人类语言数据作为副产品**；而机器人却必须**专门去创造**人类数据。这本质上是"数据寄生"和"数据耕作"的差异。

item.title: [Business of Sport] The Craziest Promotion of All Time, F1 is Back & How the Sub-2 Marathon Shows Athletics’ Big Problem
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：体育的"金字塔顶端悖论"

这期节目暴露了一个几乎没人指出的结构性怪象——一项运动越接近"人类极限的纯粹证明"，运动员反而越穷。Sebastian Sawe 跑出史上最快马拉松，奖金 4 万英镑；一个无人知晓的 Challenger 网球巡回赛冠军也能拿到这个数。这不是市场的偶然失灵，而是一个系统性的"价值蒸发"机制：

田径越是"纯粹"（谁最快、谁最远、谁最高），它就越被吸入奥运会这个四年一度的引力黑洞——而 IOC 每年从田径上获得的约 10 亿美元收入，没有一分钱按比例下行到日常职业巡回赛。换句话说：田径的"纯粹性"恰恰是它的诅咒——它太容易被国家、被奥运、被业余主义传统所征用，反而失去了讨价还价的杠杆。NBA、NFL 50% 的收入分给球员的模式之所以可能，正是因为它们"不纯粹"——它们是商业 IP，不是民族图腾。

二、跨领域连接：Chelsea、LIV Golf、F1 共享同一个"叙事免疫系统"

表面上这是三件不相干的事，但 Charlie 无意间揭示了一个深层机制——一旦某个组织在公众心智中固化成某种叙事，相同事实会得到完全不同的处理：

  • F1 在三场比赛后就要紧急修改新规、20 岁车手撞墙 50G——本应是治理灾难，却被解读成"敏捷响应"；
  • Chelsea 解雇又一个没经验的主帅——本是常规操作，却被定性为"项目失败的存在主义证据"；
  • LIV Golf 推迟一站赛事——"推迟"这个词本身就在做叙事工作，掩盖资金链断裂。

这其实是认知科学里"确认偏差 × 制度声誉"的复利效应：叙事不是描述现实的工具，叙事本身就是过滤器。Liberty Media 因为前几年口碑积累，获得了"see no evil"的赦免券；Chelsea 则被锁进了"无论做什么都是 muppet"的反向叙事监狱。这跟金融市场里"高 PE 公司错失盈利仍涨"、"低 PE 公司超预期仍跌"是同一个结构。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：足球塔楼有"结构跃迁"，但没人愿意承认它是相变而非升级

Charlie 关于 National League 三升三降的反对，是这期节目最精妙的洞见——它揭示了一个英国体育界几乎从不公开讨论的事实：EFL 第二级到第一级是"线性升级"，但 National League 到 EFL 是"相变"（phase transition）。半职业 → 全职业、塑胶草皮 → 真草、无青训 → 必备 Academy、可有可无的社区慈善 → 必须的 Foundation。这不是数量级的改变，是物质形态的改变。

跨领域一类比就清楚了——这就像生物学里单细胞生物到多细胞生物的跃迁：你不能让一个变形虫"晋升"成一只老鼠，因为它的所有底层架构都要重写。Yo-yo clubs（升降机俱乐部）在英国低级联赛的悲剧，本质上是逼一个变形虫在一年内长出心脏、肺和脊椎，然后再让它退化回去——每次往返都伤筋动骨（挖掉塑胶草皮就回不去了，因为周中场地租赁的现金流没了）。

而最讽刺的是：National League 自己用六队季后赛来制造商业戏剧性，然后又抱怨"只升一队不公平"——他们要求 EFL 为他们的商业选择买单。这跟科技公司一边制造"成长焦虑"营销一边要求政府补贴新员工培训是同一个套路。

📝 转录文本

开场：本期看点速览

Charlie: Formula 1 is back. The Miami Grand Prix this weekend.

It's probably a bit of a relief to the rulers of the sport that they've had this break.

Well, it's funny you should say that.

So, there have been a number of changes that have been put in by the FIA and Formula 1. Why did it take for a 20-year-old to smash into a wall at 194 km an hour, 50 gs of force to make this happen?

One of the most incredible sporting feats I've seen. Let's be honest, I would say 99 whatever percent weren't aware of Sebastian Sawe before this weekend. And if I want to go and watch Sebastian Sawe next week, where do I go? What do I do? What's the circuit like? What's the market like? This is all stuff that really needs to be solved for these athletes to get the recognition they deserve.

Charlie, what do you think? Isn't this perfectly Chelsea? Somehow the team still manages to compete for trophies. Does that mean a fundamental reappraisal of the project or does it simply mean, well, next time you won't hire an inexperienced manager?

You asking for my opinion?

Yeah. You're actually looking like you got a slightly thick look on your face as if you're actually waiting to be slapped.

寒暄与天气

Hello Charlie, how are you?

Charles, um I am yeah, I'm in good form. Um been enjoying the sunny weather recently. Um I was I was taking a little holiday down the West Country um over the weekend and it was beautiful and sunny. Um and really reminded me for the first time in a while of why I actually do love this country in many ways.

Makes such a difference, isn't it? It really does. I mean I know we only ever talk about the weather.

We wouldn't want to change um sort of the stereotype of Englishmen, right?

Completely agree.

周末战报：Oxford United、RAC Mons 与 Chelsea

Um, what's been happening on the sport side?

Yeah, disappointing weekend from my perspective I have to say. Um you know uh my home club Oxford United got relegated um from the Championship to League One after two seasons in the Championship. Sort of you know bittersweet afternoon but mostly bitter um winning beating Sheffer Wednesday as they needed to but because Charlton my other former club also won against Hull that meant that Oxford couldn't catch them on the final day. Um so despite getting 47 points from 45 games which would normally be enough to stay up in the championship um they are going down.

47 points quite a good season.
