item.title: [Business of Sport] Title, Europe, Relegation…Cash: It’s ALL To Play For On & Off The Pitch
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

隐藏的"反向激励结构"

英超的财务激励曲线呈现出一个反直觉的"哑铃形"——两端的影响远大于中间，但更妙的是：冠军的财务回报几乎为负。利物浦上赛季因夺冠多拿了300万英镑奖金，却给球员发了400万的奖金池。换句话说，从纯现金流角度看，利物浦"赔钱"夺了冠，曼城"赚钱"丢了冠。这在任何商业逻辑里都是荒诞的——一家公司业绩第一名比第二名净利润还低。但足球告诉我们：冠军本质上是一种"非财务资产"，是品牌资本和情感期权，而非营收事件。

跨领域连接：足球联赛 = 风险投资组合

把英超的财务结构放到VC视角看会突然清晰： - 冠军就像一家明星portfolio公司IPO——上头条但实际管理费分成有限； - 欧冠资格才是真正的"独角兽轮"——单笔注入5500万-7500万欧元，决定基金存续； - 降级则是portfolio里的"清算事件"——但这里出现了视频里最反直觉的洞察：降级对小俱乐部（伯恩利、狼队）的伤害可控，因为他们的商业模式本就预埋了降级期权；而对热刺这种"伪装成大俱乐部"的中型资产，降级是结构性灾难——年收入可能从6亿掉到3.5亿，第二年再跌到2.5亿。

这揭示了一个隐藏真相：俱乐部最危险的位置不是垫底，而是"过度商业化的中游"——你按顶级俱乐部的成本结构搭建了基础设施（球场、训练基地、商业部门），却用第8名的竞技表现来供养它。这和WeWork的悲剧是同构的：按规模化的房地产帝国设计成本，按二房东的现金流支付。

让人意外的观察：欧协联是"奢侈品的诅咒"

视频里最反常识的一段是关于切尔西的欧协联悖论：对水晶宫、布伦特福德来说，欧协联是历史性突破和真金白银的注入；对切尔西来说，赢得欧协联反而是一种"惩罚"——它堵塞了周四晚上的赛程，疲劳累积，反过来威胁次年欧冠资格，而欧冠资格才是切尔西7亿英镑亏损叙事得以成立的财务基石。

这是经济学里典型的"边际效用反转"：同一杯水，给沙漠中人是救命，给已经溺水者是负担。把它推得更远——这其实是所有处于"中产陷阱"组织的通病：你拥有的资源既不够支撑你成为顶级，又让你看不上次级机会，结果是任何"中等好事"对你都是干扰项。这就是为什么有钱人拒绝小项目、大公司错失颠覆性创新——不是他们看不见机会，而是机会的体量配不上他们的成本结构。

一个未被言明的系统性观察

视频反复在算"降级损失多少营收"，但没有人问一个更深的问题：为什么英超系统会鼓励热刺把成本基建搭到6亿？ 答案藏在转播分成的"merit ladder"和欧战奖金的指数级跳跃里——这个系统在结构上奖励"All-in冲顶"的赌徒式投资，而当赌注失败时，惩罚是非线性放大的。换言之，英超不是一个稳态商业生态，它是一个被精心设计的、强制所有参与者必须高杠杆运行的金融衍生品市场。热刺现在面临的，不是体育意外，而是衍生品到期日的强制平仓。

📝 转录文本

开场：一个让一切悬而未决的周末

Let's move on because Charlie, you very — you very gleefully, I think partly because it was in reference to one of my team's terrible performance against Manchester United at the weekend — but you very gleefully sent me a message saying what a day of football on Sunday. Um, or sorry, what a weekend of football, on Sunday.

Because in that weekend we saw the title race properly blown wide open. We saw the race for European football — from Champions League down to any European places — blown wide open. And the relegation places started to look like they were really taking shape, in the clubs most vulnerable for the dreaded drop.

And so when you said that, I just thought, you know what, he's right. And it was rooted in obviously Chelsea's misery, but it made me think far more about what these last five games now — there is something on every one of those key things that teams play for for the end of the season. It's really up for grabs.

Which is great from a fans' perspective for drama. Um, but also hugely interesting from the perspective we always look at this from, which is: what's the actual impact of a team winning the title or not? What's the impact of qualifying for Europe or not? What's the impact of relegation? The business implications of these various different fights — because they're radically different, and not in the order which you would think in normal sports.

Right? So in normal sports you'd imagine that the real financial implication — the biggest financial implication — as in golf for instance, is whoever finishes first gets a massive amount of money. Whoever finishes second gets a lot less money, and then it tails off quite quickly from there.

So should we start with the title? So let's start with the title, guys.

一、夺冠的真实财务价值：几乎为零

Yeah. So, title of the weekend: Manchester City beat Arsenal 2-1 at the Etihad, which meant that they are now three points behind Arsenal with a game in hand. They are one goal behind them on goal difference with that game in hand. So, Manchester City having been nine points behind are now well within striking distance.

Let's try and extract some of the emotion out of it and actually look at what the title means — or winning the title means — financially. Because it's not that much, as you've just highlighted. It's very minimal.

So if you look at last season in the Premier League, Liverpool were the winners — they got 53 million pounds of the sort of sliding scale money. So when you look at the Premier League TV money, what you're looking at is a certain level which is guaranteed — that's about 100 million which every team gets — and then there's a certain amount which is on a sort of sliding scale, what we call a "merit rake" — that's the technical term.

The winners last season Liverpool, on that merit rate, got 53 million, and the bottom place club Southampton got 2.5 million. Okay, it's quite a big difference. But the second got 50 million. So the difference between Liverpool and City last season was 3 million in terms of actual prize money received.

Right? So basically, in terms of businesses with turnovers of 650, 700, 750 million, we're talking about an absolutely negligible difference.

球员奖金让冠军反而"亏钱"

And even worse than that, Charlie, this is something which fans often don't fully compute. Players tend to be on bonuses for winning titles. Right? So I had a quick look at this. The Liverpool players received a bonus pot of 4 million for winning the title last year — which means that actually, net net, Liverpool were worse off for winning the title than City were for finishing second. Marginally, but you know it's very much much of a muchness.

So from Arsenal's perspective, there is no clear financial benefit to coming first rather than second — excepting this: which is that we don't know, and no one outside the C-suite at Arsenal does know, what the trigger payments are in their commercial deals.

So their commercial deals may or may not include various triggers. One of which will be qualification for the Champions League or not — that'll probably be the biggest one, by the way. Another one of which will be winning this title, and one of which will almost certainly be winning the Premier League title, at which point a bunch of their sponsors will be obligated to pay an additional sum of money.

So we don't know what that is, but I'm going to guess that we're probably talking about 20 million, 30 million, something like this. So meaningful, but not absolutely transformational. So the quest to win the title is very much a football quest and a fan quest, as opposed to one which necessarily keeps the finance director up at night.

品牌价值的微妙作用

What about brand value? Should we say loser side being able to say "champion" — commercial?

Yeah, exactly. So both existing commercial deals being triggered to pay you out more, but also your negotiation of the next round of commercial deals — whatever they might be, your next shirt sponsor. But you wouldn't place huge weight on that narrative around "okay, we're title winners, we're now commercially so much more powerful."

I mean, it's all part of the mix and it all helps, and I think it slightly depends on which club. Okay. So, do I think that Man City would be massively more powerful commercially if they won the title this season? No. Why? Because they've won it in six of the last eight years anyway. Right. So it's another Premier League title.

The thing that would be more transformational for Man City was when they won the Champions League. That would have changed the metrics around City, because they hadn't done it until that point.

In Arsenal's case, probably it would and will make a difference, because they haven't won the top flight for over 20 years. So it's re-establishing the club as being in that absolute top echelon. Which until they do win the Premier League title — you know, arguably at the moment they kind of are where Spurs were a few years ago, where Spurs kept on finishing second. If you remember under Poch towards the end of Poch's time there, they were sort of second, third, second, third, that type of thing.

That's where Arsenal have been, and winning the title this year would be taking them out of that bracket and saying, "No, we are absolutely in that conversation with Liverpool and Man City for the top clubs of England right now."

Yep. Okay. So, key point there is: look fans, we understand it's everything to you, but from your business standpoint, it's not.

二、欧战资格：真正的财务杠杆

But what is the biggest needle mover, performance side, should we say, at the top end of the league — we know what relegation is, we'll get on to that — is qualifying for Europe. And beyond that, qualifying for the Champions League versus Europa League versus the Europa Conference League.

So, the numbers around this are actually quite staggering — for what you get from the Champions League and what you get for competing in others. And this year we have five places in the Premier League that are Champions League. So normally you would only have four, but the new UEFA coefficient means that the most successful leagues in Europe get an extra place. So the Premier League has secured five, I think, with Arsenal's victory in Sporting in Lisbon against Sporting the other day.

三大欧战的入场费对比

To put some numbers into what it means to qualify for European competition:

  • Just for reaching the group stage, the league stage of the Champions League, you get €18.75 million.
  • You get €4.3 million for reaching the group stage of the Europa League, the league stage.
  • You get €3.17 million for reaching the Conference League.

So ultimately, between four to five times the revenue if you get into the Champions League compared to other European competition. And that adds up to — broadly speaking, if you qualify for the group stages of the Champions League and go to the knockout stages of the Champions League — you're going to make 60 or 70 million from your UEFA Champions League team.

I've got a decent run is between 55–75 million. That's right. It's going to be 60 to 70 million. If you're in the Europa League — well, last year Spurs received 25 million for winning it. Okay. Euros. Euros. And Chelsea received 19 million for winning the Conference League.

So Chelsea got the same for winning the Conference League — going through the whole Thursday night football from September through to May — they got the same as they would have got if they just qualified for the Champions League.

Yeah. But put it in — because Chelsea would always think they would qualify from the group stage, the knockout stage. So the basic difference in going to the Conference League and even winning it, and going to the knockout stages of the Champions League, is 50 million euros. So that is £40 million, right? So let's just keep the numbers round and say there's a 50 million swing — because they certainly couldn't guarantee winning the Conference League every single time you wanted it. There's a 50 million swing difference between Champions League football and Conference League football. 50 million.

切尔西的悬崖边

Now, bear in mind what we've said in previous podcasts about Chelsea's current run rate of losses, and the problems that that is causing them — and looks like causing them in the future — in terms of compliance with financial fair play rules, profit and sustainability or SCRs coming from UEFA, etc., etc., and what that then might mean going forward in terms of whether they're actually even able to play in European football in the future. Because they are teetering on the edge potentially of UEFA saying, "Do you know what, you've broken our rules so often that we're going to have to suspend you from European competition for a year."

So this is what Chelsea are playing for right now. And it might just be worth you outlining how the table looks right now, Charlie — because Chelsea are right on the edge.

第六到第十二名：三分之差的混战

So, I've got the table up in front of me. And just very specifically here, because there are games tonight and tomorrow night — we're recording on Tuesday — but the table as of Tuesday has Chelsea in sixth place, seven points behind Champions League football, with Liverpool in fifth place. So Chelsea are on 48 points and Liverpool are on 55 points. With five games to go, that looks really like that ship sailed.

That's gone, right?

But the biggest thing is look below, because what you have below is quite an incredible buildup of clubs competing for European football. You have:

  • Chelsea on 48 points
  • Brentford on 48 points
  • Bournemouth on 48 points
  • Brighton (who Chelsea played tonight) on 47 points
  • Everton on 47 points
  • Sunderland on 46 points
  • Fulham on 45 points

So from 6th to 12th, you're separated by three points — i.e. one win.

欧战对不同俱乐部的不对称意义

Now, you and I were having a conversation about this last night. It's a really interesting point. What I want to do is — I never want to devalue how important European football is. Crystal Palace playing in the semi-finals of the Conference League is a massive deal for the club.

And the reason why that is crucial for Palace is because until they won the FA Cup, they never won a trophy in their entire history. And they never played in European football in their entire history. So for them to be in the semi-final of any European competition is, for Palace, an amazing thing.

Fantastic. Right. Money aside, that's business and fans, it's great. And if you look at the clubs I've just mentioned below Chelsea: for Brentford, it would be the first time they play European football. For Bournemouth, the same. Brighton have done it. Everton would be right back in there. Sunderland — incredible, you know, if they could get in there. So for those clubs stacking up below, Europa League football and Conference League football would be huge — and a very welcome financial boost as well.

Yeah. For Chelsea, it would be an inconvenience.

Well, financially it wouldn't move the needle, right? So that 20 million of extra revenue — I mean, I'm not saying that they wouldn't want to have it, because at the moment they need to take every quid they can get — but the problem is, it clogs up your Thursday evenings to the point where it jeopardizes your chances of qualifying for the Champions League from the league the following year.

Okay? So in other words, you are out there slogging away in the outpost of European football every Thursday night, trying to win the Conference League, to at least repair some of the damage — the financial damage you get from not qualifying for —

Manchester United show that this year.

They did. Man United have not been playing European football this year, whereas pretty much all their other rivals have, and it's made a massive difference to them. Yeah.

You know, every weekend they're playing against tired rivals.

Yeah. And we should say while we're talking about European football — you know, I don't think many people would have had Manchester United at third and Aston Villa at fourth. No. So huge seasons for them, financially massive. Their ability to go and strengthen in a transfer market — particularly for Aston Villa, you know, who economically are sitting right on the balance, between last year people giving them no chance because they thought they had to sell a huge amount to comply with profit and sustainability.

So, you know, there's some great stories in there in European qualification. But I do think you can't underestimate the importance in the business models of that Champions League qualification — particularly for those clubs for whom that's what they're depending on.

I think I said last week, I said Chelsea's — in their justification of the record losses that they submitted to Premier League and to Europe — if they were going to hit 700 million or so, which is what they wanted to do, that relies on them being in the Champions League.

三、降级：结构性灾难还是可控风险？

Let's have a look at relegation, right? Let's have a look at the relegation area.

Because normally what's the case is, you've got clubs fighting against each other for relegation who almost psychologically have been prepared for the possibility of relegation from the start of the season. They're the smaller clubs with the smaller squads and the smaller wage bills and so on and so forth. And obviously they're used to a merit payment which is much lower because they're normally consistently down towards the bottom of the league.

So in other words, they're normally receiving let's say £110 million a year in TV revenue from the Premier League. If they get relegated to the Championship, their first year parachute payment — that's the money which the Premier League gives you after you've been relegated — would be about £55 million pounds. So they're looking at a £50 million pound revenue loss coming down from the Premier League to the Championship. They might lose some commercial revenue as well. So maybe actually that gap is more like 70 or 80.

So what they then do is they sell, sell, sell a few players, reduce their wage bill enough to then try and help those first two years of parachute payments, try and help them bounce back up again, whilst keeping an eye on what might be the case if they were not to achieve that.

狼队、伯恩利模式：把降级写进商业模型

Okay, so that's your Wolves business model. That's the Wolves business model. That's the Burnley business model. So these guys are getting relegated, but they've kept a bit to one side. They've you know made sure that their model encapsulates the possibility of relegation. Disappointing though it would be — much as it reduces the value of their clubs, and that's a very significant thing for the owners — it's not risking them from a business model perspective.

热刺：6亿营收 → 3.5亿 → 2.5亿的悬崖

Spurs, if they got relegated. I mean, we were probably the first people to discuss that as a serious possibility, which is now about two months ago.

Yeah. And you still read the comments and everyone was just like "you guys are just baiting".

Yeah, exactly. So from two months ago when we first speculated that Spurs could get relegated — and it was a video seen by almost 300,000 people, with an awful lot of comments on it, and there were an awful lot of people with Schadenfreude for Spurs but also a lot of Spurs fans going "you're just trolling us, we're not going to get relegated" — right, but two months later, that's not what Spurs fans are thinking now.

So, we've just discussed how your Burnleys and your Wolves are looking at, you know, net net a revenue diminution of about 70 million quid. Sell a couple of players, adjust your wage bill a little bit, make a few back office staff redundant, and away you go.

Okay. Spurs, if they get relegated, there's now quite a bit of speculation around what that might look like. And that is very, very different. Because their current revenue is not 150, 180 like Wolves and Burnley. It's 600.

And the question is: well, how much of that is vulnerable to a relegation? And the general estimate seems to be that they could lose 250 million of annual revenue if they go down — through a mixture of TV rights, sponsorship, merchandise, match day, absolutely everything. Because the entire club is geared up to being a top six club.

数字拆解：媒体、欧战、上座、商业

So, just looking at that — so last year Leicester got 116.9 million, including media and placement for being in the Prem. So let's say Spurs finish in the same position, 18th. So they'd lose 60 million, because they still get 50 million from a parachute. Match day — obviously you've got a 60,000 seat —

But don't forget in that 600 million is included European football as well.

Right, because of course — because they won the Europa League — that was 25 million.

Yeah, plus then all the games that they were able to. So let's just say — I don't know — let's just say it was maybe 50 million. So then you should get about 100 million for not playing in Europe and getting relegated, just from the Premier League money.

Then of course there's the stuff we don't necessarily know about, but you can assume, which is the things like the commercial kickbacks that the sponsors will have. You'll have the match day, where it'll be very interesting to see, won't it? I mean, genuinely, hospitality pricing — even match day attendance, you know, genuinely — will you get 60,000 Spurs fans going every week to — I mean, to those games? I don't know. Again, it depends on your pricing model, it depends on how the team's doing. But with their match day currently being I think around 150 million — is it Charlie, something like that, the Spurs match day? — I would be absolutely shocked if they managed to maintain that above 100. Shocked.

Right. Because the hospitality is such a big chunk of that. Merchandise, again, there'd be a big chunk of merchandise in there which would already have taken a hit from Son not being there anymore, but will now come down substantially again.

And then you've got your commercial — and of that 600, I think from memory around about 250 million sponsorships. And obviously it depends on the structuring of those deals and how long they've got to run, but I've just made a rough calculation that it would be surprising to me if they didn't drop off at least 80 to 100 in sponsorships.

So you add up all of that and you think, well that's 250.

第二年：第二次悬崖

Okay. So if their 600 comes down to 350 in year 1, but in year two — if they stayed in the Championship for another season — that 350 would probably come down to more like 250. Because you lose a chunk of your parachute payments because the next parachute payment is lower. But also some of those commercial deals that would have hung around for one more year, then come out of contract at the end of that year. And then you've got various hospitality deals — because not all hospitality is sold seasonally; quite often in big Premier League clubs, you buy three seasons worth up front — and so on and so forth.

So, you know, when you look — and I'm not doing this as "this is what we're really facing" — it's absolutely extraordinary.

Yeah.

I mean, the reason Leicester's achievement was so incredible was because it was over 38 games of a season, i.e. you have 38 opportunities to perform. People always talk about the greatest shocks, you know — and I don't know, let's use a Rugby World Cup, when South Africa lost to Japan. Incredible. Absolutely ridiculous.

One-off game.

But a one-off game. This is the same as the Leicester piece. This is a team that has all of 600 million in revenues, won the European competition the year before, having a very bad Premier League — wage bill of 27 — wage bill of 270 million, and it could get relegated. It's quite extraordinary.

西汉姆：相似但不同的风险

Where would you put West Ham in that discussion? Because again, at time of recording, what I'm really just saying — just to be clear to people looking at the league — we're really, I think, talking about West Ham or Spurs going down. Because Nottingham Forest had a big win against Burnley at the weekend, and Leeds the same — pretty much safe now, I say — on 39 points, where Spurs are on 31.

So West Ham at 33, Spurs at 31, 17th, 18th. It's looking like it's between those two. I would have had West Ham down as another club that wouldn't have expected to be in a relegation battle. But there's absolutely no way they would have been as clear in the strategic planning as Spurs would have been from it.

No. So, from memory, West Ham's revenues — which have been climbing significantly obviously since the move to the London Stadium — from memory, they're about the sort of 300, 350 million. That sort of area. I have to — I don't have it in front of me, but it was around —

I'd say between 250 to 350 somewhere, depending on what competitions they're in.

I think they were in the top 20 of the money league, weren't they? They were. They sneaked into the top 20 of the money league at around those types of levels.

So, their revenue at the moment is about half that of Spurs, and would go down — including their parachute payments — probably about, in the first season back in the Championship, 150 to 200, that kind of thing. So they would see a very, very significant revenue drop — maybe a 100, maybe 150 million — but it's not the 250 million that Spurs are facing.

And I think that is the big challenge: when you get a revenue decrease of that kind of magnitude, and when you're being forced to look at "yeah, but what would you do if you stay in the Championship one more year as well?" — effectively, what they're going to have to do is they're going to have to sell pretty much every player they possibly can. They cannot afford to take, forward, along with their massive existing cost base of running that stadium, running that training site, running the huge office staff they have, and all these kind of things — they cannot afford to take that punt on keeping loads of their best played players on the books, which to be honest with you is what normally recently relegated clubs do, to enable themselves to bounce back.

So the squad rebuilding operation there, where they go out and sign a whole bunch of players who they think can get them promoted — that'll be really interesting.

收尾：财务影响最小的，恰恰是冠军

The funny thing in all of this, to wrap it up: the thing that everyone competes for in the Premier League — i.e. the title — actually has the smallest financial impact.

Correct. Where we're really looking at the biggest needle movers on the business side is the European qualification and relegation. It's great to have all these different outcomes in play, isn't —

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 隐藏的"反向激励结构"
英超的财务激励曲线呈现出一个反直觉的"哑铃形"——两端的影响远大于中间，但更妙的是：**冠军的财务回报几乎为负**。利物浦上赛季因夺冠多拿了300万英镑奖金，却给球员发了400万的奖金池。换句话说，从纯现金流角度看，**利物浦"赔钱"夺了冠**，曼城"赚钱"丢了冠。这在任何商业逻辑里都是荒诞的——一家公司业绩第一名比第二名净利润还低。但足球告诉我们：**冠军本质上是一种"非财务资产"，是品牌资本和情感期权，而非营收事件**。
## 跨领域连接：足球联赛 = 风险投资组合
把英超的财务结构放到VC视角看会突然清晰： - **冠军**就像一家明星portfolio公司IPO——上头条但实际管理费分成有限； - **欧冠资格**才是真正的"独角兽轮"——单笔注入5500万-7500万欧元，决定基金存续； - **降级**则是portfolio里的"清算事件"——但这里出现了视频里**最反直觉的洞察**：降级对小俱乐部（伯恩利、狼队）的伤害可控，因为他们的商业模式本就**预埋了降级期权**；而对热刺这种"伪装成大俱乐部"的中型资产，降级是**结构性灾难**—

item.title: [邦妮區塊鏈 Bonnie Blockchain] 莊家調升預期：年底比特幣4000萬台幣！薩爾瓦多推手Samson Mow：這不是預測，是必然？【邦妮區塊鏈】
item.dc:creator: 邦妮區塊鏈 Bonnie Blockchain
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：比特币的"反市场营销"是它最深的护城河

Samson 不经意间揭示了一个被严重低估的悖论——所有山寨币都需要营销团队，而比特币不需要。他提到以太坊发布时配备了 30 人的营销团队、精心设计的 logo、"世界计算机"的 slick branding。这看似是产品推广的常识，但你把它放在博弈论的视角下看，会发现一件惊人的事：

任何需要被"卖"的货币，本质上都不是货币——它是一个产品。 黄金没有营销团队，美元没有营销团队（美军算不算另说），引力没有营销团队。真正的"基础层"事物会自然外溢。当 Samson 说"比特币会自己卖自己"时，他实际上在描述一个自指完备性：一个东西如果需要外部叙事来维持其价值，那么它的价值就依附于叙事的存续。比特币的"无营销"不是缺陷，而是对其本体地位的最严苛压力测试——它已经通过了。

这与生物进化中的一个原则惊人地相似：最成功的物种不需要伪装（顶级捕食者通常颜色鲜明，因为它们不需要躲藏）。Samson 用了一个词 "apex predator of money"——这不是修辞，这是分类学。

二、跨领域连接：比特币 = 信息论 × 难民经济学 × 后劳动社会的资产论

Samson 抛出的两个并列定义"Bitcoin is digital gold / Bitcoin is information"看似互不相干，但合起来构成了一个罕见的跨学科爆点：

  • 从香农信息论角度看，"信息没有交易对手风险"这句话其实是说：信息是唯一一种可以无损复制传输、却又可以通过密码学限定所有权的稀缺性载体。物理黄金的稀缺性来自原子，比特币的稀缺性来自数学。前者依赖宇宙物质守恒，后者依赖 SHA-256 的不可逆性——而后者在数学层面比前者更牢固。
  • 从难民经济学角度看，委内瑞拉黄金被英国央行扣押、俄罗斯被踢出 SWIFT、加拿大突然成为美国"威胁"——这些不是孤立事件，而是全球化退潮中"主权信用"重新被定价的过程。Samson 让我意外的一句话是："你成长在一个'相处友好'是默认设定的世界。" 这是整段访谈最具杀伤力的洞察——它指出大多数人对货币的理解，建立在一个正在消失的历史样本期之上。
  • 而最让我惊讶的连接在最后：他把比特币和 AI 时代的劳动贬值连在了一起。如果 AI 把"工作"商品化，那么唯一不会被商品化的是"资产所有权"。Samson 在重新定义"婴儿潮一代凭房产致富"的逻辑——那不是房地产神奇，而是任何"被认定为非生产性避险资产"的东西，在生产力跃迁期都会暴涨。比特币在 AI 革命中扮演的角色，等同于黄金本位崩溃后房地产的角色。这是一个结构性类比，而不是叙事类比。

三、最被忽略的系统性真相：Saylor 不是在买比特币，他在"重写市场结构"

主流分析把 Michael Saylor 视为一个激进的多头赌徒。但 Samson 漏出的细节更有意思——STRC 这个产品在做的，是把"不理解比特币的人"和"理解比特币的人"用金融工程强行对接：

"他给你 11% 固定收益。比特币涨 30%，他拿走 20%。"

这是一种风险偏好套利，本质上是把"对比特币的认知差距"货币化。在传统金融里，套利通常发生在不同市场之间（地理、时间、货币）；而 Saylor 在做的是认知套利——他把"midcurve 群体的稳定收益偏好"作为流动性来源，源源不断地把它转化为比特币持仓。

更深的一层是 Samson 提到的"双底"：比特币本身有底，strategy 也有底。当 strategy 的市值大到一个程度，它本身成为一种"准央行"行为——它在比特币市场上扮演的角色，类似日本央行在国债市场上的角色（最终买家）。这意味着比特币事实上获得了一个"私人最后贷款人"。这是货币史上前所未有的：一个公司，自发地、出于盈利动机，承担了一个去中心化货币的市场托底功能。

这件事的哲学含义比"比特币会涨"重要一万倍——它说明资本主义在比特币身上演化出了一种自组织的稳定机制，而这种机制完全不依赖于政府或央行。

如果这个机制持续存在，那么 Samson 那个"$1.33 million by end of 2026"的数字，反而是这次访谈中最不重要的内容。

But everything in the fiat system seems to be coming in an end. You could say it's pessimistic but I think it's just things have to reconcile eventually. You cannot keep printing money and increasing the debt forever.

And

my question is it it has been working and yes it's bad but why can't it just continue? Yeah the numbers are huge already. It can be even even bigger. So what to everyday people? I think Japan, the bank of Japan is now buying most of their own debt back, right? So, you can see the uh whatever you want to call it, the game of musical chairs is ending, right? Nobody wants to buy it, right? People are moving to hard assets and that's why gold rallied.

at least part of the reason why gold rallied because you know you can say it could go on forever and theoretically yeah it can keep printing forever but eventually it loses some meaning but more importantly you're seeing fra fractures in the system we're moving into a world that is more uncertain like can you keep dollars forever and trade with dollars forever this question mark

how does the transition going to happen is it Because I'm trying to picture, okay, we're we're using fiat money and then all of a sudden people are like, oh, this is not sustainable and let's, you know, add Bitcoin into this picture. How does that work?

Nobody knows, but it's going to be sudden. So, I think how it plays out is Bitcoin starts to go parabolic. You know, it goes up to 200,000 and then people start to FOMO in because suddenly they're interested again. Then it goes up to 500 and then you see countries starting to get into it because, oh, guess what? we need Bitcoin now and then that sends us up to a million.

And I think there will be a time where the fiat pricing of things doesn't match. So you will be able to buy a house for I don't know 100,000 Satoshi's or something like that. It's going to be a really really weird thing. But eventually what people will do is repric things in Satoshi's instead of in fiat currency, right? Because fiat currency is meaningless.

Google 趋势、情绪指标与"中庸曲线"

Okay. Chart shows this chart. Okay. shows that Google trend of interest in Bitcoin. 2017 it was at 100. That's the peak. 2021 it was at 63. Uh this cycle's peak 2025 October it was as at 37. And right now we're about to head into April of 2026 we're at 2728. Mhm. Pretty terrible. I don't know. I don't think so. I don't think it matters really because Bitcoin is not something for the masses yet. If it was for the masses, then we wouldn't be at these levels of price. Yeah. We'd be at millions right now.

And it it's also similar like you have the fear and greed indicator, right? And then you have um the prediction market saying Bitcoin is uh there's a chance Bitcoin is going to go down. Oh yeah, I saw that. But all of these things are sentiment.

And sentiment, you know, the normal distribution, right? There's the high IQ and the low IQ and the midcurve. Sentiment is basically the midcurve. It's like what the normal person thinks. But isn't that where the money is at? Like that's the majority of money. No, that's where the debt is. Like if you look at the normal person, they're in debt, right?

Okay,

so sentiment is a mid-curve emission. It's just like the barometer of the average person's fear and or or interest in Bitcoin or lack of interest in Bitcoin. It is not the first principal thinkers, right? Again, you should look at Warren Buffett, you know, he's not the midcurve. He's the opposite. You know, we can say either he's on this side or that side, but he's not the midcurve for certain, right? So, he's the one that's greedy when others are fearful.

Oh, that was very dangerous. Oh my god. Tell me more about Warren Buffett because he does not like Bitcoin.

Well, if Warren Buffett was our age, I think he would like Bitcoin. But there's a certain point in time at which you can't understand something that's so drastically different. You either dismiss it outright or you cannot grasp the core concepts behind it. But I think if Warren Buffett was our age, he would be all in on Bitcoin because it is disruptive and it is it is a powerful tech.

And if you look at the boomers, like they believe strongly in real estate because that was what they knew growing up and that's what benefited them. They bought a house for $1,000 and now it's worth millions of dollars. So they believe in what benefited them in the past or what was transformative for them in the past, right? And the irony is real estate only bmed because it was a new safe haven after the gold standard was abandoned. Right?

So in a in many ways if uh the gold standard wasn't abandoned they would all all the real estate boomers would be gold bugs. We're in such an interesting phase of the world where anything can happen, right? Like know oil can go up here and now it's back down and it's probably going to go back up again. Gold was up here and now it's back down. Silver was up here and now it's back down. Bitcoin is here and now it's going to go up again. Right. So, it's just a massive amount of uncertainty in the world.

Yeah. And I don't think it's going to get more certain in the next few years. You know, it definitely things are changing very rapidly. But again, going back to the very beginning of our conversation, Bitcoin is the only asset with no counterparty risk. And you have to understand that. And if you don't understand it, try to understand it because it's really important to understand that.

God Candle 与 Omega Candle

Tell us about gold candle and omega candle. A god candle is a 10k candle on the daily. Um, and it's actually happened. We've had 10k days in the past. An omega candle is a 100k candle in one day. And we haven't had it yet on the US dollar charts, but we've had it in a few other places. M

I think Iran had a Omega candle, Lebanon has it, Egypt has it in their currencies, but uh it just goes to show like the world is very turbulent right now and it's only a matter of time before you see Bitcoin go up 100,000 in a day and it could be from um 150 to 200,000 or it could be from 200,000 to 500,000 in three days, right? That's the level of uh volatility you should be expecting.

Now, if you go back and look at the the chart of uh the German mark against gold in World War II, the chart is like this over a one-year period up and down. I think that's what we're seeing now. We're seeing massive volatility. And the cool thing is the volatility to the downside is muted because we have corporations buying as a buyer of last resort right now. So the only thing up only thing is up now volatility to the upside which will shock and all people and they'll show that hey we were right.

Yeah I I think in one of your interviews you said we have nowhere to go but up or something along the line. Well there's nowhere to go if you understand everything. Where do you store your money, right? You want to buy real estate and get a Dubai happening where you're stuck, right? Real estate is highly illquid. Even if you tokenize it, it's still like highly liquid because the base asset is physical on the ground stuck in the ground. You could buy gold, but again, it's not that liquid unless it's paper gold, but then it's paper gold and it's an IOU. Same for any other metal. You could buy a stock, but a stock is a wrapper for a company with management and with problems and it can be replaced just as we saw software companies getting replaced by general AI.

And you could say, well, what about investing in Nvidia or something like that or whatever, but there's always something new, right? But a permissionless store of value that is bearer and immutable cannot be replaced. That is it. That's the apex predator of money. Does not get any better than that.

马斯克回归比特币的预言

You have a prediction that says in 2026 Elon Musk is going to come back to Bitcoin. Yeah. What is that about? Well, we know that he knows about Bitcoin. We know that he knows crypto because he likes Dogecoin. And the what prompted that was his friends like the all-in podcast guys, they're mostly in California.

And if you remember a few months back, California was slated to put through a wealth tax and everyone was leaving or actually a lot of people have left California billionaires and Elon runs in that same circle, right? So if they're understanding that their stuff is targeted, that their wealth is being targeted, they have to come to the conclusion that I need wealth stored in something that cannot be seized and that's really Bitcoin. So Elon has to understand Bitcoin eventually.

隐私、Zcash 与"被制造出来的问题"

I talked to people behind Zcash and yes, okay, maybe you can correct their narrative. Why do you think Bitcoin has privacy if we can see every transaction on chain? Bitcoin is not private, but it is pseudonymous. But more what's more likely is I'll pay you with lightning or with liquid and then you can't see because those are private. It's a Bitcoin side chain and lightning is a layer 2 microp payments network and you cannot see any of that stuff.

So that's false right out of the bag. But let's say you value privacy. You want to buy crypto from an exchange and you don't want anyone to know about it. Guess what? The exchange knows and they're reporting it to the tax authority. So you gain nothing.

So all the problems that people are trying to solve for Bitcoin, there are no problems that Bitcoin doesn't solve. There are manufactured problems that people say that Bitcoin has. Like what? I don't know. anything. Pick any altcoin like what Ethereum says that Bitcoin is not programmable. Bitcoin is programmable.

You know, there's nothing much to defend because it's a false thing, but that's what they base their entire value proposition and identity off of something that they say Bitcoin is deficient in when it's not. So you don't see a world where there will be another thing that is similar and that has more consensus. No, because you can only create digital scarcity once. Everything else is a copy of it, right? Like if you try to make a Bitcoin today, you won't be able to launch it organically like Bitcoin and then you don't have the network effects, right? But still like you won't it won't work. It'll be centralized.

为什么是 1.33 而不是 1：供应冲击的临界点

Going back to your earlier um prediction, you said $1.3 million by this year. Yeah. Why 1.3? Just to increase the number a bit. You can't stay the same. It has to go up. I think we're long overdue for a big move up. What has to happen for that to happen? Just time. Time. Everyone is accumulating a massive amount of Bitcoin, right? Sailor has 760,000 something now, right? I don't think there's that much Bitcoin out there that he can keep buying without the price coming up and the structure of STRC lets him buy without any dilution.

So, it's a very powerful instrument and then you have Adam's company BSTR coming. So just imagine four or five companies all doing this.

It's going to go up and then Black Rockck and now Morgan Stanley wants to compete with compete with Black Rockck. Then you throw a country or two into the mix. This is not enough Bitcoin and you'll have a supply shock.

Is there a scenario where it doesn't happen? Like Bitcoin is just stuck at this level and No, because it's scarce, right? Like I think this is the greatest trick that the market is playing on people that there's so much Bitcoin that it's always going to be low. But that's just not true. Bitcoin is so scarce that people can't fathom how scarce it is. There's only 21 million units of this.

And again, this is like something that the masses don't understand. The concept of a finite asset. They're familiar with assets that are kind of scarce, but the total number is unknown, right? You don't know how much gold there is. You don't know how much silver there is. You have estimates. You don't know how much oil there is, but you think oil is scarce. You know, it's going to run out eventually because there's only so many so many dinosaurs that died. So, you know, they kind of have a vague concept of scarcity, but they don't understand absolute scarcity, which is what Bitcoin is.

And when you hit that limit, when there's no free float on the exchanges, when the traders that are, you know, using Bitcoin as this kind of uh free floating asset to, you know, market make or provide liquidity to the market, when that dries up, then Bitcoin will just run.

Why would that dry up? Because if it's so volatile, then it's an interesting thing to trade. Because there's only 21 million Bitcoin, but they're trading the derivatives. They're not I I think there's both. Derivatives definitely will damp it or amplify it, but they need spot bitcoin. So we know there are two to three million BTC on exchanges kind of as this free float and that free float will be eroded. It's just a question of when.

So right now there's 1 to two million two to three million depending on who you ask on exchange worldwide. Yeah. That's not a lot. No, it's not a lot. We have 1 million left to be. Yeah. Yeah. And it's insane. Yeah. So, reality was is going to reconcile eventually and then price will go up because of that scarcity.

个人持币哲学：什么值得用比特币换？

Are you still buying? I don't know. If I had money, sure. But you think retail investors should buy? I think anybody that wants a future should buy Bitcoin.

If you talk about people accumulating as many Bitcoin as you can, what's the endgame? So, let's say I don't know, I have enough, but I still don't feel rich because I can't really sell it because the the strategy is don't sell it, right? So, I can't really enjoy my life now.

I don't think it's true that you should never sell. Like, life is finite. Time is finite for people. So you need to live your life. So this is the purpose of Bitcoin. It is not that you should buy it and never sell it. That's like it's more of a slogan, right? Never sell your Bitcoin.

And strategy can do that because it's a corporation. But at an individual level, you know, it's not like you will never sell your house. Eventually you might sell your house or you might want to, you know, mortgage it or do something with it. There's a lot of things you can do with it, right? And I think what you want is you want that hard asset that you can use when you need to use it.

Bitcoin is just a tool. It's a very good tool, but you should use it because it's money. What you should think about is it's something you can pass on to future generations that does not degrade. Right? If you buy property, you know, you have to repair it and maintain it. You don't know what's going to happen to it in 100 years for your your kids. But Bitcoin is digital. There's no physical form to it. You know, it's still going to be Bitcoin in 100 years.

Okay. All these hardship that we're endur enduring to buy Bitcoin and then what, right? I think people want to know the then what? Well, I think that you have to soften it a bit. So, like I said, never sell your Bitcoin is a nice thing to say. It's a slogan, right? But a better question is is what do you want to spend your Bitcoin on, right? Now that you have such a valuable asset, you have to really question like what am I really doing with it? Am I selling it to buy a car? Do I need the car? Or am I selling it to buy, I don't know, some plastic thing that's going to melt, right? Or am I selling it for a life experience to go somewhere and see something I really want to see?

So, it's really that you have this pristine asset that is generational and is likely going to appreciate massively. It really forces you to think carefully about what you're going to spend, which is not something that happens right now. In fact, like I said, people don't have savings. They're in debt. Most people are in in debt in the world. possibly less so in Asia but more in the US but they're spending their future money because they want instant gratification and instant reward in this fiat world and Bitcoin is the exact opposite of that and you have to question what I'm doing today is it worth it and that's only a question you yourself can answer

关于"砸盘指控"与不需要营销的资产

you reminded me of something so there are a few times when when I talk about Bitcoin and I have comments saying that, oh, don't listen to her. She's just pumping her bag. And I'm just flattered because if you think I can pump Bitcoin, I'm like, thank you. Yeah. Yeah. I I assume people would say the same thing about you talking about Bitcoin. I haven't heard that, but you know, I'm sure I haven't maybe I haven't seen it.

The Bitcoin is the only thing where it doesn't matter if you pump your bag or not because it is sort of pre-ordained where Bitcoin is going. And Bitcoin is the best asset, too. So, it's like, do I really need to pump my bag? It's kind of sells itself.

Mhm. Whereas everything else, they do need to pump their bags. They need a marketing team. Like, this is one of the big differentiators from uh Bitcoin and Ethereum, which was Ethereum launched with a 30 person marketing team. I don't think people know that, but all the if you go back in time and you look at their pitch like the world computer, it's all slick branding and graphics and they like trademark their logo. It's it's all a very well packaged thing to sell to somebody. But every altcoin is, you know, it was made to be sold to get Bitcoin.

But would you say, okay, would you say these tokens have utility or these chains? No. No. any utility can be replicated and you actually see this um like Salana you can say it's a better Ethereum it's faster more performant newer technology would you say it's useful it's it's useful as a transport layer for things but in of itself it's not useful but a transport layer could be anything like it could also be a centralized database and just like you know Salana is better than Ethereum there will be an better thing than Salana in the future and then a couple years later a better one of that. So these are not things you store value in. They're just, you know, it's like transient technology.

So they're in different categories because they're not money. Mhm. But they could be tech stocks. Yeah. Kind of. That's a very generous positive way to frame it. Yeah. How would you frame it? I don't know. A copy a clone tamu bitcoin of Bitcoin. Yeah, but Bitcoin is not trying to do all that. No, it's not. Like Bitcoin has second layer technology that can do all that. Like Tap Routt assets can do that transport layer, RGB can do that. All the Bitcoin related technologies, liquid can do that. Like it can all be done without a token. It's just people think you need a token to do something.

政策、法案与"加密币归零，比特币照样活"

I want to go back to the policy. You mentioned Clarity Act, but I think it's um so chaotic people don't know what's going on. Does that have any effect on Bitcoin and Bitcoin price?

People think it does. They think like when the Clarity Act passes then uh Bitcoin is going to go up. Yeah, I don't really think it has that big of an impact. It has more to do with banking and stable coins than anything else. like the stable coin yield that's the sticking point right now

but I guess everything leads to Bitcoin so if that is good for crypto then it's good for Bitcoin then not really I think crypto can go to zero and Bitcoin will be fine it's a different ballgame

but would you want that to happen because that would be dangerous for Bitcoin because the public will see that as oh cryptocurrency I mean, cryptocurrency does more damage to Bitcoin, I think, than anything else. Like, a lot of these draw downs that we've seen are because of things like um crypto blowups like Terra Luna. Mhm. That had an impact on Bitcoin price. Mhm. FTX, FTT token as collateral, something that came from zero went to zero. That impacted Bitcoin massively. And it changed the perception of Bitcoin, too. Because I remember we were engaging with countries and talking about Bitcoin and then that blew up and then nobody wanted to talk anymore. Yeah. Right.

So in theory, yes, in practice, it's already bad for Bitcoin, right? Scams, fraud, all of it is bad. Bitcoin has to survive on its own merit without dependency on any derivative.

新人入门：从一笔闪电网络小额开始

For a person that's starting today, let's say he's or she is new to Bitcoin, what's the first step?

I would say get a little bit of Bitcoin. Um, you can use Aqua. Um, have a friend that has Bitcoin send you a lightning transaction for, you know, a couple Satoshi's and just experience it for yourself.

I think that's the biggest thing people can't really fathom that you can just receive money with no intermediary because you've been born into and grew up in a world where you have to ask for permission to use money from somebody counterparty some some group your bank Venmo PayPal I know it's popular in Taiwan but somebody's giving you permission to transact and with Bitcoin it just removes that barrier the better way to think about a Bitcoin wallet is it's like a notebook. You you open up your notebook, someone can write a number on your notebook and that's it. It's your notebook.

Why are you doing what you do? Because it's open source. People can download it. Well, how how do you where's the catch? People are like, "How do you make money?" Well, Aqua makes money when you do a swap. if you're swapping from Bitcoin to um a stable coin or from uh liquid to lightning. But the reason why we're doing this is because we think onboarding the world to Bitcoin is the most important thing right now.

I I do think this is a very good time to learn about Bitcoin, especially when you're not affected by all these FOMO and news headlines and stuff. Um, but with Lightning, you can send, you know, hundreds Satoshi's, you know, couple cents.

And, you know, I don't think it's people like, if you have a Bitcoin friend, they'll be happy to send you a few Satoshi's. They're not going to send you one Bitcoin, but they'll send you a few Satoshi's.

给年轻观众的建议：第一性原理与 AI 时代的资产论

Anything else you would like to say to, let's say, a young viewer just starting off? Understand what it means to think from first principles, not just from wealth creation and money, but for everything in life. because that is the only way that you can really achieve success. If you do what everyone else does, you will be average. If you do what the media tells you to do or you invest based off of some guy recommending investments, you're going to end up, you know, average.

You have to think what is meaningful. What are the things happening in the world today? and extrapolate from that for yourself. And there's no other way around it. It's hard work. You have to put in the work and research, right?

Like one thing I think people should be worrying about and maybe they are is what happens to your work in the future with AI and I think it the answer is Bitcoin, right? If you have an asset, you can leverage the asset. You can borrow against it, right? If there's no more work, if work becomes a commodity because uh a computer can do it, then what value do you bring? So you need to accumulate an asset now, right?

If you go back to the boomer age, what is the asset? You have the productive asset of real estate. I can buy a house, not the one I'm living in, and I can rent out that house, right? And I get some income. Well, with Bitcoin, you can also use it too, right? You can borrow against it. You can sell options. You can do all sorts of things with it. But you have to think if the world is moving away from work being valuable, then there's no more valuable skill than having an asset. It's not a skill, but it's like a important thing. Now, not that you know how to do something, but you have an asset.

比特币是给 AI 用的吗？

Talking about this, there's this narrative that says Bitcoin is not built for human is for AI. I'm pretty sure it's for humans. It's not because you know AI agents, you know, do transactions in very micro amount and a lot of that doesn't matter. The you can say Bitcoin is for AIs, but the reason for that is because it's permissionless. The AI is not going to get a credit card, right? The AI is going to use your credit card. You're going to set up a credit card account because AIS don't get credit cards, right? And I don't think they will get credit cards. Um, AIs are not going to have bank accounts, right? It'll be your bank account that you delegate access to. The only thing the AI can really have is a Bitcoin wallet and maybe stable coins. Mhm. The speed and size of the transaction doesn't matter. If you could have a, you know, some digital bank account or digital account, you can have small accounts, small transactions, too. But it's the fact that Bitcoin is permissionless that that's what they'll use because that's the only thing they can use.

So it's just a bonus. It's not made for AI. It just happens to be perfect for AI. Yes. But you know, everyone has to spin it somehow with a new narrative. So they're saying, "Oh, cryptos for AI." It's just a new new angle that people are spinning just like the world computer or something else. But usually the reality is simpler than that.

I don't know what's the best way to describe it. You talk about Bitcoin all the time.

There's no simple It's hard. There's no simple oneshot answer. Like I give you two statements. Okay. Bitcoin is digital gold which means it is a an asset that doesn't have a physical form. The second one I would say is Bitcoin is information because it's just information. Just like why can I have no counterparty risk because it's just information I send around the world, right? I don't need a counterparty to store information. It's just ones and zeros. So I think there's no one statement that encapsulate Bitcoin. You can take these two and you can get pretty far with these two in terms of what it means and the impact on the world.

收尾：如何参与国家级采用

So if I really want to help you with this nation state adoption, what could I do? I think what you're doing now just educate people about Bitcoin and build a community. And if you have connections, engage with government. We're happy to help too, but you can also engage without us. like we go where we're called and people ask for our help because we're sort of um an expert group, right? And we can speak to our experience. We can speak to what other countries are doing, but you don't need us. You can do it fine on your own, but we're happy to help wherever we can help.

So, does it have to be lawmakers or companies or is it just nations? For us, we're focused on nations. So it would be lawmakers and politicians, but companies too are good. Like every segment has a role to play from the individual to a company to a country.

I love what you're doing. Thank you for being here. Thank you. Byebye.
