— I want to start at the very beginning, with your childhood. Can you tell me what kind of kid you think you were?

I really loved Olympiad math as a kid. Olympiad math. I started studying it in first grade. I started studying it in first grade. At first, my parents had me sit in on an Olympiad math class at an after-school program and a Chinese class. I had zero interest in the Chinese class, but I was really into Olympiad math. So I signed up. I kept taking it from first grade through sixth grade, and in middle school too. Then I realized I could do math problems faster than anyone else.

— Back in elementary school, yeah, back then, for example, when other kids were doing mental math, like in second grade, they might not even be finished yet, maybe only halfway through, and I'd already be done. It was the kind of thing I didn't even have to think about.

In today's terms, you'd call it System 1.

— Right, it was all at the surface level, I'd look once and the answer would pop out. And that gave me a huge sense of accomplishment in math. Because I think I'm someone who, compared with other people, learns relatively slowly. How should I put it? When I learn something new, I often need two or three times as long as other people. Honestly, it's still like that now. For example, when I read a piece of code, I need a lot more time than other people to understand it. all of it, the whole thing, the full context. But once I understand it, I can use it very quickly.

This isn't a perfect analogy, but, for example, when I was a kid, we often had to memorize texts, whole passages. Before going to bed, I'd try every possible way just to stumble through the whole thing once. Even with lots of pauses, as long as I could remember it, I'd sleep on it, and wake up the next day knowing it cold.

My thinking back then was, since I needed more time, I had to start learning earlier than others. So back then, I started high school math in middle school, and I'd go straight to my middle school math teacher with all kinds of high school math questions. He was always happy to answer, and I was very grateful. By eighth grade, I'd already finished the high school material, and in ninth grade I started calculus.

— In math, were you naturally just faster at it, faster than other people, while at the same time feeling you learned things more slowly?

I think it was more that I needed longer to build my knowledge tree. Normally, a knowledge tree has a root, then it branches upward, then branches upward again, and again, layer after layer. Say you take a top-level conclusion and apply it to a problem, you have to run through the whole chain and slowly figure it out. But I might just build a shortcut, go straight there, and skip that whole process.

— So from early on, you realized you might be smarter than other people?

No. I never felt that.

— No. So you're saying you just found it interesting. Then where did the core motivation to learn math early come from?

I wanted to invest in the future. I wanted to invest in my own future.

— So in eighth grade, you already realized that learning high school math early was an investment in the future.

Right. It would help with my future studies. Rather than wasting time grinding through exercises now, I'd rather learn something useful for the future, where the payoff later might be bigger.

— Was that something your parents told you?

No. My parents didn't really care. They just didn't pay much attention to how I studied.

— But I'm still really curious. Why was it, in middle school, that you already had this idea of investing in the future? Because, I'm sure you had room to spare then, right? Why not just go play? Why not basketball or video games? Why were you thinking I should study high school math and all that?

First, because I was interested in it. I just felt elementary math was too boring, and, how should I put it, slightly more interesting math was a lot more engaging.

— Then where did that interest come from? Was it innate, your interest in math?

I guess you could say that.

— Or was it that, back in elementary school, you got a sense of achievement from it, and that got you hooked?

You could say that. There was an initial sense of achievement, an initial reward. Then slowly, you start building out your math skill tree, unlocking it bit by bit, because each point gives you positive feedback. Then you unlock another point, get more positive feedback, and you just keep going down that path. But with something like basketball, I tried it, but I didn't get much positive feedback. I kept getting destroyed by my roommate. And as for sports, I practiced taekwondo. Taekwondo was okay, but once it came to sparring, I'd just get beaten up.

— So instead, it was that kind of positive feedback: you were good at it, and you didn't mind it, so gradually it became an intrinsic interest.

Right.

— So it wasn't really your family environment, it was something you developed on your own.

中学编程的起点 / The Start of Programming

— That's the math side. You started learning high school math early in middle school. So when you got to high school, why did you become interested in programming?

I started programming in seventh grade. Yeah, in seventh grade. Programming started in seventh grade. That was because when I was moving up to middle school, I went to Shidai Middle School. It was a private school, and since it was private, they had a programming class. So I just signed up for fun.

— That makes sense. There are informatics competitions in high school, but programming in middle school didn't help much with admissions, right?

Right right. Not much.

— Was that class something you found yourself, or did your parents find it for you?

My parents thought I might be interested in it. So I gave it a try. And I thought it was pretty good.

高中 OI 与高考升学 / OI and College Admissions

— All right, let's talk about high school. In high school, what I'm actually most interested in is your experience with OI provincial selection and NOI. Could you tell us about that? Why did you get into competitions in the first place?

Because there was college admissions pressure. If you didn't do competitions, then for someone outside Beijing, getting into Tsinghua or Peking University was extremely hard. Basically impossible.

— So you did OI because of admissions pressure?

Yes yes. Mostly because of admissions pressure. I'd already been doing math competitions and OI before that. Then in tenth grade, I spent some time on math competitions. I got some decent results. But if I wanted to go further, to the provincial selection in math competitions, our school didn't have the foundation for that, and it was hard to self-study. So I gave it up. And beyond that, for math problems above the provincial-selection level, I really struggled with them. Because I hadn't started that early, and studying them felt exhausting.

— You mean even starting high school math in middle school still wasn't early enough?

Starting competition math in middle school might have been early enough. I just didn't start that early. So after weighing it up, I gave it up.

— So you went into OI, the informatics competition track, and focused on that. Could you walk us through how admissions through informatics competitions usually work, what steps are involved, starting from provincials?

First there's NOIP, uh, not the beginner division, the NOIP Advanced Group, which is the provincial qualifier. It's usually around October. After that, each province has its own selection criteria for choosing a provincial team for nationals. As for the provincial selection, Fujian had two rounds. One during winter break, and another in April. After the selection, in May or June, there was usually a Tsinghua or Peking University summer camp. You could choose one. You could get some admissions concessions there. At least that was how it worked then. Then in July or August came the NOI nationals.

— So tell us, through that process, what was it like for you? The first round, second round, third round? NOIP was okay, right?

NOIP itself was okay, because I'd been doing it since middle school, so it was fine.

— And provincials?

As for provincials... In tenth grade I didn't even get past provincials. I basically couldn't solve anything. Hardly anything. Then in eleventh grade I could do a little, mostly by hacking things together. And I used some heuristics. For example, there was a problem called Minimum Two-Circle Cover. And I got 70 points on it. At first I think I had the top score overall, because I solved that problem, and that got me onto the provincial team.

But after making the provincial team, the next step was Tsinghua's summer camp. That went pretty well. I got a 60-point reduction offer. An unconditional 60-point reduction. And a conditional top-tier cutoff offer.

— How does that work?

It basically adds 60 points to your Gaokao score if you didn't get direct admission. Then, if that adjusted score cleared Tsinghua's cutoff that year, Tsinghua would admit you. And the conditional top-tier offer meant that if, at NOI, you placed in the top 150, which I think was the silver-medal cutoff, then you'd get the top-tier offer. As long as your Gaokao score cleared the top-tier cutoff, you'd be admitted unconditionally. But if you won gold, you didn't even need that. You'd get direct admission.

— Did you get that conditional top-tier offer at NOI?

Unfortunately no. Because this is another embarrassing story. I was the lowest-ranked person on Fujian's provincial team. Fujian had only one bronze medal that year. That was me. Everyone else got silver or above.

— Did that setback affect your college plans at the time?

It did, quite a bit. Because at that point, in the second half of eleventh grade, I hadn't been studying my regular school subjects, and I wasn't sure about my future either. I had no idea what I'd score on the Gaokao. There were earlier examples. Some older students, for instance, ended up needing all 60 bonus points, with not a single point to spare. I thought, what if that happened to me too? That felt pretty risky. I was really afraid of that uncertainty. But after thinking it through, I felt like I probably wasn't that bad. So I gritted my teeth and chose Tsinghua's 60-point reduction.

— So your other option was something safer?

The safer option was right there at NOI, signing a top-tier-cutoff offer from Shanghai Jiao Tong.

— So was that a really hard decision?

Not too bad. Mainly, on the mental side, I was pretty upset at the time. And the uncertainty about the future really scared me. But with my family's encouragement, I still chose it. I went with Tsinghua's 60-point reduction.

— So after OI was over, after the competitions ended, by senior year you stopped touching any of this and went all-in on the Gaokao?

Not really. I'd still sneak back to it. Like, for example, I was really into code optimization. I had unusually high standards for it. I would even get to the point where on an iPad, since Safari doesn't have a compiler, I'd just use the iPad keyboard and type the code straight in. Then I'd submit it directly. No compiling. And that whole stretch really trained my thinking. Because it required a complete grasp of the whole problem, and if something went wrong, you had to locate it fast. It really sharpens your reactions. I felt that was great training for me.

— But for you, you didn't really need that kind of training then.

Right right. It was just something I was genuinely interested in. For example, in OI there's this thing called constant-factor optimization. Like, with a given algorithm, even if the time complexity is still O(n^2), in practice there's still a constant factor in front. And in OI, the judging reflects that. For example, submissions are ranked by runtime on the test cases. The fastest one comes first. And if two are equally fast? Then they go by code length. So I'd optimize both metrics. Grinding that was really fun. Not that it was useful, but it was fun.

— You were built for this. You really liked it, right? It actually made you happy.

Yeah.

清华本科：开源作业，打破信息差 / Tsinghua: Open-Sourcing Homework

— Let's talk about Tsinghua. If I asked you about starting undergrad at Tsinghua in 2016, what would you say stands out now as something especially memorable from your first year or two? What's the first thing that comes to mind? If there is one.

I open-sourced all my homework. People had different opinions about that. For example, some older students were pretty against it. But I felt I should do it. What I did was take everything I'd collected, all those ancient homework sets, and old course materials, and open-source it all on GitHub. I left out anything with copyright issues, but everything else went public.

— Why?

Because I thought we should break that information gap. That information gap if you're trying to survive at Tsinghua, is incredibly useful. But I think everyone should have equal access to that information. Even now, if you go back and grab any CS undergrad and ask them, do you know who donated the new CS building? Even if his name is on the building, do you know who he is? No. Do you know Jiayi Weng? Probably. After all, everyone survived off my homework.

[Laughs]

— That's more useful than donating a building. [Laughs] This is where the comments should be flying by: "+1 if I've used or seen your homework." People should be posting that right now. But why? Why did you open-source your homework? Why is that fundamentally about information gaps?

I wanted to close that information gap. I didn't want the people after us to keep scrambling like that. A lot of people just aren't great at gathering information, but they're actually very capable. And really, if you can give those people a fair shot at equal access to information, they can do a lot better at Tsinghua.

— So by equal access to information, you mean knowing things like previous years' assignments, or what upperclassmen say the key exam topics used to be, that kind of information, and homework, and assignments the professor never said couldn't be shared.

Otherwise, you can easily spend ten or twenty hours on something, and still not dare ask the TA, so you just don't know what to do. Then you end up spending huge chunks of time just going down rabbit holes, and it actually doesn't help your learning much. The payoff is tiny. So I felt that back then, if I open-sourced my own homework, I could probably help people like that and help a lot more younger students later on have more time of their own to do what they actually wanted, instead of, you know, doing all this kind of life-wasting stuff.

— So you really pulled it off. Your homework has basically become a widely circulated GitHub repo at Tsinghua by now.

Yeah.

进入朱军组与图形学的诱惑 / Joining Prof. Zhu's Lab and the Graphics Detour

— Other than that, I also noticed you probably started sometime in your first few years getting into research and all that. When was that?

Probably sophomore year. After that, I asked some older students which labs they recommended. At the time, an older student gave me three names: Professor Jun Zhu, Professor Jie Tang, and Professor Peng Cui. I just assumed they were ranked by how impressive they were. Maybe that wasn't actually true, but that's how I took it then.

— So you thought the order meant something?

Yeah, I thought the order mattered. Then I applied for a CS Academic Rising Stars program, and I think all of them accepted me. So I chose Professor Jun Zhu. And in sophomore year, I first met with Professor Jun Zhu and asked what directions there were. There were three: Bayesian methods, GANs, and reinforcement learning. Those were the three options. At the time, I actually wanted to work on GANs. I thought image generation was really interesting. But I didn't know which one was GAN. So I picked RL.

— What do you mean you didn't know which one was GAN?

I really knew nothing back then. I was a total beginner. I just stumbled into RL by accident. There was nothing special about it. I just thought RL was the thing for images. That was it.

— So it was a pretty random choice.

Yeah exactly. But once I got into RL, I realized it was this game-playing thing. Mm. I thought it was pretty interesting. So I kept going.

But actually, my original thinking was that there were three big directions I really liked. One was artificial intelligence, or AI. Another was graphics, and the third was cybersecurity. Cybersecurity. I thought hackers were really cool. So when I was in college, in my spare time, I also did a lot of cybersecurity stuff. I even fixed quite a few bugs in the campus network. For example, for example, there was this thing I fixed: a loophole in transcript downloads, this one-cent transcript thing. A transcript normally cost 10 yuan. A senior and I found a bug. And then you could get one for one cent, or even download it for free. Later on, after trying it a few times, we fixed the bug. Yeah. We reported it directly to the school's academic affairs office.

— So you really enjoyed the hacking process. Cybersecurity is one kind of hacking. What about the others?

As for graphics, I felt that was hacking too, just hacking reality. From your own perspective, you can build the scene in your head, and it makes you feel like God. Like you're the creator. Back then, graphics was what interested me most. Because when I was in middle school, I saw a movie called TRON: Legacy. It was a sci-fi movie, and the visual effects were incredible. It completely blew me away. And I thought, if one day I could make effects like that, or build a virtual world like TRON, a world of my own, then that would be it for me. I'd feel complete. So I felt graphics was a way to make that happen.

So, I became really interested in graphics. Then in my sophomore year I took graphics, and because I liked it so much, I ended up spending a huge amount of time on the final project. And I think I got one of the only two A+'s in the class. I even came up with a new algorithm that could reduce the number of iterations to converge. And I used a ton of compute to render an ultra-high-resolution image, I think it was 16K. And before that, no one had rendered a 16K image before. And it had zero noise.

— You were so into graphics then. Did you keep going with it later?

No. Because I felt that if you want to do research, it's better to stay focused. You can't split your focus.

VizDoom 与对 RL 研究的失望 / VizDoom and Disillusionment with RL Research

— Because by then you'd already joined Professor Jun Zhu's group?

I'd already joined Professor Jun Zhu's group. There was a project on VizDoom. It's basically Doom, a game from the '90s. There was a competition then to use a neural network on a fixed map to beat the game. There was a spawn point and an end point. You had to kill monsters on the map, pick up medkits, avoid obstacles, and finally reach the end and clear the level.

— So this was basically your first RL project?

Yes. We won first place. Yeah.

— So back then, you really enjoyed doing RL research?

No.

— Why?

I really didn't enjoy it. Because in that setup, first, the task and the environment were far too narrow. And you had to overfit like crazy. And then basically, you had to use all kinds of tricks to keep training from collapsing. And even if training didn't collapse, you still had no idea how to tune it right. It really was alchemy. Compared with CV, the tuning was maybe 10 or 100 times harder. And it was all black magic. You had to rely on very ad hoc heuristics to avoid corner cases.

— So that was when you realized RL research back then was mostly heuristics and tuning.

Yeah exactly. Actually changing the algorithm wasn't the core issue. And because the tasks back then were too narrow, and most of the time, they just didn't work. The tasks back then, the first one was of course VizDoom. And after that, was it things like Atari, DeepMind Control Suite, and MuJoCo? I think MuJoCo and Atari were both easier than VizDoom. Yeah, VizDoom was one of the harder ones.

But in a case like that, for humans, it's a simple task, but for RL, it's an extremely hard task. Because it requires a lot of prior knowledge. You need things like knowing what counts as an obstacle. For humans, that takes no thought. But for RL, well, or for AI more broadly, that's a completely different level of difficulty.

— Right. So even back then, you already had a clear sense that the environments were too narrow, and the algorithm wasn't the bottleneck.

That's what you realize once you do it: there are just too many challenges. Right, so I didn't really enjoy the research process. So I deliberately shifted my focus to how to help this kind of research move more smoothly on the process side. So I by my senior year, was thinking about building a set of RL infrastructure or a small RL library to make it easier for people who wanted to grind in this field. That way, I felt this was my strength. I was very good at this more software-engineering side of things. I could refactor code, make it easier to use, and improve the user experience. But when it came to how to tune these things, I had a visceral aversion to it.

— It wasn't that you were bad at it. You just didn't like it and resisted doing it.

Exactly.

Mila 暑研：被 Bengio 派去做 NLP / Summer at Mila with Bengio

— And that's why you built Tianshou in your senior year. We'll come back to Tianshou in a bit. Right. So you started doing research in your sophomore year, working on VizDoom, and by junior year, you were applying for summer research positions?

Yes.

— What was that period like for you?

Applying for summer research was mainly for my senior-year applications. And then I sent a ton of cold emails, but nothing much came of them. In the end, my advisor helped connect me with Yoshua Bengio, and then I went there. Yeah. Actually, that summer research stint wasn't all that successful either.

— That was at Mila, right?

Yeah, it was at Mila. That was in 2018. No 2019. The summer of 2019. In the summer of 2019, first of all, in 2019, Yoshua won the Turing Award in April or May, I think. And I had applied before that, so that was fortunate. And later, Yoshua gave us The task he gave me and the postdoc supervising me was to build something like an MoE. MoE. There was no MoE yet back then. Right. First, it had nothing to do with RL. Second, it was pure language modeling, just Transformers. And there was no MoE back then. We didn't know what MoE was or how it worked. But he had this MoE-like idea: there'd be a router that could choose different paths, and he asked me to implement it.

— So he hired an RL person to do NLP.

Yeah. I thought that was weird. And I had to spend I'm not quick at learning new things, so it took me a long time to get up to speed on Transformers and NLP. Then I tried to cobble something together. I did get something working, but it didn't work very well.

Looking back now, for something like that to work, first, you need compute. You need strong engineering. And you need to scale it up. Back then one person had only a few GPUs. You couldn't scale something like that. So there was no way it would work. Even if the direction was right, you still couldn't make it work.

— But don't you feel like somehow fate was pushing you toward the technical path OpenAI is on now? You started with RL, then Yoshua had you do NLP. Those two pieces, somehow, before OpenAI, you'd already experienced both.

If you really want to put it that way, sure, you can force that interpretation. Ha ha. But that's just hindsight.

— But back then you obviously couldn't see the future, so what was your view of NLP after that summer?

I felt NLP tasks were too scattered. And back then a lot of people were thinking about how to apply RL to language model training, because that problem wasn't solved yet. Because with Transformers, if you ran them directly in RL, they would collapse very easily. And nobody had figured out how to stop that from happening. Later it turned out your environment had to be pure, and your reward had to be a very strong reward signal.

— What do you mean by a pure environment?

For example, text-only. Pure text. So the less pure setups people used before, you mean things like using Transformers for Atari decisions, for example by turning images into embeddings, or taking the Atari game's internal state and representing it structurally, then feeding that into a Transformer and running it. And the Transformer was randomly initialized.

At the time, first, I didn't have enough context. I didn't understand the field well enough. So I really struggled with it. I think even if I could go back now and help my past self, it would probably still be the same.

— You mean back then you were in a situation where there was just no way to make it work?

Right right. There wasn't enough compute, and back then our understanding of MoE wasn't enough either. And put it this way: even if we'd had what we know now back then, we still couldn't have done it. The engineering wasn't there. And the compute wasn't there either.

— Right. Given the resources we had then, you just couldn't do it.

申请季的失利与心态调整 / Application Season Setback

— Then after the summer research ended, you went back to Tsinghua to prepare applications. What state were you in then? Was the pressure intense?

I really wasn't in a good place. Because the summer project hadn't worked out. And classmates around me, like people who went to Stanford or did summer research at CMU, had all gotten a lot done. and publish papers. They all had first-author papers. Usually, you needed a first-author paper. Otherwise you'd get screened out. It was really cutthroat. There was no way around it. So, after my summer research, I actually only got one recommendation letter, and I did not even know what it said. So, I really was not sure.

— How did the applications turn out?

I applied for PhD programs, but I only got master's offers.

— Because at the time I was only a sophomore. When you were applying, I think I was just a freshman or sophomore. And every year on Zhihu there were posts like, how did your 19 Fall or 20 Fall applications go? That was when I saw your answer. It really stuck with me. Back then, when I looked at your profile, it mentioned a strong rec from a Turing Award winner and all that. But then I saw your results were not great, and you did not get into a PhD. I remember thinking, wow, the competition is that intense. For you personally, going through that application cycle, did it feel like a setback?

A little, maybe. But looking back, it was fine. I mean, first, in that environment, I really was heavily influenced by that immediate environment. A PhD was seen as better than a master's. That was basically the atmosphere at Tsinghua, that whole status hierarchy.

— Yes exactly. If you are inside it, it is hard to escape that influence.

And even abroad, it was treated the same as in China: a PhD was better than a master's. Because people have this ingrained belief that the higher the degree, the better, and the better it is for your future. But a lot of the time, that is not true. It really depends on what you have actually done.

And back then, I was definitely a little disappointed. And it took me a while to readjust. Later on, though, I think I had always wanted to do things that made me stand out.

— So at the time, you were probably trying to break out of that fixed evaluation system.

Yes exactly. Yes. I have always felt that GPA is not the only metric. In college, you need to find a standard that fits you, and work toward that standard. Make yourself happy. Even if you rank first in GPA, you can compare yourself with past cohorts, with other departments, or with other schools. There is always someone else. GPA does not really tell you how good you are. I think this is the better way to see it.

From the demand side, for example, if your end goal is to get a job, what will employers care about? They will care more about relevant experience. They will not care that much about your GPA. It does not mean much to them. If you have experience that closely matches the role, that alone can outweigh several years of work experience, even if you are a new grad. Because the point of hiring is to bring in someone who can do the work.

— So you realized that as an undergrad?

I realized it later, during my master's.

— But as an undergrad, you were still under that system.

As an undergrad, I tried to break out of it. But only partly. I had not fully broken free.

— What part was that?

I realized that I needed to create my own evaluation system instead of using one handed to me by others. I adopted an unofficial one, one my advisor, Professor Hang Su, suggested. Basically, I still remember this line: for evaluating a CS student, in his view, there were three metrics. One was papers. One was competitions. And one was GitHub stars, in the triple digits. And I really think that mattered because it gave me a completely different perspective: in a different arena, I could actually do things in open source. And that let me it gave me something that set me apart. Mm-hmm. That was better for my long-term growth. Instead of just spending a lot of time chasing GPA, just grinding for GPA.

— I feel the same way. I think when I was an undergrad, I also realized very early on that GPA, especially undergrad GPA, was one of those things that three or four years later, you wouldn't even put on your resume. But during those three or four years, undergrads still have to put 80 or 90 percent of their energy into it, because whether you're looking for a job, or applying abroad, or trying to get into grad school, it's all about GPA. How did you deal with that contradiction, between something that's hugely important for three years and then, three years later, completely unimportant?

I'd put in the bare minimum. By that I mean, I'd spend the minimum amount of time to get it where I needed it, to hit that bar. Anything beyond that, I wouldn't put in even one extra minute.

— Right, not one extra minute. Good enough is enough.

Good enough is enough. I mean, it's actually pretty simple. You just do the math. Like, before finals, figure out how many points you already have, and then you can decide whether that exam is worth studying for.

— So you're saying if your GPA is already high enough, you'd rather just get a 60 in that class and not care?

Yeah. That's how I am. I've always been like that.

— Right, and then, maybe 60 isn't quite enough. Like, an 87.

An 87 is a B+. And I'd already be very satisfied with that.

Tianshou 的诞生 / The Birth of Tianshou

— Right. Do you think you broke free of that, because a choice like going abroad, was that driven by that evaluation system, or by that whole atmosphere?

No. At the time, the atmosphere was already basically that studying in China was better than studying abroad. Around 2018 or 2019, by 2019 that was already true.

— Right. At Tsinghua, roughly only about 20 percent went abroad. But our class had even fewer, because of COVID, only about 5 percent went abroad. You were in the December 2019 application cycle, right? So you were probably still waiting on offers when COVID hit. And then, say, once you got an offer, the U.S. consulates shut down. Right. And you still chose to go abroad.

Yes.

— That was a hard choice, because back then there was COVID, the pandemic, and international politics were also very turbulent, and your own future was tightly bound up with both. Right. With that much uncertainty, what state of mind were you in?

I think I was probably more focused on what was right in front of me, for example, working on Tianshou at home, building tuixue.online at home, building that visa status checker. Basically, I was really just redirecting my attention so I wouldn't spend every day fixating on grand international narratives, and would focus on what was right in front of me. That probably helped me feel calmer.

— Okay. Then let's talk about two projects from that period. The first is Tianshou, and the second is tuixue.online. Let's start with Tianshou. You actually already touched on it, Tianshou. You said the motivation was that the RL scene back then was too overfit, and innovation in RL algorithms wasn't that important. Instead, what mattered was infrastructure, or just solid implementation. Tell us why you built Tianshou.

The reason was first of all, back in December 2019, I already felt, deep down, a strong urge. I thought I'd already written a lot of RL experiment code, so why not pull it all together and make my own experiments run better? That was the starting point.

Then in February, in February 2020, after the January break, I took a look at RLlib's code, the RLlib under Ray. At first, I was thinking about using RLlib and seeing if I could tweak it to support my own experiments. I spent a month on it. It was too complicated. Far too much abstraction. And RLlib alone was already pushing a few hundred thousand lines. That was completely unacceptable. I had no idea how to make the changes I wanted. So I decided to forget it. I'd just reimplement it from scratch, tear it down and start over.

— Right, start over. I'm really curious: when you were doing this, was there any practical calculation behind it? Because a lot of people then wanted to make some 'blah blah policy optimization' NeurIPS paper.

No, I didn't want to publish papers. I thought publishing papers was completely meaningless.

— Tell us why. How had you already broken out of that whole system?

First, I already had papers. I felt that one more or one less made no difference to me. And I already had enough for applications. And I had competition results too. I guess I did have a repo with a few hundred stars, like the course repo I built, but that wasn't really a serious one. I still wanted a real one in the hundreds.

— Right. Okay, so with Tianshou, after looking at RLlib, you felt it wasn't usable, and you weren't interested in papers.

Right.

— But you were very interested in open source, in a solid, useful project that could help move the field forward.

Very interested in that.

— How hard was it to build Tianshou back then?

Very easy. Two weeks.

— Just two weeks?

For the first version.

— What algorithms did the first version have?

A lot of algorithms. You just take the paper and implement it. If your abstractions are right, then implementing an algorithm is basically under 20 lines. Less than 20 lines per algorithm.

— Then how could you build a first version in two weeks when RLlib ran to hundreds of thousands of lines?

I think maybe its initial design had some problems. And then maybe once more people got involved, everyone kept contributing code, and in the end the whole project gradually decayed. So you built it all yourself? Yes.

— So this kind of efficient solo iteration, where you get the top-level design right and then execute,

Right.

— you think can actually be more powerful?

Yes. I think code, or really any project, gets most of its value from consistency. Consistency. If a project is consistent from start to finish, then it's a good project. A lot of project decay, comes from inconsistency. Say you have two people, or ten people. With ten people, everyone writes a bit of code, but nobody really knows what the others actually wrote. And some assumptions just don't get passed along in time. That leads to things like the same code getting copied and pasted everywhere, or other bad patterns, so the codebase just keeps bloating and starts to rot.

— Tianshou kind of took off right away back then. You could say it was pretty popular. What do you think Tianshou got right?

I think it captured what users needed. Researchers, or really the people doing hands-on research work back then, all had the same need: was there something easy to use and modify, an RL framework they could use right away? And Tianshou's codebase is compact, and the abstractions are pretty decent. At least if you dig into it, you'll find that if you modify this part, it'll work correctly, and it's the only place you need to touch. And also, if you want to support your feature, this is the only place you need to change. In other words, if someone wants to change something, the design already makes it so they can only change this spot.

Mm-hmm exactly. And for something like this, the usability is pretty good too. It has basically every algorithm you'd want to run.

— So back then, for the PyTorch version, did you write the first version by yourself? And later did it gradually become more of a team project? Or did it just become open source, with the lab maintaining it?

It just became open source. There really wasn't anyone in the lab maintaining it.

— Right, we just open-sourced it directly. Then how did you keep Tianshou from running into what you just described, where once too many people work on it, it stops being consistent and starts to decay?

Early on, I had enough time, so I could handle everything myself. That obviously kept it consistent. Though looking back, that wasn't ideal, not for long-term growth, but it was enough back then. Later on, my focus shifted, because once I started working, I didn't really have time anymore. So I basically handed it over and transferred maintenance to people in the community. And if they thought something made sense, they'd do it. As long as there's one person making the call, it should stay consistent.

— Looking at it now, five years into Tianshou as a community effort, do you think it's decayed?

A little bit, definitely a little. Because the context I was working from and what my successor had weren't exactly the same, there was some mismatch. So he ended up rewriting some of my code. The two of us weren't actually that consistent. So there was still some decay. But I think in the long run, that's acceptable.

tuixue.online 与公益情结 / tuixue.online and the Charity Drive

— Yeah. The second project, Ha, I used it too. tuixue.online. Why did you Weren't you back then... I was a sophomore. But later, during my summer research, right, I also had to book a visa appointment. And I needed the earliest available slot. So, why did I build something like that?

Because I needed it myself. It was basically the same as with Tianshou. I felt like my need I looked all around, and I didn't know of anything that could meet it. So I hacked together a visa appointment tracker. Otherwise I had no way to do it. Because back then you were probably in that period where one consulate would close, then another one would close. Yeah exactly. You needed to know in real time where visa slots were available.

And I figured if I needed that, like, at the beginning, I wanted to collect some ancient upperclassman's old homework. I figured a lot of people probably needed that too, so I put it out there. Then when I needed to check visa appointments, I just wrote a scraper for visa slots and open-sourced it. and let everyone use it for free. That got really popular. I checked last night, and it had over a million total visits.

— It was already over a million back then. So by now it must be over ten million.

Probably over ten million, right? But it shut down a long time ago. Once COVID passed, there just wasn't as much demand anymore. So and the U.S. consulates upgraded the website, so the old scraper stopped working. And I didn't have time to rewrite it.

— Right. So it served its purpose. In a way, neither of those projects was very strategic. Right. The first one wasn't even for applications. You built it after you'd already applied. You easily could have and even though you couldn't travel during COVID, you could've spent that time doing something more relaxing. Yeah exactly. And tuixue.online was the same. You could've just stopped after making it for yourself. So you still have this really deep, very strong urge to create something you see as useful, something you yourself need, and then share it with everyone.

Yeah. I want to do things that truly have impact. Even if they lose money. Like, doing public-good work. I think projects like Tianshou and tuixue.online are public-good projects. Yeah. Completely nonprofit. Working on projects like that makes me feel deeply fulfilled.

— So compared with money, impact fulfills you more.

Yeah.

"被多少人记住"的人生指标 / The "Be Remembered" Metric

— How long have you had this drive for impact?

In high school.

— Why did it become about impact in high school?

This may sound a little strange, but when I was in high school, one day out of nowhere, in my senior year, an idea suddenly popped into my head. Maybe it was this: sometime in the future, future me sent a message to past me, and that made me realize something. Like, if life is a game, then your final score is how many people remember your name. That's it. That's it.

— What does that mean?

It means more people need to know your name.

— So one day in high school, you realized that this really mattered.

I wanted that after I die, at the moment I die, as many people as possible would remember my name.

— Even today, you still use that as your metric?

Pretty much.

— Then I want to go a bit deeper. Why does that matter?

I didn't think it was that important back then, but I tried it a few times and realized it really is what I want. If what I make can benefit other people, then first I feel good about what I made, and second, other people affirm it too, and I get positive feedback from them.

— Can you equate that directly with fame?

Not really. Because some kinds of fame or prestige like landing some position, do make you well-known, but that kind of prestige in other people's eyes may not benefit them that much. It could even be negative. What I want is more like this: Of course, I don't think you can be good to everyone. That's very hard. But I can at least try, to be good to the people around me and do things that matter to people.

— So the metric is doing things that matter to people and having more people remember you.

Right.

— If I were to challenge that view, I'd say, why not just live happily yourself? Why do you need other people to remember you? This urge to be remembered, to have lots of people use something useful you've built, where does it come from? Are you afraid of being forgotten?

No. I think life can also be seen as an experience. Since you're already here in this world, why not make the most of it instead of wasting the journey?

— Yeah, but why does making the most of the journey require other people to remember you? I find that tension really interesting. Because the first thing you said was you wanted to break free of external standards, like GPA, like the gaokao, or whatever else. People saying a PhD is better than a master's. But on the other hand, the internal standard you've set for yourself, that intrinsic standard, is still recognition from other people.

But it's not official recognition. Not recognition from an existing system.

— Yeah. It's consensus.

So what you want to escape is the existing system and its rules of evaluation, but what you really want is heartfelt approval from each person.

— Yeah. So that kind of validation can show up as GitHub stars, or page views on tuixue.online. It can also be your Google Scholar citation count, or your OpenAI model's daily inference volume.

Right.

— Do you worry that your standard could become a new orthodoxy? Maybe the older generation judged young people by GPA, and when you become the older generation, you'll judge the next generation by impact.

That's just the standard I hold myself to. I wouldn't hold others to it.

— So you don't think your standard is universally applicable.

Right. Right. So you don't like being pushed by external standards. Right. Then is there a chance your own standard could start pushing you?

Not so far. If that ever happens, you can change it. You can change your own standard.

— So even though you have your standards, you're not a slave to them. You have your own impact metric, but you're not trapped by it.

For example, there was actually a long period when I didn't have any open-source project. and I didn't feel that it really bothered me. I don't worry about that. I think OpenAI models are the best form of impact.

— In a sense, you could say that. Right. Mm. Because an evaluation system is a very simple, direct tool for filtering people quickly. So the way society evolves naturally converges on that strategy. So it won't change anytime soon. Even if, under that system, in most people's eyes, you're one of the winners and beneficiaries, you still don't like it.

Right. It should be more personalized.

CMU 与求职：DeepSeek vs OpenAI / CMU and Job Search

— This was at Tsinghua. And then during that rough stretch of the pandemic, you got into CMU and were supposed to start in September 2020. By then the first COVID semester had passed, and classes were online, so you just stayed home and took classes remotely. For a full year, right? But even then, did you have to think about jobs from day one?

Yes.

— What was that process like?

It went like this. I was pretty careless at first, and I applied to I think 18 companies. In the end I only got offers from Google and OctoML, Professor Tianqi Chen's company. And at the time I thought, if those were my only two offers, I'd definitely choose OctoML. I didn't want to go to Google. because it didn't seem that interesting.

— What do you mean by 'interesting'?

You'd be a cog in big tech, doing work you don't really like, like frontend or backend work. Later I thought it through more clearly, and kept interviewing. Then I got an offer from High-Flyer. High-Flyer said they were starting an AI Lab, which later became DeepSeek. Right, I got the offer then. Right, and then but I didn't go.

— You didn't go. Then you went to OpenAI. So looking back now, with perfect foresight, the choice in front of you was DeepSeek versus OpenAI.

Yes.

— So even the High-Flyer offer back then wasn't for quant, it was for AI.

The High-Flyer role was RL infra. Right. If I hadn't had other offers, I would have chosen High-Flyer.

— By other offers, you mean besides OctoML?

No. I mean things like OpenAI and NVIDIA too, I think. NVIDIA. NVIDIA was also an RL systems role. FAIR was too, actually. But FAIR ultimately, for process reasons, rejected me.

— OK, so your options were High-Flyer, OpenAI, NVIDIA, and TikTok.

Right. And maybe FAIR too.

— And among those options, you ultimately chose OpenAI. How did you make that choice?

I think... What I want to emphasize is this was before ChatGPT.

— Right right. So at the time, you really didn't know ChatGPT was coming. Right exactly. So how did you make that choice?

First, OpenAI had long been doing RL. OpenAI and DeepMind were basically, in RL back then, the two best research labs. And I felt that if I could get in, that would already be huge. Actually, back then, I didn't really think I'd get in. So just getting in already felt amazing. And I also felt that I'd have a chance to see how the world's most cutting-edge research is actually done. Instead of being in some little workshop, like at a university, where a few PhD students cobble something together, without any real methodology for running the project. That just felt wrong to me. So I wanted to learn what a real methodology looks like for doing research in industry.

John Schulman 的面试 / Interview with John Schulman

— So when you joined OpenAI, was it straight into John Schulman's group?

Yes, John Schulman hired me.

— He interviewed you?

Yes, and I'm very grateful to him. I mean, I'm very grateful he gave me that opportunity. Even after he left, on the day he left, I was sad the whole afternoon. I shut my laptop and did nothing.

— So he clearly thought highly of you.

Yes.

— Why? Did you ever ask him?

Yes. He felt that, first, my GitHub looked very strong. He also agreed with that evaluation logic. Right. And for him, that was one signal in his hiring framework. Nobody had really noticed that before. Also, bringing in someone with strong engineering skills helps any project.

— Any interesting interview stories? How did you impress him?

Maybe in the final round, he gave me a problem. It was a very end-to-end one, and very open-ended. He gave me three hours, and I finished it in two. I built the whole thing from scratch, and got it done. Then I showed it to him and walked him through it. A bug did come up during the demo, but I fixed it on the spot, so it was fine. Anyway, maybe he thought I had solid engineering skills and at the same time, I was pretty capable overall. So he hired me. Also, he only used that interview question on two people. One was me, and I think the other was Andrey Mishchenko, the guy working on Codex now. Mm right.

— And you both passed?

Yes, both of us did. So the pass rate was 100%.

— Exactly. [Laughter]

工业界 vs 博士：为什么不读 PhD / Industry vs PhD

— Alright. We'll get to OpenAI in a second, but before OpenAI, I'm really curious: did you consider doing a PhD when you were job hunting?

No.

— Why not?

Once you talk to people in industry, you realize that doing a PhD, if you want to go into industry, is basically wasting your life. A master's can absolutely be your springboard, and still let you meet the bar for industry roles. For example, in your master's, or even as an undergrad, you can build up enough citations, and do some standout projects so you can compete directly with PhD candidates. Then figure out what else you can do to make them choose you with a master's instead of another PhD. I think knowing what makes you different is crucial.

— So you figured out pretty early that your future had to be in industry.

Yeah. Because I felt that academia, whether teaching or being a professor, was too cutthroat and not what I wanted. And then I'd have to go raise funding for projects. And on top of that, I'd rather not do that. It just felt way too restrictive.

— For example, let's say we're a company that's hiring. We have candidates with master's degrees and PhDs. Do you think those two paths train very different abilities? Because a PhD is more about developing academic skills, right? How to write a good paper, tell a complete story, make the figures look good, and present it well. Do you think that matters for a company? It probably helps in some ways, but compared with someone like you, with really strong engineering skills, how would you compare those two skill sets in today's AI era? Which one is more valuable?

In today's world, better engineering is clearly more valuable. But at that point in time, it really wasn't so clear. So what I did back then was try to cover both.

— I mean, you'd published papers too, and your open-source infrastructure work was popular too. And your engineering was strong. So why do you think it's so obvious now that engineering comes first?

Let me quote a colleague of mine. Basically, he was an RL PhD, and he built a famous RL framework. What he said was: Teaching a researcher to do good engineering is much harder than teaching an engineer to do good research.

Here's why: For the kind of frontier exploration research labs are doing right now, what really matters is whether the infrastructure is correct. If the infrastructure is right, then it comes down to how many iterations you can do per unit time. Because with ideas, you can always get them by talking to people, and something useful will come out. Then you validate it. As long as you can validate well, that basically becomes your research work. You don't need to do that much original thinking yourself. The deeper thinking is often done by people who've been in the field a long time, like Alec. He's been in this field since the GPT-1 days. So he probably has very strong research instincts. So when he thinks through a problem, it's more useful than an average PhD doing the same. So you just talk it through with him.

Ideas are cheap. What matters is within a given amount of time, how many good ideas you can validate. So you need correct infrastructure, correct results, and fast iteration. Exactly. And current PhDs often don't have that ability, or at least it isn't their focus. Because for them, it just isn't that important. I think the current academic training system is about finding a good research direction. But people in companies can have that too. If you've worked in this field for a long enough time, you develop research intuition. Then you start to see what's good, what isn't, what to do, and what not to do. So ideas are cheap. But strong engineering skills, building the infrastructure correctly, and iterating fast, once you have that, even agents can probably do the validation. Yeah.

Mm. From what I've seen, every company's infrastructure has bugs to some degree, and the teams that fix bugs, the ones that fix more of them, end up training better models. So maybe Llama can't match GPT because Llama has too many bugs. Maybe. I don't know. But that's the kind of thing I'd guess.

— So you knew pretty early on, and pretty clearly, what the real issue was. For the whole pipeline to work, the key isn't algorithmic innovation but the right hyperparameters, the right infra, and a good system for fast iteration

Yeah, and then I wasn't that interested in research around hyperparameter tuning It had no appeal to me at all. I preferred selling shovels.

— You like having a playground where you lay the foundation and let other people play let them publish the papers and not worry about it yourself and maybe get your name on the paper too

And then you realize that my name is on a lot of OpenAI model releases and that's because inside OpenAI I built the entire post-training RL infra

— So for the whole post-training RL infra, you were the core contributor

Yes. And then because before that, everyone used that whole post-training RL infra to train RLHF models so every time there was a major release, every time a major model shipped, my name had to go on it

— So you could say there's a bit of you behind every OpenAI model because you like selling shovels

Right and I was the shovel seller closest to the customer because RL sits at the very top of the infra stack It's a high-visibility spot. Right, because if you're too low-level, then your name won't get as much exposure If you build a data loader or storage layer, it doesn't have the same visibility but RL is something everyone wants

— Exactly. That's smart.

I thought about that too before, how I wanted my career to develop And at the time I set another metric for myself I wanted to maximize how often my name appeared on the OpenAI blog

— You're really good at designing your own reward function.

Right so once you define that reward, what do you need to do? First, you focus on infra because if you do individual research, it doesn't scale But if you do infra, everyone uses your work so that's how you scale up Right. And RL infra was something I was good at. So it was a perfect fit

给后来者的建议 / Advice to Newcomers

— So basically once you got into Tsinghua, RL infra became your main track Right. Would you still recommend that today? You've already made that choice, but a lot of our podcast audience might still be undergrads or master's students and they're still deciding between industry and academia How would you help them think through that decision, especially in 2025, under the current landscape? In the long run, I still think academia, in its current form, needs to be restructured. But right now, for ambitious young people who want to make an impact like you have, should they go get a PhD or enter industry as early as possible?

I still think entering industry early is better. Because if you do a PhD, you have no idea what the world will look like when you finish By the time you graduate, the paradigm shift may already have happened and then you'll realize what you've been working on may not matter much If your objective is to join an AI lab, then the first thing you need to figure out is what kind of people AI labs actually need If they need infra people more, then do more infra work even if you don't have a PhD because what matters more is whether your experience matches what they need and whether it's actually useful

— So what kind of people do AI labs need most right now?

I think it's still infra. Right. Infra is a bottomless pit. I mean, research-wise, there are only so many people with real research intuition since ChatGPT came out, there are only a handful of people in this industry who've been at it for, say, more than three years And so the bottleneck right now is still infra can you scale up, and by how much? meaning, in a given amount of time, how many iterations you can run. And that directly determines your productivity.

— That sounds like not a very PhD-friendly environment.

Yes. This also points to a gap, and I think it's something both of us have felt deeply. Because RL research in academia mostly just overfits to Atari and a few MuJoCo tasks, tuning them back and forth, just to see who scores higher at 100K. Right. But industry doesn't care about that at all. What industry does is use RL to solve real problems.

Exactly. Once I realized that, then, in August 2022, I started winding down Tianshou. Because Tianshou was still aimed at those toy benchmarks. And so I felt I should spend more time on something more meaningful, like, inside OpenAI, getting the RL infra right. So most of my effort went into maintaining it or developing the RL infra.

— Honestly, that view of yours was quite ahead of its time in 2022. It definitely wasn't the consensus. Right. Why didn't you close that information gap then and write a blog post telling me to stop?

I was worried that if I said it out loud, OpenAI might call it a leak or something, just in case. I mean, it wasn't that confidential, but you know.

— Haha okay.

进入 OpenAI：什么是 RL，什么是后训练 / Joining OpenAI: What Is RL?

— Let's talk about OpenAI. I'm really curious. We've already touched on it a lot, but at OpenAI, you're one of the very few people in the world who worked on ChatGPT, GPT-3.5, GPT-4, GPT-4V, GPT-4o, GPT-4.5, and GPT-5 as a core contributor on all of them. Some people contributed to the first half, others to the second half, but you were there the whole time. Your main contributions, I'd sum up in three words: reinforcement learning, post-training, and infra. Right. We'll get into those technologies and the stories behind them in a bit. But I want to start with the first question. It might be easy to answer, or very hard to answer: What is reinforcement learning?

If there is feedback, if you can model an environment and get feedback from it, that loop is reinforcement learning. Using that feedback to keep improving.

— Exactly. Then the second keyword is post-training. What is post-training for a large language model?

Back then, post-training wasn't even a thing yet. At the time, the team was just called RL, and there was no real split between pre-training and post-training.

— So when you first joined OpenAI, that wasn't a thing yet?

Right. But when you first joined OpenAI, was ChatGPT already the main focus?

No. When I first joined, there was just John Schulman's RL team, working on a follow-up to WebGPT. WebGPT used a GPT-3.5 model for browsing. But for that browsing setup, if you used 3.5 directly, the results were predictably bad. Because browsing required tool calls. So we took a step back and first focused on getting the user experience right. The one thing we really had to solve was chat. And chat could be handled through instruction following, using RLHF.

— So when you joined, GPT-3.5 already existed internally?

Yes. But the PPO pipeline back then was very hard to use. So what we used most at the time was GPT-3.5 SFT, and we iterated on that several times. GPT-4 only came later. And later, for GPT-4, Greg wrote dedicated infrastructure to make GPT-4's training infrastructure support RL training.

— So you joined in July 2022? Right. Was all of OpenAI already all-in on GPT-3.5 then? It was still a few months before launch, right? Right. Internally, could you imagine ChatGPT becoming this massively successful?

No. At the time, were you able to I mean, you could test the model internally, right? Yes. Did you realize this was going to be game-changing? No. I didn't feel that way then either. Because I felt like I could see a lot of obvious shortcomings. I mean, the first time I used it, I thought, okay, it's a talking model. That's about it. Then after using it a few more times, I realized it could help with some coding problems, but only to a point. It still couldn't solve that much for me. So I used it a few more times, and I didn't really want to keep using it. The problems it could solve for me were limited. Because you already knew about it beforehand, and you saw it improve gradually, so it didn't feel that sudden to you. But for example, when I later showed it after it launched, to people around me, they felt it came out of nowhere. And that was what I didn't expect.

Yeah right.

ChatGPT 上线：意外的爆款 / The Accidental Launch of ChatGPT

— So back then, across OpenAI as a whole, was everyone all-in on ChatGPT in July 2022?

No. Only our group was working on it. You can look at the ChatGPT blog post and scroll down. There's a contributors section. John Schulman should be listed first, and then the next twelve names, ending with Shengjia Zhao, that whole stretch, starting with Barrett right after John Schulman, from Barrett to Shengjia Zhao, was everyone in our group.

— So when was it that you realized your work at OpenAI had really made this blow up?

Probably when ChatGPT launched. I was at NeurIPS at the time. It launched on November 30. And in December, just a few days later, I realized that the people around me were all talking about ChatGPT. And they found it really useful. It even crashed OpenAI's servers several times.

— It was kind of like what happened with tuixue.online. The tuixue.online servers crashed several times too. That created this kind of organic demand and word-of-mouth effect.

Basically, everyone became an organic promoter. They'd promote the product on their own. And that's definitely something worth investing in, something worth putting effort into.

— It actually sounds like from the time you joined OpenAI to do this, even inside OpenAI, getting something like ChatGPT out wasn't really something anyone planned.

Right. It was probably a series of half-accidental, half-inevitable factors reacting together.

Yes, and when we launched ChatGPT, it was really just to see whether we could collect some real-world user data. And after that, we might shut it down five days later if nobody used it. I mean, we expected maybe ten or twenty thousand users at first, and then it would taper off and that would be the end of it. But the actual curve was exponential.

— Right.

OpenAI 的第一印象与组织文化 / First Impressions of OpenAI

— All right, that was ChatGPT's sudden breakout success. I'm also really curious: what was it like when you first joined OpenAI? What was your first impression of the company?

It felt like a bigger research lab. It actually wasn't as process-driven as I expected. But there were a lot of people there with very strong research instincts, who could point out the direction, and then you just executed.

But after Barrett, Luke, and Liam came in, the three of them from Google, and joined John Schulman's RL team, our group changed. We started bringing in Google's productivity playbook and started iterating. Google really is exceptional. There was a chart showing iterations per unit time and success rate, and the two were directly proportional. So the more iterations you can do per unit time, the more your success rate rises linearly.

— Right, that's basically an RL curve too. Because RL is constant trial and error.

Right, it's just you keep trying, and after enough attempts, you eventually reach your goal. A lot of cases in life are basically RL.

— So back then, the productivity playbook you brought in was really a philosophy.

Yes. Meaning, let's not start by chasing brilliant ideas or brilliant algorithms. Let's build solid infra first. Let infra take us from 30 iterations a week to 300 iterations a week. Yeah roughly.

— I saw an interview where someone asked Sam Altman what made OpenAI successful. He said OpenAI can produce major breakthroughs because in a small team with extremely high talent density, mediocre performance isn't tolerated. Do you agree with that?

I do. Because when talent density goes up, then unexpected things can emerge spontaneously. But in a different environment, say everyone is average, people just stay in their own lane and finish their own piece. And then things start dragging on.

— When you joined, OpenAI was only a few hundred people then, right?

I was number 280.

— You were number 280. Now OpenAI must be in the thousands.

Over 3,000 now. Over 3,000, so ten times bigger. In three years.

— Do you think OpenAI can still keep that small, elite team style of making hard-core innovations?

I think the odds are lower, but not dramatically lower. You can always carve out a small team to focus on specific research.

— What has OpenAI's leadership done that's been interesting to avoid that problem? Or preserve small teams, high talent density, and hard-core innovation? For example, simplifying the org structure, and canceling unnecessary meetings altogether?

Well, basically, I think org structure matters more.

— What counts as an efficient org structure for hard-core innovation?

Smooth information flow.

— How do you mean?

For example, if a decision gets made today, it reaches the people below without distortion. And if there's new progress below, that reaches the top without distortion too. Otherwise, the people making decisions at the top and the people doing the work below may be thinking in completely different directions, and not pulling in the same direction.

— How does OpenAI achieve that kind of information flow?

First, people like Sam and Greg, they had dedicated people for that. For Sam, he used to have a dedicated research assistant to help him stay on top of the latest internal research progress. Greg, of course, on the infra side, across the whole infra stack, he had been involved in almost all of it.

— Right so they're both extremely familiar with the tech.

And what matters is staying technically sharp. At minimum, they need to know how far the current work has gotten, what's new, and what it's useful for. So this has to be the top one or two leaders themselves, who are willing to dive in all the way into the details and understand every capillary of the company. I think managing a company and managing a codebase are actually very similar. It's about consistency. If you're not consistent, if things aren't aligned, then you end up like a person who... For example, a brick-built person whose torso moves but the feet don't, that looks very strange.

后训练实战：RLHF 先在 GPT-4 跑通 / Post-Training: RLHF First Worked on GPT-4

— Okay, let's talk about post-training. Let's start with GPT-3.5.

But actually, the PPO for GPT-3.5 wasn't that dialed in. I got GPT-4 working first. Because GPT-3.5 was using the old infra then, and the new infra had just landed in August 2022. So I first used the new infra to get the first version of PPO working, and I ran it on GPT-4. That should have been around September 2022.

— Wow, that's really interesting. So when GPT-3.5 came out, GPT-4 already existed internally. And RLHF was first made to work on GPT-4 before it worked on GPT-3.5.

Yes. But in practice, other teams had already helped us avoid a lot of pitfalls. So we reused some existing pipelines wherever we could. But for the main pieces, the core stuff, like how to train the reward model, we did that ourselves. We also collected the data ourselves. And when it came to infra issues, we handled those ourselves too.

— So back then, to get RLHF working, what were the key challenges and breakthroughs?

I think the main thing was figuring out how to measure what the performance actually looked like. Because nobody knew what good performance was supposed to look like.

— You mean after training a lot of checkpoints, you couldn't tell if it was actually better?

Right, because a single reward can lead to reward hacking. The reward might just saturate, and turn into a flat line. It slowly creeps upward and then flattens out. But the real situation is, if you looked at human reward, it might go up first and then slowly come back down. That's reward hacking. So you had no way to know which checkpoint was actually better than the others. So choosing checkpoints was a skill in itself.

But we didn't spend that much time on it. We just built a bunch of sampling-based evals to see what each benchmark actually looked like. But really, a benchmark was just a number. And if it crossed some threshold, that was enough. But even then, you couldn't really call it good, or call it bad, because every time you ran a model, the eval variance was huge. There was a lot of noise.

— So how did you solve that in the end?

In the end, you had to inspect it yourself and interact with it a few times, and then see, for example, what your own experience felt like. Then get a few more people to look at it and have everyone vote.

— So evaluating RLHF still comes down to human feedback?

Yeah exactly. That's the only way. There's no other way. Back then, the technology just wasn't there.

大模型 RL Infra 的挑战 / Challenges of Large-Model RL Infra

— So this was basically your first time building production-scale infra. Right. How do you think this kind of large-scale RL infra for large models compares with what you'd built before, like single-task RL systems such as Tianshou or toy-task RL infra?

I think the difference is huge. Because for toy tasks, the bottleneck is the environment. The model is very simple, so whether you're training or sampling actions, it's all very cheap. But with large-model RL infra, the model is huge, while the environment is very simple. The environment is just a prompt. But when you're sampling from the model, you have to think about how to do it efficiently. And when you're training, you have to think about efficiency too, because just feeding in the prompt as the environment might take only a few microseconds, but running one pass of inference or training could take hundreds of seconds or even thousands. Especially if you don't have many GPUs.

— So for future models, compared with GPT-3.5... Compared with GPT-4, what new challenges does that bring for RL infrastructure specifically?

I think it's still mainly about performance, or scaling, how to scale it up. How do you use more GPUs, and get higher throughput?

— Right exactly. So that involves more than just RL. There's also model inference, so it's more end-to-end, and you have to go deep into implementation details to do end-to-end optimization. So what you do is really an intersectional role. There's the RL side, you need to understand RL, and you need to understand ML systems, and also how language model inference works. You need to understand all that, right?

Yeah. You probably won't be in that kind of role, to be honest, when you're just a student in school.

— Yeah and that role really sharpens you, but it's also exhausting.

And later on, there was a period when I was really worn out by the work. I even ended up in the ER. I was overworking myself so hard, my head was hurting, and I couldn't take it, so I went to the ER to get checked. But the ER doctor said it was nothing serious.

— How intense was the workload then?

I'd wake up and start debugging, or dealing with issues, all the way until I went to sleep. Seven days a week. Maybe six days a week on average. Right? But after a while, you realize that's not sustainable. So first of all, your health comes first. You have to make sure your body stays healthy. So now I've built a habit of running 3,000 meters twice a week. But back at Tsinghua, I used to fail the 3,000-meter run in PE, and I really couldn't run 3,000 meters. But now I do it on my own, because I've realized how important it is.

— I think I'm really envious of what you've done at OpenAI over the past two years, because what you get to explore is something that 99.99% of people out there, including researchers and infra engineers in this field, aren't even qualified to touch. You have this incredible advantage: you get to work on the most advanced models and optimize them, and every day you're exploring the unknown. And you know that whatever you're exploring, you're probably the first person in human history to find that answer.

But I think what I do is pretty trivial. I feel like my work is mostly day-to-day maintenance, and it doesn't really require that much raw brainpower. Yeah, it's more like you just need to do the work well and do it right. And direction matters a lot. As long as you're headed in the right direction, just do what you believe is right.

RL 的下一步突破 / The Next Breakthrough in RL

— Do you think RL for large models still needs a breakthrough? Does it still need a major breakthrough, or is the paradigm already there, and we just need to scale up the infrastructure?

I think there still are. Again, you can't use the current state to predict what happens next. From here, anything could happen. There could be a new paradigm, maybe a new RL paradigm, or maybe a new pre-training paradigm. Any of that is possible. So every day you have to face unknown challenges.

But having said that, I also think I'm just lucky to be in this position. If you replaced me with anyone else, as long as they had the same context I do, they could probably do the job just fine. So yeah, I don't think this is something only I could do. It's just that if you swapped in any normal person, they could probably do it too.

— You're too modest. Looking ahead five to ten years, what do you think is still left to explore? Where will the biggest challenges be? What's the bottleneck in current model capability, and what kind of breakthrough do we need to see?

I think at the current stage, we still haven't fully scaled it up. Let's first wait for it to hill-climb gradually, as large-scale RL experiments play out, and see how far the performance can go. Then we can see what still needs to be done.

— So what you mean is, not scaling up isn't because compute is lacking, it's because you haven't exhausted the current approach yet. First, you push the current methods and the available compute to the limit, then see how far that gets you.

And there are still a lot of infra bugs.

— Even in your current infra, you're pretty sure there are bugs.

Right. You can't say it's 100% bug-free. I mean, it was built by humans, after all, and anyone can make mistakes. Also, you still have to fix that stuff. For example, once the team gets bigger, the context becomes inconsistent, and everyone writes weird things.

— So for future large models, plus reinforcement learning, in the post-training pipeline, where do you think the biggest bottleneck will be?

I think the bottleneck is bug-fixing throughput in infra. Throughput. How many bugs can you fix per unit time, and how many iterations can you do per unit time, how many of those iterations are actually correct. Everything else is secondary. This unlocks everything else.

— Yeah. Whether it's the algorithms or the environment, if you fix all the bugs, you might not need to change the algorithm at all. It will just work. So how do you improve infra efficiency? What kind of architecture do you need, what kind of people do you need, and what kind of resources do you need?

We're still figuring that out. Right. What I'm working on now is, I'm actually no longer in the very core role, but I think I should be doing something more important. So, my team is refactoring OpenAI's internal infra and building the next generation of it.

— So with each generation of infra, do you tear it down and rebuild, based on what you know now, with a better top-level architecture, and then build it again like you once built Tianshou, or is it more small patches and fixes?

Right now it's a full rebuild, because the previous generation is over three years old, and it's accumulated a lot of problems. So we're hoping to use a new infra stack to clean up a lot of the old technical debt, and within a given amount of time, give researchers better iteration speed.

— So researchers wouldn't be involved in the infra-building process? They might just provide requirements.

Right. But actually writing the code and doing distributed training, that isn't their job. Exactly. They might just flip a flag and be done.

— That sounds like OpenAI researchers might be the first kind of job AI replaces.

Yeah. [laughs] I think so. Basically, it comes down to how many experiments you can run per unit time. Researchers mostly just come up with ideas. Right ideas. And ideas are cheap to generate. How many can one person sitting there come up with? [laughs] And even idea generation itself can be modeled by AI. Right. And next, I think it replaces researchers first, and then infra engineers, and then everyone's replaced. But sales probably won't be replaced as easily, because sales is still about persuading someone to pay, and the person on the other side is human. AI may not be able to persuade people that well. That probably still depends on person-to-person communication, which matters more there.

多模态与 Agentic RL / Multimodal & Agentic RL

— Okay, we just talked about text-only GPT-3.5, and then multimodal models. What about agentic RL post-training? How different do you think that is?

Not in any essential way. There's no fundamental difference. It's really the same thing. You just add a few tool calls in the middle, and maybe a bit more environment interaction.

— Right, the environment. That's where the change is. So compared with standard LLM RL post-training, agentic RL post-training doesn't really introduce new challenges?

Right. Because fundamentally it's the same thing.

AGI 的定义 / Defining AGI

— What's your personal definition of AGI? Do you think we've reached AGI yet? If not, or if we're close, what do you think we're still missing? Can pre-training plus RL post-training get us to the AGI you have in mind?

There's a joke inside OpenAI: Ask 15 people, and you'll probably get 20 definitions of AGI. Right right.

And then, the definition I used before was: if it can complete 80 to 90 percent of the tasks I personally find meaningful, then it's probably AGI. As of now, I don't think we've reached it yet. Because right now, personally, at least from my perspective, I still wouldn't trust it to directly modify my infra code. Because that's very out of distribution. AI infra accounts for almost none of its training data. And with AI infra, the verification feedback loop is too long and too expensive. So right now it still can't really touch that area.

— Good for you. Sounds like AI won't replace you anytime soon.

But anyway, before Strawberry came out, internally we'd already been using Strawberry for a while. Back then, everyone thought, 'My job is going to be replaced,' or 'let's just write a bunch of spaghetti code,' and later on, Strawberry will clean it up for us once it's trained. Looking back now, maybe a year or two has passed, and it's still the same. The spaghetti code is still there. It doesn't really change anything.
