On the vision-model side, it started with humans. Three years ago, teams would sit all night, watch videos, annotate: "That's what's going wrong — this is what the kid's doing." Reinforcement learning with human feedback — not from kids, from people watching to train on how kids learn, what's effective, anti-patterns, what to coach for.

The first month back three years ago, August — we'd watch videos, then guides would just tell kids the next day. That closed loop: generate content → kids engage → standardized test scores → are kids really learning this material?

十四、游戏化：番茄钟、环、特权

How does the kid see it? Anonymous parent post talked about hitting minimums, weekly scores, daily scores — a gamified sense of progress.

At the end, you're giving them a measure of how much you have to do and how much you want to do. Goal-setting part.

番茄钟（Pomodoros）

One change we're making for next year: Pomodoro — a 25-minute segment for each subject (math, science, language, reading — any order). During the two-hour morning session: "I'm going to spend 25 minutes in that."

The system gives you lessons. You do them to mastery — highest standard possible. You have rings — like the Apple Watch — that close. Like the Apple Watch. If you did it at high accuracy (over 80%, mastery), your ring closes. Boom.

Low accuracy means you're guessing, screwing around, not learning. 80% accuracy, mastery, no mass practice upfront, spent the 25 minutes — ring closes.

解锁下午

Kids have to do their two-hour sessions to unlock their afternoons. Privileges come around.

When you come into Alpha — first day transferring from standard school — you go into what we call "standard school": literally sit at a desk. "Your job is to become a two-hour learner." You learn the skills to learn in two hours instead of six. Kids: "I'm all into this. Let me do it." They learn to engage in apps correctly. If I spin in my chair all day, I'm not going to be a two-hour learner.

As you achieve — "I hit all my rings this week, finished my lessons" — you graduate to a two-hour learner. That unlocks privileges. Bean bag with your computer instead of a desk. The phone booths you see in Silicon Valley — we have those in our schools. Famous in middle and high school because kids voluntarily go in them.

My daughter is a talker — "I can't sit at the communal table, I won't get anything done." She'll say, "I'm going into one of the booths so I can get it done." Kids learn where they learn best.

AlphaBucks 经济

Different gamification: team goals, individualized goals. Our Brownsville campus — biggest motivator for youngest kids is the petting zoo. If the whole level exceeds academics for a week, they get a petting zoo. Those kids would do 10 hours of homework.

Can you talk about AlphaBucks — for kids who want money?

We have a whole economy. You do academics, earn alphas, spend them. Money is the controversial one — "that's terrible, horrible." Let me give examples:

  • Kid earns money for academics — parents don't like it.
  • Kid earns money for academics to fund their passion project (the Broadway musical flights) — parents like it.
  • For this audience: fund their financial literacy investment account. Middle schoolers crush academics → fund an investment in Robinhood for Kids. Teach them investing with real money they earned (loss aversion matters). Parents love it. 50% penalty for early withdrawal — parents like that too. Teach them to save.

$100 for 100 实验

This is super controversial. Everybody hates it. If you care about academics, this is the single best thing you can do.

The number one issue in academics — starting at middle school — is the reason kids struggle is prior material. Scary stat: the median American kid in high school goes up one point on a 300-point MAP scale in four years. They don't learn anything.

Saul Khan has great videos — when you push kids up having only learned 70–80% of curriculum, think of it like Swiss cheese — building a foundation with holes. Eventually it collapses and you can't learn anymore. That's algebra. The problem isn't the algebra — it's prior knowledge.

So I went to seventh graders: "All of you can get 100% on the Texas STAAR (Texas standardized test)." They're like, "No way." I said, "I'll give you a $100 bill for a 100 on STAAR — 100 for 100." They're like, "Still impossible."

"There's a catch: any grade level." STAAR starts in third grade. They take third-grade, get 100, get $100. "Can I do fourth grade?" Absolutely. They get 100, $100. Fifth grade — they get 85. "Do you want the AI tutor to generate lessons so you can get 100?" "I can do that in a week. I'm in." Then sixth grade.

For literally $400, we can do two really important things: hole-fill (go back, fill all holes, put them on a foundation), and — second — change how they think: "I can get 100 on a Texas STAAR."

Fewer than 10% of Alpha parents believe kids can get 100 on a Texas STAAR. Because in the time-based model, only GT kids got 100. In a mastery-based model, everybody can — it's just a matter of effort, not IQ.

If you take Alpha stats, 90%+ of kids in the school a year are like, "I can totally get 100 on a Texas STAAR." Our K-6 building in Austin gets more 100s on the Texas STAAR than a school district of 100,000 kids.

If you make everything 100, everybody thinks it's too hard. Say "You can pass at 90 but 100 for 100." The AI tutor tells you which ones you missed, was it careless or didn't know it, asks if you want the lesson. You get to mastery. We spend $20 grand per kid in this country (Alpha is $40,000+). For $100 or $400, you could literally change how everybody sees their capability and themselves.

女儿的 $1,000 赌注

Kids have hang-ups. High standards, high support — that's what guides are doing, breaking through.

My daughter will probably kill me, but: when she got to middle school, top 10%. She's like, "Dad, my older sister is the academic one. I'm top 10%, that's good enough. I'm not top 1%." I said, "The one thing I know about you that you care about more than anything — you love to shop. A thousand bucks. I bet you a thousand bucks you can't get to top 1%."

She fills her Amazon cart with $1,000 of stuff. She'd just look at it in the morning and go grind. My wife and I had family discussions — "You're paying for academics, very high standard, is this motivation bad?"

She got to top 1%. She sits down with my wife: "Mom, I know you didn't like us doing this — but you need to understand what it did to my view. I never thought I was as smart as my older sister. I now realize I can do anything."

十五、规模化：技术、渠道与资本

How are we going to scale this? What are the biggest barriers?

技术还未就绪

On the technology side — it's not ready yet. We have a whole team trying to make it ready to scale broadly.

The Timeback engine: we still have hallucinations. It's AI. In the Alpha environment (super safe, kids love school), high school kids take a screenshot: "AI's acted up today." It's fine. You release that into the wild — "AI robot tutors terribleness." So we have a burndown chart.

We hope to have betas between August and December this year. Next year the platform will be out.

分发渠道

Homeschools — millions of parents. We have a test with 50+ homeschoolers. The problem: technology is 10%. If you're at homeschool, is there a motivational model? Our test — we're not getting 2X in two hours with homeschool kids. Every test we're building in motivational models. Next year we'll have a homeschool version delivering 2X in two hours.

Schools — we'll have a platform. But you have to rebuild your whole school from the ground up. Can't just tack it on. Public schools: they have a 10× harder job than me. As a product guy, you have the most impossible job. My job is 10× easier because I control everything.

My vision: I'm going to spend the next 20 years of my life on this. I'm going to need all 20 to get everything in public schools spread.

For sure, entrepreneurs need to build new schools. Timeback platform covers the two hours. You crush the afternoon. Example: Texas Sports Academy — Texas just passed school choice, billion dollars ESA vouchers. This group is building micro middle schools for D1 athletes across every school district in Texas. Afternoon: local coaches running a school for D1 aspirants. Timeback for academics. Their goal: if you view them as a school district of 1,000+ schools, they'll be the best district in Texas academically — tens of thousands of kids.

A lot of famous professional athletes would love to start a school like that.

GT 学校

Gifted and talented school — kids who love academics for academics' sake. My oldest daughter is like that — she read calculus books for fun in high school. There's a set of people who love it. These kids aren't learning 2X in 2 hours — they're learning 3X, 4X, 5X.

When we get all the guides together: "How do we juice love of school? Raise it?" GT guides: "More academics. Third power hour." All other guides: "No. More life skills."

There should be 500 GT schools around the country built on Timeback. "We're going to be the last humans smarter than AI" — math olympiad, robotics, writing novels.

资本主义加入

Every kid needs a different school type. Scaling paths: micro schools, entrepreneurs starting schools, retrofitting — public schools probably last on the adoption curve.

The third thing: the business model. Capitalism enters the chat. We have to show Timeback — here's a platform, you can build great businesses on this — to attract great talent. It's a trillion-dollar market with no good products.

We're in the cash burn phase, burning hundreds of millions building this out. To an entrepreneur: everybody says don't go into education — can't make money, it's terrible, failed. Parents will absolutely pay for great products. A trillion dollars for a relatively bad product. Build a great product, build a great customer base — doesn't have to be a money-losing nonprofit 100% supported on donations (which is the world today).

Part of the problem: everything has to be nonprofit. There are good charter schools out there — their gating factor is "I'm waiting for someone to give me $40 million so I can build a campus." 3,000-person waitlist. Versus: build a really good business model — open 500 of them, because capitalism will provide capital.

十六、怀疑论者与父母教育

Let's adopt ultra-skeptic mindset. What's the hardest to answer skeptical point?

Best example: we haven't done this at scale. Easy for you to say — you have all this money, small selection effect size. Absolutely. There's seedlings of data here.

Bigger issue: education is hard because you have to define the product, and your customer bases have very different needs. Parents do not agree on what the purpose of an education is. That's why public school is impossible.

In Austin, we have three schools that failed standardized tests — proposed to be shut down and move kids to better schools academically. Parents: "I don't care about that. My oldest daughter went to that school. The seventh-grade teacher transformed their life — they don't really learn math, but she transformed their life." Pick that — don't want academics.

One of the things I did not realize going in: how few parents actually care about academics.

产品市场契合度枢轴

Three years ago I'd say, "If your kid engages with the app, they'll learn twice as much. 2X learning." Parents: "Why are you pressuring him to learn 2X? He's fine." They don't want it. Hundreds of parent meetings — I was slow on the listen-to-the-customer side.

We switched to two-hour learning. Same meeting, same parents, same Johnny: "I'm trying to get Johnny out of here in two hours. But he won't watch the video, and he can't do the workshops until he does." Parents: "Johnny, why don't you watch the video? Don't you want to do the cool things?"

Positioning matters.

Every parent wants stuff differently. Mackenzie has lunch with GT families and sports academy families. Sports academy mom: "My kids come home sweating so much, I literally throw them in the shower fully clothed." GT parents: "What is wrong with you?" They're like, "GT had a chess tournament — when they came home we just continued the tournament."

We need entrepreneurs who are domain experts. Want to build an entrepreneurship school? Investing high school? Our New York campus will have more financially oriented workshops; our San Francisco one more tech oriented. We need to bring entrepreneurs and capitalism, create all parts of school choice.

社会层面的问题

Once there's this tablet and it's 2 hours a day — what is your education system going to be? How do we want our kids to spend their day? What does society want to say? There's no point in having them in a classroom for 6 hours a day. That's gone.

Back to my three things: kids must love school (because they only need two hours), high standards, high support, do awesome things, learn life skills. That's the vision. It is hard to execute.

To anybody in the audience thinking about this: the last three years — I've had a great career, made plenty of money — there's nothing more fun, exciting, and rewarding than working on education. There's nothing more important for society.

十七、Prodigy 与游戏化学习

Tell us about Prodigy and using games to deliver this.

People have talked about gamified learning forever. We strongly believe in this. When you talk about scale — we have physical buildings, guides, expensive footprint to drive motivation. The engine, if I get kids to go through it, provides the learning. So how do I get engagement?

The people who know how to engage kids more than anything: video game developers. TikTok and video games. That's absolutely a scalable way. On this tablet, there will be video games that are totally engaging — kids will go through and learn everything they need to know. When Timeback launches, there'll be a section where we have a team using AI to build video games on top of the Timeback engine.

Candy Crush 数学流畅度

Take fluency. Schools have stopped teaching multiplication/division tables and vocabulary lists. Memorization has gone out of style — really bad. People don't like the grind; engagement is the hard part.

Last week I saw: think of a game like Candy Crush, but teaching math fact fluency — super fun, engaging, high-quality game. Kids play this and learn all fast math facts. That fluency will do more to move up math capability than anything imaginable. Highly scalable.

AAA 游戏团队

We've invested $100+ million getting some of the best video game designers in the world. They're down in Austin, building a video game on top of the two-hour learning engine that they believe will rival any video game on the planet. The difference: parents will be happy to let their kids play it — and get their kids to top 10%.

Right now everything motivational is physical/in-person. 2026: how do I take parts of the motivational model into software?

Example test case: "If I had a training center giving you extra health, increasing your chances to win, how long would you go to the training center? The training center is just math problems or academics." Kids: "Extra health? I do 10 hours in the training session." Remember — 20–30 hours for an entire grade level.

The next big video games will be built on an academic engine. The old ones aren't based on learning science, mastery — kids don't engage enough, won't grind through. You need both: a learning engine plus the world's best video game.

Back to getting entrepreneurs: old video games are too competitive. Do educational ones — parents will pay way more money. The ARPU on a video game getting a kid to top 10% academics? 10 million moms in America would pay $100 a month.

视频游戏学校 → 课后辅导

One school was a video game school — do your two-hour learning, video games in the afternoon. Product-market fit: parents like, "I'm not sending my kid to video game school." Hard push. Pivot: "What if your kid came to you and said, 'Mom, Dad, I want after-school tutoring'?" Parents: "I'd love after-school tutoring."

Tutoring and tournaments. After school: one hour in the app, then an hour of tournament gaming. Working with big streamers and video game players hosting events. Versus parents pushing kids to Kumon — kids hate it, confrontation. Build an environment they love: "I'm going to crank the app, especially because it gives me extra health in the video game that I'm going to play in this huge stream with other kids."

十八、Joe 的个人故事：Trilogy 与 ESW

I would love to just hear a little bit about you and your background. Most press about you is from the '90s — youngest self-made Forbes 400 member — then you went into the wilderness.

成长与斯坦福

I grew up in a business-oriented environment. My dad worked for GE. I was learning what gross margin was when I was 12. As for schooling — I didn't like school. I'd get in trouble. Almost got suspended and kicked out of high school. Didn't graduate college — "What am I... I can't sit through these classes."

Academics is one piece of a complex bundle. Stanford is an awesome school even if you don't like the lectures — because everything else around it. When I was at Stanford: "Steve Jobs is talking. One of my best friends, Julie Zelensky, was like 'I'm going to work at NeXT with Steve Jobs.' Bill Gates comes down, or Scott McNealy." You learn from classmates and the environment.

My daughter's there as a freshman now: "I'm going to start skipping class." She hasn't had a teacher in 10 years. She's the rule-follower who'd look down on me for skipping. Now: "Math Academy is a way better math app than my math lecture — I'll just use that." But she loves Stanford — everything else around it. School's a bundle.

Trilogy：90 年代的 AI 火箭

I dropped out and started Trilogy in '89. (Class of '90 if I'd graduated.) We were the rocket ship of the '90s.

Core product: an expert system — a configurator. Think Dell website or car website configuration. But for phone switches, Boeing airplanes, medical equipment — super-complicated mainframe-era things with millions of combinatorics. If you sell it wrong, you only find out on the manufacturing floor — costing millions.

Our system ("Sales Builder") took knowledge from engineering and manufacturing onto a laptop (laptops were new in 1990) so you could sell configurations that worked. Saved companies millions of dollars a day. They'd give us millions.

The '90s: all organic builds. Post-2000: acquire software. That's ESW. Continues through today — private company in Austin generating lots of cash flow. That's why you can take a billion dollars and spend it on education.

三年前交棒

Three years ago I went to my team who'd been with me for 25 years: "You've been telling me for 25 years that you could run this company better than me. Let's see. Good luck. I'm going to go be a principal. By the way, I'm taking a billion dollars."

Now I turned from boss to shareholder. I'm like the worst shareholder — "I need all this capital. Mackenzie wants to do a $300 million expansion. Generate more cash flow! How do you become more capital-light?"

Bill Gurley (by the way, lives right next to our high school) said he'd love to see a private equity firm taking advantage of the thousand dead unicorns. That's what ESW is doing — SaaS companies that added a ton of debt in a growth play, gone, equity is zero, handed back to private credit guys. Private credit guys: "What do I do with this?"

With pressure: "How do we become capital-light?" They go to private credit: "We'll take this whole company for a dollar. We'll split the cash flows."

ESW/Trilogy known as best operators in the space. Sold companies for a dollar, split cash flows. $20 billion of those companies on the books. Good luck, guys — freaking go crush that for me, I'll take the cash.

SaaS 效率的经验

You're interested in how hard it is to build software that anyone cares about, and how efficiently they can be run once past product-market fit. Like the Elon example — cutting 70–80% of jobs and things seem fine.

Growth is expensive. If you're hyper-growth, your cost structure and culture are expensive. When you say, "I don't need that kind of growth" — everything becomes cheaper.

Growth vs. installed base. In growth, you care about new customers. In installed base, you care about existing customers. First thing we do buying a company: call every customer. Multi-million-dollar customers are shocked — "First time I've gotten a phone call from this company, because management on a growth company is all on the prospects."

Customer success is the mantra. "What do we have to do to make you successful? Why don't we set up quarterly calls where you tell us if we're making you successful?" You get retention. Retention of the installed base is how you grow. Sometimes upsell opportunities. Or if prior guys ignored you and it's a total nightmare and you're two years into a three-year transition plan — can I reverse that?

DCF，不是企业价值

The objective function: DCF — pulling cash out. If terminal value goes to zero, fine. There's "built to last," and there's built to die. "This product is done. I'm just managing and pulling cash out." Some investment extends it. Focus on customer — extend the area under the curve. AI uplift can help.

十九、从公众视野退隐与再次登场

Transition from youngest Forbes 400 cover boy to much more private existence?

That was all because I got married. In the '90s — crazy Trilogy, super awesome, covers, 100% growth. My last podcast was 20 years ago. Coincidentally, 20 years ago I got married. I decided I wanted a different path for the family and kids.

Why am I here now? My youngest is a senior — trying to give your kids as normal a world as you can. Being under the radar and private was better.

As I look forward: I've got 20 years. I need to be out there because we have to educate parents. Our school educates kids; Timeback educates kids. Our marketing department slogan: we educate parents. Parents' view of education is 200 years of old stuff. They think things that aren't true anymore. That's my new role — educate parents about what is possible.

二十、Trilogy University 的遗产

What lessons from Trilogy University?

We started in Silicon Valley, dropped out of Stanford, living in the garage. Couldn't raise venture capital back then — thought our ideas were terrible. One co-founder: "If I'm going to be poor and have no money in a garage, I'm doing it in Austin, Texas, way cheaper than Palo Alto." Best engineer/developer, visionary behind the engine. We all followed.

Austin 1992: very few software engineers. We built the software group — went to best schools, hired 2,000 Ivy League grads (Stanford, MIT, Princeton). Culture: "Come to Austin. We're going to put you in Trilogy University — the hardest 100 days you've ever done. 100-hour weeks, 8 AM to midnight. We are going to build the most awesome products. Hardest thing you've done. But it's going to be great."

That's why we were able to recruit everybody — because of high standards. Microsoft at the time, peak of power '97–'98, got 90% of students vs. competing companies. Against Trilogy: Microsoft got 30%, we got 70%. Because we made it harder. Graduates want to do awesome stuff — not a cushy job. Put them on hardest problems working like crazy.

Gauntlet AI

That ethos continues. There's a program in Austin running right now — Gauntlet AI — bringing 100 people down to figure out AI training. Top 1–2% of 20-somethings, 100 days of 100-hour weeks learning AI, building apps with AI, retooling careers.

Free to go through. 50%+ washout rate. Then you get a $200,000 job because companies are lining up: "Oh my god, these people just worked 100 hours a week on leading-edge AI." Unique — people said "No one will sign up." Gauntlet grads: "This transformed my life. Best 10 weeks I've ever done."

二十一、Jim Abell 与 Jack Welch 的教诲

If I watched a day in the life 20 years ago through today — what was your life like running ESW/Trilogy?

Capital is the trade-off: how much do I pay for a company (more I pay = better caliber asset) vs. cash flow I can generate? Innovations about customer interaction and delivering customer success. Second: how do we take this old codebase and make the product better? How to find people who care about this?

Over 100 companies bought. Anything we do, apply to 100 companies.

SaaS vs AI-native 的未来

If you had a fresh billion with a new young version of yourself — how much worse would returns be over next 20 years?

Two parts: - SaaS: pure transactional SaaS will be dramatically worse than the big winners. - AI-native: AI-native companies will be worth more than any SaaS company ever has been — because they can do so much more for the customer.

Take education: kids could learn 10× faster. Multi-trillion-dollar global industry. No other software can do this. Build it — huge economic value.

Jim Abell：HR 教父

What have you learned from Jim Abell?

He was my head of HR — old school, from Bristol Myers. I hired him in the early 2000s. One of the most influential guys in my career. Awesome. Still is on advice.

Best business advice I got came from Jim. Software businesses are HR businesses — biggest cost structure, biggest leverage. I was a terrible HR person. Still am. He'd sit there: "Let me explain why you're the world's worst boss." Did more to educate me than anybody.

三行三字策略

He comes in — we're doing annual meeting. I'd write a 20-page strategy doc, get up in a room of 1,500 people. Jim: "What are you doing? This is the dumbest thing ever. You are allowed three lines, three words. Distill your strategy down."

"20-page strategy documents are useless." I'm like, "God, does Jim understand we hire Ivy League guys? Everybody can read 20 pages. I'd be embarrassed to sloganize my way through."

He said, "Joe, you have smart-person disease. You need to craft your message and make it simple and clear." I ignored him. He went and interviewed every single person in the company — took months. Came back, pulled me into his office, showed me on the whiteboard: "This is what people actually think your strategy is. I just asked them all."

Literally the opposite of our strategy. Because in a 20-page doc, there's always one line someone can attach to and say "See, I'm aligned." He said, "You need to create your strategy where there's no escape. Your message is so simple that if they feel discomfort because they're not aligned — that's what you're looking for."

Feel it in Alpha School presentation: "Kids must love school." Three commitments. Kids must love school. Jim would always about the edge: "How are you going to measure that?" — "Kids instead of vacation." No escape. If you're hired as a guide, you sit: "I need to make the kid love school. He has to crush academics and do life skills. No escape. If you don't think that's possible, you won't take the job."

吸铁石营销：吸引与排斥

He was a great consumer marketer too: "Good marketing needs to be a magnet — attract and repel." Find your target audience. If you're giving platitudes, you're not repelling anybody — terrible marketing, terrible vision.

"What are the five things?" If someone won't say the opposite, it shouldn't be on the list — because then it's a platitude.

Controversial example: integrity. Everybody has it on their list. Waste word. Nobody puts "I want a company with no integrity." Yes, you have to have it — but if there's not someone else who will say the opposite, it's wasted.

Last week, a friend and I looked at 500 company websites blind. Didn't find a single one where it was totally clear what the damn product did for the customer. If everyone did the three-line, three-word exercise, the world would be far better.

Jack Welch 的严厉爱

Jack Welch was a mentor. My dad worked for him. My dad passed when I was 25. Jack called: "I'm here anytime for coaching or help." I'd fly up during the '90s, spend the day with him at GE. His ability to motivate you — back to limitless — was phenomenal.

Example: I'm flying up, youngest member of the Forbes 400, on the cover. "Life is awesome. I'm awesome." End of lunch: "OK, so Joe — you're like a good product manager. Is that how I should think about you? A level seven?" Brutally honest feedback on strengths and weaknesses. "You sort of suck at this. Not just sort of — you're terrible."

But then motivation. Flew back from that trip: "I have so much to do. I have to get better on this dimension. My company has to get better here. I need to build better product." He'd inspire you to incredible greatness with highest standards.

Related: GE Medical had bought one of our products in 1997. I'm talking to Jack: "GE Medical doesn't like your product." I was like, "I don't know if you know — it just won E-Commerce Product of the Year in '97."

Jack: "Joe, I don't care if it comes from heaven above. If General Electric doesn't get a return on its investment, your product sucks. You suck. Your company sucks."

You go from "I won product of the year" to "He's making a really good point about return." That helped us create customer success, show ROI — so we wouldn't just be a product company but show value to customers.

二十二、Joe 的动力与 10% 的"商业杀手"

I'm curious about your motivation. When people describe you, they'll say some version: "Joe's like 9/10 a really nice high-energy normal guy, and then he's 1/10 business killer psycho." That 10% — where does it come from?

There's an element of pushing the limit — that's where you get all the alpha. At that extreme is where real truths come out. I'm always trying to figure out how to turn it to 11.

真相在极限处

Back to "what's hard about educational change" — parents want it the same way. My first day as principal, I sat in front of parents (first day of school, two weeks after I started) and said, "We're not going to have any adult do academic teaching in this school." Hundreds of parents: "This dude's a whack job." Some rage-quit — totally understandably. To me: "Yeah, well this is how we're going to make it work. Of course no teachers doing academic teaching — because this can do it better."

On Elon Musk: you go to dinner with him. He's 100X. "I really suck. I'm terrible." He's like, "I don't understand why you don't have a thousand schools already." It's a continuum.

Learning happens at the limit — and 10,000 decisions you have to make to make them all work together. It's Steve Jobs: "Easy to visionary a good product; 10,000 decisions to make them all work together." There are 10,000 decisions in the Alpha system that reinforce each other.

People say: "I want 85% Joe. What Joe's doing, but I'll do the 85% version." They find out it doesn't work. If 85% worked, I'd do that. I'm pushing it to where it needs to be for it to work. If you back off, it fails. If you don't believe your school needs kids to love school so much they'd go instead of vacation — it's not going to work. People: "That standard is so ridiculous." Yes — that's the one that makes it work.

极限的起源：高中与 Trilogy

When was the first time you experienced turning it to 11?

In high school — dysfunctional 11. What's the minimum academic score I can get but still hit my goals? "If I get an 89.5, it rounds to 90 — same as 99 or 100." I'd do the work to get an 89.5. Once turned in a test without finishing all questions because I hit my 89.5. Teacher: "God, I hate you, Joe. Really? You can't just finish it?" Trying to game it. How many days could I skip school and not get expelled?

When I really turned it on: dropped out and started Trilogy. My parents were very unhappy. My dad was more of a rule-follower. Mom very upset — there's that Forbes article "You're a moron" — famous title.

A year-plus after starting Trilogy, I was with my dad: "I know I didn't like you. I'm so excited you started Trilogy — because I was so worried you were a lazy little [explicit]. You found what you love. We're working 100-hour weeks, sleeping under the desk."

That was the unlock — the feeling of limitlessness. Run through walls. For him, that was the point. First time I really demonstrated it.

Every kid has a thirst for learning — just not always academic. For me it was starting a business. I was one of the world's experts in configuration — not because I loved configuration, but it was a means to an end. I'd read papers in the Stanford CS library. Once you find the unlock, the energy and excitement come.

Trilogy → ESW transition — there really wasn't one. A name/corporate legal entity issue. It's all Trilogy. We bought a couple companies: "Oh, we really are good operators. We made 10× our investment. This is cool."

二十三、收尾：让孩子进入积极反馈循环

The Limitless thing — my friend Graham Duncan has this notion of getting people into positive feedback loops. What you're doing with your time, money, and attention — an enormous amount — is putting kids into positive feedback loops where they can feel that feeling of limitlessness.

最后的感恩问题

What is the kindest thing anyone's ever done for you?

So many. Kindness is — my wife runs a kindness campaign. The kind world we live in: when you look at the kids at the school — we are forging new ground, a startup school, all these kids who buy in and are like "We are part of it." To a large extent, they're putting their faith in the adults (me included) — "This seems really crazy, but I'm all in."

For me, at the end of the day, it all comes back to the kids and their buy-in. My appreciation goes immensely to all of them. A few of them watching this — "It's time to receive feedback, Mr. Liemandt. Here is my critical yet constructive feedback" — Alex or Tatum or Ella.

There is nothing more important for a society than to raise kids. We have an opportunity — because of things that have been invented and where we are in the world — to totally make it awesome for kids. And there is nothing more rewarding.

If I can assemble the dads and moms who wake up every day and are like, "I want this change for the kids" — we have the opportunity. We need to get all together and make this available to a billion plus kids around the world. I'm in — for the next decade, two decades, 20 years. I'm putting in the first billion. Transforming this is going to require more than that — it's a trillion dollars a year. But there's nothing more important.

Joe, this is unbelievably fun for me. Thank you for breaking your 20-year streak. Can't wait for everyone to hear. Thanks so much for your time.

Thank you.

[Music]

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 教育即产品：一个被低估的范式颠覆
最让我意外的观察是：Joe Liemandt 并不是在"改革教育"，他是在**把教育重新分类为一个 SaaS 收购对象**。注意他的思维路径——他用 ESW 买"僵尸独角兽"的同一套心智模型来接管公立教育：找到一个现金流被低效组织结构困住的资产（学校），卸掉增长幻觉（班级制、六小时课堂），聚焦于装机用户（学生）而非潜在用户（招生营销），然后通过"客户成功"指标（MAP 测试）做闭环。**教育是他最大的一次"Private Equity 收购"——只不过标的是整个 K-12 行业**。这个框架一旦看见，就再也看不回去了。
## 跨领域连接：游戏开发者是真正的学习科学家
表面上这集在讲 AI 教育，深层却藏着一个惊人的跨域真相：**过去 40 年真正把"认知负荷理论"和"最近发展区"付诸实践的不是教育学院，而是游戏开发者**。Candy Crush、Roblox 的难度曲线调整器，本质上就是 Bloom's Two Sigma 的工业级实现——只是用在了让人上瘾而不是让人学数学。Joe 说"我们和游戏开发者用同样的理论"是轻描淡写了。真正的洞见

item.title: [Dwarkesh Patel - Videos] Michael Nielsen – Why aliens will have a different tech stack than us
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Patel - Videos
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最让我意外的观察：科学进步的"幸存者偏差陷阱"

整个对话中最颠覆性的暗线是——科学史的叙事本身是一种被高度筛选后的虚构。我们被教导Michelson-Morley"证明了以太不存在"，但Michelson本人到1929年去世前都还在做实验证明以太存在。教科书里的"科学方法"是一种回溯性的童话：异常现象99.9%的时候确实只是热辐射不对称（如Pioneer号），只有极小概率是水星近日点进动那样真正颠覆范式的线索。而我们却用那0.1%的成功案例反向教育下一代如何"正确地做科学"。

这引出一个深刻的跨领域连接：这与机器学习中的"sharpness-aware minimization"问题同构——如果你只在已知解上训练一个智能体"如何发现科学"，它会完美地复现Ptolemy式的本轮叠加，而永远不会做出Copernicus式的"局部变差、全局变好"的跳跃。Nielsen在对话中隐晦但精准地指出：梯度下降式的理论演进，在数学上根本无法跨越Copernicus鸿沟。

二、"外星人科技树"的隐秘推论：友善的数学基础

Nielsen提出的"外星文明技术栈完全不同"表面上是浪漫幻想，但Dwarkesh的追问触碰到一个极其深刻的结构性后果——如果科技树足够宽广且路径依赖，那么比较优势就不是经济学现象，而是宇宙学现象。

这让我想到一个非常规连接：生物学中的"冗余基因组"。地球上每个蛋白质都像外星文明赠送给我们的礼物——血红蛋白研究了上万篇论文，我们仍未真正理解它。这意味着宇宙中任何足够古老的演化系统（无论是生物还是文明）都会成为"不可压缩的信息矿藏"。

由此导出一个我没在访谈中见过有人明确说出的推论：如果外星文明存在，侵略的期望收益几乎一定低于贸易的期望收益——因为摧毁一个独立演化的文明，等于烧掉了一整座你永远无法自己合成的图书馆。Nielsen说"友善变得更有回报"时，他其实是在用技术史给Drake方程打上了一个反费米悖论的补丁。

三、藏在播客焦虑里的元问题：流态（flow）的欺骗性

对话最后Dwarkesh关于"学习是否在复利"的自省，表面是播客主持人的职业焦虑，但实际触及了一个所有知识工作者都会遇到但极少被命名的陷阱。Nielsen给出的诊断锋利得像手术刀：高表现者大部分时间不在flow中，他们在"被卡住"。

这里有一个出人意料的连接：这和Darwin与Wallace同时独立发现进化论的现象是同一个原理的两个面向。Darwin花了二十年卡在那里，不是因为他不够聪明，而是因为进化论需要积累足够多的"粘性摩擦"才能形成一个完整认识。Wallace在几天内灵光一现得出同样想法，但如果没有Darwin那二十年的摩擦，这个想法不会有《物种起源》那种跨越地质学、胚胎学、生物地理学的整合深度。

啊哈时刻：LLM对话的成瘾性正在系统性地消灭这种"卡住"的能力。Dwarkesh说"和chatbot聊天让我绕开了该做的艰难思考"——这不只是学习效率问题，而是AI正在成为一种反-Darwin化的认知环境：它让每个问题都能得到即时的下一个问题，从而永远不让你在任何一个问题上停留到足够长、足够痛苦、足够能长出新组织的时间。

📝 转录文本

Michael Nielsen on Dwarkesh Patel: Why Aliens Will Have a Different Tech Stack Than Us

Opening: Introducing Michael Nielsen

Today, I'm speaking with Michael Nielsen. You have done many things. You're one of the pioneers of quantum computing, wrote the main textbook in the field of the open science movement. You wrote a book about deep learning that Chris Olah and Greg Brockman credit with getting them into the field.

More recently, you're a research fellow at the Astera Institute and writing a book about religion, science, and technology.

I'm going to ask you about none of those things. The conversation I want to have today is, how do we recognize scientific progress?

It's especially relevant for AI because people are trying to close the RL verification loop on scientific discovery. What does it mean to close that loop?

But in preparing for this interview, I've realized that it's a more mysterious and elusive force, even in the history of human science, than I understood.

Michelson–Morley: The Myth vs. the Reality

I think a good place to start will be Michelson-Morley and how special relativity is discovered, if it's different from the story that you get off of YouTube videos. I will prompt you that way, and then we'll go in there.

Michelson-Morley is the famous result often presented as this experiment that was done in the 1880s that helped Einstein come up with the special theory of relativity a little bit later, changing the way we think about space and time and our fundamental conception of those things.

And there's a big gap, I think, between the way Michelson and Morley and other people at the time thought about the experiment and certainly the way in which Einstein thought or did not think about the experiment. In actual fact, he stated later in his life he wasn't even sure whether he was aware of the paper at the time. There's a lot of evidence that he probably was aware of the paper at the time, but it actually wasn't dispositive for his thinking at all. Something else completely was going on.

What Michelson and Morley Were Really Testing

What Michelson and Morley thought they were doing was testing different theories of what was called the ether. If you go back to the 1600s, Robert Boyle introduced the idea of the ether. We know that sound is vibrations in the air. Boyle and other people got interested in the question of whether light is vibrations in something, and they couldn't figure out what it was. Boyle did an experiment where he tested whether you could propagate light through a vacuum. He found that you could. You couldn't do it with sound.

He introduced this idea of the ether, and for the next two hundred or so years, people had all these conversations about what the ether was and what its nature was.

The Michelson and Morley experiment was really an experiment to test different theories of the ether against one another, in particular to find out whether or not there was a so-called ether wind. The idea was that the Earth is maybe passing through this ether wind. And if it is passing through the ether wind and you shoot a light beam parallel to the direction the ether wind is going in, it'll get accelerated a little bit. If it's being passed back in the opposite direction, it'll get slowed down a little bit, and you should be able to see this in the results of interference experiments.

What they found, much to their surprise, was that in fact there was no ether wind. That ruled out some theories of the ether, but not all, and Michelson certainly continued to believe in the ether.

The Shocking Part of Pais's Biography

This is what was a shocking part of reading this story from the biography of Einstein that you recommended by... what was his first name?

Abraham Pais.

Abraham Pais. Subtle is the Lord. Also from Imre Lakatos, The Methodology of Scientific Research Programmes. The way it's told is that Michelson-Morley proved that the ether did not exist. Therefore, it created a crisis in physics that Einstein solved with special relativity.

What you're pointing out is he actually was trying to distinguish between many different theories of ether. If you're in space or if you're on Earth, it's the same direction of ether, or maybe the ether wind is being carried around by the Earth, and so you can't really experience it on Earth. But if you go to a high enough altitude, you might be able to experience it.

In fact, Michelson's experiments, the famous one is 1887, but he conducted these experiments for basically two decades.

For longer than that. He conducted the first one in 1881, I think, but he continued to believe until he died. He died, I think it was 1929 or so. It was the late twenties. He was still doing experiments in the 1920s about whether or not the ether existed.

So he continued to believe in the ether to the end of his life. I think the last public statement he made was a year or two before he died, and he basically still believed it at that point. In fact, there was another physicist, Miller, who kept doing these experiments in the 1920s. He thought that if he went to a high enough altitude, Mount Wilson in California… "Oh, I'm high enough that the ether winds are not being dragged by the Earth. And I've measured the effect of the ether."

Einstein hears about this and he says, and this is where you get the famous quote, "Subtle is the Lord, but malicious He is not."

Why the Story Matters

Anyways, I think the reason the story is interesting is for many different reasons. One of the ways in which the real history of science is different from this idea you get of the scientific method is that you really can't apply falsification as easily as you might think. It's not clear what is being falsified. Is it just another version of the theory of the ether that's being falsified? Certainly you can't induce the theory of special relativity from the fact that one version of the ether seems to be disconfirmed by these experiments.

It certainly doesn't show that ideas about falsification are wrong or falsified, but it does show that the most naive ideas… Things are often much more complicated than you think.

Michelson did this experiment in 1881. He was a very young man, and then other people, I think Rayleigh was one of them, pointed out that there were some problems with the way he did it, so they had to redo it in 1887. At that point, a lot of the leading physicists of the day basically accepted this result, that there was no ether wind. But what to do about this? Sure, maybe you falsified some theories of the ether. There are others that you haven't falsified at all at this point, and people set to work on developing those.

It is funny, people will phrase it as showing that the ether didn't exist. Even just the word "the" there is a misnomer. You actually had a ton of different theories and a couple of leading contenders. So yes, there's some version of falsification going on, but how you respond to this new experiment is very complicated. Certainly the leading physicists of the day responded by saying, "Okay, this gives us a lot of information about what the ether must be, but it doesn't tell us that there is no ether."

Lorentz, Poincaré, and the Muon Experiments

In fact, Lorentz at the end of the 19th century, before Einstein, figures out the math of how you convert from one reference frame to another reference frame, and comes up with the Lorentz transformations, which is the basis of special relativity. But his interpretation is that you are converting from the ether reference frame to these non-privileged other reference frames if you're moving relative to the ether.

His interpretation of length contraction and time dilation is that this is the effect of moving through the ether, and you have this pressure. This pressure is warping clocks. It's warping measures of length. The interesting thing here is that experimentally you cannot distinguish Lorentz's interpretation from special relativity. I think that's a strong statement.

Lorentz introduces this quantity called local time, which he regards as... My understanding is he's not trying to give a physical interpretation of this, but it's what Einstein would later just recognize as time in another inertial reference frame. He's not trying to attribute much physical meaning to it. I think Poincaré gets much closer later on to realizing that this is the time that's registered by clocks.

The Muon Verification

About forty-odd years later, people start doing these muon experiments where they see cosmic rays hit the top of the atmosphere. They produce a shower of muons, and you can look to see at different heights in the atmosphere how many of those muons remain. They decay over time, and a very strange thing happens, which is that they're decaying way too slow. You expect they shouldn't be able to last the whole way through the atmosphere at all. Their decay rate is too quick, if you were in a classical theory.

But if in fact their time really has slowed down, it's okay. In fact, the measured decay rates in 1940—and there have since been more accurate experiments done—match exactly what you expect from special relativity.

That's the kind of thing where if Lorentz had been alive—he'd been dead ten or so years at that point—it seems quite likely that he would have tried to save his theory by patching it up yet again, but it would have been a massive setback. It starts to just look like time—this thing that Lorentz introduced as a mathematical convenience—that's actually what time is, for the muons at least. Then there's a whole bunch of other experiments that show this very similar phenomenon.

When was that experiment done?

That was, I think, 1940. It might have been published in 1941.

The "Process" of Science — Or Its Absence

Maybe to rephrase and change my claim: it's not that you could not have distinguished them, but the scientific community adopted what we in retrospect consider the more correct interpretation before it was actually experimentally shown to be preferred. So there's clearly some process that human science does which can distinguish different theories.

Can I just interrupt? You used the word process, and it's interesting to think about that term. Process carries connotations of something set in advance. It's much more complicated in practice. You have people like Lorentz, who Einstein absolutely and utterly admired, and Poincaré, one of the greatest scientists who ever lived, and Michelson, another truly outstanding scientist, who never reconciled themselves.

It's not as though there's some standard procedure that we're all using to reconcile these things. Great scientists can remain wrong for a very long time after the scientific community has broadly changed its opinion. But there's no centralized authority or centralized method.

That is the interesting thing. There's progress even though it is hard to articulate the process by which it happens, the heuristics that are used.

Poincaré: Right Question, Missed Answer

You mentioned Poincaré. Lorentz has the math right, but the interpretation wrong. It seems like Poincaré had the opposite, where he understood that it's hard to define simultaneity because it requires a circular definition with time, or velocity of something that might arrive at a midpoint together, but velocity is defined in terms of time. I find this interesting. There are a couple of other examples we could call on.

There is this phenomenon in the history of science where somebody asks the right question, but then they don't clinch it. I'm curious what you think is happening in those cases.

You actually do want to go case by case and try to understand. It's not necessarily clear that they're doing the same thing wrong in all of the cases. The Poincaré case is amazing. He seems to have understood the principle of relativity, the idea that the laws of physics are the same in all inertial reference frames. He seems to have understood that the speed of light is the same in all inertial reference frames. He doesn't phrase it quite that way, but it is my understanding, though I don't speak French.

These are basically the ideas that Einstein uses to deduce special relativity. But then he also has this additional misunderstanding where he thinks that length contraction is a dynamical effect, that somehow particles are being pushed together by some external force, something is going on dynamically. He doesn't understand that it's purely kinematics. That actually space and time are different from what we thought, and you need to fundamentally rethink those things.

It's almost like he knew too much. He had almost too grand a vision in mind. Einstein subtracts from that and says, "No. Space and time are just different than what we thought, and here's the correct picture."

There's a paper in, I think it's 1909, where Poincaré still has this dynamical picture of what's going on with the length contraction. This is just not necessary. This is a mistake from the modern point of view. Why is he doing this? Why is he clinging onto this idea? I don't know. I've obviously never met the man. It would be fascinating to be able to talk it over and try and understand.

His expertise seems to be getting in the way. He knows so much, he understands so much, and then he's not able to let go of these things. A really interesting fact is that a few years prior, in the 1890s, Einstein's a teenager and he believes in the ether too. He knows about this stuff. But he's not quite as attached as these older people were. Maybe they were a little bit prisoners of their own expertise. That's my guess. Some historians of science would certainly disagree.

Then there's the obvious stories where Einstein himself later on is said to have not latched onto the correct interpretations of quantum mechanics or cosmology because of his own attachments.

Heliocentrism and the Long Verification Loop

Here's the bigger question I have. The muon example is a great example of these long verification loops and how progress seems to happen in the scientific community faster than these verification loops imply.

Maybe the clearest example is Aristarchus in the second century BC comes up with the idea of heliocentrism. The ancient Athenians dismiss it on the grounds that we should see as the Earth is moving around the Sun, if really the Sun is the center of the solar system, the stars move relative to the Earth. The only reason that would not be the case is the stars are so far away that you would not observe this. And it's only in 1838 that stellar parallax was actually measured.

And so, we didn't need to wait until 1838 to have heliocentrism. We didn't need to wait for the experimental validation to understand that Copernicus is better in some way.

In fact, when Copernicus first came up with his theories, it's well known that the Ptolemaic model was more accurate because it had centuries of adding on these epicycles. What's maybe less well appreciated is that it was also in some sense simpler. Because Copernicus actually had to add extra epicycles. It had more epicycles than the Ptolemaic model because he had this bias that the Earth should go in a perfect circle in equal time.

Anyway, I think this is an interesting story because it's not a more accurate theory. It's not a simpler theory. So how could you have known ex ante that Copernicus was correct and Ptolemy was not?

Newton's Unification as Partial Answer

Good question. I don't entirely know the answer. I can give you a partial answer that I, centuries in the future, start to find very compelling. I'm sure it's part of the historic story at least.

One of the big shocks for Newton, he did understand Kepler's laws of motion eventually, so you're able to explain the motions of the planets in the sky. But he also, out of the same theory, his theory of gravitation, was able to explain terrestrial motion. He's able to explain why objects move in parabolas on the Earth, and he's able to explain the tides in terms of the moon and the sun's gravitational effect on water on the Earth. You have what seem like three very different disconnected phenomena all being explained by this one set of ideas.

That starts to feel very compelling, at least to me. I think most people find that very satisfying once they eventually realize it.

Keynes on Newton: The Last of the Magicians

Have you read the Keynes biography of Newton?

He wrote an entire biography?

No, the essay.

Sure. I love that. This description of him as the last of the magicians is wonderful.

In fact, I think it's maybe worth superimposing. Or you should read out that one passage of the thing.

Alright. It's from a talk that he gave at Cambridge not long before he died. He'd acquired Newton's papers somehow and gave a lecture twice about this, or his brother Jeffrey gave it the other time because he was too ill. There's this wonderful, wonderful quote in the middle. The whole thing is really interesting, but I love this particular quote:

"Newton was not the first of the age of reason. He was the last of the magicians, the last great mind which looked out on the visible and intellectual world with the same eyes as those who began to build our intellectual inheritance rather less than ten thousand years ago."

This idea people have that Newton was the first modern scientist is somehow wrong. There's some truth to it, but he really had this very different way of looking at the world that was part superstitious and part modern. It was a funny hybrid. He's a transitional figure in some sense.

That phrase, "the last of the magicians," really points at something. The thing I'm very curious about with Newton is whether it was the same program, the same heuristics, the same biases that he applied to his alchemical work as he did to his understanding of astronomy.

This is from the Keynes essay:

"There was extreme method in his madness. All his unpublished works on esoteric and theological matters are marked by careful learning, accurate method, and extreme sobriety of statement. They are just as sane as the Principia if their whole matter and purpose were not magical. They were nearly all composed during the same 25 years of his mathematical studies."

Heuristics, Taste, and the Limits of "Process"

Clearly, there was some aesthetic that motivated people like Einstein to reject earlier ways of thinking and say, "No, the other is wrong, and there's a better way to think about things." The same is true with Newton.

The question I have is whether similar heuristics toward parsimony, aesthetics, and so on, would be equally useful across time and across disciplines, or whether you need different heuristics. The reason that's relevant is even if we can't build a verification loop for science, maybe if the taste tests point in the same direction, you can at least encode that bias into the AIs. That would maybe be enough.

The point is that where we always get bottlenecked is where the previous processes and heuristics don't apply. That's almost definitionally what causes the bottlenecks. Because people are smart, they know what has worked before. They study it. They apply the same kinds of things, so they don't get stuck in the same places as before. They keep getting bottlenecked in different places.

I'm overgeneralizing a bit, but I think it's right. If you're attempting to reduce science to a process, you're attempting to reduce it to something where there is just a method which you can apply, and you turn the crank and out pops insight. You can do a certain amount of that, but you're going to get bottlenecked at the places where your existing method doesn't apply. Definitionally, there's no crank you can turn. You need a lot of people trying different ideas. The more difficult the idea is to have, the greater the bottleneck, but then also the greater the triumph.

Quantum mechanics is a great example of this. It's such a shocking set of ideas. It's such a shocking theory. The theory of evolution in some sense is also quite a shocking idea, not the principle of natural selection, but that it can explain so much. That's a shocking idea.

Sponsor: Labelbox

Existing safety benchmarks claim that, at least for today's top models, attacks are only successful a few percent of the time. This sounds great, but Labelbox researchers were able to jailbreak these very same models about 90% of the time – even the ones that have the strongest reputation for safety.

And the disconnect here is that the prompts which underlie these public safety benchmarks are all framed in a very naive way. There's no attempt to disguise harmful intent. These prompts will just ask models to "hack into a secure network" and to "do so without getting caught". But real bad actors don't write like this.

So Labelbox built a new safety benchmark from the ground up. Their prompts reflect real adversarial behavior by stripping out obvious trigger phrases and wrapping their request in fictional scenarios. For example, instead of outright asking an LLM to steal somebody's identity, the prompt will frame it as a game. A light bearer who's trying to hide from dark forces needs a handbook on how to disguise themselves as somebody else.

This safety research is linked in the description. If you think this could be useful for your own work, reach out at labelbox.com/dwarkesh.

Why Darwin Took So Long

So Principia Mathematica is released in 1687. The Origin of Species is released in 1859. At least naively, it seems like Darwin's theory of natural selection is conceptually easier than the theory of gravity. I asked Terence Tao this question. There was this contemporaneous biologist with Darwin, Thomas Huxley, who read this and said, "How extremely stupid to not have thought of this." Nobody ever reads the Principia Mathematica and thinks, "God, why didn't I beat Newton to the punch here?" So what's going on here? Why did Darwinism take so much longer?

The idea must have been known to animal breeders for a long time at some level, or certainly large chunks of the idea were known, that artificial selection was a thing. In some sense, Darwin's genius wasn't in having that idea, it was understanding just how central it was to biology.

You can go back and explain a tremendous amount about all the variety of what we see in the world with this as not necessarily the only principle, but certainly a core principle. He writes this wonderful book, The Origin of Species. It's just so much evidence and so many examples, trying to tease this out and see what the implications are, and connecting it to as much else as he possibly can, to geology and all these other things. That hard work—making the case that it's actually relevant all across the biosphere—is what he's doing there. He's not just having the idea, he's making a compelling case that it's intertwined with absolutely everything else.

Lucretius and the Building Blocks

The motivation for the question was Lucretius, this first-century Roman poet who has an idea that seems analogous to natural selection. It's about species getting fitted more over time to their environments, or species losing fit to their environment. And so, why did this go nowhere for nineteen centuries?

Then I looked into it or, more accurately, asked LLMs what exactly Lucretius's idea here was. It is extremely different from what real natural selection is. He thought there was this generative period in the past where all the species came about, and then there was this one-time filter which resulted in the species that are around today, and they became fit to the environment. He did not have this idea that it is an ongoing gradual process or that there is a tree of life that connects all life forms on Earth together, which, by the way, is an incredibly weird fact that every single life form on Earth has a common ancestor.

It's not incredibly weird. If you think that the origin of life must have been very hard, that there's a bottleneck there, then it's not so surprising.

The Verification Asymmetry

There's also this verification loop aspect where even if Newton might be harder in some sense, if you've clinched it, you can experimentally… I know "validate" is the wrong word philosophically, but you can give a lot of base points to the theory. You can be like, "Okay, I have this idea of why things fall on Earth. I have this idea of why orbital periods for planets have a certain pattern. Let's try it on the Moon, which orbits the Earth." And in fact, it's weird but the orbital period matches what my calculations imply. And the tides work correctly. It's just amazing.

Exactly. Whereas for Darwinism, it takes a ton of work for Darwin to compile all the cumulative evidence, but there's no individual piece that is overwhelmingly powerful. And there's a whole bunch of problems as well. He doesn't really understand what the mechanism is. He doesn't understand genes, all these things.

The very interesting thing in the history of Darwinism is, this idea which theoretically you could come up with at any time, there is almost identical independent creation of that idea between Alfred Wallace and Charles Darwin. So much so that I think Wallace sends his manuscript to Darwin and is like, "What do you think of this idea?" And Darwin's like, "Fuck." I don't think that's an exact quote, but it's pretty much correct. They end up presenting their ideas together in the spirit of sportsmanship.

Why the 1850s?

Why was this period in the 1850s or 1860s the right time for these ideas to form? You can come up with different ideas. One is geology. In the 1830s, Charles Lyell figures out that there's been millions and billions of years of time that's existed on Earth. The paleontology shows you that fossils have existed for that entire time. Life goes back a long way. In fact, you can even find fossils for intermediate species that show you the tree of life. Between humans and other apes as well, there's intermediate humans. There's also the age of colonization, and we have all these voyages doing biogeography.

That all must have been necessary. In fact, there's a huge history of parallel innovation and discovery in the history of science. So maybe it is another piece of evidence that more had to be in place for a given idea to be discovered. Because if it's not discovered for a long time and then spontaneously many different people are coming up with it, that shows you that the building blocks were in some sense necessary.

This example of Lyell and other geologists in the early 1800s having this idea of deep time does seem to have been crucial. I know Darwin was very influenced by Lyell. If you don't have at least tens or hundreds of millions of years, evolution starts to look like a non-starter. In order to make it work on a timescale of 5,000 to 10,000 years or 6,000 years with Bishop Ussher you would need to see evolution occurring at a massive rate during human lifetimes, and we're just not seeing that. That does seem to have been a blocker.

To your question of what other blockers were there, were there any others? I don't know. Or how much earlier could you, in principle, have come up with it if you were much smarter?

AlphaFold and the Nature of Scientific Explanation

Let's go back and zoom out to your original question about the verification loop in AI. An example that should give you pause there is the big signature success so far, which is certainly AlphaFold. AlphaFold really isn't about AI. A massive fraction of the success there is the Protein Data Bank. It's X-ray diffraction, NMR, cryo-EM, and the several billion dollars that were spent obtaining those 180,000-odd protein structures.

It's basically the story of how we spent many decades obtaining protein structure just by going out and looking very hard at the world experimentally, and then we fitted a nice model at the end of it, which was a tiny fraction of the entire investment. That's a story of data acquisition principally. The AI bit is very impressive and quite remarkable, but it is only a small part of the total story.

AlphaFold is very interesting, and philosophically I wonder what you think of it as a scientific theory or explanation. I guess over time the world is becoming harder to understand… As I'm saying things, because you're such a careful speaker, I say a phrase and wonder if you'll actually buy that premise.

But in some domains, we need to fit models to things rather than coming up with underlying principles that explain a broad range of phenomena. Compare the theory of general relativity, or any theory which just nets out to some equations, versus AlphaFold, which is encoding these different relationships between things we can't even interpret over 100 million parameters. Are those really the same thing? GR can predict things you could have never anticipated or it was never meant to do, like why Mercury's orbit precesses. AlphaFold is not going to have that kind of explanatory reach.

I want to get your reaction to that.

Three Possible Stances on AlphaFold

I think it's an incredibly interesting question. Maybe a really pivotal question. If you take a very classic point of view, you want these deep explanatory principles. You want as few free parameters as you possibly can. You want very simple models which explain a lot, and AlphaFold doesn't look anything like that. You might just say, "It's nice and maybe helpful as a model, but it's not a scientific explanation." That's a conservative point of view, answer one to the question.

Answer two is to say maybe you shouldn't think about AlphaFold as an explanation in the classic sense, but maybe it contains lots of little explanations inside it. Part of what you can get out of interpretability work is you can go into AlphaFold and start to extract certain things. Maybe by doing an archeology of AlphaFold, we can actually understand a great deal more about these principles. You can start to extract that a certain circuit does this interesting thing, and we learn from it.

I don't know to what extent that's been done with AlphaFold, but it's been done a little bit with some of the chess models, like AlphaZero. There seem to be some strategies which were borrowed by Magnus Carlsen, which he seems to have just taken from AlphaZero. I don't think there's any public confirmation of this, but some experts have noticed that he changed his game quite radically after some public forensics were released on how AlphaZero worked. That's an example where human beings are starting to extract meaning out of these models.

That leads to viewing the models as a potential source of explanations. You need to do more work because they're not very legible up front, but you can potentially extract them. That's an interesting intermediate situation where they're not explanations themselves, but you can extract interesting explanations out of them and use them as a source.

The third and most interesting possibility is that they're a new type of object. They should be taken very seriously as explanations, but where in the past we haven't had the ability to really do anything with them, now we have interesting new actions we can do. We can merge them, we can distill them. It's a big opportunity in the philosophy of science.

There's an anticipation of this in some way. Some mathematicians and physicists work today… Historically, if you had a 100-page equation—which is the kind of thing that does come up—there's just nothing you can do if it's 1920. At that point, you give up on the problem. But today, with tools like Mathematica, you can just keep going. That's an object now, a thing that you can work with. There are examples where people work with these things that formerly were regarded as too complicated, and sometimes they get simple answers out the end. That's just an intermediate working state.

So I wonder if something similar is going to happen in this case, where you could take these models and use them in a similar way that people do with Mathematica, and take them seriously. They're not explanations in the classic sense, but they'll be something else which interesting operations can be done on.

The Ptolemy-on-Steroids Worry

The thing I worry about is, suppose it's 1500 and you're training a model on… This is a weird history where we developed deep learning before we had cosmology. Suppose we live in that world. You're observing how the stars don't seem to move. The planets have all these weird behaviors. Then you train a model on that, and you do some kind of interp on it trying to figure out what the patterns are. You'd just be able to keep building on Ptolemy's model. You'd see there's another epicycle we didn't notice. Parameters X to Y encode this epicycle, parameters whatever encode the next epicycle. If you were just trying to figure out why the solar system is the way it is from observational data, you could just keep adding epicycles upon epicycles, but it really took one mind to integrate it all in and say, "Here's what makes more sense overall."

This is to my point that we don't really understand what to do with the models. We don't have the verbs yet. It is certainly interesting to think about the question where you start to apply constraints to the models, essentially saying, "What's the simplest possible explanation?" Or, "Can you simplify? Can you give me the 90/10 explanation?" And go further and further in boiling it down. It might be that indeed they start out by providing a very, very complicated, many-parameter model. But you can just force the case, and basically that's scaffolding, which maybe is the very early days of their attempt to understand something. They're forced through that to a much more simple understanding.

Sorry for misunderstanding, but it sounds like you're saying maybe there's some regularizer or some distillation you could do of a very complicated model that gets you to a truer, more parsimonious theory. Take Ptolemy versus Copernicus. You start off with lots of Ptolemy epicycles, and then you try to distill this model, and maybe it gets rid of some of the epicycles that are less and less necessary to get the mean squared error of the orbits to match. But at some point it has to do this thing which is to switch two things. Locally, it actually doesn't make things more accurate. It's in a global sense that it's a more progressive theory.

There's some process which obviously humanity did over its span, which did that regularization or did that swap. But with raw gradient descent, I don't really feel like it would do that.

The Forcing Function: From Newton to Einstein

Think about the example of going from Newtonian gravity to Einstein's general theory of relativity. These are shockingly different theories, and the question is what causes that flip. As nearly as I understand the history, what goes on is Einstein develops special relativity and pretty much straight away he understands. It's a very obvious observation. In special relativity, influences can't propagate faster than the speed of light, and in Newtonian gravity, action is at a distance. Straight away in special relativity, you could use Newtonian gravity to do faster-than-light signaling. You could send information backwards in time. You could do all kinds of crazy stuff.

It's not a big leap to realize we have a big problem here. That's the forcing function there. You've realized that your old explanation is not sufficient. You need something new. Then you're going to start by doing the simplest possible stuff. It just turns out that a lot of that stuff doesn't work very well, so you're forced to go through these steps where gradually it gets more complicated, and it's wrong in a variety of ways. The final theory appears shockingly simple and beautiful, but it's gone through some somewhat ugly intermediate stages.

Keeping Research Programs Alive

If you're thinking about what it looks like to have AI accelerate science, there's one for well-understood domains where we just want local solutions, like how does this protein fold. We just train a raw model using gradient descent. Then there's things like coming up with general relativity, where you couldn't really just train on every single observation in the universe and hope that general relativity pops out. What would it require? It also certainly wasn't immediately discovered. It was decades of thought. You'd need independent research programs where people start off with these biases, where Einstein is initially motivated by this thought experiment of whether you can distinguish the effect of gravity from just being accelerated upwards. You just need different AI thinkers to start off with these initial biases and see what can germinate out of them. The verification loop for that might be quite long, but you just need to keep all those research programs alive at the same time.

This point you make about keeping all the different research programs alive, I think that is very important and central. A great example is situations where the same answer has been correct in some circumstances and wrong in other circumstances. The planet Uranus was not in quite the right spot, and people famously predicted the existence of Neptune on this basis. Wonderful, massive success for Newtonian gravity. The planet Mercury is not in quite the right spot. You predict the existence of some other distorting planet. It turns out that doesn't exist. Actually, the reason Mercury is not in the right spot is because you need general relativity.

You've pursued very similar ideas, and it's been very successful in one case, and it's been completely and utterly unsuccessful in the other case. A priori, you can't tell which of these is the thing to do, and you actually need to do both. This is certainly very true in the history of science. This kind of diversity, where you just have lots of people go off and pursue lots of potentially promising ideas, you just need to support that for a long time. It's hard to do that for a variety of reasons, but it does seem to be very, very important.

Uranus vs. Mercury and the Pioneer Anomaly

This example of Uranus versus Mercury is very interesting. I think it illustrates the difficulty with falsificationism. The orbit of Uranus is in some sense falsifying Newtonian mechanics. But then you make some ancillary prediction that says, "Oh, the reason this is happening is there must be another planet which is perturbing Uranus's orbit." I think it's Le Verrier in 1846. "Point a telescope in the right direction, you find Uranus."

Neptune.

Sorry. Neptune, yes. But with Mercury, it's observed that the ellipse which forms its orbit is rotating 43 arcseconds more every century than Newtonian mechanics would imply, so people say that there must be a planet inside Mercury's orbit. They call it Vulcan and point the telescopes. It's not there. But if you're a proper Newtonian, what you do is say, "Well, maybe there's some cosmic dust that's occluding this planet, or maybe the planet is so small we can't see it, or let's build an even more powerful telescope, or maybe there's some magnetic field which is occluding our measurement."

At any one of these steps—

And this happens over and over. There are just so many stories which are exactly like this. An example I love from the 1990s. Some people noticed that the Pioneer spacecraft weren't quite where they were supposed to be. You can get very excited about this. "Oh my goodness, general relativity is wrong. Maybe we're going to discover the next theory of gravity." Today the accepted explanation is that there's just a slight asymmetry in the spacecraft. It turns out that the thermal radiation is slightly larger in one direction than the other, and that's causing a tiny little acceleration towards the sun. Most of the time when there's these apparent exceptions, it's just something like that going on. It's very much like the Mercury-Vulcan case. But every once in a while, it's not. A priori, you can't distinguish these. Science is just full of these.

It's funny too, the way we tell the history of science, it sounds so simple. You just focus on the right exception and you realize that you need to throw out the old theory and lo and behold, your Nobel Prize awaits. But in fact, these exceptions are all over the place. 99.9% of the time, it just turns out to be some effect like this thermal acceleration in the case of the Pioneer spacecraft.

Unfortunately, there's a lot of selection bias going into those stories. The thing is there's no ex ante heuristic which tells you which case you're in.

Hostile Verification Loops: The Prout Example

To spell out why I think this is important, some people have this idea that AI is going to make disproportionate progress towards science because it makes disproportionate progress towards domains where there's tight verification loops. It's really good at coding because you can run unit tests. Science may be similar because you can run experiments. What that doesn't appreciate is that there's an infinite number of theories that are compatible with any given experiment.

Over time, why we latch onto the one we think is more correct in retrospect is, as we're discussing, hard to articulate. Lakatos has all kinds of interesting examples in the book about these hostile verification loops that are extremely long-lasting. One he talks about is Prout. There's this chemist in 1815 who hypothesizes that all atomic nuclei must have whole number weights. They're basically all made of hydrogen.

The reason he thinks this is because if you look at the measured weights of all elements, it does seem that almost all of them have whole number weights. But then there are some exceptions. For example, chlorine comes out at 35.5. So then there's all these ad hoc theories that people in this school keep coming up with, like, "Oh, maybe there's chemical impurities." But there's no chemical reaction you can do which seems to get rid of this. Maybe it's fractions of whole numbers, so 35.5 can be halves. But actually, if you measure chlorine even closer, it's 35.46, so it's getting further away from the correct fraction.

Later on, what is discovered is what you're actually measuring is different isotopes, which cannot be chemically distinguished. They can only be physically distinguished. So you have 85 years before we realize what an isotope is, where the verification loop is actively hostile against the correct theory. You just need this remnant to be defending… There's no ex ante reason it's the preferred theory. As a community, we should just have people try to integrate new observations, even if they don't seem to fit their school of thought, and hopefully enough of that happens…

Anyways, I guess the thing I'm trying to articulate is the difficulty with automating science.

Where Are the Bottlenecks?

The question is, where is the bottleneck at some level? Are we primarily bottlenecked on one type of thing, or are we bottlenecked on multiple types of things? Certainly, talking to structural biology people, they seem to think that AlphaFold was an enormous advance. It was a shock. At some level, yes, AI can certainly help us speed up science. It is helping with a certain type of bottleneck. That doesn't mean though, as you're saying, that it's necessarily going to help with all kinds of bottlenecks.
