The reason this is so big — maybe obvious to folks at this point, but I'll just go through it quickly. If you kind of trace all the way back to the 1930s, there's a great book called Rise of the Machines that kind of goes through this. If you trace all the way back to the 1930s, there was actually a debate among the people who actually invented the computer. And it was this debate between whether the computer — they kind of understood the theory of computation before they actually built the things.

They had this big debate over whether the computer should be basically built in the image of what at the time were called adding machines or calculating machines — where, you know, think of sort of essentially cash registers. IBM is actually the successor company to the National Cash Register Company of America. And that was of course the path that the industry took: building these kind of hyper-literal mathematical machines that could execute mathematical operations billions of times per second, but of course had no ability to kind of deal with human beings the way humans like to be dealt with. And so couldn't understand human speech, human language, and so forth.

That's the computer industry that got built over the last 80 years, and that's the computer industry that's built all the wealth and financial returns of the computer industry over the last 80 years across all the generations of computers from mainframes through to smartphones.

But they knew at the time — they knew in the 30s — actually they understood the basic structure of the human brain. They had a theory of sort of human cognition, and actually they had the theory of neural networks. The first neural network academic paper was published in 1943, which was over 80 years ago, which is extremely amazing.

There's an interview you can read — or you can watch an interview on YouTube — with these two authors, McCulloch and Pitts. You can watch an interview I think with McCulloch on YouTube from like, I don't know, 1946 or something. He was on TV in the ancient past, and it's literally an amazing interview because it's him in his beach house and for some reason he's not wearing a shirt — and he's like talking about this future in which computers are going to be built on the model of a human brain through neural networks.

And that was the path not taken. Basically what happened was the computer industry got built in the image of the adding machine. The neural network basically didn't happen, but the neural network as an idea continued to be explored in academia and sort of advanced research by a rump movement that was originally called cybernetics and then became known as artificial intelligence basically for the last 80 years.

And essentially it didn't work. Essentially it was basically decade after decade after decade of excessive optimism followed by disappointment. When I was in college in the 80s, there had been a famous kind of AI boom-bust cycle in the 80s in venture and in Silicon Valley. It was tiny by modern standards, but at the time was a big deal. By the time I got to college in '89, in computer science departments, AI was kind of a backwater field and everybody kind of assumed that it was never going to happen.

But the scientists kept working on it, to their credit, and they built up this kind of enormous reservoir of concepts and ideas. And then basically we all saw what happened with the ChatGPT moment. All of a sudden it crystallized. It's like: oh my god, it turns out it works.

Where We Are Now

That's the moment we're in now. And really significantly, that was less than three years ago. That was the summer of — it was the Christmas of '22. So we're sort of three years in to basically what is effectively an 80-year revolution of actually being able to deliver on all the promise that the people on the alternate path — the sort of human cognition model path — kind of saw from the very beginning.

The great news with this technology is it's already kind of ultra-democratized. The best AI in the world is available. Launch ChatGPT or Grok or Gemini or these other products that you can just use and you can just kind of see how they work. Same thing for video — you can see with Sora and Veo kind of state-of-the-art. With music, you can see Suno and Udio and so forth.

The Magic of Silicon Valley

And now Silicon Valley is responding with this just incredible rush of enthusiasm. And really critically, this gets to the magic of Silicon Valley: Silicon Valley long since has ceased to be a place where people make silicon. That not long ago moved out of California and then ultimately out of the US, although we're trying to bring it back now.

But the great kind of virtue of Silicon Valley over the last 80 years of its existence is its ability to kind of recycle talent from previous waves of technology and new waves of technology, and then inspire an entire new generation of talent to basically come join the project. Silicon Valley has this recurring pattern of being able to reallocate capital and talent and build enthusiasm and build critical mass and build funding support and build human capital — and build everything, enthusiasm — for each new wave of technology.

So that's what's happening with AI. I think probably the biggest thing I could just say is: I'm surprised, I think essentially on a daily basis, of what I'm seeing. And we're in the fortunate position to kind of get to see it from two angles.

One is we track the underlying science and research work very carefully. I would say every day I see a new AI research paper that just completely floors me — of some new capability or some new discovery or some new development that I would have never anticipated, that I'm just like wow, I can't believe this is happening.

And then on the other side of course, we see the flow of all of the new products and all the new startups. I would say we're routinely seeing things and again kind of have my jaw on the floor.

Messy, But Early

It feels like we've unlocked this giant vista. I do think it's going to kind of come in fits and starts. These things are messy processes. This is an industry that kind of routinely gets out over its skis and overpromises. So there will certainly be points where it's like, wow, this isn't working as well as people thought, or wow, this turns out to be too expensive and the economics don't work or whatever.

But against that, I would just say the capabilities are truly magical. And by the way, I think that's the experience that consumers are having when they use it. And I think that's the experience that businesses are having for the most part when they're working on their pilots and looking at adoption. And then it translates to the underlying numbers.

We're just seeing — this new wave of AI companies is growing revenue like, just like actual customer revenue, actual demand translated through to dollars showing up in bank accounts, at like an absolutely unprecedented takeoff rate. We're seeing companies grow much faster. The key leading AI companies — companies that have real breakthroughs and have very compelling products — are growing revenues faster than any way I've certainly ever seen before.

Just from all that, it kind of feels like it has to be early. It's kind of hard to imagine that we've topped out in any way. It feels like everything is still developing. Quite frankly, it feels like the products — to me it feels like the products are still super early. I'm very skeptical that the form and shape of the products that people are using today is what they're going to be using in five or 10 years. I think things are going to get much more sophisticated from here. So I think we probably have a long way to go.

Section 2: Business Models & The Cost Question

Q: One of the big knocks is yes, the revenue is immense, but the expenses seem to also be keeping pace. What are people missing as a part of that discussion?

Yeah. So just start with core business models. This industry basically has two core business models: the consumer business model and the quote-unquote enterprise or infrastructure business model.

Consumer: The Internet Is the Carrier Wave

Look, on the consumer side, we just live in a very interesting world now where the internet exists and is fully deployed. I'll give you an example. Sometimes people ask us, "Is AI like the internet revolution?" It's like, well, a little bit, but the thing with the internet was we had to build the internet. We had to actually build the network, and ultimately it involved enormous amounts of fiber in the ground and enormous numbers of mobile cell towers and enormous numbers of shipments of smartphones and tablets and laptops in order to get people on the internet. There was this just incredible physical lift to do that.

And by the way, people forget how long that took. The internet itself is an invention of the 1960s, 1970s. The consumer internet was a new phenomenon in the early '90s. But we didn't really get broadband to the home until the 2000s. That really didn't start rolling out actually until after the dot-com crash, which is fairly amazing. And then we didn't get mobile broadband until like 2010. And people actually forget — the original iPhone dropped in 2007. It didn't have broadband. It was on a narrowband 2G network. It did not have anything resembling high-speed data. So it wasn't really until about 15 years ago that we even had mobile broadband.

The internet was this massive lift — but the internet got built, and smartphones proliferated. So now you have 5 billion people on planet Earth that are on some version of mobile broadband internet. Smartphones all over the world are selling for as little as 10 bucks. You have amazing projects like Jio in India that are bringing the remaining population of planet Earth that hasn't been online until now online. So we're talking five billion, six billion people.

The reason I go through that is the consumer AI products could basically deploy to all of those people basically as quickly as they want to adopt. The internet's the carrier wave for AI to be able to proliferate at kind of light speed into the broad base of the global population. That's a potential rate of proliferation of a new technology that's just far faster than has ever been possible before.

You couldn't download electricity. You couldn't download indoor plumbing. You couldn't download television. But you can download AI. This is what we're seeing: the AI consumer killer applications are growing at an incredible rate.

Monetization and Pricing Courage

And then they're monetizing really well. Generally speaking, the monetization is very good — by the way, including at higher price points. One of the things I like about watching the AI wave is the AI companies I think are more creative on pricing than the SaaS companies and the consumer internet companies were. It's for example now becoming routine to have $200 or $300 per month tiers for consumer AI, which I think is very positive because I think a lot of companies cap their opportunity by capping their pricing too low, and I think the AI companies are more willing to push that. Which I think is good.

So I think that's reason for considerable rational optimism for the scope of consumer revenues that we're going to be talking about here.

Enterprise: What Is Intelligence Worth?

And then on the enterprise side, the question is basically just: what is intelligence worth? If you have the ability to inject more intelligence into your business and you have the ability to do even the most prosaic things — like raise your customer service scores, increase upsells, reduce churn, run marketing campaigns more effectively — all of which AI is directly relevant to — these are like direct business payoffs that people are seeing already.

And then if you have the opportunity to infuse AI into new products — and all of a sudden your car talks to you, and everything in the world kind of lights up and starts to get really smart — what's that worth? Again there, you just observe it and you're like, wow, the leading AI infrastructure companies are growing revenues incredibly quickly. The pull is really tremendous. It feels like this just incredible product-market fit.

Tokens by the Drink & Hyper-Deflation

And the core business model is actually quite interesting. The core business model is basically tokens by the drink. It's sort of tokens of intelligence per dollar.

And oh, and then by the way — this is the other fun thing — if you look at what's happening with the price of AI, the price of AI is falling much faster than Moore's law. I could go through that in great detail, but basically all of the inputs into AI on a per-unit basis, the costs are collapsing. And as a consequence there's kind of this hyper-deflation of per-unit cost, and then that is driving more than a corresponding level of demand growth with elasticity.

Even there, it feels like we're just at the very beginning of figuring out exactly how expensive or cheap this stuff is getting. There's just no question tokens by the drink are going to get a lot cheaper from here. That's just going to drive enormous demand. And then everything in the cost structure is going to get optimized.

Shortages Become Gluts

When people talk about the chips or the unit input costs for building AI — you now have these laws of blind demand that are going to kick in. In any market that has sort of commodity-like characteristics, the number one cause of a shortage is a glut, and the number one cause of a glut is a shortage. To the extent you have a shortage of GPUs or whatever, or shortage of whatever FETs chips, or shortage of whatever data center capacity — if you look at just the history of humanity building things in response to demand, if there's a shortage of something that can be physically replicated, it does get replicated.

There's going to be just enormous buildout. There's hundreds of billions — or at this point trillions — of dollars maybe going into the ground in all these things. The per-unit cost of the AI companies are going to drop like a rock over the course of the next decade.

The economic questions of course are very real, and of course there's microeconomic questions around all these businesses, but the macro forces — at least here I think — are very strong. And yeah, given the underlying value of this technology to both the consumers and the enterprise users, and given the just incredibly aggressive discovery that's happening of all the ways that people can use this in their lives and in their businesses, it's really hard for me to see how it both doesn't grow a lot and generate just enormous revenue.

Section 3: GPU Shelf Life & Big vs. Small Models

Q: It was like two or three weeks ago where AWS was saying the GPUs they've been using, they've been able to extend back to even like seven-plus years. So the shelf life of the GPUs they're using is now extending in ways they can optimize better than maybe the last couple of cycles. Is that the right way to think about it?

Yeah, that's right. And then that also gets to this other question where there's different theories on it, which is basically big models versus small models.

The Small-Model Chase Function

A lot of the data center build is oriented around hosting, training and serving the big models, for all the obvious reasons. But there's also the small model revolution happening at the same time. If you just kind of track — you can get the various research firms have these charts — but if you track the capability of the leading-edge models over time, what you find is after 6 or 12 months there's a small model that's just as capable. There's this kind of chase function that's happening, which is the capabilities of the big models are basically being shrunk down and provided at smaller size and therefore smaller cost, quite quickly.

Case Study: Kimi

I'll give you the most recent example that just hit over the last two weeks — and again, this is a thing that's just kind of shocking. There's this Chinese company that has a — well, I forget the name of the company, but it's the company that produces the model called Kimi (spelled K-i-m-i), which is one of the leading open-source models out of China. The new version of Kimi is a reasoning model that, at least according to the benchmarks so far, is basically a replication of the reasoning capabilities of GPT-5.

These new models of GPT-5 were a big advance over GPT-4, and of course GPT-5 costs a tremendous amount of money to develop and to serve. And all of a sudden, 6 months later, you have an open-source model called Kimi — and I don't know if they had it — it's either shrunk down to be able to run on either one MacBook or two MacBooks.

If you're a business and you want to have a reasoning model that's GPT-5 capable, but you're not going to pay the whatever GPT-5 costs, or you're not going to want to have it be hosted and you want to run it locally — you can do that. That's just another breakthrough. It's just another Tuesday, another huge advance. It's like, oh my god. And then of course it's like, all right, well what is OpenAI going to do? Well, obviously they're going to go to GPT-6.

So there's this kind of laddering that's happening where the entire industry is moving forward. The big models are getting more capable. The small models are kind of chasing them. And then the small models provide a completely different way to deploy at very low price points.

The Supercomputer Analogy

We'll see what happens. There are some very smart people in the industry who think that ultimately everything only runs in the big models — because obviously the big models are always going to be the smartest, and so therefore you're always going to want the most intelligent thing because why would you ever want something that's not the most intelligent thing for any application.

The counterargument is: there's a huge number of tasks that take place in the economy and in the world that don't require Einstein. Where a 120 IQ person is great — you don't need a 160 IQ PhD in string theory. You just have somebody who's competent and capable, and it's great.

I tend to think the AI industry is going to be structured a lot like the computer industry ended up getting structured: you're going to have a small handful of basically the equivalent of supercomputers, which are these like giant, you know, kind of what we call god models that are running in these giant data centers. And then — I'm not convinced on this, but my working assumption is — what happens is then you have this cascade down of smaller models, all the way to the very small models that run on embedded systems, run on individual chips inside every physical item in the world.

The smartest models will always be at the top, but the volume of models will actually be the smaller models that proliferate out. That's what happened with microchips. It's what happened with computers, which became microchips. And then it's what happened with operating systems and with a lot of everything else that we built in software. So I tend to think that's what will happen.

Section 4: The Chip Wars

Just quickly on the chip side — again, if you look at the entire history of the chip industry, shortages become gluts. Anytime there's a giant profit pool in a new chip category, somebody has a lead for a while and kind of gets, let's say, the profits appropriate to what we call robust market share. But in time, that draws competition. And of course that's happening right now.

Nvidia is an absolutely fantastic company, fully deserves the position that they're in, fully deserves the profits that they're generating. But they're now so valuable, generating so many profits, that it's the Bat-Signal of all time to the rest of the chip industry to figure out how to advance the state-of-the-art AI chips. And that's already happening. You've got other major companies like AMD coming at them. And then you've got, really significantly, the hyperscalers building their own chips. A bunch of the big tech companies are building their own chips. And of course then the Chinese are building their own chips as well.

It's pretty likely in 5 years that AI chips will be cheap and plentiful, at least in comparison to the situation today. Which again I think will tend to be extremely positive for the economics of the kinds of companies that we invest in.

Q: And startups are also starting to go after new chip design as well, which is exciting.

The GPU Happenstance

Yeah. Well, that's the other thing — you have these disruptive startups. Actually, just for a moment on the chips: we were not really big investors in chips because it's kind of a big-company thing. But it's a little bit of historical happenstance that AI is running on quote-unquote GPUs — GPU stands for graphical processing unit.

Just for people who haven't tracked this: there were basically two kinds of chips that made the personal computer happen. The so-called CPU (central processing unit), which classically was the Intel x86 chip — that's kind of the brain of the computer. And then there was this other kind of chip called the GPU, or graphical processing unit, that was the sort of second chip in every PC that does all the graphics. This is graphics — 3D graphics for gaming, or for CAD/CAM, or for anything else, Photoshop, anything that involves lots of visuals.

The canonical architecture for a personal computer was a CPU and a GPU — by the way, same thing for smartphones. Over time these have kind of merged, and so a lot of CPUs now have GPU capability built in. Actually, a lot of GPUs now have CPU capability built in. So this has gotten fuzzy over time, but that was the classic breakdown.

The fact that that was the classic breakdown kind of meant that while Intel had a monopoly for a long time on CPUs, there was this other market of GPUs which Nvidia basically fought the GPU wars for 30 years and came out the winner — was the best company in the space. But it was a hyper-competitive market for graphics processors. It was actually not that high margin and it was actually not that big.

And then basically it just turned out that there were two other forms of computation that were incredibly valuable that happened to be massively parallel in how they operate — which happened to be very good fits for the GPU architecture. And those two basically highly lucrative additional applications were cryptocurrency, starting about 15 years ago, and then AI, starting about four years ago.

Nvidia, I would say very cleverly, set itself up with an architecture that works very well for this. But it's also just a little bit of a twist of fate that it just turns out that if AI is the killer app, it just turns out that the GPU architecture is the best legacy architecture devoted to it.

Purpose-Built AI Chips

I go through that to say: if you were designing AI chips from scratch today, you wouldn't build a full GPU. You would build dedicated AI chips that were much more specifically adapted to AI, and would just be much more economically efficient. And John to your point, there are startups that are actually building entirely new kinds of chips oriented specifically for AI.

We'll have to see what happens there. It's hard to build a new chip company from scratch. It's possible that one or more of those startups makes it on their own — some of them are doing very well. It's also possible of course that they get bought by big companies that have the ability to scale them.

And of course, we'll also see: the Koreans are going to play here for sure. The Japanese are going to play. And then the Chinese in a major way as well. They have their own native chip ecosystem that they're building up. There are going to be many choices of AI chips in the future. That'll be a giant battle that we observe very carefully, and that we make sure our companies basically are able to take full advantage of.

Section 5: China, Open Source, and the New Cold War

Q: You mentioned Kimi earlier. It seems like some of the best open-source models today are from China. Should this be worrisome? How are you thinking and talking about this in DC? I know you were just there last week. How much of this is a concern for US companies, particularly just having seen the rise of China do unnatural things in solar markets, car markets? Are they kind of flooding the ecosystem so that they can eventually take share and increasingly own the ecosystem?

A New Cold War — But More Complicated

Yeah, so a couple things. You want to start these discussions by saying: look, there's vigorous debate in the US and around the world of how much are we in a new cold war with China, and exactly how hostile should we view them. It's very tempting — and I think a very good case is made — that we're in like a new cold war that in a lot of ways is like the US versus USSR in the 20th century.

The counterargument would be: it is more complicated than that, because the US and the USSR were never really intertwined from a trade standpoint. A big part of that quite frankly was that the USSR never really made anything that anybody else needed — I guess other than weapons. The USSR's primary exports were literally like wheat and oil. Whereas of course China exports a tremendous number of physical things, including like a huge part of the entire supply chain of parts that basically go into everything that American manufacturers make.

By the time an American company brings a toy to market, or a car, or a computer or a smartphone or whatever, it's got a lot of componentry in it that was made in China. So there is a much tighter interlinkage between the American and Chinese economies than there was the American and Soviet economies.

And maybe Adam Smith or whatever might say that's good news for peace and that both countries need each other. By the way, the other part of that argument is that the Chinese — the Chinese governance model is based on high employment, because if China ended up with like 25 or 50% unemployment that would cause civil unrest, which is the one thing that the CCP doesn't want. So the corresponding part of the trade pressure is: China needs the American export market. The American consumer is like a third of the global economy — a third of global consumer demand. China needs the US export market, or it has — all of a sudden a lot of its factories would go kind of instantly bankrupt, and that would cause mass unemployment and unrest in China.

So there is this complicated intertwined relationship.

The DC Mood

Having said that, the mood in DC basically for the last 10 years on a bipartisan basis has been that we — the US — need to take China more seriously as a geopolitical foe. Under that school of thought there's the military dimension — the risk of some kind of war in the South China Sea, the risk of some kind of war around Taiwan. That has everybody in Washington on high alert. There's also this economic question around the de-industrialization of the US, potential re-industrialization, and what that means about dependence on China. And then there's this AI question.

The AI question is an economic question, but it's also a geopolitical question: AI is essentially only being built in the US and in China. The rest of the world either can't build it or doesn't want to, which we could talk about. So it's basically US versus China. And then AI is going to proliferate all over the world, and is it going to be American AI that proliferates all over the world, or is it going to be Chinese AI that proliferates all over the world?

Just generally across party lines in DC, that's kind of how they look at it.

China Is in the Game

And the Chinese are in the game. With software — DeepSeek was kind of the big firing of the starting gun in the software race. And now you've got, I think, four — it's like DeepSeek, which is an AI model from actually a hedge fund in China. Then Qwen is the model from Alibaba. Kimi is from another startup called Moonshot — the company's called Moonshot. And then there's also Tencent and Baidu. And ByteDance. That are all primary companies doing a lot of work in AI. There's somewhere between three to six primary AI companies. And then there's tremendous numbers of startups.

So they're in the race on software. They are working to catch up on chips. They're not there yet, but they're working incredibly hard to catch up. Just as an example of that: at least the common understanding in the US is that the reason you haven't seen the new version of DeepSeek yet is that basically the Chinese government has instructed them to build it only on Chinese chips, as a motivator to get the Chinese chip ecosystem up and running. The main chip company there is Huawei, although there could be more in the future.

And then there's everything to follow, which is basically AI in kind of robotic form. There's this basically global technological-economic robotics competition that's kicking off. China kind of starts out ahead on robotics because they're just ahead on so many of the components that go into robots — because the kind of entire supply chain of electromechanical things basically moved from the US to China 30 years ago and has never come back.

The DeepSeek Supernova

So that's kind of the DC lens on it. DC is watching it quite carefully. The big kind of supernova moment this year was the DeepSeek release. The DeepSeek release was surprising on a number of fronts.

One was just how good it was — and again, along this line of: it took the capability set that were running in large models in the cloud and kind of shrunk it into a reduced-size, smaller version of sort of equivalent capabilities that you could run on small amounts of local hardware.

And then it was also a surprise that it was released as open source — and particularly open source from China, because China does not have a long history of open source.

And then it was also a surprise that it actually came from a hedge fund. It didn't come from a big R&D university research lab. It didn't come from a big tech company. It came from a hedge fund. And, as far as we can tell, it basically is this somewhat idiosyncratic situation where you just have this incredibly successful quant hedge fund with all these super-geniuses, and the founder of that hedge fund basically decided to build AI. At least external indications are this was a surprise even to the Chinese government. It's impossible to prove what the Chinese government was surprised by or not, but at least the atmospherics are that this was not exactly planned — this was not a national champion tech company at the time that DeepSeek was released. It kind of came out of left field.

Which by the way is very encouraging for the field: that it was possible for somebody to do that kind of who was unknown. Because it kind of means that maybe you don't need all these super-genius superstar researchers — maybe actually smart kids can just build this stuff, which I think is the direction things are headed.

Dumping, or Real Race?

That kicked off — I don't know, "copycats" is the wrong word — but it feels like the success of DeepSeek, and the success of DeepSeek from China as open source, kind of kicked off a trend in China releasing these open-source models.

Look, the cynics in DC would say: yeah, they're dumping. They're obviously dumping. They see that the West has this opportunity to build this AI industry. They're trying to commoditize it right out of the gate. There's probably something to that. The Chinese industrial economy does have a history of, let's say, subsidized production that leads to selling things below cost in some cases.

But I think also — it's like, that's almost too cynical of a view also, because it's just like, all right wow, they're really in the race. Like, open source, closed source, whatever — they're actually really in the race.

How DC Policy Changed

We've talked in the past, I think, on LP calls about these policy fights that we've been having in DC for the last two years. There was a pretty big push within the US government two years ago to basically restrict, or outright ban, a lot of AI.

It's very easy for a country that is the only game in town to have those conversations. It's quite another thing if you're actually in a foot race with China. So I think actually the policy landscape in DC has, I would say, improved dramatically as a consequence of an awareness now that this is actually a two-horse race, not a one-horse race.

Section 6: Policy & Regulation — 1,200 State Bills

Q: On the point, I'll jump ahead to policy and regulation, because it seems like the current stance on 50 different sets of AI laws by state seems like a catastrophic way to effectively put us with one of our hands tied behind our back in terms of the AI race. What's the state of play on that? Are folks recognizing that would be catastrophic for progress and development? Where do most people stand on that topic today?

Federal: Better Than Two Years Ago

Yeah, so it's a little bit complicated. I'll rewind to say: like, two years ago I was very worried about really ruinous federal legislation on AI. We engaged very heavily at that point, which we've talked about in the past. And I think the good news on that is: I think the risk of that, sitting here today, is very low. There's very little mood in DC on either side of the aisle — essentially there's very little interest in doing anything that would prevent us from beating China. So on the federal side, things are much better now. There will be issues and there are tensions in the system, but things are looking pretty good.

The State Problem: ~1,200 Bills

That has translated — Jen, to your point — that's translated a lot of the attention to the states. Basically what's happened is: under our system of federalism, the states get to pass their own laws on a lot of things. And with these things it's always a combination. A lot of well-meaning people are trying to figure out what to do at the state level. And then of course there's a lot of opportunism, where AI is just the hot topic. If you're an aggressive up-and-coming state legislator in some state and you want to run for governor and then president, you want to kind of attach yourself to the heat. There's a political motivation to do state-level stuff.

Sitting here today, we're tracking on the order of 1,200 bills across the 50 states. And by the way, not just the blue states — also the red states. I've for the last like 5 years spent a lot of time complaining about what Democratic politicians are threatening to do to attack — there's also a lot of Republicans. Republicans are not a block on this. There are quite a few local Republican officials in different states that also I think have, let's say, misinformed or ill-advised views, and are trying to put together bad bills.

It's a little bit weird that this is happening. The federal government does have regulation of interstate commerce. Technology, AI, kind of by definition is interstate — there's no AI company that just operates in California or just operates in Colorado or Texas. AI of all technologies is obviously something national in scope. It's sort of obvious that the federal government should be the regulator, not the states. But the federal government needs to assert itself, needs to step in.

The Moratorium That Didn't Happen

There was actually an attempt to do that. There was an attempt to add a moratorium of state-level AI regulation that basically would reserve the right of the federal government to regulate AI and prevent the states from moving forward with these bills. That was, I think, part of the negotiation for the quote "one big beautiful bill." And then that — there was a deal behind that, and that deal kind of blew up at the last minute, and that moratorium didn't happen.

In fairness to the critics of that moratorium — it probably was too much of a stretch. Well, it was — I'm sorry — it was definitely too much of a stretch to get enough support to pass, but it was also probably too much of a stretch in terms of restricting the states from certain kinds of regulation that they really should be able to do. So it just didn't quite come together.

There's very active discussions in DC right now about the next turn on that. The administration is very supportive of the idea of the federal government being in charge of this, as part of it being an actual 50-state issue — and an issue of national importance. Most Congress people on both sides of the aisle kind of get this. We just have to figure out a way to land this, but I think that'll happen.

Colorado, Europe & SB 1047

Some of the state-level bills are wild. Colorado passed a very draconian regulation bill last year, against furious objections from the local startup ecosystem in and around Denver and Boulder. Actually, they're now actually trying to reverse their way out of that bill a year later — some of the nuance of it, like the algorithmic discrimination and how to mitigate some of the extreme versions of what they had proposed.

Yeah. So the really draconian one — the one we really fought hard — was the one in California, which was called SB 1047. It basically was modeled after what was called the EU AI Act, the European Union's AI Act.

And this is the backdrop to all the US stuff: the EU passed this bill called the AI Act, I don't know, whatever, two years ago, and it basically has killed AI development in — well, it's actually killed AI development in Europe to a large extent. It's so draconian that even big American companies like Apple and Meta are not launching leading-edge AI capabilities in their products in Europe. That's how draconian that bill was.

It's a classic kind of European thing where they just thought that — they have this kind of view that, well, you know — they literally say this, by the way: "If we can't be the leaders in innovation, at least we can be the leaders in regulation." And then they pass this incredibly ruinous self-harm kind of thing, and then a few years pass and they're like: "Oh my god, what have we done." And so they're kind of going through their own version of that.

By the way, when I talk about Europe, I tend to be very dark about the whole thing. I will tell you — the darkest people I know about Europe are the European entrepreneurs who moved to the US. They're just absolutely furious about what's happening in Europe on this stuff.

But even there — it's so bad in Europe, they shot themselves in the foot so badly — that there's actually a process now at the EU to try to unwind that. They're trying to unwind the GDPR. For people tracking Europe: Mario Draghi, the former prime minister of Italy, did this thing about a year ago called the Draghi Report, which is the report on European competitiveness, and he outlined in great detail all the ways that Europe was holding itself back — and part of it was overregulation, areas like AI. So they're trying to reverse out of that — or making gestures. We'll see what happens.

California's Copycat

In the middle of all that, California sort of inexplicably decided to basically copycat the EU AI Act and try to apply it to California. Which might strike you as completely insane — to which I would say: yes, welcome to California. It was this basically Sacramento political dynamic that kind of got crazy. It would have completely killed AI development in California. Fortunately, our governor vetoed it at the last minute. It did pass both houses of the legislature, but he vetoed it at the last minute.

It, to Jen to your point, would have done a whole bunch of things that were ruinously bad. But one of the things it would have done: it would have assigned downstream liability to open-source developers.

We talked about this Chinese open source thing. OK, so you've got Chinese out there with open source. Now you're going to have American companies that have open-source AI. And by the way, you're also going to have American academics and just independent people in their nights and weekends developing open source — which is a key way that all this technology proliferates.

This law would have assigned downstream liability to any misuse of open source, back to the original developer of the open source. So you're an independent developer, or you're an academic, or you're a startup — you develop and release an AI model, the AI model works fine the day you release it, it's great. But like 5 years later it gets built into a nuclear power plant, and then there's a meltdown of the nuclear power plant, and then somebody says: "Oh, it's the fault of the AI" — the legal liability for that nuclear meltdown, or for any other practical real-world thing that would follow in the out years, would then be assigned back to that open-source developer.

Of course, this is completely insane. It would completely kill open source. It would completely kill startups doing open source. It would completely kill academic research, like in its entirety — anything in the field. That's the level of playing with fire that these state-level politicians have become enamored with.

Like I said, I think the good news is the feds understand this. I suspect that this is going to get resolved, but it does need to get resolved, because, just as a country, it just doesn't make any sense to let the states kind of operate suicidally like this.

Little Tech and Why Us

And so that's what we're doing. We talk about this — we call this our "little tech" agenda. We're extremely focused on the freedom of startups to innovate. We are not trying to argue many other issues. We operate in a completely bipartisan fashion. We have extensive support on both sides of the aisle and for both sides of the aisle. It's a truly bipartisan effort — very policy-based, and I think very much aligned with the interests of the country broadly. That is what we're doing.

And then the other question we get — in some cases from LPs, but in a lot of cases actually from employees — is: "OK, why us?" With any policy question like this, there's always this collective action question, which is just like tragedy of the commons: in theory, every venture firm, every tech company, whatever, should be weighing in on these things. In practice what happens is: most of them just simply don't. At some point it falls on somebody's shoulders to fight these things. Ben and I just basically concluded that the stakes here were just way too high. If we're going to be the industry leader, we just have to take responsibility for our own destiny. For better or for worse, I think that's the cost of doing business, of being the leader in the field right now.

Section 7: Usage-Based vs. Seat Pricing — A Trillion-Dollar Question

Q: Before we get off the topic of AI, I want to go back to one question that was submitted in. Do you think usage-based or utility is the right way to price AI compared to seats?

Ah, that is a fantastic question. So this is one of these giant — this is in my list of what I call the "trillion-dollar questions," where depending on how this is answered will drive trillions of dollars of market value.

The Gift the Big Clouds Gave Startups

So yeah, usage-based pricing — it's actually fairly amazing if you think about this from a startup standpoint, from a venture standpoint. It's actually fairly amazing what's happened. And I'm not really talking about this in public because I don't want it to stop. I think it's actually quite amazing.

Which is: you have these technology companies — these big tech companies, with these incredible R&D capabilities — that are building these big models, these big AI models with this incredible new kind of intelligence. And then it turns out that they were already in a war. They were already in the cloud war — the war for cloud services. This is AWS versus Azure versus Google Cloud, and all these other cloud efforts.

What actually happened was — there's an alternate universe in which they basically just kept all of their magic AI secret and captive, and just used it in their own business, or used it to compete with more companies in more categories. But instead what they've done is they've basically — if "commoditized" is too strong a word — they have proliferated their magic new technology through their cloud business. Which is this business that just has these incredible scale components to it, and this sort of hyper-competition between the providers, and these prices that come down very fast.

You've got the most magic new technology in the world, and then it's basically being served up by those companies as a cloud business and made basically available to everybody on the planet to just click and use, for relatively small amounts of money, and on a usage basis. Which means — and usage is great for startups, because it means you can start easily. There's basically no fixed cost for a startup building an AI app. They don't have giant fixed cost, because they can just tap into the OpenAI or Anthropic or Google or Microsoft or whatever "tokens by the drink" — intelligence tokens by the drink — offering, and just get going.

From the startup standpoint, it's this marvelous thing where the most magical thing in the world is available by the drink. It's absolutely amazing. That model's working. Those companies are happy and they're growing really fast, and they're happily reporting massive cloud revenue growth. They're happy with the margins and so forth. Generally it's working. Those businesses are likely to get much larger.

Price by Value, Not by Cost

But to the question: that doesn't mean that the optimal pricing model for, for example, all of the applications should be tokens by the drink. And in fact, very much I think not the case. We spend a lot of time working — we actually have dedicated experts on pricing in our firm. We spend a lot of time with our companies working on pricing, because it's really this magical art and science that a lot of companies don't take seriously enough.

A core principle of pricing is: you don't want to price by cost if you can avoid it. You want to price by value. You want to price where you're getting a percentage of the business value — especially when you're selling to businesses, you want to price as a percentage of the business value that you're getting.

You do have some AI startups that are pricing by the drink for certain things that they're doing, but you have many others that are exploring other pricing models. Some that are just replications of SaaS pricing models, but you also have other companies exploring pricing models for: well, if the AI can actually do the job of a coder, or the AI could do the job of a doctor, or a nurse, or a radiologist, or a lawyer, or a paralegal, or a teacher — can you price by value, and can you get a percentage of the value of what otherwise would have been literally a person?

Or, by the way, equivalently: can you price by marginal productivity? If you can take a human doctor and make them much more productive because you give them AI — can you price as a percentage of the productivity uplift from the symbiotic relationship between the human being and the AI?

High Prices Are Underrated

What we see in startup land is like a lot of experimentation happening on these pricing models. And I think that's super healthy.

I was in this little speech on this — high prices are really underappreciated. High prices are often a favorite of the customer. It's actually really funny. A lot of the naive view on pricing is: the lower the price, the better it is for the customer. The more sophisticated look at it is: higher prices are often good for the customer, because a higher price means that the vendor can make the product better faster. Companies with higher prices, higher margins, can actually invest more in R&D, and can actually make the product better.

Most people who buy things aren't just looking for the cheapest price. They want something that's going to work really well. Often high prices — the customer doesn't ever say this, it'll never show up in a survey — but the high price can actually be a gift for the customer, because it can make the vendor better, can make the product better, and ultimately make the customer better off.

I'm very encouraged by the degree to which the AI entrepreneurs are willing to run these experiments. We'll have to see where it pans out, but at least so far, I feel good about the attitude of the industry about it.

Section 8: Open vs. Closed Source

Q: Awesome. I had probably 10 more follow-up questions, but I'm actually going to go back to a topic you had briefly, the trillion-dollar questions. Will open source or closed source win? Feels like we've come out on this debate — or where do you put that?

Still Open

No, I think this is still open. I think this is still very open.

The closed-source models keep getting better. By the way, generally if you just take the temperature of the people working at the big labs who work on the big proprietary models, what they'll tell you is progress is continuing at a very rapid pace. There's this periodic concern that shows up online or in the market — which is like, maybe the capabilities of these models are topping out. There's certain areas in which people are working, but the people working at the big labs are like: "Oh no, we have 800 new ideas. We have tons of new ideas. We have tons of new ways of doing things. We might need to find new ways to scale, but we have a lot of ideas on how to do that. We know a lot of ways to make these things better, and we're basically making new discoveries all the time."

I would say generally the people working across all the big labs are pretty optimistic. So I think the big models are going to continue to get better very quickly here.

And then the open-source models continue to get better. Every month or something there's another big release of something like this Kimi thing, where it's just like: "Wow, that's amazing. They really shrunk that down and got that capability on a very small form factor."

Open Source as Education

The other really nice benefit of open source is that open source is the thing that's easy to learn from. If you're a computer science professor who wants to teach a class on AI, or if you're a computer science student trying to learn about it, or if you're just a normal engineer in a normal company trying to learn this new thing — or, by the way, somebody in a basement at night with a startup idea — the existence of these state-of-the-art open-source models is amazing, because that's the education that you need. These open-source models actually show you how to do everything.

What that's leading to, right, is the proliferation of the knowledge about how to build AI is expanding very fast. Again, as compared to a counterfactual world in which it was all basically bottled up in two or three big companies. So the open-source thing is also just proliferating knowledge, and then that knowledge is generating a lot of new people.

As you guys have all seen sitting here today, AI researchers are at an enormous premium. AI researchers today are getting paid more than professional athletes. That's a supply-demand imbalance — there aren't enough of them to go around. But again: shortages create gluts. The number of smart people in the world who are coming up to speed very quickly on how to build these things — some of the best AI people in the world are like 22, 23, 24. Kind of by definition, they haven't been in the field that long — they can't have been experts their whole lives. They kind of have to have come up to speed over the course of the last four or five years. If they've been able to do that, then there's going to be a lot more in the future that are going to do that. The spread of the level of expertise on this technology is happening now very quickly.

Maybe Both

I think it's still a race. And by the way, look, the long-term answer may well just be both. If you believe my pyramid industry structure, then there will certainly be a large business of whatever is the smartest thing, almost regardless of how much it costs. But there will also be this giant volume market of smaller models everywhere — which is what we're also seeing.

Section 9: Incumbents vs. Startups & The "GPT Wrapper" Myth

Q: The other question you had posed at that point in time was: will incumbents versus startups win? And at that point in time, I think there was a mixed bag of where the incumbents were approaching AI. I think that's radically changed in the last two years. And then on the counter example — the blossoming of startups increasingly now maybe migrating into the incumbent category, just how big they are since that time. You want to take that question and give your assessment of where the state of the world is?

Incumbents and New Incumbents

Yeah. Look, big companies are definitely playing hard. Google's playing hard. Meta's playing hard. Amazon, Microsoft — there's a bunch of these companies that are in there very aggressively. And then you've got these what we call the "new incumbents," like Anthropic and OpenAI.

But you also have — in the last two years, you've had this birth of all of a sudden brand-new companies that are almost instant incumbents. You could say xAI is one of those. Mistral, by the way, Mistral is the great outlier to my Europe thing from earlier. Mistral is actually doing very well as sort of the European — kind of French national, European continental AI champion — the exception that proves the rule. But there's a bunch of these now that are doing quite well and are kind of becoming new incumbents.

And then of course there's tons of startups. There's actual foundation-model startups. We funded Ilya, out of OpenAI, to do a new foundation model company. We funded Mira Murati, also out of OpenAI. We funded Fei-Fei Li out of Stanford to do a world foundation model company. There are new swings — all early, but very promising — to build new incumbents quickly.

The Application Layer Explosion

And then on top of that, there's just this giant explosion of AI application companies. Startups that basically take the technology and then field it in a specific domain — whether that's law or medicine or education or creativity or whatever. Again here, it's amazing how sophisticated things are getting very quickly.

Talk about the application companies for a moment. An application company — like, a classic example is like Cursor. They take the core AI capability, which they purchase by the drink from Anthropic or OpenAI or Google — tokens by the drink — and then they build basically a code editor (what we used to call an IDE, integrated development environment), basically a software creation system. So they build an AI coding system on top of the Anthropic or OpenAI or whatever big models, field it — and the critique of those companies in the industry has been: "Oh, those are what are called GPT wrappers" — that's kind of the pejorative.

The idea basically being: well, they're not actually doing anything that's going to preserve value, because the whole point of what they're doing is they're surfacing AI, but it's not their AI. The AI that's being surfaced is from somebody else. So these are kind of pass-through shell things that ultimately won't have value.

Actually, the Opposite Is Happening

It actually turns out what's happening is kind of the opposite of that. Which is: the leading AI application companies like Cursor — first of all, what they're discovering is they're not just using a single AI model. They actually — as these products get more sophisticated — they actually end up using many different kinds of models that are kind of custom-tailored to the specific aspects of how these products work. They may start out using one model, but they end up using a dozen models, and then in the fullness of time it might be 50 or 100 different models for different aspects of the product.

And then B, they end up building a lot of their own models. A lot of the leading-edge application companies are actually backward-integrating and actually building their own AI models. Because they have the deepest understanding of their domain, and they're able to build the model that's best suited to that. And then by the way, also AI open source — they're also able to pick up and run open-source models. If they don't like the economics of buying intelligence by the drink from a cloud service provider, they can pick up one of these open-source models and implement it instead. Which these companies are also doing.

The best of the AI application companies — they are actually full-fledged deep technology companies, actually building their own AI.

Q: Small models, though, right Mark? When you think about god models versus small models, as you were describing that — but that would be "small." Would you categorize that as small?

Well, some of them. I will let them announce whatever they're doing whenever it's appropriate. But some of them are now also doing big-model development.

No Permanent Lead

Again, this is also part of the learning just in the last two years. Here's a big learning just from the last two years, which is very interesting. Two years ago, or three years ago for sure, you would have said: wow, OpenAI is way out ahead, and it's probably going to be impossible for anybody to catch up. And then it's like: OK, well, Anthropic caught up. But they came out of OpenAI, and so they had all the secrets, whatever, and so knew how to do it. OK, they caught up, but surely nobody can catch up after them. And then very quickly after that, there were a raft of other companies that caught up very fast.

xAI is maybe the best example of that. Elon's company, xAI — that's the company name. Grok is the consumer product version of it. xAI basically caught up to state-of-the-art OpenAI-Anthropic level in less than 12 months from a standing start. That kind of argues against any kind of permanent lead by any one incumbent that's just going to basically be able to lock the entire market down — if you can catch up like that.

And then, as we've discussed, the China part is all new in the last year. The DeepSeek moment I think was in January or February of this year — less than 12 months ago. And now you've got like four Chinese companies that have effectively caught up.

So it's like: all right. I mean again, these are trillion-dollar questions, not answers. But it's just like, wow, OK, like it's one of these things where once somebody proves that it's capable, it seems to not be that hard for other people to be able to catch up — even people with far less resources.

I don't know what that does. Maybe it makes you slightly more skeptical in the long run about the economics of the big players. On the other hand, maybe it makes you more bullish about the startup ecosystem. It certainly should make you more bullish about startup application companies being able to do interesting things, which is why we're so excited about that. It should make you probably a bit more excited about — certainly about China. On the other hand, the Chinese competition putting pressure on the American system to not screw itself up is very positive, so it should probably make you a little bit more bullish on the US.

Yeah, these are live dynamics, and I think we still need more time to pass before we know the exact answer.

Why Open Questions Are a Venture Advantage

I should say this, because sometimes I freak people out when I say these are open questions. When a company is confronted with fundamentally open strategic or economic questions, it's often a big problem — because a company needs to have a strategy, and the strategy needs to be very specific. A company has to make very specific concrete choices about where it deploys investment dollars and personnel. The strategy has to be logical and coherent or the company kind of collapses into chaos. Companies need to answer these questions, and if they get the answers wrong, they're really in trouble.

Venture — we have our issues in venture, but a huge advantage we have is: we don't have to. We can bet on multiple strategies at the same time. And we are doing this. We are betting on big models and small models and pre-trained models and open-source models, and foundation models and applications, and consumer and enterprise.

The portfolio approach — the nature of it is: we are aggressively investing behind every strategy that we've identified that we think has a plausible chance of working, even when that's contradictory to another strategy we're investing in. One is just: the world's messy, and probably a bunch of things are going to work, and so there's not going to be clean yes-or-no answers to a bunch of this. A lot of the answers to this I think are just going to be "and" answers. But the other is: if one of these strategies doesn't work — we're not trying to hedge per se, but we're going to have representation in the portfolio of the alternate strategy, and so we're going to have multiple ways to win.

That's the goal. That's the theory of why we are kind of taking the approach in the space that we're taking. And that's why I have a big smile on my face when I say that there are these big open questions — because I think that actually works to our advantage.

Section 10: a16z Inside Baseball

Q: It's a good segue to a16z questions, because we've gotten a few in so far, and we had a few that were sent in ahead as well. I'll start with a broad topic: what is something you and Ben disagree and commit on?

Disagree and Commit

Disagree-and-commit. You know, we agree — I mean, we — Ben — I was going to say, you know, we're an old married couple, so we argue constantly. But we've been —

(Interviewer: "The romance is dead.")

The romance is long dead. Yes. Yes. Yes. The fire has long since gone out. Yes, if you — yes. We're in the park squabbling all the time.

We debate everything. We argue about everything. That said, one of the things that's made our partnership work is we do tend to come to the same conclusion. Each of us is open to being persuaded by the other one, and so we end up coming to the same conclusion most of the time.

I would say, sitting here today, there are zero issues where I'm sitting here and I'm like: I can't believe I'm putting up with this crazy thing on his part that he's doing, that I really disagree with but I feel like I have to commit to. I don't think vice versa. We don't have any of those.

The Public Footprint Debate

Quite honestly, the biggest thing that he and I discuss — this is not the most important thing we're doing, but it is a topic since somebody asked the question — the biggest thing he and I discuss, where I don't know, maybe I'm always second-guessing myself — or I never quite know where I should come out on it — that he and I talk about a lot, is just basically the public footprint of the company.

So, like, our presence in the world, in terms of public statements, controversy, how we vocalize and express our views on things. There's a real, very important tension. Generally speaking, the more out there we are, and the more outspoken we are, and the more controversial we are, the better for the business — in the sense of: the entrepreneurs love it. The founders want to work with, it is very clear at this point, the founders want to work with people who basically are brave and controversial and take controversial stands and articulate things clearly. And they want that for a bunch of reasons.

One is because it's a demonstration of courage, which they appreciate. But the other is because it teaches them who we are before they even meet us. And that has just proven to be this incredible competitive advantage.

Long-term LPs will know: this is why we started with a very active marketing strategy from the very beginning, and it completely worked. The whole thing was: if we're able to broadcast our message, and we're able to basically be very clear in what we believe — even to the point where it's controversial — the best founders in the world are going to understand us before they even walk in the door. They're going to know us even before they've met us. As opposed to everybody else in venture, at least at the time, that was basically just keeping everything quiet, where the founder just has no idea who these people are and what they believe.

That worked incredibly well. It continues to work incredibly well. It's generally true across the industry — it's generally the case.

The Externalities

On the other hand, there are externalities to being publicly visible, and to being controversial, on many fronts. We are, I would say — we're trying very hard to thread this needle. We're not backing off of generally being a company that does a lot of outbound. Eric Wurzelberg and the team that he's built, that we've talked to you guys about in the past — is already off to the races. We're tripling down on the idea of basically being the leaders in articulating the tech and business issues that matter. The issues for sure that people need to be able to understand. That's proven to be very effective.

By the way, a fair amount of our comms are actually aimed at Washington. If you're a policy maker in Washington, and you're sitting there 3,000 miles away, and your entire information source is East Coast newspapers that hate Silicon Valley — that's bad. Our ability to broadcast informed points of view on technology — we meet people in DC all the time who say: "Yeah, most of what I know about this topic I learned from you guys, because I listened to the podcast, I read the articles, I watched the YouTube channel." We're going to continue to do that.

Overall we're kind of on our front foot on that stuff. But yeah, he and I do go back and forth a bit on exactly how many third-rail topics should we touch, and how frequently. We are trying to moderate that.

(Interviewer: "As Elizabeth Taylor said, 'As long as they spell our name right,' it's oftentimes could be good in most scenarios, particularly when it comes to little tech. And also I think embedded in that question is probably some degree of the relationship that you and Ben have, which is now going on 30 plus years at this point. So much so that Mark has become one person representing both — some people refer to Mark as 'Andre and Horowitz,' Mark have combined just into one person. That's the result of 30-plus years working together.")

Yes.

Section 11: Reorganizing Around AI — What Did You Get Right?

Q: It's been 2 years since you've reorganized around AI, launched American Dynamism. What do you think you got most right? And in hindsight, is there anything that you underestimated or missed in that decisioning process?

Riding the Wave

No, I mean, look, we made plenty of mistakes. I think those were the right calls. AI was — like I said — the whole theory of venture that we've had from the beginning, that many people before us have had as well — that's very correct I think. The whole theory is: the money in venture is made when there's a fundamental architecture shift. When there's a fundamental change in the technology landscape.

That's been true for venture basically forever. The reason is: if you have a fundamental change in technology, then you have this period of creativity in which you can have basically very aggressive kind of people start these new companies, and they have this kind of shot to come in and win categories before big companies can respond. If there's no fundamental change in technology, it's very hard to make startups work, because the big companies just end up doing everything. So venture kind of lives or dies on the basis of these waves of transitions.

There's always this question. The best venture capital firms in history, I think, are the ones that were the most aggressive at being able to navigate from wave to wave.

The Kleiner Perkins Example

Look, I was a beneficiary of this when I came to Silicon Valley in '94. There was no venture firm in 1994 that was like "the internet venture capital firm" — it just didn't exist. But there were a set of venture capital firms at the time — our firm Kleiner Perkins — that said: "Oh, this is a new architecture. This is a new technology change. It seems totally crazy. Everybody says you can't make money on it. Whatever, whatever. These kids are nuts. But we're going to make those bets."

They were willing to invest. And by the way, KP in the '90s invested not only in us, but also in Amazon and then Google, and company after company after company. They invested in @Home, which basically made home broadband work. They invested in a fleet of companies. And they were a venture capital firm that had started in the 1970s around really what was at the time called minicomputers — which was like three generations of technology back — and they had navigated from wave to wave. The same thing is true for Sequoia. The same thing is true for basically any successful venture firm that has been in business for 30 or 40 or 50 years.

In this business of all businesses, you need to get onto the new thing. Quite honestly, it was pretty amazing that most of the venture ecosystem just decided to sit crypto out. The number of VCs that we talked to between — call it — the release of the Bitcoin white paper in 2009 and the beginning of the crypto war in 2021, who just basically said "Oh, we're not going to do crypto" — I never quite know what to do with the VC who says: "Oh, there's a new wave of technology, and I'm very deliberately not going to participate in it." And I'm always like: "Is that not the job?"

I was fairly amazed by the VCs that didn't make the jump to crypto. They looked briefly smart during the crypto wars, I would say, of the last three or four years. I think they probably look maybe a little bit less smart now. AI is another one of these where there are certain firms that are jumping all over it, and there are certain firms that are just kind of sitting back and letting it happen. And by the way, there were certain firms that never made it to the internet. There were firms that were very well known in the 80s and very successful, that just did not make the jump to the internet, and basically just petered out.

Long-winded way of saying: I think in this business, of all businesses, you have to jump on the new wave. I think we got the magnitude of it right — that this is a fundamental, fundamental transformation — inside the firm.

American Dynamism and the Flywheel

AD is doing great. AD itself I believe is also a beneficiary of AI, in two ways. One is a lot of the kinds of products that AD companies build themselves benefit from AI. And then also, AI is a driver of demand in other sectors of AD — like energy and materials. I think that generally is very consistent and is working well.

By the way, crypto's back to being, I would say, an exciting industry as a consequence of all the policy changes. And there's even going to be, I think, intersections — quite a few intersections between AI and crypto. And then biotech — also bio and healthcare, I think, are obviously going to be transformed by AI both on the healthcare side and on the actual drug discovery side — and that's underway.

The individual efforts in the firm feel good and suitable for the time. The interactions between the teams, and the hybrid ideas — the companies that are coming at these things from multiple angles — feels really good.

Maybe the corollary question is: what do we feel like we're missing right now? I think the answer is really: I don't think right now we're missing a vertical. As of right now, there's not like a specific vertical where we just like — oh, we just need the equivalent of a new unit, or the equivalent of a new fund or whatever. I don't see that at the moment. I think it's more executing extremely well in the verticals that we have in front of us, and being the best possible partner to the portfolio companies.

Section 12: Society's Tech Adoption & Revealed Preferences

Q: Actually on the point of AD — because AI is creating, and there's a lot of talk around AI taking jobs, etc. — ironically enough, the jobs in AD sectors have never been more in demand in the physical world related to energy, related obviously to data center build, etc. So like the pendulum, it seems like also is swinging from just an accelerant standpoint from a society point of view. You talked about the importance of society also needing to be ready for tech adoption. Have you seen that accelerating recently? What's your sentiment of how to actually increase that, just to also make sure the convergence of adoption also falls in line with how quickly tech is actually being implemented?

300 Years of Tech Panics

Yeah. So look, we've talked about this before, but for a very long time, tech was just not a very relevant — look, if you go back 300 years, there's just recurring waves of total panic and freak-out caused by new technology. Or even you go back 500 years — you go back to the printing press, which basically was hand in hand with the creation of Protestantism, which really changed things.

There were just always kind of continuous panics there. There have been multiple waves of automation panics for the last 200 years. A lot of the foundational panic under Marxism was basically a fear of the elimination of jobs through the application of automation. A lot of the same arguments you hear today about how "AI is going to centralize all the wealth in a handful of a few people, and everybody else is going to be poor and immiserated" — that basically is what Marx used to say. Which I think was wrong then, is wrong now — we can talk about.

And then even in the 1960s there was this whole panic around AI replacing all the jobs. There's this great — it's long forgotten, but it was a big deal at the time — during the Johnson administration, you read these AI-pause letters today — like the one that just came out a few weeks ago that Prince Harry of all people headlined, where he talks about AI is going to ruin everything. And in 1964, there was basically a group of the leading lights in academia, science, and public affairs — there was this thing called the Triple Committee, or the Committee for the Triple Revolution. If you do a Google search on "Committee for the Triple Revolution Johnson White House" or whatever, this thing will pop up. It was a very similar kind of manifesto of: "We need to stop the march of technology today, or we're going to ruin everything."

And then even in the course of the last 20 years, there was a big panic around outsourcing in the 2000s — outsourcing was going to take all the jobs. And then it was actually robots, weirdly enough, in the 2010s — which is amazing because robots didn't even work in the 2010s, and they kind of still don't. But there's a panic around that. And now there's kind of whatever level of AI panic.

"The Dog That Caught the Bus"

I would just say: we in Silicon Valley have always wanted the work that we do to matter. We spend most of our time, quite honestly, with people telling us that everything we're doing is stupid and won't work — that's the default position. And then basically that flips at some point into panic about how it's going to ruin everything.

It's easy sitting out here to be cynical about that, especially when you see the patterns over time. My view is: we need to be actually very respectful of that, and we need to be very aware of that. I use the metaphor of "the dog that caught the bus" — we always wanted to work on things that matter. We are working on things that matter. People in the rest of society actually really do care about these things. And it's our responsibility to think that all through very carefully, and to do a good job — not just building the technology, but also explaining it.

I think we have a real obligation to really explain ourselves and engage on these issues.

Revealed Preferences

In terms of how to measure how it's going — it's the classic social science question: if you want to understand patterns of people, there's basically two ways to understand what people are doing and thinking. One is to ask them. And the other is to watch them.

Every social scientist, every sociologist will tell you this: basically, you can ask people — the way you do that is surveys, focus groups, polls — what they think. But then you can watch them, and you can do what's called "revealed preferences," which is: you actually watch their behavior. What you often see, in many areas of human activity — including politics, and many different aspects of society and culture over time — is the answers that you get when you ask people are very different than the answers that you get when you watch them.

The reason — you could have a bunch of theories as to why. The Marxists claim that people have "false consciousness." The explanation I believe is just: people have opinions on all kinds of things, particularly when they're in a context where they get to express themselves, and they'll have a tendency to express themselves in very heated ways. And then if you just watch their behavior, they're often a lot calmer, a lot more measured, and a lot more rational in what they do.

AI: They Love It

What's playing out in AI right now is: if you run a survey or a poll of what, for example, American voters think about AI, it's just like they're all in a total panic — it's like "Oh my god, this is terrible, this is awful, it's going to kill all the jobs, it's going to ruin everything." The whole thing. If you watch the revealed preferences, they're all using AI. They're downloading the apps. They're using ChatGPT in their job. They're — you see this online all the time now — "I'm having an argument with my boyfriend or girlfriend, I don't understand what's happening, I take the text exchange, I cut and paste it into ChatGPT, and I have ChatGPT explain to me what my partner is thinking and tell me how I should answer so that he or she is not mad at me anymore." Or "I have this skin condition and doctors, you know, da-da-da, and I take a photo and I'm finally learning about my own health." Or "I use it in my job — I had to get this report ready for Monday morning and I ran out of time, and ChatGPT really saved my bacon."

People in their daily lives — you just look at the data — they are not only using this technology, they love this technology. And they love it and they're adopting as fast as they possibly can.

I tend to think the public discussion of this is going to ping-pong back and forth for a while, because there is this divergence between what people are saying and what people are doing. But I do think that the "what people are doing" part is obviously the part that ultimately wins. And I think, by the way, I think this technology is going to be exactly the same as every other one: the thing that's going to happen here is this is just going to proliferate really broadly, it's going to freak everybody out, and then 20 years from now everybody's going to be like "Oh thank god we've got it. Like, wouldn't life be miserable if we didn't have this?" Or, you know, 5 years from now, or one year from now, people are going to reach that conclusion.

I'm very optimistic about where this lands. It's just that there will be turbulence along the way.

(Interviewer: "I'm smiling because I also witnessed that in the wild. Literally late last week I was on the plane. The guy next to me was talking to his chat, I could see him, and he was like, 'Help me draft an escalation letter to United for the delay on this flight.'
